<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define marginal probability distribution. How is the marginal PMF of $X$ obtained from the joint PMF of $X$ and $Y$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A marginal probability distribution is the probability distribution of one random variable computed from a joint distribution by **ignoring (summing/integrating out) the other variables**.

For a discrete bivariate random variable $(X, Y)$, the marginal PMF of $X$ is:

$$P_X(x) = \sum_{y \in \Omega_Y} P_{X,Y}(x, y)$$

The summation is performed over the entire support of $Y$, holding $X$ fixed. The resulting values must satisfy $P_X(x) \ge 0$ and $\sum_x P_X(x) = 1$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State two properties of a marginal probability distribution.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Non-negativity:** $P_X(x) \ge 0$ for all $x \in \Omega_X$ (or $f_X(x) \ge 0$ in continuous case). **(1 mark)**
2. **Total Probability:** $\sum_x P_X(x) = 1$ for discrete; $\int f_X(x) dx = 1$ for continuous. **(1 mark)**
3. **Derived from Joint:** It is obtained by summing or integrating the joint distribution over the unwanted variable. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**The joint PMF of two discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$ is given by:**

| $X \backslash Y$ | $Y = 0$ | $Y = 1$ | $Y = 2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$X = 0$** | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.10 |
| **$X = 1$** | 0.15 | 0.25 | 0.20 |

**(a)** Find the marginal PMF of $X$ and the marginal PMF of $Y$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute $P(X = 1 \mid Y = 1)$ and $E[X]$. Are $X$ and $Y$ independent? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Marginal PMFs (7 marks)**

**Marginal PMF of $X$ (sum each row):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X(0) &= P_{X,Y}(0,0) + P_{X,Y}(0,1) + P_{X,Y}(0,2) \\
&= 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X(1) &= P_{X,Y}(1,0) + P_{X,Y}(1,1) + P_{X,Y}(1,2) \\
&= 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marginal PMF of $Y$ (sum each column):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_Y(0) &= 0.10 + 0.15 = 0.25 \\
P_Y(1) &= 0.20 + 0.25 = 0.45 \\
P_Y(2) &= 0.10 + 0.20 = 0.30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Validation:** $0.40 + 0.60 = 1$ ✓ and $0.25 + 0.45 + 0.30 = 1$ ✓ **(Full 7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Conditional, Expected Value, Independence (7 marks)**

**Conditional Probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 1 \mid Y = 1) &= \frac{P_{X,Y}(1,1)}{P_Y(1)} = \frac{0.25}{0.45} = \frac{5}{9} \approx 0.556
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected Value of $X$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= \sum_x x \cdot P_X(x) \\
&= 0 \cdot 0.40 + 1 \cdot 0.60 \\
&= 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Independence Test:**

For independence, $P_{X,Y}(x, y) = P_X(x) \cdot P_Y(y)$ for all $(x, y)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X(0) \cdot P_Y(0) &= 0.40 \times 0.25 = 0.10 \\
P_{X,Y}(0, 0) &= 0.10 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X(0) \cdot P_Y(1) &= 0.40 \times 0.45 = 0.18 \\
P_{X,Y}(0, 1) &= 0.20 \quad \times
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.18 \neq 0.20$, $X$ and $Y$ are **NOT independent**. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**The joint PDF of random variables $X$ and $Y$ is:**

$$f_{X,Y}(x, y) = 2(x + y), \quad 0 \le x \le 1, \; 0 \le y \le 1$$

**(a)** Derive the marginal PDF $f_X(x)$ and the marginal PDF $f_Y(y)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify the independence of $X$ and $Y$, and compute $P(0 \le X \le 0.5)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derive the Marginal PDFs (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Validate the joint PDF**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 2(x+y)\,dx\,dy &= 2\int_0^1\int_0^1 (x+y)\,dx\,dy \\
&= 2\left[\int_0^1 \frac{x^2}{2} + xy \Big|_0^1 dy \right] \\
&= 2\left[\int_0^1 \left(\frac{1}{2} + y\right) dy \right] \\
&= 2\left[\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\right] = 2 \neq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The joint PDF must be normalized. The correct form is:

$$f_{X,Y}(x, y) = (x + y), \quad 0 \le x \le 1, \; 0 \le y \le 1$$

Re-validating: $\int_0^1\int_0^1 (x+y)\,dx\,dy = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1$ ✓

**Step 2: Marginal PDF of $X$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_X(x) &= \int_0^1 f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy = \int_0^1 (x+y)\,dy \\
&= \left[xy + \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^1 = x + \frac{1}{2}, \quad 0 \le x \le 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Marginal PDF of $Y$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_Y(y) &= \int_0^1 f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx = \int_0^1 (x+y)\,dx \\
&= \left[\frac{x^2}{2} + xy\right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2} + y, \quad 0 \le y \le 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Validation:** $\int_0^1 (x + 1/2)\,dx = 1/2 + 1/2 = 1$ ✓ **(Full 7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Independence Verification and Probability (7 marks)**

**Independence Check:**

For independence, $f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_X(x) \cdot f_Y(y) &= \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right)\left(y + \frac{1}{2}\right) \\
&= xy + \frac{x}{2} + \frac{y}{2} + \frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since this does not equal $f_{X,Y}(x, y) = (x + y)$, the variables are **NOT independent**.

**Computing $P(0 \le X \le 0.5)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(0 \le X \le 0.5) &= \int_0^{0.5} f_X(x)\,dx \\
&= \int_0^{0.5} \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right) dx \\
&= \left[\frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x}{2}\right]_0^{0.5} \\
&= \frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.5}{2} \\
&= 0.125 + 0.25 \\
&= 0.375
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Full 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Skipping validation:** Students often forget to check $\sum P = 1$ or $\int f = 1$ on the joint distribution *before* marginalizing. If the joint is unnormalized, the marginal will be wrong — and the examiner will deduct 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing marginal with conditional:** Marginal = $P_X(x) = \sum_y P_{X,Y}(x,y)$. Conditional = $P_{X \vert Y}(x \vert y) = P_{X,Y}(x,y) / P_Y(y)$. Mixing these is the **#1 most common error**.
> 3. **Independence test mistake:** Verifying independence requires checking $P_{X,Y}(x,y) = P_X(x) \cdot P_Y(y)$ for **all** $(x,y)$ pairs. A single counterexample suffices to prove dependence.
> 4. **Integration limits:** In continuous problems, students often write $\int_0^1$ when the support is different (e.g., $0 \le x \le 2$). Always read the support of the joint PDF carefully.
> 5. **Forgetting the "Given" notation:** In conditional probability questions, writing $P(X=1, Y=1)$ instead of $P(X=1 \mid Y=1)$ costs a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Marginal Probability (Module 2)**

- ✅ Marginal probability is the **projection** of a multivariate distribution onto a single axis.
- ✅ **Discrete case:** Use **summation** $\sum_y P_{X,Y}(x,y)$.
- ✅ **Continuous case:** Use **integration** $\int f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy$.
- ✅ The result must satisfy the **total probability axiom** = 1.
- ✅ Marginalization is the **dual operation** of conditioning: it gives the *unconditional* distribution.
- ✅ The marginal PMF/PDF **does not** contain any information about the relationship (dependence/independence) between $X$ and $Y$.
- ✅ **Chain Rule:** $P_{X,Y}(x,y) = P_Y(y) \cdot P_{X \vert Y}(x \vert y)$ — the joint is the product of marginal and conditional.
- ✅ **Independence Test:** $P_{X,Y}(x,y) = P_X(x) \cdot P_Y(y)$ for all $(x,y)$.
- ✅ **Law of Total Probability:** $P_X(x) = \sum_y P_{X \vert Y}(x \vert y) \cdot P_Y(y)$.
- ✅ In engineering data analytics, marginalization is used in **dimensionality reduction**, **latent variable models**, and **sensor data fusion**.
- ✅ Always **validate** the joint distribution before marginalizing — unnormalized joint = wrong marginal.
- ✅ Marginal ≠ Conditional. Know the difference: marginal *ignores*; conditional *conditions on*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditional Frequency Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conditional Frequency Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Conditional Frequency Distribution (CFD)** is a data structure that aggregates multiple frequency distributions indexed by a *condition*. In formal terms, a CFD is a mapping from each *condition* $c \in C$ to a frequency distribution $F_c$, where $F_c$ records the count of every *event* $e$ that co-occurs with that condition.

Mathematically, the CFD can be expressed as:

$$\text{CFD} : C \times E \rightarrow \mathbb{N}_{\geq 0}$$

Where:
- $C$ = the set of all **conditions** (e.g., genre labels, language codes, decades)
- $E$ = the set of all possible **events** (e.g., words, tokens, bigrams)
- $\mathbb{N}_{\geq 0}$ = the set of non-negative integers (frequency counts)

In the **NLTK (Natural Language Toolkit)** framework, this is implemented as the `nltk.ConditionalFreqDist` class, which is a sub-class of `collections.defaultdict`, where each value is a `FreqDist` object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Conditional Frequency Distributions are a core concept in **Module 2: Lexical Resources and Text Processing** of PCCDT503. Students must understand both the conceptual model and the practical NLTK API to perform genre-comparative text analytics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **massive library** containing thousands of books. You want to know which words are most common in **romance novels** versus **science fiction novels** versus **mystery novels**.

A simple **Frequency Distribution** would tell you the word counts of one book. But a **Conditional Frequency Distribution** is like having a **filing cabinet with three drawers** — one labeled "Romance," one labeled "Sci-Fi," one labeled "Mystery." When you open a drawer, you find the complete word-count list for that genre only.

In essence, the **"condition"** is the *filter* or *category* (genre), and the **"frequency distribution"** is the *contents* inside that drawer (word counts).

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple English Summary:** A Conditional Frequency Distribution is a collection of frequency distributions, where each one is "tagged" with a condition. It lets you compare how often events (like words) happen under different circumstances (like different genres, languages, or time periods).

### 1.3 Why CFDs Matter in Data Analytics

CFDs are the foundational tool for **comparative text analytics**. Common applications include:
- Comparing vocabulary across **genres** in the Brown Corpus
- Analyzing **language evolution** across decades in the Inaugural Address Corpus
- Tracking **named entity** distributions across news categories
- Studying **bigram** patterns conditioned on word classes (e.g., modal verbs)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a CFD

A CFD operates on **pairs of (condition, event)** tuples. Internally, every time a pair $(c, e)$ is observed, the count $\text{CFD}[c][e]$ is incremented by 1.

The general construction pattern is:

$$\text{CFD} = \text{build\_cfd}(\{(c_1, e_1), (c_2, e_2), \ldots, (c_n, e_n)\})$$

Each condition $c_i$ acts as a *key* into the outer dictionary, and the value is a complete `FreqDist` of all events observed under that condition.

### 2.2 Comparison: Frequency Distribution vs Conditional Frequency Distribution

| Feature | Frequency Distribution (FD) | Conditional Frequency Distribution (CFD) |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Single mapping $E \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ | Nested mapping $C \rightarrow (E \rightarrow \mathbb{N})$ |
| Dimensions | 1-D (event only) | 2-D (condition + event) |
| Purpose | Counts of events in one context | Counts of events across many contexts |
| NLTK Class | `FreqDist` | `ConditionalFreqDist` |
| Storage | `defaultdict(int)` | `defaultdict(FreqDist)` |
| Tabulation | `fd.most_common(n)` | `cfd.tabulate()` |
| Visualization | `fd.plot(n)` | `cfd.plot(n, conditions)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students confuse `FreqDist` with `ConditionalFreqDist`. Remember the **"Conditional"** prefix means there is an *extra outer layer* of grouping by some condition.

### 2.3 Operational Steps in Building a CFD

The construction of a CFD typically follows a **four-step pipeline**:

1. **Identify Conditions:** Determine the categorical labels (e.g., genres, languages) that will serve as conditions.
2. **Pair Generation:** Iterate through the source corpus and emit $(c, e)$ tuples where $c$ is the condition and $e$ is the event.
3. **Aggregation:** Increment $\text{CFD}[c][e]$ for every observed pair.
4. **Query/Visualization:** Use methods like `cfd.conditions()`, `cfd[c]`, `cfd[condition][event]`, `cfd.tabulate()`, and `cfd.plot()`.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Method Cheat Sheet

| Method / Attribute | Signature | Mathematical Meaning | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| `cfd.conditions()` | $\text{conditions}()$ | $\text{keys}(C)$ | Sorted list of conditions |
| `cfd[condition]` | $\text{index}(\text{condition})$ | Returns $F_c$ | `FreqDist` for that condition |
| `cfd[condition][event]` | $\text{double-index}$ | $\text{count}(e \mid c)$ | Integer count of event under condition |
| `cfd.N()` | $\text{total-counts}()$ | $\sum_{c \in C} \sum_{e \in E} \text{CFD}[c][e]$ | Total number of (condition, event) pairs |
| `cfd.tabulate(...)` | Tabular display | Matrix view of $\text{CFD}[c][e]$ | Prints aligned table to console |
| `cfd.plot(...)` | Graphical display | Bar/line chart of frequencies | Renders a matplotlib chart |
| `cfd.most_common()` | $\text{top-events}()$ | $\arg\max_e \text{CFD}[c][e]$ | List of `(event, count)` tuples |

> [!NOTE]
> The conditional probability of an event $e$ given a condition $c$ can be derived from a CFD using:
> 
> $$P(e \mid c) = \frac{\text{CFD}[c][e]}{\sum_{e'} \text{CFD}[c][e']}$$
> 
> This is **NOT** a built-in method but a derived computation, frequently asked in KTU theory exams.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CFDs are extensively used in:
- **Information Retrieval (IR):** Comparing term frequencies across document categories for search ranking.
- **Sentiment Analysis:** Tracking polarity-bearing words across product reviews from different domains.
- **Computational Linguistics:** Building comparative lexicons across languages using parallel corpora.
- **Bioinformatics:** Comparing k-mer distributions across gene families or species.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Building a CFD from the Brown Corpus (by Genre)

This is the **canonical KTU example**. The Brown Corpus contains 500 texts categorized into 15 genres such as `news`, `romance`, `humor`, `religion`, `science_fiction`, etc.

**Step 1 — Import and prepare the corpus:**

```python
import nltk
from nltk.corpus import brown
from nltk.probability import ConditionalFreqDist

# Ensure corpus is downloaded
# nltk.download('brown')
```

**Step 2 — Define conditions (genres) and the target word set:**

We pick a small set of *target words* to track across genres:

```python
genres = ['news', 'religion', 'hobbies', 'science_fiction', 'romance', 'humor']
target_words = ['can', 'could', 'may', 'might', 'must', 'will']
```

**Step 3 — Build the CFD by iterating over the corpus:**

```python
cfd = ConditionalFreqDist(
    (genre, word.lower())
    for genre in genres
    for word in brown.words(categories=genre)
)
```

**Step 4 — Query the CFD:**

```python
# List all conditions (genres)
print(cfd.conditions())
# Output: ['humor', 'hobbies', 'news', 'religion', 'romance', 'science_fiction']

# Get frequency distribution of target words in 'news'
print(cfd['news'].most_common(10))

# Count of 'can' in 'romance'
print(cfd['romance']['can'])
```

**Step 5 — Tabulate for visual comparison:**

```python
cfd.tabulate(conditions=genres, samples=target_words)
```

**Expected output (approximate):**

| Genre \ Word | can | could | may | might | must | will |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| humor | 68 | 30 | 38 | 21 | 27 | 86 |
| hobbies | 120 | 75 | 80 | 35 | 50 | 145 |
| news | 155 | 110 | 95 | 28 | 90 | 270 |
| religion | 100 | 60 | 80 | 22 | 60 | 110 |
| romance | 105 | 95 | 75 | 45 | 50 | 130 |
| science_fiction | 30 | 25 | 12 | 12 | 14 | 38 |

**Interpretation:** The word `will` dominates across all genres, but `news` and `hobbies` have the highest counts. The `science_fiction` genre has notably lower counts because it has fewer total words.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: CFD for Inaugural Addresses (by Decade)

The **Inaugural Address Corpus** contains US presidential speeches. We can build a CFD conditioned on *decades* to track word usage over time.

**Step 1 — Build the CFD:**

```python
from nltk.corpus import inaugural
# nltk.download('inaugural')

cfd_inaug = ConditionalFreqDist(
    (target, fileid[:4])              # condition = first 4 chars = year
    for target in ['america', 'citizen', 'freedom', 'duty', 'world']
    for fileid in inaugural.fileids()
    for word in inaugural.words(fileid)
    if word.lower().startswith(target)
)
```

**Step 2 — Tabulate:**

```python
cfd_inaug.tabulate(samples=['1789', '1800', '1900', '2000'])
```

**Interpretation:** You can visually see whether words like `america` or `freedom` are being used more or less in different centuries.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Deriving Conditional Probability from a CFD

Given a CFD, derive $P(\text{``the''} \mid \text{``news''})$:

**Mathematical formulation:**

$$P(w \mid g) = \frac{\text{CFD}[g][w]}{\sum_{w'} \text{CFD}[g][w']}$$

**Step-by-step Python derivation:**

```python
genre = 'news'
target_word = 'the'

# Numerator
numerator = cfd[genre][target_word]

# Denominator: sum of ALL word counts in 'news'
denominator = sum(cfd[genre].values())

# Conditional probability
prob = numerator / denominator
print(f"P({target_word} | {genre}) = {prob:.6f}")
```

**Typical output (approximate):** $\approx 0.0640$ (i.e., 'the' accounts for ~6.4% of all words in news genre).

### 3.4 Common Pitfalls in CFD Construction

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Case sensitivity:** `brown.words()` may return mixed case. Always normalize with `.lower()` or `.upper()` before building the CFD, otherwise `The` and `the` will be counted as different events.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Punctuation leakage:** Punctuation tokens like `.` and `,` will appear in the word stream. Filter them out:
>
> ```python
> for word in brown.words(categories=genre):
>     if word.isalpha():                 # keeps only alphabetic tokens
>         # emit tuple
> ```
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Memory bloat:** For massive corpora, building a CFD on every word is expensive. Restrict to a *samples* list or use `cfd.tabulate(samples=...)` to limit output.

### 3.5 Cumulative Aggregation Logic

When processing the pair list $\{(c_1, e_1), (c_2, e_2), \ldots\}$, the CFD performs the following for each pair $(c, e)$:

$$\text{CFD}[c][e] \leftarrow \text{CFD}[c][e] + 1$$

If the condition $c$ has not been seen before, the underlying `defaultdict` automatically creates a new empty `FreqDist()` and inserts the first event. If the event $e$ is new under condition $c$, the inner `FreqDist` also creates a new count of 1.

This is why CFDs are *zero-effort to populate* — the `defaultdict` mechanics handle all the "first time" edge cases for you.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of a ConditionalFreqDist

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a CFD routes an incoming pair (condition, event) through its internal storage layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Pair: condition c, event e"] --> B{"Does condition c exist in CFD"}
    B -- No --> C["Create new FreqDist F sub c"]
    C --> D["Initialize F sub c e equals 1"]
    B -- Yes --> E["Retrieve existing FreqDist F sub c"]
    E --> F{"Does event e exist in F sub c"}
    F -- No --> G["Set F sub c e equals 1"]
    F -- Yes --> H["Increment F sub c e by 1"]
    D --> I["Store updated CFD in memory"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Ready for next pair"]
```

### 4.2 Layered Data Structure View

The diagram below shows the nested dictionary nature of a CFD, where the outer key is the *condition* (e.g., genre) and the inner value is a full `FreqDist` mapping events to counts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CFD["ConditionalFreqDist"] --> K1["Condition news"]
    CFD --> K2["Condition romance"]
    CFD --> K3["Condition humor"]
    K1 --> FD1["FreqDist for news"]
    K2 --> FD2["FreqDist for romance"]
    K3 --> FD3["FreqDist for humor"]
    FD1 --> W1a["can 155"]
    FD1 --> W1b["will 270"]
    FD1 --> W1c["must 90"]
    FD2 --> W2a["can 105"]
    FD2 --> W2b["will 130"]
    FD3 --> W3a["can 68"]
    FD3 --> W3b["will 86"]
```

### 4.3 CFD Construction Pipeline

This Mermaid block diagram depicts the typical end-to-end pipeline from raw corpus to visualized CFD.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Raw Corpus: brown or inaugural"] --> P1["Tokenization: words function"]
    P1 --> P2["Filter: stopwords, punctuation, case normalization"]
    P2 --> P3["Pair Generator: emit condition, event tuples"]
    P3 --> P4["ConditionalFreqDist Constructor"]
    P4 --> Q1["Query: cfd conditions"]
    P4 --> Q2["Query: cfd c event"]
    P4 --> Q3["Visualize: cfd tabulate"]
    P4 --> Q4["Visualize: cfd plot"]
    P4 --> Q5["Derive: conditional probability P e given c"]
```

### 4.4 Method Dispatch Map

The following Mermaid diagram maps the major NLTK CFD methods to their roles in the analytics workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["cfd conditions"] --> R1["Discovery: list of all genres or labels"]
    M2["cfd sub c"] --> R2["Retrieval: full FreqDist for genre c"]
    M3["cfd sub c sub e"] --> R3["Lookup: integer count of word e in genre c"]
    M4["cfd N"] --> R4["Aggregation: total pairs recorded"]
    M5["cfd tabulate"] --> R5["Reporting: aligned console table"]
    M6["cfd plot"] --> R6["Reporting: matplotlib chart"]
    M7["cfd most common"] --> R7["Ranking: top events by count"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** In exam answers, you are often asked to *"explain the methods of a ConditionalFreqDist"*. A labeled diagram like this is a high-scoring, visual answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]*
**Define a Conditional Frequency Distribution. How does it differ from a regular Frequency Distribution?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Conditional Frequency Distribution (CFD)** is a collection of frequency distributions, each indexed by a *condition*. Formally, it is a mapping from conditions $C$ to frequency distributions over events $E$, i.e., $\text{CFD} : C \times E \rightarrow \mathbb{N}_{\geq 0}$.

A regular **Frequency Distribution (FD)** records counts of events in a single, undifferentiated stream. A CFD adds an *outer dimension* (the condition) and is therefore **two-dimensional**, allowing comparison of event frequencies across different categories.

For example, an FD counts how many times each word appears in one text. A CFD counts how many times each word appears *per genre* — news, romance, humor, etc. — enabling comparative analysis.

In NLTK, FD is implemented by `FreqDist` while CFD is implemented by `ConditionalFreqDist`, a sub-class of `defaultdict(FreqDist)`.

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]*
**List any four methods of the `ConditionalFreqDist` class in NLTK with their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`cfd.conditions()`** — Returns the list of all conditions (e.g., all genres) for which frequency distributions exist.
2. **`cfd[condition]`** — Returns the `FreqDist` associated with a particular condition.
3. **`cfd[condition][event]`** — Returns the integer count of the event under the given condition.
4. **`cfd.tabulate(conditions, samples)`** — Prints a tabular matrix of counts for the specified conditions and sample events.
5. **`cfd.plot(conditions, samples)`** — Plots a chart of the counts for visual analysis.
6. **`cfd.N()`** — Returns the total number of (condition, event) pairs recorded.

(Any four correctly described are sufficient for full marks.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice**. Question A and Question B below are **independent alternatives** — a student answers one.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

*(a)* **[7 Marks]** Explain in detail the structure of a Conditional Frequency Distribution. How is it implemented in NLTK? Describe the construction process with a suitable example using the Brown Corpus.

*(b)* **[7 Marks]** Write a Python program to build a CFD from the Brown Corpus that compares the frequencies of the modal verbs `['can', 'could', 'may', 'might', 'must', 'will']` across the genres `['news', 'romance', 'humor', 'religion']`. Display the result using `tabulate()` and interpret the output.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation [7 Marks]:**

A **Conditional Frequency Distribution (CFD)** is a 2-D data structure that maps *conditions* to *frequency distributions*. In NLTK, it is implemented by the class `nltk.ConditionalFreqDist`.

**Internal Structure:**
- The outer container is a `defaultdict(FreqDist)`.
- Each unique *condition* $c$ becomes a key in the outer dict.
- The value at each key is a `FreqDist` object holding event-count pairs.
- On encountering a new pair $(c, e)$, if $c$ is unseen, a fresh `FreqDist` is auto-created; if $e$ is unseen under that $c$, its count is initialized to 1. Otherwise the count is incremented.

**Construction Process (Brown Corpus example):**

1. **Import NLTK modules** and load the Brown Corpus with `from nltk.corpus import brown`.
2. **Select target genres** as conditions, e.g., `genres = ['news', 'romance', 'humor']`.
3. **Select target events** (optional), e.g., modal verbs.
4. **Generate (condition, event) pairs** via a generator expression.
5. **Pass the pairs to the `ConditionalFreqDist` constructor.**
6. **Use built-in methods** to explore, tabulate, and plot.

**Sample Code:**

```python
import nltk
from nltk.corpus import brown
from nltk.probability import ConditionalFreqDist

cfd = ConditionalFreqDist(
    (genre, word.lower())
    for genre in ['news', 'romance', 'humor']
    for word in brown.words(categories=genre)
    if word.isalpha()       # filter punctuation
)
```

This produces a CFD with three outer keys (the three genres), each containing the full frequency map of every lowercased, alphabetic word in that genre.

**(b) Program and Interpretation [7 Marks]:**

```python
import nltk
from nltk.corpus import brown
from nltk.probability import ConditionalFreqDist

modals = ['can', 'could', 'may', 'might', 'must', 'will']
genres = ['news', 'romance', 'humor', 'religion']

cfd = ConditionalFreqDist(
    (genre, word.lower())
    for genre in genres
    for word in brown.words(categories=genre)
)

cfd.tabulate(conditions=genres, samples=modals)
```

**Expected output (sample):**

| | can | could | may | might | must | will |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| news | 155 | 110 | 95 | 28 | 90 | 270 |
| romance | 105 | 95 | 75 | 45 | 50 | 130 |
| humor | 68 | 30 | 38 | 21 | 27 | 86 |
| religion | 100 | 60 | 80 | 22 | 60 | 110 |

**Interpretation:**
- The modal verb `will` is the most frequent across all four genres, indicating its strong role in expressing futurity in English.
- `news` has the highest counts overall, consistent with its higher average text length and assertive style.
- `romance` and `religion` use `may` more prominently than `humor`, suggesting more conditional or optative language.
- `humor` has the lowest counts across all modals, partly because humor texts are shorter and more dialogue-driven.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

*(a)* **[7 Marks]** Describe with a suitable example how Conditional Frequency Distributions are used to study language change over time. Use the **Inaugural Address Corpus** in your explanation.

*(b)* **[7 Marks]** Given a CFD built on the Brown Corpus, derive the conditional probability $P(w = \text{``america''} \mid \text{genre} = \text{``news''})$ using first principles. Write the Python code to compute this and explain each step.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Language Change with Inaugural Corpus [7 Marks]:**

The **Inaugural Address Corpus** contains 58 US presidential inaugural speeches from 1789 to the present. Each speech is identified by a `fileid` whose first four characters are the year, e.g., `1789-Washington.txt`, `2021-Biden.txt`.

By constructing a CFD conditioned on *decades*, we can track how frequently certain words have been used in inaugural addresses over the past 230+ years.

**Example code:**

```python
from nltk.corpus import inaugural
from nltk.probability import ConditionalFreqDist

cfd = ConditionalFreqDist(
    (fileid[:4], word.lower())
    for fileid in inaugural.fileids()
    for word in inaugural.words(fileid)
    if word.isalpha()
)

# Compare decade-by-decade usage
cfd.tabulate(samples=['1789', '1820', '1900', '1960', '2000'])
```

**Use case:** A researcher can observe:
- The word `america` has surged in usage in the 20th and 21st centuries compared to the 18th.
- The word `citizen` has become more frequent post-1900.
- The word `constitution` remains consistently high.

This kind of diachronic analysis is a textbook example of CFD utility and is commonly asked in KTU exams.

**(b) Deriving Conditional Probability [7 Marks]:**

**Mathematical derivation:**

The conditional probability of a word $w$ given a genre $g$ is:

$$P(w \mid g) = \frac{\text{count}(w, g)}{\text{count}(*, g)} = \frac{\text{CFD}[g][w]}{\sum_{w'} \text{CFD}[g][w']}$$

**Step-by-step Python code:**

```python
genre = 'news'
target_word = 'america'

# Numerator: count of 'america' in 'news' genre
numerator = cfd[genre][target_word]

# Denominator: total word count in 'news' genre
denominator = sum(cfd[genre].values())

# Conditional probability
prob = numerator / denominator

print(f"Numerator   = CFD['news']['america']  = {numerator}")
print(f"Denominator = sum of CFD['news'].values() = {denominator}")
print(f"P('{target_word}' | '{genre}') = {prob:.6f}")
```

**Explanation of each step:**

1. **Numerator** `cfd[genre][target_word]` — directly accesses the count of `america` in the `news` frequency distribution.
2. **Denominator** `sum(cfd[genre].values())` — sums every word count in `news` to get the total token count.
3. **Division** — produces the conditional probability, a real number in $[0, 1]$.
4. The result tells us: *given that a word is drawn from the 'news' genre, what is the probability that it is 'america'?*

This derivation is a **frequently asked KTU question** because it tests both the theoretical understanding of CFD and the practical ability to compute conditional probability from it.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in CFD Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to filter punctuation.** If you do not write `if word.isalpha()` (or equivalent), the CFD will count `.`, `,`, `--` as events, polluting every frequency distribution. **-1 to -2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Case sensitivity oversight.** `The` and `the` will be counted as different words unless you call `.lower()`. KTU evaluators check this carefully. **-1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Confusing `cfd.N()` with `len(cfd.conditions())`.** `cfd.N()` returns the **total number of (condition, event) pairs summed over all counts**, NOT the number of conditions. Many students write the wrong method. **-2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Writing `FreqDist` instead of `ConditionalFreqDist` in code.** Auto-checkers and human evaluators both flag this. Always write the full class name.
>
> 5. **In explanation questions, omitting the nested dictionary structure.** A CFD is *literally* `defaultdict(FreqDist)`. Skipping this technical detail costs you 1–2 marks in part (a) of 14-mark questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A CFD is a 2-D mapping $\text{CFD} : C \times E \rightarrow \mathbb{N}_{\geq 0}$, where $C$ is the set of conditions and $E$ is the set of events.
- **NLTK Class:** `nltk.probability.ConditionalFreqDist`, internally a `defaultdict(FreqDist)`.
- **Construction:** Build via a generator yielding `(condition, event)` tuples, e.g., `ConditionalFreqDist((genre, word) for ...)`.
- **Key Methods:** `conditions()`, `cfd[c]`, `cfd[c][e]`, `N()`, `tabulate()`, `plot()`, `most_common()`.
- **Conditional Probability Formula:** $P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{CFD}[c][w]}{\sum_{w'} \text{CFD}[c][w']}$.
- **Canonical Examples:** Brown Corpus (genre comparison) and Inaugural Address Corpus (temporal comparison).
- **Preprocessing Best Practices:** Always `.lower()` to normalize case, and `if word.isalpha()` to drop punctuation.
- **Difference from FD:** FD is 1-D (events only); CFD is 2-D (conditions + events) — remember the **extra outer layer**.
- **Visualization Tools:** `tabulate()` for console tables, `plot()` for matplotlib charts.
- **Real-World Applications:** Genre analysis, diachronic linguistics, sentiment domain comparison, search-engine term weighting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphical Representation of Two Nominal or Ordinal Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Graphical Representation of Two Nominal or Ordinal Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Data Analytics*, **Graphical Representation of Two Nominal or Ordinal Variables** refers to the family of statistical plots and tabular visualizations used to display the joint, marginal, and conditional distributions of two categorical (qualitative) variables measured on the same set of observations.

- A **Nominal Variable** is a categorical variable whose levels have *no inherent order* (e.g., Gender: Male, Female, Other; Blood Group: A, B, AB, O).
- An **Ordinal Variable** is a categorical variable whose levels have a *natural ranking* but the gaps between levels are not necessarily equal (e.g., Education: Primary < Secondary < Graduate < Postgraduate; Rating: Poor < Fair < Good < Excellent).

When we have two such variables, say $X$ (with $r$ levels) and $Y$ (with $c$ levels), recorded on $n$ observations, we obtain a **two-way contingency table** (also called a *crosstab* or *cross-classification table*) of dimension $r \times c$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The focus is on *visually* summarizing the relationship between two qualitative variables. The visual tools commonly tested are: **Clustered (Grouped) Bar Charts, Stacked Bar Charts, 100% Stacked Bar Charts, Spine Plots, Mosaic Plots, Dot Plots, and Heat Maps.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a classroom survey of **200 students** where each student is classified by two attributes:
- **Attribute 1 (X):** Stream → *Engineering, Management, Arts*
- **Attribute 2 (Y):** Preference → *Online, Offline*

A single bar chart could only show "how many students prefer Online vs Offline" — it would *ignore* the stream. To *simultaneously* see how preference *changes* across streams, we need a **two-variable graphical technique**. Think of it like a **two-way mirror**: one axis shows the stream, the other shows preference, and the visual height/area tells us how the two interact. It is the qualitative analogue of a **scatter plot** for two numerical variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two categorical variables need special plots:** Unlike numerical variables (where scatter plots use position along a continuous axis), categorical variables cannot be meaningfully placed on a continuous axis. Therefore, we rely on **bar length, area proportion, color density, or dot stacking** to encode the frequency or proportion in each $(X_i, Y_j)$ combination.

### 1.3 Standard Statistical Metrics (KTU Highlight)

The following quantities appear repeatedly in KTU board exams for this topic:

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Joint Frequency | $n_{ij}$ | Count of observations in row $i$ and column $j$ |
| Marginal Frequency | $n_{i\cdot}$, $n_{\cdot j}$ | Row/column totals |
| Total Sample Size | $n$ | $\sum_i \sum_j n_{ij}$ |
| Joint Relative Frequency | $f_{ij}$ | $n_{ij}/n$ |
| Conditional Relative Frequency | $f_{j \mid i}$ | $n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$ |
| Expected Frequency (under independence) | $E_{ij}$ | $(n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j})/n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Notation Rule:** Always use a **dot** to denote summation over the dropped index. $n_{i\cdot} = \sum_{j=1}^{c} n_{ij}$ is the *i-th row total*, and $n_{\cdot j} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} n_{ij}$ is the *j-th column total*. This is a board-favorite notation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Contingency Table (Foundation of All Two-Categorical Plots)

A two-way contingency table is the numerical backbone from which *every* graphical representation of two nominal/ordinal variables is derived.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & Y_1 & Y_2 & \cdots & Y_c & \text{Row Total} \\
\hline
X_1 & n_{11} & n_{12} & \cdots & n_{1c} & n_{1\cdot} \\
\hline
X_2 & n_{21} & n_{22} & \cdots & n_{2c} & n_{2\cdot} \\
\hline
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
\hline
X_r & n_{r1} & n_{r2} & \cdots & n_{rc} & n_{r\cdot} \\
\hline
\text{Col Total} & n_{\cdot 1} & n_{\cdot 2} & \cdots & n_{\cdot c} & n \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

- The cell $(i, j)$ contains the **joint frequency** $n_{ij}$.
- The **marginal distribution of X** is read from the rightmost column; the **marginal distribution of Y** is read from the bottom row.
- The **conditional distribution of Y given X = X_i** is the row $i$, normalized by $n_{i\cdot}$.

### 2.2 The Major Graphical Techniques (Logic, Why, and How)

**1. Clustered (Grouped) Bar Chart**
- **Logic:** Place side-by-side bars of $Y$'s levels *within* each level of $X$.
- **Why:** Lets the eye compare $Y$ across each $X_i$ directly.
- **How to Read:** Bar height = frequency or relative frequency in cell $(i,j)$.
- **Best For:** Comparing absolute counts; small $r$ and $c$.

**2. Stacked Bar Chart**
- **Logic:** Within each bar representing $X_i$, stack segments for each level of $Y$.
- **Why:** Shows both the row total (overall bar height) and the composition of $Y$ within $X_i$.
- **How to Read:** Segment height = $n_{ij}$; total bar height = $n_{i\cdot}$.
- **Pitfall:** Hard to compare middle segments because baselines differ.

**3. 100% Stacked Bar Chart**
- **Logic:** Same as stacked, but every bar is normalized to 100%.
- **Why:** Directly compares the *proportional* composition of $Y$ across levels of $X$, ignoring row totals.
- **How to Read:** Segment height = $f_{j \mid i} = n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$.
- **Best For:** Comparing conditional distributions when row totals differ greatly.

**4. Spine Plot (or Mosaic Plot)**
- **Logic:** A 100% stacked bar chart *where the bar widths are proportional to the row totals* $n_{i\cdot}$.
- **Why:** Simultaneously conveys marginal of X (width) and conditional of Y given X (height).
- **How to Read:** Width $\propto n_{i\cdot}$; segment height $\propto f_{j \mid i}$.
- **Engineering Use:** Often used in quality engineering and survey analytics.

**5. Dot Plot (Cleveland Dot Plot for Two Categoricals)**
- **Logic:** Replace bars with dots to reduce ink and improve precision.
- **Why:** Cleaner visualization; better when $c$ is large.
- **How to Read:** Dot position = frequency or proportion.

**6. Heat Map**
- **Logic:** A grid where each cell is shaded by color intensity according to $n_{ij}$ or $f_{ij}$ or a *residual* $(O_{ij} - E_{ij})/E_{ij}$.
- **Why:** Excellent for large tables; color encodes the third dimension (magnitude).
- **KTU Trick:** Heat maps of *Pearson residuals* highlight which cells deviate from independence.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-tested. Memorize the symbols and units carefully.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Joint Relative Frequency:} \quad & f_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n} \\[4pt]
\text{Conditional Relative Frequency:} \quad & f_{j \mid i} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{n_{i\cdot}} \\[4pt]
\text{Expected Frequency (under } H_0: \text{independence):} \quad & E_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n} \\[4pt]
\text{Chi-Square Statistic:} \quad & \chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sum_{j=1}^{c} \dfrac{(n_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}} \\[4pt]
\text{Pearson Residual:} \quad & r_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij} - E_{ij}}{\sqrt{E_{ij}}} \\[4pt]
\text{Standardized Residual:} \quad & d_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij} - E_{ij}}{\sqrt{E_{ij} \left(1 - \dfrac{n_{i\cdot}}{n}\right) \left(1 - \dfrac{n_{\cdot j}}{n}\right)}} \\[4pt]
\text{Phi Coefficient (for } 2 \times 2 \text{):} \quad & \phi = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{n}} \\[4pt]
\text{Contingency Coefficient:} \quad & C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + n}} \\[4pt]
\text{Cramer's V:} \quad & V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{n \cdot \min(r-1,\, c-1)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Quantity | Formula | Range / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $f_{ij}$ | $n_{ij}/n$ | $0 \le f_{ij} \le 1$; sums to 1 over the table |
| $f_{j \mid i}$ | $n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$ | Sums to 1 across $j$ for each fixed $i$ |
| $E_{ij}$ | $n_{i\cdot} n_{\cdot j}/n$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| $\chi^2$ | $\sum (O-E)^2/E$ | $\chi^2 \geq 0$; $\chi^2 = 0 \Leftrightarrow$ independence |
| $\phi$ | $\sqrt{\chi^2/n}$ | For $2 \times 2$ tables only; $-1 \leq \phi \leq 1$ |
| $C$ | $\sqrt{\chi^2/(\chi^2+n)}$ | $0 \leq C < 1$ |
| $V$ | $\sqrt{\chi^2/[n \min(r-1,c-1)]}$ | $0 \leq V \leq 1$; **dimensionless** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - In **manufacturing quality control**, clustered bar charts compare defect types across production lines.
> - In **medical research**, contingency tables and mosaic plots relate treatment groups to outcomes (e.g., Recovery vs No Recovery).
> - In **A/B testing** for software products, chi-square tests on $2 \times 2$ tables determine if a new feature changes user click-through rates.
> - In **survey analytics for civil/transport engineering**, modal choice (Car/Bus/Train) is cross-tabulated with income band.

### 2.4 Logic Flow — How to Choose the Right Plot (Decision Tree)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start} &\rightarrow \text{Two categorical variables?} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Do you want absolute counts?} \\
& \quad \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \textbf{Clustered Bar Chart} \\
& \quad \rightarrow \text{No (proportions matter)} \rightarrow \textbf{100\% Stacked Bar Chart} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Do you want to highlight deviations from independence?} \\
& \quad \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \textbf{Heat Map of Pearson Residuals} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Do you want to show marginal of X AND conditional of Y together?} \\
& \quad \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \textbf{Spine / Mosaic Plot}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Expected Frequency Under Independence

**Step 1 — State the Assumption of Independence.**

Under the null hypothesis $H_0$, the two categorical variables $X$ and $Y$ are independent. Formally:
$$
P(Y = Y_j \mid X = X_i) = P(Y = Y_j)
$$

**Step 2 — Express the Joint Probability.**

Independence means:
$$
P(X = X_i,\ Y = Y_j) = P(X = X_i) \cdot P(Y = Y_j)
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate Probabilities from Sample Frequencies.**

The maximum-likelihood estimate of $P(X = X_i)$ is the marginal relative frequency:
$$
\hat{P}(X = X_i) = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot}}{n}
$$
Similarly:
$$
\hat{P}(Y = Y_j) = \dfrac{n_{\cdot j}}{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Expected Joint Probability.**

$$
\hat{P}(X = X_i,\ Y = Y_j) = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot}}{n} \cdot \dfrac{n_{\cdot j}}{n} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Expected Frequency.**

Multiply by the total sample size $n$:
$$
E_{ij} = n \cdot \hat{P}(X = X_i,\ Y = Y_j) = n \cdot \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n^2} = \dfrac{n_{i\cdot} \cdot n_{\cdot j}}{n}
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive KTU Pattern)

> **[Worked Example — KTU Board Style]**
> A college surveys **200 final-year students** on their *placement status* and *internship completion*. The data is:
>
> | | Placed | Not Placed | Row Total |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Internship Completed** | 70 | 30 | 100 |
> | **No Internship** | 50 | 50 | 100 |
> | **Column Total** | 120 | 80 | 200 |

#### (a) Compute the joint and marginal relative frequencies.

**Solution:**

We compute $f_{ij} = n_{ij}/n$ for each cell.

- $f_{11} = 70/200 = 0.35$
- $f_{12} = 30/200 = 0.15$
- $f_{21} = 50/200 = 0.25$
- $f_{22} = 50/200 = 0.25$

Row marginals:
- $f_{1\cdot} = 100/200 = 0.50$
- $f_{2\cdot} = 100/200 = 0.50$

Column marginals:
- $f_{\cdot 1} = 120/200 = 0.60$
- $f_{\cdot 2} = 80/200 = 0.40$

Verification: $\sum f_{ij} = 0.35 + 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 1.00$ ✓

#### (b) Compute the conditional relative frequency of *Placement Status* given *Internship Status*.

**Solution:**

For row 1 (Internship Completed):
$$
f_{\text{Placed} \mid \text{Internship}} = \dfrac{n_{11}}{n_{1\cdot}} = \dfrac{70}{100} = 0.70
$$
$$
f_{\text{Not Placed} \mid \text{Internship}} = \dfrac{n_{12}}{n_{1\cdot}} = \dfrac{30}{100} = 0.30
$$

For row 2 (No Internship):
$$
f_{\text{Placed} \mid \text{No Intern}} = \dfrac{n_{21}}{n_{2\cdot}} = \dfrac{50}{100} = 0.50
$$
$$
f_{\text{Not Placed} \mid \text{No Intern}} = \dfrac{n_{22}}{n_{2\cdot}} = \dfrac{50}{100} = 0.50
$$

**Interpretation:** Students who completed internships have a **70% placement rate**, vs **50%** for those who did not. The difference suggests an association between internship completion and placement.

#### (c) Compute the Expected Frequencies under the assumption of independence.

**Solution:** Apply $E_{ij} = n_{i\cdot} n_{\cdot j}/n$.

- $E_{11} = (100 \times 120)/200 = 60$
- $E_{12} = (100 \times 80)/200 = 40$
- $E_{21} = (100 \times 120)/200 = 60$
- $E_{22} = (100 \times 80)/200 = 40$

#### (d) Compute the Chi-Square Statistic $\chi^2$.

**Solution:**

$$
\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \dfrac{(n_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
$$

Compute each term:
- Cell (1,1): $(70 - 60)^2 / 60 = 100/60 = 1.6667$
- Cell (1,2): $(30 - 40)^2 / 40 = 100/40 = 2.5000$
- Cell (2,1): $(50 - 60)^2 / 60 = 100/60 = 1.6667$
- Cell (2,2): $(50 - 40)^2 / 40 = 100/40 = 2.5000$

Sum:
$$
\chi^2 = 1.6667 + 2.5000 + 1.6667 + 2.5000 = 8.3334
$$

With degrees of freedom $(r-1)(c-1) = 1 \times 1 = 1$, the critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ is **3.841**. Since $8.33 > 3.84$, we **reject independence**.

#### (e) Compute Phi Coefficient.

**Solution:**

$$
\phi = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8.3334}{200}} = \sqrt{0.04167} \approx 0.204
$$

A phi of 0.204 indicates a *weak-to-moderate* positive association.

### 3.3 Derivation of Cramer's V (Generalization of Phi)

For $r \times c$ tables with $r, c > 2$, phi is no longer bounded by 1. Cramer's V normalizes $\chi^2$ by the maximum possible value.

$$
V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2/n}{\min(r-1,\, c-1)}}
$$

This gives $0 \leq V \leq 1$ regardless of table dimensions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Visualization

| Visualization | Best Use Case | What It Highlights | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clustered Bar | Compare counts across combinations | Absolute magnitudes | Cluttered when $c$ is large |
| Stacked Bar | Show composition + totals | Row totals + composition | Hard to compare middle segments |
| 100% Stacked Bar | Compare compositions across groups | Conditional proportions | Loses row total information |
| Mosaic / Spine Plot | Show both marginal + conditional | Width $\propto$ row total, height $\propto$ cond. freq. | Can look complex |
| Heat Map | Large tables, residual analysis | Magnitude or deviation via color | Less intuitive for small tables |
| Dot Plot | Many categories | Precise counts | Less visual impact |
| Side-by-side Pie | $r=2$ only, simple cases | Proportions within each $X_i$ | Harder to compare angles precisely |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Raw Data to Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Categorical Data: Two Variables X and Y] --> B[Construct Contingency Table n_ij]
    B --> C{Analytical Goal?}
    C --> D1[Show Absolute Counts]
    C --> D2[Show Proportions]
    C --> D3[Show Deviations from Independence]
    C --> D4[Show Marginal AND Conditional Together]

    D1 --> E1[Clustered Bar Chart]
    D2 --> E2[100% Stacked Bar Chart]
    D3 --> E3[Heat Map of Pearson Residuals]
    D4 --> E4[Mosaic or Spine Plot]

    B --> F[Compute Expected Frequencies E_ij]
    F --> G[Compute Chi-Square Statistic]
    G --> H{Is chi-square significant?}
    H --> I1[Yes: Variables are Associated]
    H --> I2[No: Variables are Independent]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a 100% Stacked Bar Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X1 [X = Level 1 - e.g., Internship Completed]
        A1[Y_1 segment - Placed]
        A2[Y_2 segment - Not Placed]
    end

    subgraph X2 [X = Level 2 - e.g., No Internship]
        B1[Y_1 segment - Placed]
        B2[Y_2 segment - Not Placed]
    end

    A1 -->|height = f_j_given_i| A2
    B1 -->|height = f_j_given_i| B2

    X1 -->|all bars total 100 percent| X2
```

### 4.3 Decision Map for Choosing the Right Test Statistic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Two Categorical Variables] --> Q1{Table size?}
    Q1 --> Q2[2x2 Table]
    Q1 --> Q3[r x c Table, r or c greater than 2]

    Q2 --> Stat1[Use Phi Coefficient]
    Q3 --> Stat2[Use Cramer's V]

    Q1 --> Q4{Need general association?}
    Q4 --> Q5[Yes - Use Contingency Coefficient C]
    Q4 --> Q6[No - Use Chi-Square directly]

    Stat1 --> Out1[Range -1 to 1]
    Stat2 --> Out2[Range 0 to 1]
    Q5 --> Out3[Range 0 to less than 1]
```

### 4.4 Component Map of a Mosaic Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MosaicPlot [Mosaic Plot Architecture]
        M1[Full Width = Total Sample n]
        M2[Bar Width for X_i is proportional to n_i_dot]
        M3[Within each bar, vertical segments for each Y_j]
        M4[Segment Height is proportional to f_j_given_i]
        M1 --> M2
        M2 --> M3
        M3 --> M4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Insight:** In a **Mosaic Plot**, *if* the variables are independent, then every column of cells has the *same vertical proportions*. Any deviation from identical proportions across columns is a visual cue for association — exactly the same deviation that $\chi^2$ measures numerically.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a *contingency table*. What is the difference between joint frequency and marginal frequency?

**Model Answer:**
A contingency table is a two-way frequency table that displays the joint distribution of two categorical variables $X$ (with $r$ levels) and $Y$ (with $c$ levels). The cell $(i, j)$ contains the **joint frequency** $n_{ij}$, the count of observations with $X = X_i$ and $Y = Y_j$. **Marginal frequencies** are the row totals $n_{i\cdot} = \sum_j n_{ij}$ (marginal of $X$) and column totals $n_{\cdot j} = \sum_i n_{ij}$ (marginal of $Y$), representing the *one-way* distributions of $X$ and $Y$ alone.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is a *100% stacked bar chart*? How does it differ from a regular stacked bar chart?

**Model Answer:**
A stacked bar chart displays the **absolute joint frequencies** $n_{ij}$ stacked within each level of $X$, so the total bar height equals the row total $n_{i\cdot}$. A **100% stacked bar chart** normalizes every bar to the same total height (100%), so the segments represent **conditional relative frequencies** $f_{j \mid i} = n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$. The 100% stacked version is used to compare *proportional compositions* across categories of $X$ when row totals differ.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** The table below shows the preference for **online vs offline learning mode** among students from three engineering branches. Construct an appropriate graphical representation and interpret the result.

| | Online | Offline | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CS** | 60 | 40 | 100 |
| **EC** | 45 | 55 | 100 |
| **ME** | 30 | 70 | 100 |

Compute the conditional relative frequencies and identify which branch shows the strongest preference for online learning. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **expected frequencies** under independence and the **chi-square statistic**. State whether the branch and learning mode are associated at $\alpha = 0.05$ (critical value = 5.991 for $df = 2$). **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

The appropriate plot is a **100% stacked bar chart** (since branch sizes are equal here, both stacked and 100% stacked give the same picture; but 100% stacked generalizes).

**Conditional relative frequencies** $f_{j \mid i} = n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$:

- CS: $f_{\text{Online} \mid \text{CS}} = 60/100 = 0.60$; $f_{\text{Offline} \mid \text{CS}} = 40/100 = 0.40$
- EC: $f_{\text{Online} \mid \text{EC}} = 45/100 = 0.45$; $f_{\text{Offline} \mid \text{EC}} = 55/100 = 0.55$
- ME: $f_{\text{Online} \mid \text{ME}} = 30/100 = 0.30$; $f_{\text{Offline} \mid \text{ME}} = 70/100 = 0.70$

**Interpretation:** The **CS branch** shows the strongest preference for online learning, with 60% of its students preferring online mode. The ME branch shows the weakest online preference at only 30%.

##### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Expected frequencies** $E_{ij} = n_{i\cdot} n_{\cdot j}/n$:

Totals: $n_{\cdot 1} = 60 + 45 + 30 = 135$ (Online); $n_{\cdot 2} = 120$ (Offline); $n = 300$.

- $E_{11} = (100 \times 135)/300 = 45$
- $E_{12} = (100 \times 120)/300 = 40$
- $E_{21} = (100 \times 135)/300 = 45$
- $E_{22} = (100 \times 120)/300 = 40$
- $E_{31} = (100 \times 135)/300 = 45$
- $E_{32} = (100 \times 120)/300 = 40$

**Chi-square statistic:**
$$
\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}
$$

Compute each term:
- $(60-45)^2/45 = 225/45 = 5.000$
- $(40-40)^2/40 = 0/40 = 0.000$
- $(45-45)^2/45 = 0/45 = 0.000$
- $(55-40)^2/40 = 225/40 = 5.625$
- $(30-45)^2/45 = 225/45 = 5.000$
- $(70-40)^2/40 = 900/40 = 22.500$

$$
\chi^2 = 5.000 + 0.000 + 0.000 + 5.625 + 5.000 + 22.500 = 38.125
$$

Since $38.125 > 5.991$, we **reject the null hypothesis of independence** at $\alpha = 0.05$. Branch and learning mode are strongly associated.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the **Mosaic Plot** as a graphical representation of two categorical variables. Describe how its bar widths and segment heights encode information. Mention one engineering scenario where it is preferred over a stacked bar chart. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A survey of 400 employees on **Job Satisfaction (Low, Medium, High)** and **Department (HR, Finance, Tech)** yields the following contingency table. Compute Cramer's V and interpret the strength of association.

| | Low | Medium | High | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HR** | 30 | 40 | 30 | 100 |
| **Finance** | 20 | 50 | 30 | 100 |
| **Tech** | 10 | 50 | 140 | 200 |
| **Total** | 60 | 140 | 200 | 400 |

**(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

A **Mosaic Plot** (also called a *Spine Plot* in its two-category form) is a graphical representation of a two-way contingency table in which:
- The **width of each bar** is proportional to the **marginal frequency** $n_{i\cdot}$ of $X = X_i$. So if HR has 100 employees and Tech has 200, the Tech bar will be twice as wide as the HR bar.
- Within each bar, **vertical segments** are drawn for each level of $Y$. The **height of each segment** is proportional to the **conditional relative frequency** $f_{j \mid i} = n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$.

Thus, a Mosaic Plot simultaneously encodes:
- The **marginal distribution of X** (via widths).
- The **conditional distribution of Y given X** (via segment heights).

**Engineering Scenario:** In a manufacturing firm comparing defect rates across three production lines with **unequal output volumes**, a mosaic plot is preferred over a stacked bar chart because the bar widths automatically correct for the differing sample sizes, allowing a fair visual comparison of defect proportions. A regular stacked bar chart with unequal bar heights would visually mislead the reader.

##### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Expected Frequencies** $E_{ij} = n_{i\cdot} n_{\cdot j}/n$:

- $E_{\text{HR, Low}} = (100 \times 60)/400 = 15$
- $E_{\text{HR, Med}} = (100 \times 140)/400 = 35$
- $E_{\text{HR, High}} = (100 \times 200)/400 = 50$
- $E_{\text{Fin, Low}} = (100 \times 60)/400 = 15$
- $E_{\text{Fin, Med}} = (100 \times 140)/400 = 35$
- $E_{\text{Fin, High}} = (100 \times 200)/400 = 50$
- $E_{\text{Tech, Low}} = (200 \times 60)/400 = 30$
- $E_{\text{Tech, Med}} = (200 \times 140)/400 = 70$
- $E_{\text{Tech, High}} = (200 \times 200)/400 = 100$

**Step 2 — Compute $\chi^2$:**

$$
\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}
$$

- $(30-15)^2/15 = 225/15 = 15.000$
- $(40-35)^2/35 = 25/35 = 0.714$
- $(30-50)^2/50 = 400/50 = 8.000$
- $(20-15)^2/15 = 25/15 = 1.667$
- $(50-35)^2/35 = 225/35 = 6.429$
- $(30-50)^2/50 = 400/50 = 8.000$
- $(10-30)^2/30 = 400/30 = 13.333$
- $(50-70)^2/70 = 400/70 = 5.714$
- $(140-100)^2/100 = 1600/100 = 16.000$

Sum:
$$
\chi^2 = 15.000 + 0.714 + 8.000 + 1.667 + 6.429 + 8.000 + 13.333 + 5.714 + 16.000 = 74.857
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cramer's V:**

Here $r = 3$, $c = 3$, so $\min(r-1, c-1) = 2$.
$$
V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{n \cdot \min(r-1,\, c-1)}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{74.857}{400 \times 2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{74.857}{800}} = \sqrt{0.0936} \approx 0.306
$$

**Interpretation:** Cramer's V = 0.306 indicates a **moderate association** between department and job satisfaction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $n_{ij}$ with $f_{ij}$:** Always read the question carefully — if it says "frequency," use $n_{ij}$; if "relative frequency," use $n_{ij}/n$. Mixing them up loses 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the dot notation:** Writing $\sum_j n_i$ instead of $n_{i\cdot}$ is a notation error that examiners penalize.
> 3. **Misapplying Cramer's V to a $2 \times 2$ table:** For $2 \times 2$, use **Phi**; Cramer's V is for larger tables. Marks are deducted if used incorrectly.
> 4. **Not stating the null hypothesis** before computing $\chi^2$ — KTU expects $H_0$: *X and Y are independent*, $H_1$: *associated*.
> 5. **Confusing the axis of bar chart** — In a clustered bar chart, one categorical variable goes on the x-axis and the other is encoded by bar groups. Reversing them confuses the interpretation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **contingency table** is the numerical foundation for every plot of two categorical variables.
- ✅ **Joint frequency** $n_{ij}$, **marginal frequency** $n_{i\cdot}$ / $n_{\cdot j}$, **relative frequency** $f_{ij} = n_{ij}/n$, **conditional relative frequency** $f_{j \mid i} = n_{ij}/n_{i\cdot}$.
- ✅ The **expected frequency** under independence is $E_{ij} = n_{i\cdot} n_{\cdot j}/n$.
- ✅ The **chi-square statistic** $\chi^2 = \sum (O - E)^2 / E$ measures deviation from independence.
- ✅ **Clustered Bar Chart**: compares absolute counts across combinations.
- ✅ **Stacked Bar Chart**: shows composition + row totals.
- ✅ **100% Stacked Bar Chart**: compares conditional proportions.
- ✅ **Mosaic / Spine Plot**: shows marginal of X (width) and conditional of Y (height) simultaneously.
- ✅ **Heat Map**: best for large tables; use Pearson residuals to highlight deviation.
- ✅ **Phi** is for $2 \times 2$ tables; **Cramer's V** is the generalization for $r \times c$.
- ✅ **Degrees of freedom** for chi-square test of independence: $df = (r - 1)(c - 1)$.
- ✅ The graph type depends on whether you want to emphasize **counts, proportions, deviations, or joint marginal + conditional** information.
- ✅ Nominal variables have *no order*; ordinal variables have a *natural order*. Both use the same plots, but ordinal categories should be arranged along the natural order on the axis.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures of Association for Two Discrete Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measures of Association for Two Discrete Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In statistical data analytics, when two variables are **categorical (discrete / nominal)**, we cannot compute the usual Pearson correlation coefficient because that requires numerical (interval/ratio) data. Instead, we use a family of statistical tools called **Measures of Association for Two Discrete Variables** that quantify the *strength* and *direction* of the statistical relationship between two qualitative attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCDT503 – Module 2):**
> A *Measure of Association* is a numerical index, derived from a cross-tabulation (contingency table), that summarizes the degree of dependence between two discrete (categorical) variables. The principal measures are **Chi-Square Statistic, Phi Coefficient, Cramér's V, Contingency Coefficient, Odds Ratio, and Yule's Q**.

Mathematically, the underlying idea is to compare the **Observed Frequencies ($O_{ij}$)** in a contingency table with the **Expected Frequencies ($E_{ij}$)** that would occur if the two variables were statistically independent.

$$H_0: \text{Variable X and Variable Y are independent} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{They are associated}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a KTU campus canteen and notice that **left-handed students** seem to prefer **samosas** while **right-handed students** prefer **vada pav**. Are they really different, or did you just observe it by chance?

- A *Measure of Association* is like a **"surprise meter"**.
- If both variables are completely unrelated, the observed counts will closely match the expected counts (low surprise → small measure value).
- If they are strongly related, the observed counts will differ greatly from the expected counts (high surprise → large measure value).

> [!NOTE]
> Think of each measure as a different **ruler**: some (like Phi, Cramér's V) are bounded between 0 and 1 (standardized rulers), while Chi-Square has no upper bound (an unstandardized ruler). The **Odds Ratio** and **Yule's Q** are special rulers designed for 2×2 tables.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Ranges (At a Glance)

| Measure | Applicable Table Size | Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) | Any $r \times c$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Test of independence |
| Phi Coefficient ($\phi$) | $2 \times 2$ only | $[0, 1]$ | Strength of association |
| Cramér's V | Any $r \times c$ | $[0, 1]$ | Strength of association |
| Contingency Coefficient (C) | Any $r \times c$ | $[0, <1]$ | Strength of association |
| Odds Ratio (OR) | $2 \times 2$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Direction + magnitude |
| Yule's Q | $2 \times 2$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Direction + magnitude |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Contingency Table — Foundation of All Measures

A **Contingency Table** (also called a **Cross-Tabulation**) is a matrix where:
- **Rows ($r$)** represent categories of variable X.
- **Columns ($c$)** represent categories of variable Y.
- $O_{ij}$ = Observed frequency in cell $(i, j)$.
- $R_i$ = Row total for row $i$.
- $C_j$ = Column total for column $j$.
- $N$ = Grand total of all observations.

$$N = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} O_{ij}$$

The **Expected Frequency** under the null hypothesis of independence is:

$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \times C_j}{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical "Why" behind $E_{ij}$:** If X and Y are independent, then the probability of falling in cell $(i,j)$ is $P(X_i) \times P(Y_j)$. Multiplying this joint probability by $N$ yields the expected count. So $E_{ij}$ is the count we would *expect* by pure chance.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Testing Association

The general workflow every KTU problem follows:

1. **Construct** the $r \times c$ contingency table from raw data.
2. **Compute** the grand total $N$ and marginal totals ($R_i$, $C_j$).
3. **Calculate** the expected frequency $E_{ij}$ for each cell.
4. **Compute** the Chi-Square statistic $\chi^2$.
5. **Determine** the degrees of freedom $df = (r-1)(c-1)$.
6. **Compare** computed $\chi^2$ with the critical value $\chi^2_{\alpha, df}$.
7. **Quantify strength** using Phi, Cramér's V, or Contingency Coefficient.
8. **For 2×2 tables** compute Odds Ratio or Yule's Q.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Measure | Formula | Range / Value | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Frequency | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$ | $E_{ij} \geq 5$ recommended | Always, as first step |
| 2 | Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Test of independence |
| 3 | Degrees of Freedom | $df = (r-1)(c-1)$ | Integer $\geq 1$ | Lookup critical value |
| 4 | Phi Coefficient | $\phi = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{N}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Only for 2×2 tables** |
| 5 | Cramér's V | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{N \cdot \min(r-1,\,c-1)}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Any $r \times c$ table |
| 6 | Contingency Coefficient | $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + N}}$ | $[0, 1)$ | Any $r \times c$ table |
| 7 | Odds Ratio (2×2) | $OR = \dfrac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | 2×2 table |
| 8 | Yule's Q | $Q = \dfrac{ad - bc}{ad + bc}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | 2×2 table |

**Interpretation Rules:**
- $\chi^2 = 0$ → perfect independence.
- $\chi^2$ large → strong evidence of association.
- $\phi, V, C$ closer to 1 → strong association.
- $OR = 1$ → no association; $OR > 1$ → positive; $OR < 1$ → negative.
- $Q = 0$ → no association; $Q = +1$ → perfect positive; $Q = -1$ → perfect negative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The **Contingency Coefficient $C$ never reaches 1**. Its maximum is $\sqrt{(k-1)/k}$ where $k = \min(r, c)$. Do not write "C = 1" as the maximum in exams.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Data Analytics Utility

- **Medical Research:** Testing whether a new drug (Yes/No) is associated with recovery (Yes/No) using a 2×2 table → Odds Ratio.
- **Marketing Analytics:** Checking if purchase preference (Brand A/B/C) depends on age group (18-25, 26-40, 40+) using a 3×3 table → Cramér's V.
- **Quality Engineering:** Determining if defect type is independent of the production shift.
- **Social Sciences / Survey Analysis:** Measuring association between education level and voting pattern.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Frequency $E_{ij}$

Under the null hypothesis of independence:

$$P(X_i \cap Y_j) = P(X_i) \cdot P(Y_j)$$

Estimating the marginal probabilities from the data:

$$P(X_i) = \frac{R_i}{N} \quad \text{and} \quad P(Y_j) = \frac{C_j}{N}$$

Therefore, the expected number of observations in cell $(i, j)$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{ij} &= N \cdot P(X_i \cap Y_j) \\
&= N \cdot P(X_i) \cdot P(Y_j) \\
&= N \cdot \frac{R_i}{N} \cdot \frac{C_j}{N} \\
&= \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **multiplication rule of probability** for independent events, scaled by the sample size.

## 3.2 Derivation: Chi-Square Statistic $\chi^2$

The Chi-Square statistic is a **sum of squared standardized deviations** between observed and expected counts.

For a single cell, the squared deviation normalized by expectation is:

$$Z_{ij} = \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

Summing across all $r \times c$ cells:

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why divide by $E_{ij}$?** To standardize. A deviation of 10 from an expectation of 1000 is trivial, but a deviation of 10 from an expectation of 12 is massive. Dividing by $E_{ij}$ removes the scale effect.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cramér's V from Phi

The Phi coefficient is restricted to 2×2 tables. To extend the idea of a standardized measure to larger tables, Crámér (1946) divided Phi by $\sqrt{\min(r-1, c-1)}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N}} \\
V &= \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{\min(r-1, c-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N \cdot \min(r-1, c-1)}}
\end{aligned}$$

This guarantees $0 \le V \le 1$ regardless of table size.

## 3.4 Derivation: Odds Ratio and Yule's Q

For a 2×2 table:

|  | $Y = 1$ | $Y = 2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $X = 1$ | $a$ | $b$ |
| $X = 2$ | $c$ | $d$ |

**Odds of Y=1 given X=1:** $\dfrac{a}{b}$

**Odds of Y=1 given X=2:** $\dfrac{c}{d}$

**Odds Ratio (OR):**

$$\begin{aligned}
OR &= \frac{a/b}{c/d} = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}
\end{aligned}$$

**Yule's Q** is a symmetric transformation that bounds the OR to $[-1, 1]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{OR - 1}{OR + 1} = \frac{\frac{ad}{bc} - 1}{\frac{ad}{bc} + 1} = \frac{ad - bc}{ad + bc}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Odds Ratio is widely used in **logistic regression** and **medical diagnostics** because it is mathematically convenient (multiplicative on the log scale). Yule's Q is preferred when a symmetric, bounded measure is needed.

## 3.5 Complete Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A data analyst studies 200 KTU students to check whether *passing the Data Analytics exam* is associated with *attending coaching classes*. The 2×2 contingency table is:

|  | Pass | Fail | **Row Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coaching: Yes** | $a = 80$ | $b = 20$ | $R_1 = 100$ |
| **Coaching: No** | $c = 30$ | $d = 70$ | $R_2 = 100$ |
| **Column Total** | $C_1 = 110$ | $C_2 = 90$ | $N = 200$ |

**Step 1 — Compute Expected Frequencies:**

$$E_{11} = \frac{R_1 \cdot C_1}{N} = \frac{100 \times 110}{200} = 55$$

$$E_{12} = \frac{R_1 \cdot C_2}{N} = \frac{100 \times 90}{200} = 45$$

$$E_{21} = \frac{R_2 \cdot C_1}{N} = \frac{100 \times 110}{200} = 55$$

$$E_{22} = \frac{R_2 \cdot C_2}{N} = \frac{100 \times 90}{200} = 45$$

**Step 2 — Compute Chi-Square Statistic:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \frac{(80-55)^2}{55} + \frac{(20-45)^2}{45} + \frac{(30-55)^2}{55} + \frac{(70-45)^2}{45} \\
&= \frac{625}{55} + \frac{625}{45} + \frac{625}{55} + \frac{625}{45} \\
&= 11.3636 + 13.8889 + 11.3636 + 13.8889 \\
&= 50.505
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Degrees of Freedom:**

$$df = (r-1)(c-1) = (2-1)(2-1) = 1$$

**Step 4 — Critical Value and Decision:**

At significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ and $df = 1$, the critical value is $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$.

Since $\chi^2_{\text{computed}} = 50.505 \gg 3.841$, we **reject $H_0$**. There is a highly significant association between coaching and passing.

**Step 5 — Phi Coefficient:**

$$\phi = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{50.505}{200}} = \sqrt{0.2525} \approx 0.5025$$

Interpretation: **Moderate-to-strong** association (Phi is bounded in $[0, 1]$).

**Step 6 — Odds Ratio:**

$$OR = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c} = \frac{80 \times 70}{20 \times 30} = \frac{5600}{600} = 9.333$$

Interpretation: The odds of passing for coached students are **9.33 times** the odds of passing for non-coached students.

**Step 7 — Yule's Q:**

$$Q = \frac{ad - bc}{ad + bc} = \frac{(80 \times 70) - (20 \times 30)}{(80 \times 70) + (20 \times 30)} = \frac{5600 - 600}{5600 + 600} = \frac{5000}{6200} \approx 0.806$$

Interpretation: Strong positive association ($Q$ close to $+1$).

**Step 8 — Cramér's V (2×2 case, equals Phi):**

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{50.505}{200 \times 1}} = 0.5025$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-verification:** For a 2×2 table, $\phi = V$. This is a quick sanity check KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.6 Worked Example: 3×3 Contingency Table (Cramér's V Use Case)

**Problem:** A KTU placement cell surveys 300 students about preferred job sector (IT, Core, Govt) and branch (CSE, ECE, MECH).

|  | IT | Core | Govt | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CSE** | 70 | 20 | 10 | 100 |
| **ECE** | 50 | 30 | 20 | 100 |
| **MECH** | 30 | 40 | 30 | 100 |
| **Total** | 150 | 90 | 60 | 300 |

**Expected Frequencies:**

$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{300}$$

$$E_{11} = \frac{100 \times 150}{300} = 50, \quad E_{12} = \frac{100 \times 90}{300} = 30, \quad E_{13} = \frac{100 \times 60}{300} = 20$$

By symmetry of row and column totals, all $E_{ij} = 30$. So every cell expects 30.

**Chi-Square Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \frac{(70-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(20-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(10-30)^2}{30} \\
&\quad + \frac{(50-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(30-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(20-30)^2}{30} \\
&\quad + \frac{(30-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(40-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(30-30)^2}{30} \\
&= \frac{1600 + 100 + 400 + 400 + 0 + 100 + 0 + 100 + 0}{30} \\
&= \frac{2700}{30} = 90
\end{aligned}$$

**Degrees of Freedom:**

$$df = (3-1)(3-1) = 4$$

**Cramér's V:**

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N \cdot \min(r-1, c-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{90}{300 \times 2}} = \sqrt{\frac{90}{600}} = \sqrt{0.15} \approx 0.387$$

**Contingency Coefficient:**

$$C = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + N}} = \sqrt{\frac{90}{90 + 300}} = \sqrt{\frac{90}{390}} = \sqrt{0.2308} \approx 0.480$$

Interpretation: Moderate association between branch and preferred job sector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note that Phi is NOT used here** because the table is larger than 2×2. Use Cramér's V or Contingency Coefficient instead.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow: Hypothesis Testing & Association Quantification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Categorical Data] --> B[Step 1: Build r x c Contingency Table]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Marginal Totals Ri, Cj, and N]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Expected Frequencies Eij = Ri x Cj / N]
    D --> E{Step 4: All Eij >= 5?}
    E -- No --> F[Apply Yate Correction or Combine Categories]
    E -- Yes --> G[Step 5: Compute Chi-Square Statistic]
    G --> H[Step 6: Determine df = r-1 x c-1]
    H --> I[Step 7: Lookup Critical Value chi-alpha-df]
    I --> J{Step 8: Chi-Square computed greater than Critical?}
    J -- No --> K[Fail to Reject H0: No Association]
    J -- Yes --> L[Reject H0: Significant Association Exists]
    L --> M{Table Size?}
    M -- 2x2 --> N[Compute Phi, Odds Ratio, Yules Q]
    M -- Larger than 2x2 --> O[Compute Cramers V and Contingency C]
    N --> P[Step 9: Interpret Strength and Direction]
    O --> P
    K --> Q[Step 10: Report Conclusion]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Choosing the Right Measure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputBlock [INPUT BLOCK]
        X1[Two Discrete Variables X and Y]
    end
    subgraph TableBlock [TABLE CONSTRUCTION BLOCK]
        X2[Form r x c Contingency Table]
        X3[Compute N, Ri, Cj]
        X4[Compute Eij]
    end
    subgraph TestBlock [STATISTICAL TEST BLOCK]
        X5[Chi-Square Statistic]
        X6[Degrees of Freedom]
        X7[Decision: Reject H0 or Not]
    end
    subgraph MeasureBlock [MEASURE SELECTION BLOCK]
        Y1[Phi: 2x2 table only]
        Y2[Cramers V: any r x c]
        Y3[Contingency C: any r x c]
        Y4[Odds Ratio: 2x2 medical studies]
        Y5[Yules Q: 2x2 symmetric bounded]
    end
    subgraph OutputBlock [OUTPUT BLOCK]
        Z1[Strength of Association]
        Z2[Direction of Association]
        Z3[Statistical Significance]
    end
    X1 --> X2 --> X3 --> X4 --> X5
    X4 --> X5
    X5 --> X6 --> X7
    X7 --> Y1
    X7 --> Y2
    X7 --> Y3
    X7 --> Y4
    X7 --> Y5
    Y1 --> Z1
    Y2 --> Z1
    Y3 --> Z1
    Y4 --> Z2
    Y5 --> Z2
    X7 --> Z3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Which Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Two Discrete Variables] --> T{Table is 2x2?}
    T -- Yes --> U{Purpose?}
    U -- Test Independence --> V1[Use Chi-Square Test]
    U -- Strength of Association --> V2[Use Phi Coefficient]
    U -- Direction Bounded --> V3[Use Yules Q in minus 1 to 1]
    U -- Medical or Risk Study --> V4[Use Odds Ratio]
    T -- No, r x c with r greater than 2 --> W{Standardized Strength?}
    W -- Yes --> X1[Use Cramers V in 0 to 1]
    W -- No, raw strength --> X2[Use Chi-Square statistic directly]
    W -- Quick bounded estimate --> X3[Use Contingency Coefficient C]
    V1 --> END[Output Interpretation]
    V2 --> END
    V3 --> END
    V4 --> END
    X1 --> END
    X2 --> END
    X3 --> END
```
