# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Differentiate between nominal and ordinal data with one suitable example each. State which charts are appropriate for visualizing each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Nominal data consists of discrete categories with no inherent order (e.g., blood group, gender, eye color). Ordinal data consists of categories with a meaningful ranking but unequal or undefined intervals (e.g., customer satisfaction rating on a 1–5 Likert scale, education level: high school < bachelor's < master's).

> **Charts (1.5 marks):** Nominal → **Bar Chart** (preferred) or **Pie Chart** (≤6 categories). Ordinal → **Stacked Bar Chart** (preserves order) or **Dot Plot** ranked by category. Line charts must be avoided for both, as they imply continuity.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**Why is a line chart not appropriate for visualizing nominal data? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Justification (2 marks):** A line chart connects data points with straight segments, implying a **continuous trend** or *interpolation* between successive points. Nominal categories (e.g., Apple, Banana, Cherry) have **no inherent order and no numerical distance** between them, so drawing a line from "Apple" to "Banana" suggests a smooth transition that is mathematically false.

> **Example (1 mark):** Plotting monthly fruit sales where months are nominal labels would falsely imply that February's sales are an "interpolation" between January and March — a meaningless concept for unordered categories. A **bar chart** should be used instead.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE format: Answer **any one full question** from the module choice. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Stevens' four levels of measurement with examples. For each level, list **two appropriate visualization techniques** and justify why they are suitable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A survey records the following data for 8 employees:

- **Department:** HR, IT, Finance, IT, HR, IT, Finance, IT
- **Performance Rating:** 3, 4, 5, 2, 4, 5, 3, 4 (Likert 1–5)
- **Years of Experience:** 1, 5, 8, 2, 6, 10, 4, 7
- **Monthly Bonus (₹k):** 2, 6, 10, 3, 7, 12, 5, 9

For each variable, classify its scale and recommend the most appropriate visualization. Compute the **median** and **IQR** for Years of Experience.

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Stevens' Four Levels (4 marks):**

1. **Nominal Scale** — Unordered categorical labels. Operations: equality $(=)$, counting. Example: Department (HR, IT, Finance).
2. **Ordinal Scale** — Ordered categories, no equal-interval guarantee. Operations: ranking $(<, >)$. Example: Performance Rating (1–5 Likert).
3. **Interval Scale** — Ordered, equal intervals, **no true zero**. Operations: addition, subtraction. Example: Temperature in °C (0°C ≠ "no temperature").
4. **Ratio Scale** — Ordered, equal intervals, **true zero exists**. Operations: all four arithmetic operations including ratio. Example: Monthly Bonus, Years of Experience.

**Visualization Pairings & Justification (3 marks):**

| Scale | Chart 1 | Chart 2 | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal | Bar Chart | Pie Chart | Bar shows magnitude per category without implying order. Pie shows share of whole. |
| Ordinal | Stacked Bar | Dot Plot | Stacked bar preserves Likert ordering along $x$-axis. |
| Interval | Histogram | Line Chart | Both treat axis as continuous, no zero-based ratio required. |
| Ratio | Box Plot | Scatter Plot | Supports IQR, mean, ratio-comparisons. |

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Scale Classification (3 marks):**

| Variable | Scale | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Department | Nominal | Categories with no order. |
| Performance Rating | Ordinal | 1 < 2 < … < 5, but gap not equal. |
| Years of Experience | Ratio | True zero (0 yrs = no experience). |
| Monthly Bonus | Ratio | True zero (₹0 = no bonus). |

**Recommended Visualizations (1 mark):**
- Department → **Bar Chart**.
- Performance Rating → **Stacked Bar Chart** (Likert 1–5).
- Years of Experience → **Histogram or Box Plot**.
- Monthly Bonus → **Histogram, Scatter Plot (vs. experience)**.

**Median and IQR of Years of Experience (3 marks):**

Sorted data: $\{1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10\}$, $n = 8$ (even).

$$
\text{Median} = \frac{x_{(4)} + x_{(5)}}{2} = \frac{5 + 6}{2} = 5.5 \text{ years}
$$

Lower half: $\{1, 2, 4, 5\}$ → $Q_1 = \frac{2+4}{2} = 3$.

Upper half: $\{6, 7, 8, 10\}$ → $Q_3 = \frac{7+8}{2} = 7.5$.

$$
\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 7.5 - 3 = 4.5 \text{ years}
$$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss why the **mean** is a valid measure of central tendency only for interval and ratio data. With reference to the formula

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

explain why it fails for nominal and ordinal data. Provide one example of failure for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital records **patient satisfaction scores** on a 1–10 scale and **waiting time (in minutes)**. Which of these is interval and which is ratio? Justify. For each, recommend **two visualizations** and describe what insight each chart reveals.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Why Mean Fails for Nominal (3 marks):**

The mean formula

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

requires **arithmetic addition and division**, which are only valid when the data is numerical and supports these operations. Nominal data (e.g., Blood Group: A, B, AB, O) has no numerical encoding that permits meaningful addition.

> **Example of failure:** If patient genders are coded as M=1, F=2, the mean becomes 1.4 — a value "1.4" which corresponds to *no real gender*. This is mathematically and semantically nonsensical.

**Why Mean Fails for Ordinal (2 marks):**

Ordinal data supports ranking but the **distances between ranks are not equal**. The mean treats them as equal.

> **Example of failure:** Survey responses: Strongly Disagree (1), Disagree (2), Neutral (3), Agree (4), Strongly Agree (5). If the mean is 3.2, it suggests the "average" opinion lies between Neutral and Agree — but the gap between 3 and 4 is not numerically equivalent to the gap between 4 and 5 in psychological terms. Hence, the mean distorts the true central tendency. **Median or Mode is preferred.**

**When Mean is Valid (2 marks):**

- **Interval data:** Equal intervals → addition valid (e.g., average temperature of 25.5°C is meaningful).
- **Ratio data:** All arithmetic operations including ratios valid (e.g., average income ₹50,000 is meaningful, and income ratio comparisons are valid).

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Scale Identification (3 marks):**

| Variable | Scale | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Patient Satisfaction (1–10) | **Ordinal** (or Interval) | Equal-interval Likert is often treated as **Interval** in practice (parametric tests), but strictly, psychological distance between 1 and 2 ≠ 7 and 8. KTU answer key accepts **Interval** for Likert. |
| Waiting Time (minutes) | **Ratio** | True zero (0 min = no wait), equal intervals, all arithmetic valid. |

**Visualizations and Insights (4 marks):**

**Satisfaction (Interval):**
- **Histogram:** Reveals the *shape* of the satisfaction distribution — is it skewed toward high or low scores? Identifies modal satisfaction.
- **Bar Chart (grouped by department):** Compares *average satisfaction* across departments, exposing which unit needs service improvement.

**Waiting Time (Ratio):**
- **Box Plot:** Displays the *five-number summary* — median wait, spread (IQR), and outliers (e.g., patients waiting > 60 min). Tukey's fences flag operational bottlenecks.
- **Histogram / Density Plot:** Shows the *frequency distribution* of wait times; reveals whether the hospital's wait is normally distributed or right-skewed (long-tail delays).
- **Scatter Plot (vs. time-of-day):** Reveals *temporal patterns* — e.g., wait times spike during 11 AM – 1 PM.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing "interval" with "ratio"** — students frequently say "temperature is ratio." Loss: **1 mark** per occurrence. (Hint: 0°C ≠ no temperature.)
> 2. **Recommending a line chart for ordinal Likert** — violates the "no continuity" rule. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the true-zero test** — when asked to distinguish interval vs. ratio, students skip the zero check. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Computing mean for nominal/ordinal** — examiner's auto-flag. Loss: **2 marks** + concept-mark penalty.
> 5. **Pie chart with >6 slices or for ordinal** — examiner penalizes. Use bar chart instead.
> 6. **Not providing a justification** — KTU awards marks for *reasoning*, not just naming the chart. Always write "because…" or "since…".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **30-Second Pre-Exam Recall Sheet:**

- ✅ **Stevens' 4 scales:** Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** "**N**o **O**rder, **O**rdered, **I**nterval, **R**atio" → **NOIR**.
- ✅ **True zero present only in Ratio.**
- ✅ **Equal intervals present in Interval and Ratio.**
- ✅ **Mode → nominal only. Median → ordinal+. Mean → interval+ratio.**
- ✅ **Line chart → continuous $x$-axis (interval/ratio).** Never for nominal.
- ✅ **Pie chart → nominal, ≤6 categories.** Avoid for ordinal.
- ✅ **Histogram → interval/ratio only** (binned numeric axis).
- ✅ **Box plot → ratio/interval** (needs quartiles).
- ✅ **Scatter plot → two ratio/interval variables.**
- ✅ **Stacked bar → ordinal** (preserves Likert order).
- ✅ **Bar chart → universal safest choice for categorical.**
- ✅ **Box plot outlier rule:** outside $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}, \; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}]$.
- ✅ **Frequency formulas:**
  - Relative Frequency $= f_i / n$.
  - Percentage $= (f_i / n) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Five-Number Summary:** $\{x_{\min}, Q_1, \text{Median}, Q_3, x_{\max}\}$.
- ✅ **Decision rule:** Match chart's geometric primitive to variable's mathematical operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Preprocessing - Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Preprocessing - Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Preprocessing – Cleaning (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> *Data cleaning* (also called *data cleansing* or *data scrubbing*) is the critical preliminary phase in the Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD) pipeline that detects, corrects, or removes **corrupt, inaccurate, incomplete, or irrelevant** records from a dataset. Its objective is to transform "dirty" real-world data into a **consistent, noise-free, and analytically reliable** form suitable for downstream mining, modelling, and inference tasks.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, data cleaning directly supports:
- **CO3**: *Apply data preprocessing techniques to prepare raw datasets for analytical modelling.*
- **CO4**: *Evaluate the impact of preprocessing on mining algorithm performance.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Recipe Preparation" Metaphor**
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a complex dish. Before you can cook, you must:
> 1. **Wash the vegetables** (remove dirt / noise).
> 2. **Throw away rotten items** (handle missing or corrupt entries).
> 3. **Standardize your cuts** (uniform binning / normalization).
> 4. **Discard inedible parts** (irrelevant attributes).
>
> Data cleaning is the **"mise-en-place"** of data analytics — the organised preparation of raw data so that the actual "cooking" (modelling) yields trustworthy results. Garbage In = Garbage Out (GIGO) is the fundamental principle: no sophisticated algorithm can rescue bad data.

### 1.3 Why Data Cleaning is the Most Time-Consuming Phase

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark (Anaconda / KDnuggets Surveys)**
> Data scientists typically spend **60 – 80 %** of their project time on data preparation and cleaning. A clean dataset of mediocre models will outperform a dirty dataset fed into the most advanced algorithm.

### 1.4 Principal Forms of "Dirty" Data

| Dirty Data Form | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Missing Values** | Absent attribute values | Age column blank for a customer |
| **Noisy Data** | Random variance / measurement error | Sensor reading `49.9` instead of `50.0` |
| **Inconsistent Data** | Contradictory codes / formats | `Bengaluru` vs `Bangalore` vs `BLR` |
| **Duplicate Records** | Identical tuples | Same customer inserted twice |
| **Outliers (legitimate)** | Valid but extreme values | CEO salary in employee dataset |
| **Irrelevant Attributes** | Useless for mining | Row IDs, timestamps of ingestion |

---

### 1.5 The Formal Data Quality Dimensions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A dataset is deemed "clean" only when it satisfies the following six dimensions — a concept frequently tested in KTU 2-mark questions:
> 1. **Accuracy** – Conformance to the true value.
> 2. **Completeness** – All required values are present.
> 3. **Consistency** – Same information has identical representation.
> 4. **Timeliness** – Data is up-to-date.
> 5. **Believability** – Trustworthiness of source.
> 6. **Interpretability** – Ease of understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Multi-Phase Theoretical Framework of Data Cleaning

Data cleaning, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus, is decomposed into **four inter-locking sub-phases**:

#### Phase 1 — *Missing Value Analysis*
- Detection of null / NaN / blank entries.
- Mechanisms for handling: **Deletion** vs **Imputation**.

#### Phase 2 — *Noisy Data Smoothing*
- Removal of random measurement variance.
- Techniques: **Binning, Regression, Clustering, Combined Inspection**.

#### Phase 3 — *Inconsistency Resolution*
- Harmonization of conflicting categorical codes.
- Standardization of measurement units, date formats, and string cases.

#### Phase 4 — *Process Automation & Auditing*
- Mapping rules using **metadata** and ETL pipelines.

### 2.2 Detailed Mechanisms for Handling Missing Data

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Categorisation — Always quote the "Two-Track" approach in exams.**

**Track A — Ignore / Delete the Tuple**
- Used only when the class label is missing (classification tasks).
- Pitfall: Inefficient when the dataset is small — leads to **information loss**.

**Track B — Fill in (Impute) the Missing Value**
- **B.1 Manual Filling** – Domain expert supplies value (expensive, only feasible for tiny datasets of < 1,000 records).
- **B.2 Global Constant** – Replace all missing entries with a placeholder like `"Unknown"` or `-1`. Fastest but introduces **bias**.
- **B.3 Attribute Mean** – Replace with $\bar{x}$ of the column. Best for symmetric, normal-like distributions.
- **B.4 Attribute Mean within Class** – Conditional mean $\bar{x}_C$ where $C$ is the class label. **Most preferred in KTU board answers**.
- **B.5 Most Probable Value** – Use regression, decision trees, or EM-algorithm predictions. Highest accuracy; computationally heaviest.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your exam-day survival kit. Memorize the *formulas*; understand the *use-case* in the rightmost column.**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Type | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attribute Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Numeric | Symmetric numeric column |
| 2 | Class-Conditional Mean | $\bar{x}_C = \dfrac{1}{\vert C \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C} x_i$ | Numeric (per class) | Classification datasets |
| 3 | Binning — Equal-Width Bin Width | $w = \dfrac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{k}$ | Numeric | Uniform distribution |
| 4 | Binning — Equal-Frequency Bin Size | $s = \lceil n / k \rceil$ | Integer | Skewed distribution |
| 5 | Z-Score Noise Filter | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Outlier detection |
| 6 | Median Absolute Deviation | $\text{MAD} = \text{median}(\vert x_i - \tilde{x} \vert)$ | Numeric | Robust outlier detection |
| 7 | Min-Max Normalization | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min$ | Numeric | Bounded scaling to $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | Z-Score Normalization | $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu_A}{\sigma_A}$ | Numeric | Unbounded scaling, $\mu=0$ |
| 9 | Decimal Scaling | $v' = \dfrac{v}{10^{j}}$ | Numeric | Preserve digit shape |
| 10 | Tukey's IQR Filter | $\text{Lower} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}, \quad \text{Upper} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ | Numeric | Box-plot outlier flagging |
| 11 | Linear Regression Smoothing | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Numeric | Two correlated attributes |
| 12 | EM Iterative Mean Update | $\mu^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[x_i \mid \theta^{(t)}]$ | Numeric | Probabilistic imputation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** In tables inside the answer sheet, if you are asked to write the *bin width* for equal-frequency binning, students frequently write $w = (x_{\max} - x_{\min})/k$. That formula is for **equal-width** binning, not equal-frequency. Read the question stem carefully.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Healthcare:** Missing ECG readings in patient records must be imputed — a wrongly imputed value can misclassify arrhythmia.
> - **Banking:** Inconsistent currency codes (USD vs $ vs US Dollar) cause failed transactions in reconciliation systems.
> - **IoT / Smart Manufacturing:** Sensor noise smoothing via binning reduces false-alarm rates in predictive maintenance.
> - **E-Commerce:** Deduplication of customer records prevents double-counting in CRM analytics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are *fully worked* analytical derivations that appear verbatim in KTU End-Semester Examinations. Pay attention to the **inline text rows** that explain each algebraic transition.

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Equal-Frequency Binning (Smoothing by Bin Means)

**Problem:** Given the sorted dataset of sorted prices (in ₹ thousands) of 12 used cars:
$S = \{ 4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 31 \}$.
Perform **equal-frequency binning** with **bin depth = 4** using **bin means** for smoothing. Show every step.

**Step 1 — Determine the number of bins.**
Number of bins $k$ is obtained by dividing the dataset size $n$ by the desired bin depth $s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 12, \quad s = 4 \\
k &= \frac{n}{s} = \frac{12}{4} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* With 12 records and 4 records per bin, we obtain exactly 3 bins.

**Step 2 — Partition the sorted data into 3 equal-frequency bins.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bin 1} &: \{ 4, \ 8, \ 9, \ 15 \} \\
\text{Bin 2} &: \{ 21, \ 21, \ 24, \ 25 \} \\
\text{Bin 3} &: \{ 26, \ 28, \ 29, \ 31 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each bin contains exactly 4 sorted values — equal frequency is preserved.

**Step 3 — Compute the bin mean for each bin.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= \frac{4 + 8 + 9 + 15}{4} = \frac{36}{4} = 9.0 \\
\mu_2 &= \frac{21 + 21 + 24 + 25}{4} = \frac{91}{4} = 22.75 \\
\mu_3 &= \frac{26 + 28 + 29 + 31}{4} = \frac{114}{4} = 28.5
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Smoothing by *bin means* replaces every element in a bin with that bin's mean.

**Step 4 — Replace each value with its corresponding bin mean (smoothing step).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bin 1} &: \{ 9, \ 9, \ 9, \ 9 \} \\
\text{Bin 2} &: \{ 22.75, \ 22.75, \ 22.75, \ 22.75 \} \\
\text{Bin 3} &: \{ 28.5, \ 28.5, \ 28.5, \ 28.5 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Smoothing by bin medians (alternative).**
For verification, the medians are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{median}_1 &= \frac{8 + 9}{2} = 8.5 \\
\text{median}_2 &= \frac{21 + 24}{2} = 22.5 \\
\text{median}_3 &= \frac{28 + 29}{2} = 28.5
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* When the question specifies "smoothing by bin medians", use the central value of each bin instead of the mean.

**Step 6 — Smoothing by bin boundaries (alternative).**
For Bin 1, boundaries are 4 and 15. Each value is replaced by the closer boundary: $\{4, 8, 9, 15\}$ becomes $\{4, 8, 9, 15\}$ — only the value $8$ stays as $8$, and $4$ becomes $4$, $9$ becomes $9$, $15$ becomes $15$. The maximum (15) and minimum (4) of a bin are called its *boundaries*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three classical smoothing methods** (board-favourite):
> 1. **Smoothing by Bin Means** – Replace every value by the bin's mean.
> 2. **Smoothing by Bin Medians** – Replace every value by the bin's median.
> 3. **Smoothing by Bin Boundaries** – Replace every value by the nearest boundary (min or max of the bin).

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Imputation Using Class-Conditional Mean

**Problem:** Consider the following classification training data with one missing value:

| Customer | Income (₹ Lakh) | Class |
|---|---|---|
| C1 | 5 | High |
| C2 | 6 | High |
| C3 | 7 | High |
| C4 | **?** | High |
| C5 | 2 | Low |
| C6 | 3 | Low |
| C7 | 1 | Low |

Impute the missing income of C4 using the **class-conditional mean** approach.

**Step 1 — Identify the class of the missing record.**
The class label of C4 is known: `High`.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean income restricted to the `High` class only.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{\text{High}} &= \frac{5 + 6 + 7}{3} = \frac{18}{3} = 6.0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Only the three observed `High`-class incomes (5, 6, 7) participate in the mean.

**Step 3 — Replace the missing value.**

$$
\text{Income of C4} = 6.0 \ \text{Lakh}
$$

*Explanation:* Imputation preserves the *expected class-conditional distribution* — a critical property for downstream Naïve Bayes and Logistic Regression classifiers.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Min-Max Normalization on a Noisy Feature

**Problem:** A noisy sensor produces the following readings (in °C): $A = \{ 8, 12, 14, 16, 24, 26, 30 \}$. Apply **min-max normalization** to map them into the range $[0.0, 1.0]$.

**Step 1 — Identify the extremes of the attribute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_A &= 8, \quad \max_A = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — State the min-max formula.**

$$
v' = \frac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for the new range $[0, 1]$ (i.e., $new\_max = 1, \ new\_min = 0$).**

$$
v' = \frac{v - 8}{30 - 8} = \frac{v - 8}{22}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each normalized value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_{8} &= \frac{8 - 8}{22} = 0.0000 \\
v'_{12} &= \frac{12 - 8}{22} = 0.1818 \\
v'_{14} &= \frac{14 - 8}{22} = 0.2727 \\
v'_{16} &= \frac{16 - 8}{22} = 0.3636 \\
v'_{24} &= \frac{24 - 8}{22} = 0.7273 \\
v'_{26} &= \frac{26 - 8}{22} = 0.8182 \\
v'_{30} &= \frac{30 - 8}{22} = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The smallest value maps to 0 and the largest to 1, with all others scaled linearly in between.

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Outlier Detection via Tukey's IQR Method

**Problem:** A box-plot is to be drawn for $A = \{ 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 100 \}$. Identify outliers using the **1.5 × IQR rule**.

**Step 1 — Sort the data (already sorted).**

**Step 2 — Compute the quartiles $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3$.**
For $n = 9$, the median ($Q_2$) is the 5th value, $Q_2 = 16$.
$Q_1$ = median of the lower half (excluding $Q_2$) = median of $\{12, 13, 14, 15, 17\}$ → Wait, with $n=9$, lower half is first 4 values: $\{12, 13, 14, 15\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= \frac{13 + 14}{2} = 13.5 \\
Q_2 &= 16 \\
Q_3 &= \frac{18 + 19}{2} = 18.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inter-quartile range.**

$$
\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 18.5 - 13.5 = 5.0
$$

**Step 4 — Define the upper and lower Tukey fences.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Fence} &= Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 13.5 - 7.5 = 6.0 \\
\text{Upper Fence} &= Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 18.5 + 7.5 = 26.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Flag the outliers.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower outliers: } & \{ x \in A \mid x < 6.0 \} = \emptyset \\
\text{Upper outliers: } & \{ x \in A \mid x > 26.0 \} = \{ 100 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The value $100$ is flagged as a **mild outlier** (a *whisker* in the box-plot extends only to 26). The rest of the data lies inside the safe zone.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Cleaning Strategy

| Situation | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Less than 5 % missing, MCAR | Listwise deletion | Simple, low bias |
| 5 – 40 % missing, numeric | Mean / Median / Class-conditional mean | Preserves sample size |
| Over 40 % missing | Drop the attribute or use advanced EM | Prevents artificial bias |
| Numeric noise, low cardinality | Binning | Visually interpretable |
| Two correlated numeric attributes | Linear regression imputation | Captures inter-feature relation |
| Categorical inconsistency | Lookup-table mapping (metadata) | Centralized rule governance |
| High-dimensional outliers | Clustering-based detection (k-Means) | Group-wise separation |
| Massive data, low budget | Global constant fill (e.g., -1) | Acceptable trade-off |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — The Data Cleaning Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D] --> B[Audit and Profiling]
    B --> C{Is Data Dirty?}
    C -- No --> Z[Clean Dataset D Prime]
    C -- Yes --> D[Missing Value Detector]
    C -- Yes --> E[Noise Detector]
    C -- Yes --> F[Inconsistency Detector]
    D --> D1[Deletion Track]
    D --> D2[Imputation Track]
    D1 --> G[Merge Cleaned Streams]
    D2 --> G
    E --> E1[Binning]
    E --> E2[Regression Smoothing]
    E --> E3[Clustering Based Detection]
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F --> F1[Metadata Rule Engine]
    F1 --> G
    G --> H[Post Cleaning Validation]
    H --> I{Quality Threshold Met?}
    I -- No --> J[Iterate with Field Expert]
    J --> B
    I -- Yes --> Z
```

*Explanation:* The diagram maps the *flow* of a record through the cleaning pipeline. Detection branches (D, E, F) operate in parallel; their outputs converge at the merge node G; validation node H performs quality checks; iterative loops are permitted until thresholds are met.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — Decision Tree for Missing Value Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Missing Value Detected] --> B{Percentage Missing}
    B -- Less than 5 percent --> C[Use Listwise Deletion]
    B -- 5 to 40 percent --> D{Attribute Type}
    B -- Greater than 40 percent --> E[Drop Attribute or Use EM]
    D -- Numeric --> F{Class Available?}
    D -- Categorical --> G[Use Mode Imputation]
    F -- Yes --> H[Class Conditional Mean]
    F -- No --> I[Global Mean Imputation]
    H --> J[Validate Distribution]
    I --> J
    C --> J
    G --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K{Acceptable Bias?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Accept Imputation]
    K -- No --> M[Return to Strategy Selector]
    M --> A
```

*Explanation:* This decision logic mirrors the choice matrix presented in Section 3.5. KTU examiners reward candidates who can articulate the *branching conditions* — these reflect engineering judgement, not just formulas.

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Binning Workflow (Smoothing by Bin Means)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sorted Data] --> B[Choose Bin Depth s]
    B --> C[Compute Number of Bins k = n / s]
    C --> D[Partition into k Bins]
    D --> E[Compute Mean of Each Bin]
    E --> F[Replace All Bin Members by Mean]
    F --> G[Smoothed Output]
    G --> H{Choose Method}
    H -- Means --> I[Done]
    H -- Medians --> J[Replace by Median]
    H -- Boundaries --> K[Replace by Nearest Boundary]
    J --> I
    K --> I
```

*Explanation:* Binning workflow is linear. The method-selection branch (H) is what differentiates "smoothing by means", "smoothing by medians", and "smoothing by boundaries". KTU Part A questions often test whether the student can label this branch correctly.

---

### 4.4 Diagram D — Outlier vs. Noise Distinction (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspicious Data Point] --> B{Lies Outside 1.5xIQR}
    B -- No --> C[Considered Noise]
    B -- Yes --> D{Domain Validates It}
    D -- Yes --> E[Valid Outlier Keep It]
    D -- No --> F[Erroneous Outlier Remove or Cap]
    C --> G{Use Binning or Regression}
    G --> H[Apply Smoothing]
    E --> I[Retain for Analysis]
    F --> J[Flag and Impute]
```

*Explanation:* KTU students often confuse "noise" with "outliers". Noise is a *random error* in measurement (solved by binning/regression). An outlier is a *legitimate but extreme* value (kept or specially handled). This diagram forces the candidate to disambiguate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *These are short-answer conceptual questions, expected in approximately 6 minutes each.*

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**"Differentiate between noise and outliers in the context of data cleaning. Why is the distinction important?"** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Noise** refers to a *random error component* $e$ in a measured variable $x_{\text{measured}} = x_{\text{true}} + e$. It has zero mean and finite variance. Common in sensor data. Treated using **smoothing techniques** such as binning or regression. *(1 mark)*

2. **Outliers** are *legitimate data points* that lie far from the bulk of the distribution (e.g., the CEO's salary in a company's employee list). They are *not* errors. They are detected using **statistical fences** (Tukey's IQR, z-score) and are *retained* if valid, or *capped / imputed* if erroneous. *(1 mark)*

3. **Importance of distinction:** Misclassifying a valid outlier as noise destroys valuable information (e.g., fraud detection). Conversely, treating noise as a valid outlier biases descriptive statistics and model training. Correct identification ensures both **data integrity** and **information preservation**. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Explain any three techniques used for handling missing values in a dataset."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Ignore the Tuple:** When the class label is missing (in classification), the record is dropped. Simple, but reduces sample size — applicable only when $n$ is large. *(1 mark)*

2. **Fill with Attribute Mean:** Missing numeric values are replaced by the column mean $\bar{x}$. Useful for symmetric numeric attributes; less appropriate for skewed distributions where the **median** is preferred. *(1 mark)*

3. **Fill with Most Probable Value:** Use predictive modelling (e.g., regression, decision trees, EM algorithm) to estimate the missing value. Most accurate; computationally expensive — used when the missingness pattern is *not at random*. *(1 mark)*

*(Other acceptable: Manual filling, Global constant, Class-conditional mean.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B of ESE features an internal choice. You must answer ONE of the two alternatives. Each alternative is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.*

---

#### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **four major steps of data quality assessment and cleaning** as a process. Use a labelled flowchart in your answer. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** Consider the following dataset of exam scores (out of 100) of 16 students arranged in ascending order:
> $\{ 12, 18, 25, 30, 34, 38, 42, 45, \ 48, 52, 56, 60, 68, 75, 82, 95 \}$.
> Apply **equal-frequency binning** with **bin depth = 4** and perform **smoothing by bin means** and **smoothing by bin boundaries**. Show all intermediate steps. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

> **Step 1 — Definition of Data Quality:** Data quality assessment is the systematic evaluation of a dataset's fitness for use, measured against the dimensions of *accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, believability, and interpretability*. *(1 mark)*

> **Step 2 — Step (i): Data Auditing:** Identify missing values, outliers, and inconsistent values using *metadata-driven rules* and *profiling tools* (e.g., frequency analysis, mean, median, mode, variance per attribute). *(1.5 marks)*

> **Step 3 — Step (ii): Definition of Workflow / Pipeline:** Define the order of cleaning steps, the acceptable thresholds (e.g., maximum 30 % missing), and the imputation strategies for each attribute. *(1 mark)*

> **Step 4 — Step (iii): Execution of Cleaning:** Apply the workflow — handle missing values (deletion or imputation), smooth noise (binning / regression / clustering), resolve inconsistencies (lookup tables), and deduplicate. *(1.5 marks)*

> **Step 5 — Step (iv): Post-Cleansing Verification:** Re-run the audits to confirm quality metrics. If metrics fail, return to Step (ii). Document the cleaning rules as **metadata** for future ingestion. *(1 mark)*

> **Labelled Flowchart (1 mark):**
> `Raw Data → Audit → Workflow → Execute → Verify → (Iterate) → Clean Data`

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Determine the number of bins.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 16, \quad s = 4 \\
k &= \frac{16}{4} = 4 \ \text{bins}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Partition into 4 equal-frequency bins.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bin 1} &: \{ 12, 18, 25, 30 \} \\
\text{Bin 2} &: \{ 34, 38, 42, 45 \} \\
\text{Bin 3} &: \{ 48, 52, 56, 60 \} \\
\text{Bin 4} &: \{ 68, 75, 82, 95 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Smoothing by Bin Means.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= \frac{12+18+25+30}{4} = \frac{85}{4} = 21.25 \\
\mu_2 &= \frac{34+38+42+45}{4} = \frac{159}{4} = 39.75 \\
\mu_3 &= \frac{48+52+56+60}{4} = \frac{216}{4} = 54.00 \\
\mu_4 &= \frac{68+75+82+95}{4} = \frac{320}{4} = 80.00
\end{aligned}
$$

After mean-smoothing:

$$
\{21.25, 21.25, 21.25, 21.25, \ 39.75, 39.75, 39.75, 39.75, \ 54, 54, 54, 54, \ 80, 80, 80, 80\}
$$

**Step 4 — Smoothing by Bin Boundaries.**
For each value, replace it by the closer of the bin's min or max.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bin 1 bounds} &: \min=12, \max=30 \\
\text{Replacement} &: 12 \to 12, \ 18 \to 30, \ 25 \to 30, \ 30 \to 30 \\
\text{Bin 2 bounds} &: \min=34, \max=45 \\
\text{Replacement} &: 34 \to 34, \ 38 \to 45, \ 42 \to 45, \ 45 \to 45 \\
\text{Bin 3 bounds} &: \min=48, \max=60 \\
\text{Replacement} &: 48 \to 48, \ 52 \to 60, \ 56 \to 60, \ 60 \to 60 \\
\text{Bin 4 bounds} &: \min=68, \max=95 \\
\text{Replacement} &: 68 \to 68, \ 75 \to 95, \ 82 \to 95, \ 95 \to 95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark split-up (7 marks):** Bin computation (1), partition (1), mean calculation (2), boundary replacement (2), final array presentation (1).

---

#### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

> **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **min-max normalization** and **z-score normalization** techniques. State one advantage and one limitation of each. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A sensor produces the following voltage readings (in Volts):
> $V = \{ 2.1, 2.4, 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 3.0, 3.1, 5.5 \}$.
> Detect the outliers using the **1.5 × IQR rule**. Justify whether the value 5.5 should be treated as noise or as a valid outlier. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**1. Min-Max Normalization** performs a *linear transformation* to map value $v$ of attribute $A$ into the range $[new\_min, new\_max]$:

$$
v' = \frac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min
$$

*Example:* Age attribute $A = \{ 20, 30, 40, 50 \}$, mapped to $[0,1]$ gives $0.0, 0.333, 0.666, 1.0$.
*Advantage:* Preserves the *shape* of the distribution and the *relationships* between values.
*Limitation:* Highly sensitive to outliers — a single extreme value compresses all others near 0. *(3.5 marks)*

**2. Z-Score Normalization** (zero-mean, unit-variance) transforms $v$ to:

$$
v' = \frac{v - \mu_A}{\sigma_A}
$$

where $\mu_A$ is the mean and $\sigma_A$ is the standard deviation.
*Example:* For the same ages, $\mu = 35, \sigma \approx 12.91$. The value 50 maps to $(50-35)/12.91 \approx 1.16$.
*Advantage:* Robust when the actual min/max are unknown; useful for algorithms assuming Gaussian inputs (e.g., k-NN, PCA).
*Limitation:* Does not bound the output to a fixed interval — values can still be extreme. *(3.5 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3$.**
Sorted $V$ (already sorted). With $n = 10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= \frac{v_{(5)} + v_{(6)}}{2} = \frac{2.7 + 2.8}{2} = 2.75 \\
Q_1 &= \text{median of } \{2.1, 2.4, 2.5, 2.6\} = \frac{2.4 + 2.5}{2} = 2.45 \\
Q_3 &= \text{median of } \{2.9, 3.0, 3.1, 5.5\} = \frac{3.0 + 3.1}{2} = 3.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute IQR.**

$$
\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 3.05 - 2.45 = 0.60
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Tukey fences.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower} &= Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR} = 2.45 - 0.90 = 1.55 \\
\text{Upper} &= Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR} = 3.05 + 0.90 = 3.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the outliers.**

$$
V_{\text{outliers}} = \{ v \in V \mid v < 1.55 \ \text{or} \ v > 3.95 \} = \{ 5.5 \}
$$

**Step 5 — Justify the noise vs. valid-outlier decision.**

- The value $5.5$ is **statistically an outlier** because it lies far above the upper Tukey fence (3.95).
- However, whether it is *noise* or a *valid outlier* depends on the **domain knowledge**:
  - If the sensor specification allows voltages up to $5.5$ V (e.g., a transient surge), it is a **valid outlier** — keep it.
  - If the sensor's normal operating range is $[2.0, 3.5]$ V, then $5.5$ V is **noise / sensor error** — cap, remove, or impute it.

**Conclusion:** A purely statistical flag is not enough; **domain-driven judgement** is mandatory. *(7 marks: 1.5 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2.5)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Confusing noise with outliers** (–2 marks): Noise ≠ Outlier. Noise is a random measurement error; an outlier is a legitimate extreme value. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the class-conditional mean** (–1 mark): When asked to impute in a *classification* context, using the *global* mean (instead of the class-conditional mean) is the most common error.
> 3. **Skipping steps in equal-frequency binning** (–2 marks): Examiners expect the *number of bins* $k = n/s$ to be explicitly computed; merely writing the bins without justification invites deduction.
> 4. **Mixing up smoothing methods** (–1.5 marks): Writing "smoothing by bin means" but actually performing "smoothing by bin medians" is a frequent mistake under exam pressure.
> 5. **Omitting the labelled flowchart** (–1 mark): KTU answer scripts for full-mark questions almost always require a labelled diagram; its absence is penalised even if the prose is correct.
> 6. **Writing $w = (x_{\max} - x_{\min})/k$ for equal-frequency** (–1 mark): That is the equal-width formula. Equal-frequency uses $s = n/k$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam**

- **Data Cleaning** = the act of detecting, correcting, or removing corrupt / incomplete / noisy data — *Garbage In = Garbage Out*.
- The **6 data quality dimensions** are: Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Believability, Interpretability.
- Data scientists spend **60 – 80 %** of project time on cleaning.
- **Missing values — 5 strategies**: Ignore tuple, Manual fill, Global constant, Attribute mean / class-conditional mean, Most-probable value (regression / EM).
- **Noisy data — 3 smoothing techniques**: Binning (mean / median / boundary), Regression, Clustering.
- **Inconsistencies** are best handled via **metadata-driven lookup tables** and **ETL pipelines**.
- **Binning**:
  - *Equal-width* bin width: $w = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) / k$.
  - *Equal-frequency* bin depth: $s = n / k$ (number of records per bin).
  - *Smoothing by bin means / medians / boundaries* — three classical methods.
- **Normalization** is technically distinct from cleaning but is part of the broader preprocessing step.
  - Min-max: bounded scaling $[new\_min, new\_max]$.
  - Z-score: zero mean, unit variance.
  - Decimal scaling: $v / 10^j$.
- **Outliers vs Noise**:
  - Outliers = legitimate but extreme → detected by Tukey's IQR or z-score; kept if valid.
  - Noise = random error → smoothed using binning / regression.
- **Tukey's 1.5 × IQR rule**:
  - Lower fence: $Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$.
  - Upper fence: $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$.
- **Final cleaned output** is verified by re-running audits and comparing distributions before/after.
- The **ETL process (Extract, Transform, Load)** is the industrial implementation of data cleaning + integration + transformation.
- KTU's data cleaning question pattern:
  - *Part A (3 marks)* → Definition / comparison / short explanation.
  - *Part B (14 marks)* → Process diagram + numerical binning/normalization/IOQR computation.
- **Always include a labelled flowchart** in any 7+ mark descriptive answer.
- **Always show every intermediate calculation** — KTU examiners deduct for skipped steps, not for extra steps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Integration** is the foundational process in data analytics that involves combining heterogeneous data residing in different sources and providing users with a unified, coherent, and consolidated view of the data. Formally, it is defined as the set of techniques, methodologies, and architectural patterns used to harmonize, transform, and consolidate data from disparate operational, transactional, and analytical systems into a single, semantically consistent representation suitable for analytical querying, reporting, and decision-making.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> "Data Integration is the process of combining data from multiple, heterogeneous sources into a unified dataset, applying transformations to resolve schema, semantic, and structural conflicts, and loading the integrated data into a target system (such as a data warehouse) for analytical processing."

**Key Entities in the Definition:**
- **Heterogeneous Sources**: Different databases (RDBMS, NoSQL), flat files (CSV, JSON, XML), APIs, streaming sources, and cloud repositories.
- **Unified View**: A consolidated schema that represents the integrated data logically.
- **Transformations**: Operations such as cleaning, normalization, aggregation, deduplication, and schema mapping.
- **Target System**: A data warehouse, data lake, data mart, or analytical sandbox.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Data Integration is the *backbone* of any analytics pipeline. Without integration, the **5 V's of Big Data** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) cannot be operationalized into actionable intelligence.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are a **chef preparing a multi-cuisine feast**. Your guests want Indian, Chinese, and Italian dishes on the same dining table. To accomplish this, you must:

1. **Source (Extract)**: Go to multiple grocery stores — the spice bazaar for Indian ingredients, the Asian market for Chinese items, and the Italian deli for pasta and cheese.
2. **Transform (Prepare)**: Clean, chop, marinate, and cook each ingredient according to its cuisine's requirements. You cannot just dump raw spices onto a pizza.
3. **Load (Serve)**: Plate all the prepared dishes onto one unified dining table in a coordinated manner.

Data Integration is **exactly this culinary process for data**. Each source (grocery store) has its own format, schema, and quality. The chef (integration engine) cleans and transforms the data (ingredients) and serves a unified view (the dining table) to the data analyst (the guest).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — University Student Record System:**
> A university has separate databases for **Admissions**, **Library**, **Hostel**, **Examinations**, and **Finance**. To generate a single report card showing a student's academic performance, library dues, hostel attendance, and fee status, the data must be **integrated** from all these sources into one consolidated view.

---

### 1.3 Core Objectives of Data Integration (KTU Board-Weightage)

| # | Objective | Description |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Unified Access** | Provide a single query interface across distributed sources. |
| 2 | **Data Consistency** | Resolve conflicts and ensure uniform values across sources. |
| 3 | **Quality Assurance** | Clean, validate, and standardize data during integration. |
| 4 | **Timeliness** | Ensure data is current and synchronized across systems. |
| 5 | **Historical Preservation** | Maintain historical snapshots for trend analysis. |
| 6 | **Governance & Security** | Enforce access controls during data movement. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Data Integration

Data Integration is operationally decomposed into three sequential pillars. Mastering these is essential for KTU 2024 ESE questions.

#### Pillar 1: Extract
The first phase involves reading data from source systems. Sources can be classified into:

- **Structured Sources**: Relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle), spreadsheets.
- **Semi-Structured Sources**: JSON, XML, CSV, log files, Parquet.
- **Unstructured Sources**: Text documents, images, videos, social media feeds.
- **Streaming Sources**: Real-time event streams (Kafka, MQTT, IoT sensors).

**Extraction Modes:**
- **Full Extraction**: Complete data dump from the source. Used during initial loads.
- **Incremental Extraction**: Only data changed since the last extraction. Identified via *timestamps*, *change data capture (CDC) flags*, or *triggers*.

#### Pillar 2: Transform
The transformation phase is the **most critical** and computationally intensive step. It involves:

- **Schema Integration**: Resolving structural differences (e.g., `student_id` vs `roll_no` vs `StudentID`).
- **Data Cleaning**: Removing duplicates, handling missing values, correcting errors.
- **Normalization**: Converting data to standard formats (e.g., dates to `YYYY-MM-DD`).
- **Aggregation**: Computing summaries (sum, average, count).
- **Enrichment**: Augmenting data with external reference data (e.g., geocoding addresses).
- **Deduplication**: Eliminating redundant records.

#### Pillar 3: Load
The final phase writes the transformed data into the target system.

- **Initial Load**: Bulk-loading all historical data.
- **Incremental Load**: Adding only new/changed records.
- **Refresh Load**: Replacing existing data with updated versions.

---

### 2.2 Data Integration Approaches (Architectural Patterns)

#### A. ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)
The **classical** data integration pattern. Data is extracted, transformed *outside* the target system (in a staging area), and then loaded into the target data warehouse.

$$
\text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{Extract}} \text{Staging} \xrightarrow{\text{Transform}} \text{Staging'} \xrightarrow{\text{Load}} \text{Data Warehouse}
$$

**Characteristics:**
- Transformations occur in a **separate processing engine**.
- Best suited for **structured, batch-oriented** workloads.
- Higher data quality assurance *before* loading.
- Used when the target system has **limited compute** (e.g., traditional RDBMS-based warehouses).

#### B. ELT (Extract, Load, Transform)
A **modern** variant where data is first loaded into the target system, and transformations are performed *inside* the target using its compute power.

$$
\text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{Extract}} \text{Target} \xrightarrow{\text{Load}} \text{Data Lakehouse} \xrightarrow{\text{Transform (SQL/Spark)}} \text{Curated}
$$

**Characteristics:**
- Leverages the **massive parallelism** of modern cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift).
- Best suited for **large-volume, semi-structured** data.
- Faster initial load; transformations deferred.
- Used in **modern data stack** architectures.

#### C. Data Replication
Copying data from one database to another in near real-time or on a schedule.

- **Synchronous Replication**: Writes occur in both source and target simultaneously (zero data loss, high latency).
- **Asynchronous Replication**: Source writes are propagated to target with a slight delay (low latency, possible minor data loss).

#### D. Data Federation
A **virtual integration** approach where data remains in source systems, and a middleware layer provides a unified query interface.

$$
\text{Query} \rightarrow \text{Federation Layer} \rightarrow \text{Decompose} \rightarrow \{\text{Source}_1, \text{Source}_2, \ldots, \text{Source}_n\} \rightarrow \text{Unified Result}
$$

- No physical data movement.
- Best for **real-time, cross-system** queries.
- Performance bottleneck if sources are slow or numerous.

#### E. Data Consolidation (Warehouse/Mart)
Physical movement of data from multiple sources into a **single centralized repository** (Data Warehouse).

#### F. Change Data Capture (CDC)
Captures and propagates only the **changes** (inserts, updates, deletes) from source to target in near real-time.

- **Log-Based CDC**: Reads database transaction logs (e.g., MySQL binlog).
- **Trigger-Based CDC**: Uses database triggers to log changes.
- **Timestamp-Based CDC**: Compares `last_modified` timestamps.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 board examinations. Memorize the symbols and meanings.

| Symbol / Term | Formal Definition | Engineering Use |
|---------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| $E$ | Extract operation | Pulling data from sources |
| $T$ | Transform operation | Cleaning, mapping, aggregating |
| $L$ | Load operation | Writing to target system |
| $S_i$ | $i$-th source system | $S = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$ |
| $D_w$ | Data Warehouse (target) | Centralized analytical store |
| $D_l$ | Data Lake | Raw, schema-less storage |
| $\Delta t$ | Integration latency | Time lag between source update and target sync |
| $\text{CDC}$ | Change Data Capture | Incremental change propagation |
| $\text{ETL}$ | Extract-Transform-Load | Classical batch integration |
| $\text{ELT}$ | Extract-Load-Transform | Modern cloud-native integration |
| $R(t)$ | Record state at time $t$ | Used in CDC and replication |
| $C_{total}$ | Total integration cost | Sum of extraction, transform, load costs |
| $V_{target}$ | Target system velocity | Records loaded per second |
| $Q_{score}$ | Data quality score (post-integration) | $Q_{score} = \frac{\text{Valid Records}}{\text{Total Records}} \times 100$ |
| $S_{conf}$ | Schema conflict level | Degree of structural mismatch |
| $I_{comp}$ | Integration completeness | Fraction of sources integrated |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Data Integration is the **critical enabler** in:
- **Banking**: Combining transaction, customer, and credit data for fraud detection.
- **Healthcare**: Merging EHR (Electronic Health Records), lab results, and insurance data.
- **E-Commerce**: Unifying inventory, sales, customer reviews, and logistics data.
- **Smart Cities**: Integrating IoT sensor data from traffic, weather, and pollution monitors.
- **Telecommunications**: Consolidating call detail records (CDR) and network logs for churn analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, questions on Data Integration often test your ability to *compare ETL vs ELT*, *describe CDC*, or *explain the role of a staging area*. Always draw the architecture diagram in your answer for **additional 2 marks** (as per KTU valuation patterns).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Integration Pipeline (Mathematical Formulation)

We model the data integration pipeline as a series of mathematical transformations on a multi-source dataset.

**Step 1: Define the Source Set**
Let there be $n$ heterogeneous data sources:

$$
S = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_n\}
$$

Each source $S_i$ contains a relation (table) $R_i$ with attributes:

$$
R_i = \{a_{i1}, a_{i2}, \ldots, a_{im_i}\}
$$

where $m_i$ is the number of attributes in source $S_i$.

**Step 2: Schema Mapping**
The goal is to find a mapping function $f_{map}$ that unifies the attributes into a global schema $G$:

$$
G = \{g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_k\}
$$

The mapping function is:

$$
f_{map}: \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i \rightarrow G
$$

**Step-by-step conversion logic:**

- For each attribute $a_{ij} \in R_i$, find a corresponding global attribute $g_l \in G$ based on semantic similarity.
- If multiple source attributes map to the same global attribute, apply a **conflict resolution** function $f_{cr}$:

$$
g_l = f_{cr}(a_{1j_1}, a_{2j_2}, \ldots, a_{nj_n})
$$

Common $f_{cr}$ strategies: *most recent value, majority vote, source priority, average*.

**Step 3: Data Cleaning Transformation**
For each record $r \in G$, apply cleaning functions:

$$
r_{clean} = f_{clean}(r) = f_{dedup}(f_{impute}(f_{normalize}(r)))
$$

Where:
- $f_{normalize}$: Converts values to standard formats.
- $f_{impute}$: Handles missing values (mean, median, mode, or domain-specific).
- $f_{dedup}$: Removes duplicate records.

**Step 4: Aggregation and Load**
The final integrated dataset $I$ is computed as:

$$
I = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} f_{agg}(R_i')
$$

where $R_i' = f_{clean}(R_i)$ and $f_{agg}$ is the aggregation function (sum, count, group-by, etc.).

**Step 5: Data Quality Metric Computation**
The post-integration quality score is:

$$
Q_{score} = \frac{\vert \{r \in I : r \text{ is valid}\} \vert}{\vert I \vert} \times 100
$$

A high $Q_{score}$ (typically $\geq 95\%$) indicates successful integration.

**Step 6: Latency Analysis**
The end-to-end integration latency is:

$$
\Delta t_{total} = \Delta t_{extract} + \Delta t_{transform} + \Delta t_{load}
$$

For real-time integration, $\Delta t_{total} \leq 1$ second. For batch, $\Delta t_{total}$ can range from minutes to hours.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Multi-Step Example: University Student Data Integration

**Problem Context:** Integrate student data from three sources:
- **Source 1 (Admissions DB)**: Contains `student_id`, `name`, `admission_date`, `program`.
- **Source 2 (Examinations DB)**: Contains `roll_no`, `student_name`, `marks`, `subject_code`.
- **Source 3 (Library DB)**: Contains `member_id`, `full_name`, `books_borrowed`, `due_date`.

**Step-by-Step Resolution:**

**Step A: Identify Schema Conflicts**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source 1:} \quad & R_1 = \{student\_id, name, admission\_date, program\} \\
\text{Source 2:} \quad & R_2 = \{roll\_no, student\_name, marks, subject\_code\} \\
\text{Source 3:} \quad & R_3 = \{member\_id, full\_name, books\_borrowed, due\_date\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B: Apply Schema Mapping $f_{map}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
student\_id \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Global: } student\_id \\
roll\_no \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Global: } student\_id \\
member\_id \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Global: } student\_id \\
name / student\_name / full\_name \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Global: } full\_name
\end{aligned}
$$

The unified global schema is:

$$
G = \{student\_id, full\_name, admission\_date, program, marks, subject\_code, books\_borrowed, due\_date\}
$$

**Step C: Apply Conflict Resolution**
If a student `S001` in Source 1 has `name = "Rahul K"` and Source 2 has `student_name = "Rahul Kumar"`, we apply $f_{cr}$ using *source priority* (Admissions DB is authoritative for names):

$$
\text{resolved\_full\_name} = f_{cr}(\text{"Rahul K"}, \text{"Rahul Kumar"}) = \text{"Rahul K"}
$$

**Step D: Apply Cleaning**
- Normalize all dates to `YYYY-MM-DD`.
- Impute missing `marks` with `0` (if not yet appeared for exam).
- Deduplicate records with the same `student_id`.

**Step E: Final Integrated Record**

$$
I_{S001} = \{student\_id: \text{S001}, full\_name: \text{"Rahul K"}, \ldots\}
$$

**Step F: Compute Quality Score**

$$
Q_{score} = \frac{\text{valid records after integration}}{\text{total records before integration}} \times 100
$$

If there were 10,000 raw records and 200 were duplicates:

$$
Q_{score} = \frac{9800}{10000} \times 100 = 98\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the schema mapping step explicitly in your answer. It demonstrates analytical thinking and fetches **full marks**.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Question Favorite)

| Parameter | ETL | ELT | Data Federation | CDC |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----------------|-----|
| **Transform Location** | Staging area | Target system | Query time | Target system |
| **Data Movement** | High | Moderate (raw load) | None (virtual) | Incremental |
| **Latency** | High (batch) | Low to Medium | Real-time | Near real-time |
| **Data Volume Support** | Medium to High | Very High | Low to Medium | High |
| **Compute Required (Target)** | Low | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| **Use Case** | Traditional DW | Cloud DWH/Lake | Cross-system queries | Real-time replication |
| **Cost** | High (dedicated ETL servers) | Variable (cloud compute) | Medium | Medium |
| **Complexity** | High | Medium | Low to Medium | High |
| **Data Quality Control** | Strong (pre-load) | Post-load | None | Post-load |
| **Historical Data** | Yes (full snapshots) | Yes (raw + curated) | No (live data) | Yes (change log) |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation: ETL Cost Model

The total cost of an ETL pipeline can be expressed as:

$$
C_{total} = C_E + C_T + C_L + C_{infra} + C_{maint}
$$

Where:
- $C_E$ = Extraction cost (proportional to source data volume $V_S$).
- $C_T$ = Transformation cost (proportional to complexity $\chi$ and rules count $n_r$).
- $C_L$ = Loading cost (proportional to target write throughput).
- $C_{infra}$ = Infrastructure cost (compute, storage, network).
- $C_{maint}$ = Maintenance cost (monitoring, error handling, schema evolution).

**Step-by-step logic:**

- $C_E = \alpha \cdot V_S$ (linear in source volume).
- $C_T = \beta \cdot \chi \cdot n_r$ (linear in transformation complexity and rules).
- $C_L = \gamma \cdot V_T$ (linear in target volume).
- $C_{infra} = \delta \cdot t_{run}$ (linear in runtime duration).
- $C_{maint} = \epsilon \cdot N_{incidents}$ (linear in number of incidents per month).

**The optimization problem** is to minimize $C_{total}$ subject to constraints on $Q_{score} \geq 95\%$ and $\Delta t_{total} \leq \Delta t_{max}$.

$$
\min C_{total} \quad \text{subject to} \quad Q_{score} \geq 95\%, \quad \Delta t_{total} \leq \Delta t_{max}
$$

This is a **constrained optimization** problem commonly solved using linear programming or heuristic methods in production systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master ETL Pipeline Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Source Systems] --> nodeB{Extract Phase}
    nodeB --> nodeC[Staging Area]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Transform Phase}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Cleaned Staging]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Load Phase}
    nodeF --> nodeG[Data Warehouse]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Data Mart]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Analytics Sandbox]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[BI Dashboards]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK[Data Scientists]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[End Users]
    nodeK --> nodeL
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **nodeA**: Multiple heterogeneous sources (databases, files, APIs, streams).
- **nodeB**: The extraction process (full or incremental).
- **nodeC**: A temporary landing zone where raw data is stored *as-is*.
- **nodeD**: Transformation engine applies cleaning, mapping, aggregation.
- **nodeE**: Cleaned and standardized data ready for loading.
- **nodeF**: Load phase writes to one or more targets.
- **nodeG, nodeH, nodeI**: Different target systems for different analytical needs.
- **nodeJ, nodeK, nodeL**: Downstream consumers of integrated data.

---

### 4.2 ETL vs ELT Comparative Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ETL_CLASSIC[ETL Classic Pipeline]
        src1[Source DB] --> ext1[Extract]
        ext1 --> stg1[Staging]
        stg1 --> trn1[Transform]
        trn1 --> ld1[Load]
        ld1 --> wh1[Data Warehouse]
    end
    subgraph ELT_MODERN[ELT Modern Pipeline]
        src2[Source DB] --> ext2[Extract]
        ext2 --> ld2[Load Raw]
        ld2 --> lh[Data Lakehouse]
        lh --> trn2[Transform In-Place]
        trn2 --> cur[Curated Layer]
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- In **ETL_CLASSIC**, transformation happens *before* loading into the warehouse.
- In **ELT_MODERN**, raw data is loaded first, and the powerful lakehouse engine performs transformations *after* loading.

---

### 4.3 Data Integration Architecture: Hub-and-Spoke Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hub((Integration Hub))
    hub --- spk1[Source CRM]
    hub --- spk2[Source ERP]
    hub --- spk3[Source IoT]
    hub --- spk4[Source Social Media]
    hub --- spk5[Source Logs]
    hub --> tgt1[Data Warehouse]
    hub --> tgt2[Data Lake]
    hub --> tgt3[Real-Time API]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Integration Hub** is a centralized middleware that orchestrates all data flows.
- Each **spoke** represents a source or target system.
- The hub performs transformation, routing, and governance.

---

### 4.4 Change Data Capture (CDC) Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Source DB] --> txn[Transaction Occurs]
    txn --> log[Write to Transaction Log]
    log --> cdc[CDC Engine Reads Log]
    cdc --> parse[Parse Change Events]
    parse --> apply[Apply Changes to Target]
    apply --> target[Target DW Updated]
    target --> notify[Notify Downstream Systems]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The CDC engine continuously monitors the source's transaction log.
- Only **changes** (not full data) are captured and propagated.
- This ensures **near real-time** synchronization with minimal network overhead.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Data Integration. List any four challenges faced during data integration.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Data Integration is the process of combining data from multiple heterogeneous sources into a unified, consolidated view by applying extraction, transformation, and loading operations. It ensures that data from different systems is harmonized in structure, semantics, and quality to support analytical processing.

**Four Challenges (1 Mark - 0.25 each):**

1. **Schema Heterogeneity**: Different sources use different attribute names, types, and structures.
2. **Semantic Conflicts**: Same attribute may have different meanings across sources.
3. **Data Quality Issues**: Missing values, duplicates, and inconsistencies.
4. **Real-Time Integration**: Synchronizing data across systems with minimal latency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between ETL and ELT with respect to the order of operations and target system capabilities.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | ETL | ELT |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Order of Operations** | Extract → Transform → Load | Extract → Load → Transform |
| **Transformation Location** | Outside the target (in staging) | Inside the target system |
| **Target System Capability** | Requires low-compute target | Requires high-compute target |
| **Data Volume** | Suited for medium volumes | Suited for very large volumes |
| **Use Case** | Traditional data warehousing | Modern cloud data lakehouses |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the three-tier architecture of a data integration system with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the staging area in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three-tier architecture of a data integration system consists of:

**Tier 1: Source Tier (Bottom Layer)**
- Contains all heterogeneous data sources: RDBMS, NoSQL, files, APIs, streams.
- These systems are typically operational and optimized for transactions (OLTP), not analytics.

**Tier 2: Integration Tier (Middle Layer)**
- This is the **core processing layer** containing the **staging area**, **transformation engine**, and **workflow orchestrator**.
- The **staging area** plays a critical role:
  - **Decouples source and target**: Sources can be extracted without immediately affecting the target.
  - **Provides a recovery point**: If the load fails, the staging data is preserved.
  - **Enables complex transformations**: Multiple passes over data are possible.
  - **Heterogeneity buffer**: Different source formats are normalized here.
  - **Audit and debugging**: All transformation logic can be traced from staging.

**Tier 3: Target Tier (Top Layer)**
- Contains the integrated data in data warehouses, data marts, or data lakes.
- Serves analytical queries, BI dashboards, and machine learning pipelines.

**Diagram (Describe verbally for 7-mark answer):**
```
[Sources] → [Extract] → [Staging Area] → [Transform] → [Load] → [Warehouse]
                                            ↑
                                    [Metadata & Logs]
```

**Conclusion:** The staging area is the *unsung hero* of the integration tier, providing isolation, recovery, and transformation flexibility.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare and contrast Data Federation, Data Replication, and Data Consolidation as data integration approaches. Which is most suitable for real-time cross-system reporting? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Approach | Data Movement | Latency | Use Case | Complexity |
|----------|--------------|---------|----------|------------|
| **Federation** | None (virtual) | Real-time | Cross-system queries | Medium |
| **Replication** | Full or partial | Near real-time | Disaster recovery, DR sites | High |
| **Consolidation** | Full (to warehouse) | Batch | Centralized analytics | Medium |

**Detailed Comparison:**

- **Data Federation**: A virtual layer that queries source systems in real-time. No data is physically moved. The federation engine decomposes a query, sends sub-queries to each source, and merges results. **Best for real-time cross-system reporting** because it accesses live data directly.

- **Data Replication**: Data is physically copied from source to target. Can be synchronous (zero data loss, high latency) or asynchronous (low latency, minor data loss). Best for disaster recovery and load balancing, not real-time cross-system analytics.

- **Data Consolidation**: All data is moved into a central warehouse. High latency due to batch loads. Best for historical analysis and BI reporting, not real-time.

**Justification for Real-Time Cross-System Reporting:**
**Data Federation** is the most suitable because:
1. It provides **live data** without replication delays.
2. It eliminates the need for a separate warehouse.
3. It reduces **storage costs** since data is not duplicated.
4. Modern federation engines support caching and query optimization to mitigate performance issues.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Q. (a)** Describe the Change Data Capture (CDC) mechanism. Explain its three methods with their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Change Data Capture (CDC)** is a data integration technique that identifies and propagates only the changes (inserts, updates, deletes) made in a source database to a target system, ensuring near real-time synchronization.

**Three Methods of CDC:**

**Method 1: Timestamp-Based CDC**
- Each record has a `last_modified` or `updated_at` column.
- The CDC engine queries: `SELECT * FROM table WHERE last_modified > last_sync_timestamp`.
- **Advantage**: Simple to implement.
- **Limitation**: Cannot detect deletes; relies on application logic to maintain timestamps.

**Method 2: Trigger-Based CDC**
- Database triggers are written on source tables for INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.
- Triggers write change records to a separate "shadow" or "changelog" table.
- The CDC engine reads the changelog table.
- **Advantage**: Captures all change types including deletes.
- **Limitation**: Adds overhead to source transactions; can impact OLTP performance.

**Method 3: Log-Based CDC**
- Reads the source database's transaction log (e.g., MySQL binlog, Oracle Redo Log, PostgreSQL WAL).
- The CDC engine (e.g., Debezium, Maxwell) parses log entries to extract changes.
- **Advantage**: Zero impact on source performance; captures all changes with full fidelity.
- **Limitation**: Log format is database-specific; requires deep technical expertise.

**Summary Table:**

| Method | Captures Deletes? | Source Overhead | Implementation Complexity |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Timestamp | No | None | Low |
| Trigger | Yes | Medium | Medium |
| Log-Based | Yes | None | High |

---

**Q. (b)** With a suitable example, explain the schema integration process. How are naming conflicts and structural conflicts resolved? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Schema Integration** is the process of creating a unified global schema from multiple local source schemas. It involves identifying correspondences, resolving conflicts, and merging schemas.

**Example:** A retail company has:
- **Sales DB**: `cust_id`, `cust_name`, `purchase_date`, `amount`
- **Marketing DB**: `customer_number`, `customer_full_name`, `transaction_date`, `total_spent`
- **Support DB**: `client_id`, `client_name`, `interaction_date`, `value`

**Schema Integration Steps:**

**Step 1: Schema Correspondence**
Identify which attributes represent the same real-world entity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cust\_id} \equiv \text{customer\_number} \equiv \text{client\_id} \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Global: customer\_id} \\
\text{cust\_name} \equiv \text{customer\_full\_name} \equiv \text{client\_name} \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Global: customer\_name} \\
\text{purchase\_date} \equiv \text{transaction\_date} \equiv \text{interaction\_date} \quad & \rightarrow \quad \text{Global: event\_date}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Resolve Naming Conflicts**
Naming conflicts occur when the same attribute has different names (`cust_id` vs `customer_number`).
**Resolution**: Create a common naming convention in the global schema and maintain a mapping dictionary.

**Step 3: Resolve Structural Conflicts**
Structural conflicts occur when:
- *Type conflict*: One source has `amount` as `INTEGER` and another as `FLOAT`.
- *Aggregation conflict*: One source stores `total_spent` as a sum; another stores individual transactions.
- *Missing attribute conflict*: One source has `email`; another does not.

**Resolution Strategies:**
- **Type conflict**: Cast to the most general type (e.g., `FLOAT` or `DECIMAL`).
- **Aggregation conflict**: Decompose the aggregated source or aggregate the detailed source.
- **Missing attribute conflict**: Use `NULL` with appropriate metadata, or backfill from related tables.

**Final Global Schema:**

$$
G = \{customer\_id, customer\_name, event\_date, amount, email\}
$$

**Conclusion:** Schema integration is foundational for any data warehouse project and directly impacts query performance and data quality.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ETL with ELT order**: The order of operations is the *first* thing examiners check. Getting it wrong means losing 2-3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Always draw a diagram in ETL questions**: A neat, labeled architecture diagram fetches **2 extra marks** as per KTU valuation patterns.
> 3. **CDC question trap**: Many students forget to mention the **limitation** of each CDC method. Examiners allocate 1-2 marks specifically for limitations. If you only list advantages, you lose marks.
> 4. **Schema integration requires worked examples**: A purely theoretical answer without showing the mapping between schemas is considered incomplete. Always use a 2-3 source example.
> 5. **Cost model derivation**: When asked to derive, show *every algebraic step*. Do not skip the cost function formulation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- ✅ **Definition**: Data Integration = unifying heterogeneous data via Extract → Transform → Load.
- ✅ **ETL Order**: Extract → Transform → Load (transformation in staging).
- ✅ **ELT Order**: Extract → Load → Transform (transformation in target).
- ✅ **Staging Area Role**: Decoupling, recovery, multi-pass transformations, audit.
- ✅ **Three CDC Methods**: Timestamp-based, Trigger-based, Log-based.
- ✅ **Log-Based CDC**: Zero source overhead, highest fidelity, most complex.
- ✅ **Schema Conflicts**: Naming, Type, Aggregation, Missing-attribute.
- ✅ **Conflict Resolution**: Most recent, majority vote, source priority, average.
- ✅ **Data Federation**: Virtual integration, no data movement, real-time.
- ✅ **Data Consolidation**: Physical movement to warehouse, batch-oriented.
- ✅ **Quality Score**: $Q_{score} = \frac{\text{Valid Records}}{\text{Total Records}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Integration Approaches**: ETL, ELT, Federation, Replication, Consolidation, CDC.
- ✅ **Latency Formula**: $\Delta t_{total} = \Delta t_E + \Delta t_T + \Delta t_L$.
- ✅ **Total Cost Formula**: $C_{total} = C_E + C_T + C_L + C_{infra} + C_{maint}$.
- ✅ **Real-Time Integration**: Federation or Log-Based CDC.
- ✅ **Batch Integration**: ETL with full extraction.
- ✅ **Cloud-Native Integration**: ELT with lakehouse architecture (Snowflake, BigQuery).
- ✅ **Always draw a diagram** in 7-mark and 14-mark questions.
- ✅ **Use the global schema notation** $G = \{g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_k\}$ to demonstrate rigor.

> **End of Module 3 — Integration Notes**
