topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving as much of the significant information (variance, structure, or class separability) as possible. It is a foundational preprocessing step in the Data Analytics pipeline.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCDT503 - Data Analytics)**, *Reduction* specifically refers to techniques that reduce the **number of random variables (features)** under consideration, falling into two broad categories:

| Category | Core Idea | Output Type |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Feature Selection** | Choose a subset of original features | Same original features, fewer of them |
| **Feature Extraction / Projection** | Build new features from old ones | New engineered variables (e.g., PCs) |

The classical formal definitions are:

- **Feature Selection**: Given an original feature set $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, find a subset $X' \subset X$ such that an evaluation criterion $J(X')$ is optimized.
- **Feature Extraction (PCA)**: Find an orthogonal projection matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ ($k < n$) such that the projected data $Y = XW$ maximizes retained variance.

## 1.2 The Curse of Dimensionality — Why Reduce?

> [!NOTE]
> As the number of dimensions $d$ grows, the volume of the feature space increases **exponentially**. Data becomes sparse, distances become meaningless, and overfitting becomes severe. This phenomenon is the **Curse of Dimensionality**, formalized by Richard Bellman (1961).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a library with 10,000 books, but your shelf only fits 100.**
>
> - **Feature Selection** is like a librarian picking the **100 most useful books** and discarding the rest — the books themselves stay the same; you just keep fewer of them.
> - **Feature Extraction (PCA)** is like **blending 10 related books into 1 "summary volume"** — the new book is a new creation (a Principal Component), not one of the originals.
>
> Both methods shrink the shelf space (dimensions), but the **mechanism is fundamentally different**. This distinction is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols Used in Reduction

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value/Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| $n$ | Number of samples (rows) | 100 – 1,000,000 |
| $d$ / $p$ | Original dimensionality | 10 – 10,000 |
| $k$ | Reduced dimensionality | $k \ll d$ |
| $\lambda_i$ | Eigenvalue of $i^{th}$ PC | **Variance** captured by PC $i$ |
| $\eta_i$ | Explained variance ratio | $\eta_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_j \lambda_j}$, unitless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| $\mathbf{w}_i$ | Eigenvector (loadings) | Unit vector, $\|\mathbf{w}_i\| = 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Reduction Methods

### A. Feature Selection (FS)
Selects a **subset** of original features based on relevance.

**1. Filter Methods** (model-independent, fast)
- **Pearson Correlation** — removes redundant correlated features
- **Chi-Square Test ($\chi^2$)** — for categorical targets
- **Information Gain / Mutual Information** — measures dependency
- **ANOVA F-test** — for numerical features vs categorical target

**2. Wrapper Methods** (model-dependent, accurate but slow)
- **Forward Selection** — start empty, add features one by one
- **Backward Elimination** — start full, remove features one by one
- **Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)** — recursive pruning by model weights

**3. Embedded Methods** (selection happens during training)
- **L1 Regularization (Lasso)** — drives irrelevant weights to **0**
- **Tree-based Importance** — Random Forest / XGBoost feature importances

### B. Feature Extraction (FE)
Creates **new features** by projection/transform.

- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — unsupervised, maximizes variance
- **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** — supervised, maximizes class separation
- **t-SNE / UMAP** — non-linear, for visualization
- **Factor Analysis (FA)** — assumes latent factors drive observed variables
- **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** — matrix factorization backbone

---

## 2.2 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — The KTU Backbone

### 2.2.1 The Core Idea

PCA finds new orthogonal axes (Principal Components) along which the **variance of the data is maximized**. The first PC captures the most variance, the second captures the next most (orthogonal to the first), and so on.

### 2.2.2 The Mathematical Pipeline

**Step 1 — Center the Data**
Subtract the mean from each feature so that each column has zero mean.

$$
\mathbf{X}_{centered} = \mathbf{X} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}
$$

where $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ is the row vector of column means.

**Step 2 — Compute the Covariance Matrix**
For standardized zero-mean data, the covariance matrix is:

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}
$$

$\mathbf{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, symmetric and positive semi-definite.

**Step 3 — Eigendecomposition**
Solve the characteristic equation:

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{w}_i
$$

where $\lambda_i$ is the $i^{th}$ eigenvalue and $\mathbf{w}_i$ is its eigenvector.

**Step 4 — Sort and Select Top-k Components**
Sort eigenvalues in descending order: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d$.
Pick the top-$k$ eigenvectors and form the projection matrix:

$$
\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{w}_1 \; \mathbf{w}_2 \; \dots \; \mathbf{w}_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}
$$

**Step 5 — Project Data**
Transform data to the new $k$-dimensional subspace:

$$
\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_{centered} \cdot \mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam gold** — memorize these before every PCA question.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Mean-centered matrix | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$ | Each column has $\mu = 0$ |
| 2 | Covariance matrix | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$ | Symmetric, $d \times d$ |
| 3 | Characteristic equation | $\vert \Sigma - \lambda \mathbf{I} \vert = 0$ | Solve for $\lambda_i$ |
| 4 | Eigenvector condition | $\Sigma \mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{w}_i$ | $\|\mathbf{w}_i\| = 1$ |
| 5 | Projection matrix | $\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{w}_1 \dots \mathbf{w}_k]$ | Orthonormal columns |
| 6 | Transformed data | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}_k$ | $n \times k$ output |
| 7 | Variance of $i^{th}$ PC | $\text{Var}(\mathbf{y}_i) = \lambda_i$ | Equal to eigenvalue |
| 8 | Explained variance ratio | $\eta_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^d \lambda_j}$ | $\sum \eta_i = 1$ |
| 9 | Cumulative variance | $V_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^k \lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^d \lambda_j}$ | Choose $k$ s.t. $V_k \geq 0.95$ |
| 10 | Reconstruction error | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=k+1}^d \lambda_i$ | Lost variance when truncating |
| 11 | Standardization (z-score) | $z_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$ | Required when features have different scales |
| 12 | Pearson correlation | $r = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2}}$ | For filter FS, remove if $\vert r \vert > 0.9$ |
| 13 | Information gain | $IG(X, Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$ | Higher IG = more important feature |
| 14 | LDA between-class scatter | $S_B = \sum_c n_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$ | Maximized in LDA |
| 15 | SVD form of PCA | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma}_S \mathbf{V}^T$ | $\mathbf{W}_k = \mathbf{V}_k$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Always **standardize** features before PCA when units differ (e.g., "age" in years vs "salary" in lakhs). Otherwise, the high-variance feature dominates the first PC — a classic KTU pitfall.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Image Processing** | Face recognition (Eigenfaces), compression |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression analysis (10,000+ genes → 50 PCs) |
| **Finance** | Risk factor modeling, portfolio compression |
| **IoT / Sensor Networks** | Edge-device feature compression |
| **NLP** | Word embeddings reduction, topic modeling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: PCA on a 2D Toy Dataset (Hand-Computed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks **numerical PCA problems** on small (2×2 or 3×3) datasets. Practice this derivation until you can reproduce it cold.

### Given Data Matrix (n=5 samples, d=2 features)

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 2 \\ 5 & 3 \\ 6 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### Step 1 — Compute Column Means

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{2+3+4+5+6}{5} = \frac{20}{5} = 4
$$

$$
\bar{x}_2 = \frac{1+2+2+3+4}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4
$$

### Step 2 — Center the Data

$$
\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - [\bar{x}_1, \bar{x}_2] = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1.4 \\ -1 & -0.4 \\ 0 & -0.4 \\ 1 & 0.6 \\ 2 & 1.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Logic:** Centering shifts the origin to the centroid of the data, ensuring the first PC passes through the data mean.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Covariance Matrix

$$
\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\ -1.4 & -0.4 & -0.4 & 0.6 & 1.6 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1.4 \\ -1 & -0.4 \\ 0 & -0.4 \\ 1 & 0.6 \\ 2 & 1.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing element-by-element:

$$
(\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c)_{11} = (-2)^2 + (-1)^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 2^2 = 4+1+0+1+4 = 10
$$

$$
(\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c)_{12} = (-2)(-1.4) + (-1)(-0.4) + (0)(-0.4) + (1)(0.6) + (2)(1.6)
$$

$$
= 2.8 + 0.4 + 0 + 0.6 + 3.2 = 7.0
$$

$$
(\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c)_{22} = (-1.4)^2 + (-0.4)^2 + (-0.4)^2 + (0.6)^2 + (1.6)^2
$$

$$
= 1.96 + 0.16 + 0.16 + 0.36 + 2.56 = 5.20
$$

Dividing by $n-1 = 4$:

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 7.0 \\ 7.0 & 5.20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 1.75 \\ 1.75 & 1.30 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Logic:** $\Sigma_{ij}$ is the covariance between feature $i$ and feature $j$. The diagonal entries are variances, the off-diagonal entries are covariances.

---

### Step 4 — Solve the Characteristic Equation

$$
\det(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0
$$

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 - \lambda & 1.75 \\ 1.75 & 1.30 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

$$
(2.5 - \lambda)(1.30 - \lambda) - (1.75)^2 = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
3.25 - 2.5\lambda - 1.30\lambda + \lambda^2 - 3.0625 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 3.80\lambda + 0.1875 = 0
$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{3.80 \pm \sqrt{(-3.80)^2 - 4(1)(0.1875)}}{2} = \frac{3.80 \pm \sqrt{14.44 - 0.75}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{3.80 \pm \sqrt{13.69}}{2} = \frac{3.80 \pm 3.70}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{3.80 + 3.70}{2} = 3.75, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{3.80 - 3.70}{2} = 0.05
$$

**Logic:** Eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix are always real and non-negative. Here, $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2$ means PC1 dominates — most variance lies along one direction.

---

### Step 5 — Find the First Eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_1$

Solve $\Sigma \mathbf{w}_1 = \lambda_1 \mathbf{w}_1$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 1.75 \\ 1.75 & 1.30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} \\ w_{12} \end{bmatrix} = 3.75 \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} \\ w_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

From the first row:

$$
2.5 w_{11} + 1.75 w_{12} = 3.75 w_{11} \implies 1.75 w_{12} = 1.25 w_{11}
$$

$$
w_{12} = \frac{1.25}{1.75} w_{11} = 0.7143 \, w_{11}
$$

Apply the unit norm constraint $w_{11}^2 + w_{12}^2 = 1$:

$$
w_{11}^2 + (0.7143 w_{11})^2 = 1 \implies w_{11}^2 (1 + 0.5102) = 1
$$

$$
w_{11}^2 = \frac{1}{1.5102} = 0.6622 \implies w_{11} = 0.8138
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8138 \\ 0.5812 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Logic:** This vector points along the direction of maximum variance in the data. The angle it makes with the x-axis is $\arctan(0.5812/0.8138) \approx 35.5°$.

---

### Step 6 — Compute Explained Variance Ratio

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{3.75}{3.75 + 0.05} = \frac{3.75}{3.80} = 0.9868
$$

$$
\eta_2 = 1 - 0.9868 = 0.0132
$$

**Conclusion:** PC1 alone captures **98.68%** of the total variance. We can reduce $d=2 \to k=1$ with negligible information loss.

---

### Step 7 — Project Data to 1-D

$$
\mathbf{W}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8138 \\ 0.5812 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \cdot \mathbf{W}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1.4 \\ -1 & -0.4 \\ 0 & -0.4 \\ 1 & 0.6 \\ 2 & 1.6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.8138 \\ 0.5812 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Sample-wise computation (row 1):

$$
y_1 = (-2)(0.8138) + (-1.4)(0.5812) = -1.6276 - 0.8137 = -2.4413
$$

Sample-wise (row 2):

$$
y_2 = (-1)(0.8138) + (-0.4)(0.5812) = -0.8138 - 0.2325 = -1.0463
$$

Sample-wise (row 3):

$$
y_3 = (0)(0.8138) + (-0.4)(0.5812) = -0.2325
$$

Sample-wise (row 4):

$$
y_4 = (1)(0.8138) + (0.6)(0.5812) = 0.8138 + 0.3487 = 1.1625
$$

Sample-wise (row 5):

$$
y_5 = (2)(0.8138) + (1.6)(0.5812) = 1.6276 + 0.9299 = 2.5575
$$

Final 1-D representation:

$$
\mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.4413 \\ -1.0463 \\ -0.2325 \\ 1.1625 \\ 2.5575 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Reduction Method?

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Rationale |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| Features highly correlated, unsupervised | **PCA** | Maximizes variance on orthogonal axes |
| Classification task, labeled data | **LDA** | Maximizes class separability (Fisher) |
| Visualization (2D/3D plot) | **t-SNE / UMAP** | Non-linear, preserves local neighborhoods |
| High-dimensional gene data, want interpretation | **Factor Analysis** | Assumes latent factors |
| Many irrelevant features, want model accuracy | **Lasso (L1)** | Embedded, sparse solution |
| Small dataset, exhaustive search feasible | **Wrapper (Forward/Backward)** | Optimal subset, no model overhead |
| Streaming / real-time feature filtering | **Filter (Mutual Info)** | Fast, online-capable |

> [!WARNING]
> **PCA is NOT feature selection.** Students often confuse the two. PCA **creates new variables** (PCs); feature selection **keeps original variables**. Mixing them up costs marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Maximization Objective (Why Eigenvectors?)

We want to find $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes variance of projected data $\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}$:

$$
\text{Var}(\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{n-1} (\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w})^T (\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}
$$

Since $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$, the objective becomes:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1
$$

Using a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - 1)
$$

Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$:

$$
2 \Sigma \mathbf{w} - 2\lambda \mathbf{w} = 0 \implies \Sigma \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}
$$

**This is the eigenvalue equation.** Thus, the directions of maximum variance are exactly the eigenvectors, and the variance captured equals the eigenvalue $\lambda$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define the *Curse of Dimensionality*. Why does it necessitate dimensionality reduction?

**Model Answer:**

> **Curse of Dimensionality** refers to the set of phenomena that arise when analyzing data in high-dimensional spaces (typically $d > 10$), where the volume of the space grows exponentially with $d$, causing data to become sparse and distances to lose discriminative power.
>
> It necessitates dimensionality reduction because: (i) sparse data leads to **overfitting** in models, (ii) distance metrics become **unreliable** as nearest and farthest neighbors converge, (iii) computational cost grows with $d$, and (iv) many features may be **redundant or noisy**, degrading model accuracy.

*(Marks: Definition 1, Explanation 2)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Differentiate between **Feature Selection** and **Feature Extraction** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Feature Selection | Feature Extraction |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Operation** | Picks a subset of original features | Creates new features from combinations |
| **Output** | Same original features (fewer) | New synthetic features |
| **Interpretability** | High — original meaning retained | Low — new components are abstract |
| **Example** | Removing low-correlation features using Chi-Square | Generating Principal Components via PCA |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Module-3 questions offer **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both options are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** algorithm in detail with its mathematical formulation. List the steps involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following centered data matrix. Compute the covariance matrix, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and the **first principal component** for the dataset. Project all points onto PC1. **(7 Marks)**

$$
\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 3 \\ 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a): PCA Algorithm

**Definition:**
PCA is an unsupervised linear dimensionality reduction technique that transforms data into a new coordinate system where the greatest variance lies along the first axis (PC1), the second greatest along PC2 (orthogonal to PC1), and so on.

**Step-by-step Algorithm:**

1. **Mean-center** the data: $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$
2. **Compute the covariance matrix**: $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$
3. **Eigendecomposition**: Solve $\Sigma \mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{w}_i$
4. **Sort** eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d$
5. **Form** projection matrix $\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{w}_1, \dots, \mathbf{w}_k]$
6. **Project** data: $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}_k$
7. **Evaluate** via explained variance ratio $\eta_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_j \lambda_j}$

**Mathematical Justification:**
PCA maximizes projected variance $\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ subject to $\|\mathbf{w}\| = 1$. The Lagrangian yields the eigenvalue equation, proving eigenvectors are the optimal directions.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b): Numerical PCA

**Step 1 — Covariance Matrix**

$$
\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1+4+9+16+25 & 2+2+12+12+30 \\ 2+2+12+12+30 & 4+1+16+9+36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 55 & 58 \\ 58 & 66 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 55 & 58 \\ 58 & 66 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13.75 & 14.50 \\ 14.50 & 16.50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic Equation**

$$
(13.75 - \lambda)(16.50 - \lambda) - (14.50)^2 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 30.25\lambda + 226.875 - 210.25 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 30.25\lambda + 16.625 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{30.25 \pm \sqrt{915.0625 - 66.5}}{2} = \frac{30.25 \pm \sqrt{848.5625}}{2} = \frac{30.25 \pm 29.13}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = 29.69, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.56
$$

**Step 3 — First Eigenvector**

From $(13.75 - 29.69)w_{11} + 14.50 w_{12} = 0$:

$$
-15.94 w_{11} + 14.50 w_{12} = 0 \implies w_{12} = 1.0993 \, w_{11}
$$

Normalizing with $w_{11}^2 + (1.0993 w_{11})^2 = 1$:

$$
w_{11}^2 (1 + 1.2085) = 1 \implies w_{11}^2 = 0.4528 \implies w_{11} = 0.6729
$$

$$
\mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6729 \\ 0.7398 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Project to PC1**

$$
\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} (1)(0.6729) + (2)(0.7398) \\ (2)(0.6729) + (1)(0.7398) \\ (3)(0.6729) + (4)(0.7398) \\ (4)(0.6729) + (3)(0.7398) \\ (5)(0.6729) + (6)(0.7398) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1525 \\ 2.0856 \\ 4.9791 \\ 4.9122 \\ 7.8057 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Explained Variance**

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{29.69}{29.69 + 0.56} = \frac{29.69}{30.25} = 0.9815 \quad (98.15\%)
$$

**Conclusion:** PC1 captures **98.15%** of variance. Reduction to $k=1$ is justified.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **three categories of Feature Selection** methods: Filter, Wrapper, and Embedded. Compare them in terms of speed, accuracy, and model dependency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate **Forward Selection** and **Backward Elimination** algorithms. State one limitation of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a): Three FS Categories

**1. Filter Methods:**
- Independent of any ML model
- Use statistical measures: Pearson correlation, Chi-square, Information Gain, ANOVA
- **Pros**: Fast, scalable, works on high-dim data
- **Cons**: Ignores feature interactions

**2. Wrapper Methods:**
- Use a predictive model to score feature subsets
- **Forward Selection**: Start empty, add features that improve model performance
- **Backward Elimination**: Start full, remove least significant features
- **Pros**: Captures feature interactions, often more accurate
- **Cons**: Computationally expensive (retrains model repeatedly)

**3. Embedded Methods:**
- Feature selection happens **during** model training
- Examples: L1 (Lasso) regularization, Decision Tree importance
- **Pros**: Balanced speed and accuracy
- **Cons**: Tied to specific model type

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Filter | Wrapper | Embedded |
|--------|--------|---------|----------|
| Speed | **Fast** | Slow | Medium |
| Accuracy | Moderate | High | High |
| Model Dependency | None | Required | Specific |
| Feature Interaction | Ignored | Captured | Partial |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b): Forward & Backward Selection

**Forward Selection Example:**
Start with empty set $S = \{\}$. Evaluate all 5 features one-by-one with model accuracy. Add the feature giving highest accuracy. Repeat until no improvement.

- Step 1: Try each feature → pick best, say $f_3$, $S = \{f_3\}$, accuracy = 80%
- Step 2: Try $S = \{f_3, f_1\}, \{f_3, f_2\}, \{f_3, f_4\}, \{f_3, f_5\}$ → pick $S = \{f_3, f_1\}$, accuracy = 88%
- Step 3: Continue...
- **Limitation**: Greedy — once added, features are never removed. May miss optimal subset.

**Backward Elimination Example:**
Start with $S = \{f_1, f_2, f_3, f_4, f_5\}$. Remove the feature whose removal least degrades accuracy.

- Step 1: Try removing each → keep best, $S = \{f_1, f_2, f_4, f_5\}$, accuracy = 89%
- Step 2: Try removing each → keep best, $S = \{f_2, f_4, f_5\}$, accuracy = 88%
- Continue until accuracy drops below threshold.
- **Limitation**: Cannot add removed features back. Computationally heavy for large $d$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Reduction questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PCA with feature selection** — PCA creates new features, not selects old ones. Marks deducted if the distinction is not clear. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to mean-center before PCA** — Without centering, eigenvectors are biased. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Not normalizing eigenvectors to unit length** — Eigenvectors must satisfy $\|\mathbf{w}\| = 1$. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up $\lambda_1$ vs $\lambda_2$ ordering** — PC1 is the **largest** eigenvalue, always. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the standardization step** when features have different units (e.g., height in cm vs weight in kg). (-1 mark)
> 6. **Using population formula $1/n$** instead of sample formula $1/(n-1)$. Technically either works for PCA on centered data, but the KTU convention is $n-1$. (-1 mark)
> 7. **Writing equations without explaining** the meaning — KTU awards marks for *interpretation*, not just equations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Reduction**

- **Curse of Dimensionality** = data sparsity + distance unreliability in high-dim spaces; motivates reduction.
- **Reduction has TWO branches**: **Feature Selection** (keep originals) vs **Feature Extraction** (build new).
- **Three FS categories**: Filter (fast, model-free), Wrapper (slow, accurate), Embedded (in-training).
- **PCA** is the **most-tested** reduction method at KTU — must know every step.
- **PCA pipeline (memorize order)**: Center → Covariance → Eigendecomposition → Sort → Select-k → Project.
- **Covariance formula**: $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$ — symmetric $d \times d$.
- **Eigenvalue equation**: $\Sigma \mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{w}_i$ — solve for both $\lambda$ and $\mathbf{w}$.
- **PC1 has the LARGEST eigenvalue** — sorted in descending order.
- **Eigenvectors must be normalized** to unit length: $\|\mathbf{w}_i\| = 1$.
- **Projected variance of PC $i$** equals $\lambda_i$ — never forget this.
- **Explained variance ratio** $\eta_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum \lambda_j}$ — used to choose $k$.
- **Standard rule**: choose $k$ such that cumulative variance $\geq 0.95$ (95%).
- **Always standardize features** before PCA when units/scales differ.
- **PCA is unsupervised**; **LDA is supervised** (uses class labels).
- **L1 (Lasso) regularization** is an embedded feature selection method that drives irrelevant weights to zero.
- **Pearson correlation $\vert r \vert > 0.9$** is a common filter threshold for removing redundant features.
- **Reconstruction error** = sum of discarded eigenvalues = $\sum_{i=k+1}^d \lambda_i$.
- **SVD form**: $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{U} \Sigma_S \mathbf{V}^T$ — columns of $\mathbf{V}$ are the principal components.
- **t-SNE / UMAP** are for **visualization only** — do not use them as input to downstream models.
- **KTU favorite trick question**: "Is PCA feature selection?" — Answer: **NO, it is feature extraction.**
- **KTU favorite numerical**: 2×2 covariance matrix with small integers — practice the quadratic formula derivation.
- **Limitations of PCA**: Linear assumption, sensitive to outliers, assumes variance = importance (not always true), PCs are not interpretable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Transformation**, in the context of KTU 2024 Data Analytics, is defined as the disciplined process of converting raw, heterogeneous data into a unified, structured, and analysis-ready format using mathematical, statistical, or logical operations. It is a critical phase of the **Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD)** pipeline that bridges the gap between *Data Preprocessing* and *Data Mining / Modeling*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Transformation is positioned in Module 3 as a precursor to mining algorithms. It ensures that downstream algorithms (k-NN, Neural Networks, Clustering, PCA) operate on consistent, scaled, and noise-free data.

Formally, a transformation function $T: V \rightarrow V'$ maps a value $v \in V$ from the original data domain to a transformed value $v' \in V'$, such that:

$$
v' = T(v)
$$

where $T$ is chosen to satisfy properties required by the target algorithm (e.g., scale-invariance, zero-mean, unit-variance, monotonicity, or discretized-categorical).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Recipe Kitchen:**
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a dish. Your ingredients (raw data) come in wildly different forms — whole spices, pre-ground powders, dried herbs, fresh leaves. Before you can cook, you must **standardize** them: toast and grind the whole spices, wash and chop the herbs, measure the powders. This preparation stage is exactly what *Transformation* does to data. The stove (mining algorithm) cannot work efficiently on unprocessed ingredients.

**Why do we transform data?**
- **Scale Harmonization:** Salary in *Rupees* (lakhs) vs. Age in *years* — algorithms biased toward larger magnitudes.
- **Noise Reduction:** Smoothing erratic sensor readings.
- **Compression:** Converting continuous ranges into discrete bins for decision trees.
- **Interpretability:** Mapping decimal numbers (0.7834) into user-friendly scales (78.34%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used in Transformation:**
> - **Standard Score (Z-score):** Dimensionless; expected range $\approx [-3, +3]$ for normal data.
> - **Min-Max Range:** Typically $[0, 1]$ or $[-1, +1]$.
> - **Decimal Scaling Power:** $j \in \mathbb{Z}$ (smallest integer such that transformed magnitude $< 1$).

---

## 1.3 Categories of Transformation (KTU Classification)

| S.No | Transformation Family | Purpose | KTU Cognitive Level |
|:----:|:----------------------|:--------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **Smoothing** | Remove noise | Understand / Apply |
| 2 | **Normalization** | Scale harmonization | Apply / Analyze |
| 3 | **Discretization** | Continuous $\rightarrow$ Categorical | Apply |
| 4 | **Concept Hierarchy Generation** | Granularity abstraction | Analyze |
| 5 | **Attribute / Feature Construction** | Derive new informative attributes | Create |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Smoothing (Noise Removal)
Smoothing is applied **first** because noise disrupts every downstream operation. Three principal methods are used:

1. **Binning:** Sort values, distribute into equal-frequency or equal-width buckets, and replace each value by the **bin mean**, **bin median**, or **bin boundary**.
2. **Regression:** Fit a linear or polynomial regression function $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ and use the predicted value as the smoothed surrogate.
3. **Clustering:** Group similar values; outliers form singleton clusters and can be detected/removed.

### 2.1.2 Normalization (Scale Harmonization)
Performed **after** cleaning and smoothing. Required by distance-based algorithms such as **k-NN, K-Means, SVM, and PCA** because Euclidean distance is scale-dependent.

### 2.1.3 Discretization (Continuous $\rightarrow$ Categorical)
Discretization converts continuous attributes into categorical ones. This is essential for **Naïve Bayes, Decision Trees (C4.5, CART), and association rule mining**.

### 2.1.4 Concept Hierarchy Generation
A top-down or bottom-up replacement of low-level values with higher-level concepts. Example: *city* $\rightarrow$ *state* $\rightarrow$ *country*.

### 2.1.5 Attribute Construction
New attributes are engineered from existing ones to improve predictive power.
Example: $\text{Area} = \text{Length} \times \text{Width}$, $\text{BMI} = \frac{\text{Weight}}{\text{Height}^2}$.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-favorite** and frequently tested in KTU ESE questions worth 7–14 marks.

| # | Method | Formula | Range / Boundary | Units |
|:-:|:-------|:--------|:-----------------|:------|
| 1 | **Min-Max Normalization** | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new_{\max} - new_{\min}) + new_{\min}$ | $[new_{\min}, \, new_{\max}]$; typically $[0,1]$ | Same as $v$ |
| 2 | **Z-Score (Standard Score)** | $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu_A}{\sigma_A}$ | Theoretical $(-\infty, +\infty)$; practical $[-3, +3]$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Decimal Scaling** | $v' = \dfrac{v}{10^{j}}$, where $j$ = smallest integer s.t. $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$ | $\vert v' \vert < 1$ | Scaled by $10^{-j}$ |
| 4 | **Equal-Width Binning Width** | $W = \dfrac{\max - \min}{k}$, where $k$ = number of bins | Bin edges: $\min + i \cdot W$ | Same as $v$ |
| 5 | **Equal-Frequency Binning Count** | $n_{\text{bin}} = \lceil N / k \rceil$, where $N$ = total values | Each bin $\approx N/k$ values | Count (integer) |
| 6 | **Mean Replacement (Smoothing)** | $\mu_{\text{bin}} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} v_i$ | Within bin | Same as $v$ |
| 7 | **Linear Regression Smoothing** | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Continuous | Same as $y$ |
| 8 | **Concept Hierarchy Lift** | Level $L_{i+1} = \text{Generalize}(L_i)$ | Abstract $\rightarrow$ Specific or vice versa | Categorical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Normalization is mandatory in **signal processing** (audio amplitude scaling), **image processing** (pixel intensity $[0,255] \rightarrow [0,1]$ for CNNs), **IoT sensor fusion** (multi-sensor scale matching), and **financial analytics** (volatility-normalized returns).

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Method

- **Min-Max:** Preserves the original distribution shape; sensitive to outliers because $\min$ and $\max$ are used.
- **Z-Score:** Robust to outliers; produces a distribution with mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$ — essential for **Gaussian-based** models.
- **Decimal Scaling:** Lightweight; useful when only order-of-magnitude variation matters.
- **Equal-Width Binning:** Simple; fails on skewed data.
- **Equal-Frequency Binning:** Better for skewed data; produces balanced bins.
- **Clustering-Based Discretization:** Most accurate but computationally expensive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Min-Max Normalization

**Problem (KTU-style):**
Given the dataset of monthly incomes (in ₹ thousands): $\{12, 18, 25, 40, 60, 75, 100\}$. Transform these values into the range $[0, 1]$ using **Min-Max Normalization**.

### Step 1 — Identify Minima and Maxima
$$
\min_A = 12, \quad \max_A = 100
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Min-Max Formula
Using the simplified version where $[new_{\min}, new_{\max}] = [0, 1]$:

$$
v' = \frac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Each Transformed Value

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_{12} &= \frac{12 - 12}{100 - 12} = \frac{0}{88} = 0.0000 \\[4pt]
v'_{18} &= \frac{18 - 12}{100 - 12} = \frac{6}{88} = 0.0682 \\[4pt]
v'_{25} &= \frac{25 - 12}{100 - 12} = \frac{13}{88} = 0.1477 \\[4pt]
v'_{40} &= \frac{40 - 12}{100 - 12} = \frac{28}{88} = 0.3182 \\[4pt]
v'_{60} &= \frac{60 - 12}{100 - 12} = \frac{48}{88} = 0.5455 \\[4pt]
v'_{75} &= \frac{75 - 12}{100 - 12} = \frac{63}{88} = 0.7159 \\[4pt]
v'_{100} &= \frac{100 - 12}{100 - 12} = \frac{88}{88} = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verification
The minimum transformed value must be $0$ and the maximum must be $1$. Both conditions are satisfied. All transformed values lie in $[0, 1]$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Z-Score Normalization

**Problem:** For the same dataset, compute the Z-score normalized values.

### Step 1 — Compute Mean
$$
\mu_A = \frac{12 + 18 + 25 + 40 + 60 + 75 + 100}{7} = \frac{330}{7} = 47.1429
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Standard Deviation (Population Formula)
$$
\sigma_A = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (v_i - \mu_A)^2}
$$

Computing each squared deviation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(12 - 47.1429)^2 &= (-35.1429)^2 = 1235.020 \\[2pt]
(18 - 47.1429)^2 &= (-29.1429)^2 = 849.306 \\[2pt]
(25 - 47.1429)^2 &= (-22.1429)^2 = 490.306 \\[2pt]
(40 - 47.1429)^2 &= (-7.1429)^2 = 51.020 \\[2pt]
(60 - 47.1429)^2 &= (12.8571)^2 = 165.306 \\[2pt]
(75 - 47.1429)^2 &= (27.8571)^2 = 776.020 \\[2pt]
(100 - 47.1429)^2 &= (52.8571)^2 = 2794.020 \\[2pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum and Divide
$$
\sum (v_i - \mu_A)^2 = 1235.020 + 849.306 + 490.306 + 51.020 + 165.306 + 776.020 + 2794.020 = 6361.000
$$

$$
\sigma_A = \sqrt{\frac{6361.000}{7}} = \sqrt{908.714} \approx 30.145
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Z-Score Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_{12} &= \frac{12 - 47.143}{30.145} = \frac{-35.143}{30.145} \approx -1.166 \\[4pt]
v'_{18} &= \frac{18 - 47.143}{30.145} = \frac{-29.143}{30.145} \approx -0.967 \\[4pt]
v'_{25} &= \frac{25 - 47.143}{30.145} = \frac{-22.143}{30.145} \approx -0.735 \\[4pt]
v'_{40} &= \frac{40 - 47.143}{30.145} = \frac{-7.143}{30.145} \approx -0.237 \\[4pt]
v'_{60} &= \frac{60 - 47.143}{30.145} = \frac{12.857}{30.145} \approx +0.427 \\[4pt]
v'_{75} &= \frac{75 - 47.143}{30.145} = \frac{27.857}{30.145} \approx +0.924 \\[4pt]
v'_{100} &= \frac{100 - 47.143}{30.145} = \frac{52.857}{30.145} \approx +1.754 \\[4pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Equal-Width Discretization

**Problem:** Discretize the dataset into **3 equal-width bins**.

### Step 1 — Compute Bin Width
$$
W = \frac{\max - \min}{k} = \frac{100 - 12}{3} = \frac{88}{3} \approx 29.333
$$

### Step 2 — Define Bin Edges
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bin 1: } & [12, \, 12 + 29.333) = [12, 41.333) \\
\text{Bin 2: } & [41.333, \, 12 + 2 \cdot 29.333) = [41.333, 70.667) \\
\text{Bin 3: } & [70.667, 100]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Assign Each Value
| Original Value | Bin Assigned | Label |
|:--------------:|:------------:|:-----:|
| 12 | Bin 1 | Low |
| 18 | Bin 1 | Low |
| 25 | Bin 1 | Low |
| 40 | Bin 1 | Low |
| 60 | Bin 2 | Medium |
| 75 | Bin 3 | High |
| 100 | Bin 3 | High |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Min-Max | Z-Score | Decimal Scaling |
|:---------|:-------:|:-------:|:---------------:|
| Output range | $[0,1]$ (configurable) | Unbounded (practical $[-3,3]$) | $\vert v' \vert < 1$ |
| Outlier sensitivity | **High** | Low | Moderate |
| Distribution shape preserved | Yes | Centered & scaled | Scaled only |
| Cost of computation | Low | Moderate (mean, std) | Very Low |
| KTU Recommendation | Distance-based algos | Statistical/Gaussian models | Quick normalization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Transformation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the end-to-end **Data Transformation Pipeline** as mandated in the KTU Module 3 syllabus. It maps the interactions between smoothing, normalization, discretization, and feature construction.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode[Raw Untransformed Data] --> smoothNode{Stage 1: Smoothing}
    smoothNode --> binSm[Binning Method]
    smoothNode --> regSm[Regression Smoothing]
    smoothNode --> clustSm[Cluster Based Smoothing]

    binSm --> normNode
    regSm --> normNode
    clustSm --> normNode

    normNode{Stage 2: Normalization}
    normNode --> minMaxN[Min Max Normalization]
    normNode --> zScoreN[Z Score Normalization]
    normNode --> decN[Decimal Scaling]

    minMaxN --> discNode
    zScoreN --> discNode
    decN --> discNode

    discNode{Stage 3: Discretization}
    discNode --> eqWidth[Equal Width Binning]
    discNode --> eqFreq[Equal Frequency Binning]
    discNode --> clustD[Clustering Based Binning]

    eqWidth --> hierNode
    eqFreq --> hierNode
    clustD --> hierNode

    hierNode{Stage 4: Concept Hierarchy}
    hierNode --> hierUp[Bottom Up Generalization]
    hierNode --> hierDown[Top Down Specialization]

    hierUp --> featNode
    hierDown --> featNode

    featNode[Stage 5: Feature Construction]
    featNode --> finalNode[Analysis Ready Transformed Data]

    finalNode --> mineNode[Sent to Mining Algorithm]
```

---

## 4.2 Transformation Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1[Start: Choose Transformation] --> q2{Is data noisy?}
    q2 -->|Yes| sm[Smoothing Required]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Algorithm scale sensitive?}
    q3 -->|Yes| nm[Normalization Required]
    q3 -->|No| q4{Need categorical output?}
    q4 -->|Yes| dm[Discretization Required]
    q4 -->|No| q5{Need abstracted levels?}
    q5 -->|Yes| ch[Concept Hierarchy Required]
    q5 -->|No| fc[Feature Construction Only]
    sm --> endN[Transformed Output]
    nm --> endN
    dm --> endN
    ch --> endN
    fc --> endN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Hint:** Each block represents a functional stage. In a real KTU examination, students are expected to draw (or describe) such a pipeline and justify the chosen transformation methods.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define data transformation. List any four techniques used for data transformation.**

**Model Answer:**
Data transformation is the process of converting raw data into a suitable format for data mining by applying mathematical or logical operations. The four primary techniques are:

1. **Smoothing** — removes noise using binning, regression, or clustering.
2. **Normalization** — scales values to a common range (e.g., Min-Max, Z-score).
3. **Discretization** — converts continuous data into categorical bins.
4. **Concept Hierarchy Generation** — generalizes low-level data to high-level concepts.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing techniques with brief purpose)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain min-max normalization with its formula. Why is it needed?**

**Model Answer:**
Min-max normalization linearly rescales data to a specified range, typically $[0, 1]$. The formula is:

$$
v' = \frac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new_{\max} - new_{\min}) + new_{\min}
$$

**Need:** Distance-based algorithms (k-NN, K-Means, PCA) are sensitive to the magnitude of attributes. Without normalization, attributes with larger numerical ranges dominate the distance computation, leading to biased results.

*(1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark need)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain different normalization techniques used in data transformation with suitable formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Data normalization rescales attribute values so they lie within a comparable range. The three principal methods are:

**1. Min-Max Normalization:**
$$
v' = \frac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A} \cdot (new_{\max} - new_{\min}) + new_{\min}
$$
- Range: $[new_{\min}, new_{\max}]$, commonly $[0,1]$.
- **Pros:** Preserves relationships. **Cons:** Sensitive to outliers.

**2. Z-Score Normalization (Standard Score):**
$$
v' = \frac{v - \mu_A}{\sigma_A}
$$
- Resulting distribution has mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$.
- **Pros:** Robust to outliers. **Cons:** Does not bound the range.

**3. Decimal Scaling:**
$$
v' = \frac{v}{10^j}, \quad \text{where } j \text{ is smallest integer such that } \max(\vert v' \vert) < 1
$$
- **Pros:** Simple. **Cons:** Crude scaling.

Normalization is essential for distance-based, gradient-based, and principal-component algorithms.

---

**(b)** Consider the data values: $200, 300, 400, 600, 1000$. Apply **min-max normalization** to transform these values to the range $[0, 1]$. Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify min and max.
$$
\min_A = 200, \quad \max_A = 1000
$$

**Step 2:** Apply formula $v' = \dfrac{v - 200}{1000 - 200} = \dfrac{v - 200}{800}$.

**Step 3:** Calculate each value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_{200} &= \frac{200 - 200}{800} = \frac{0}{800} = 0.000 \\[4pt]
v'_{300} &= \frac{300 - 200}{800} = \frac{100}{800} = 0.125 \\[4pt]
v'_{400} &= \frac{400 - 200}{800} = \frac{200}{800} = 0.250 \\[4pt]
v'_{600} &= \frac{600 - 200}{800} = \frac{400}{800} = 0.500 \\[4pt]
v'_{1000} &= \frac{1000 - 200}{800} = \frac{800}{800} = 1.000 \\[4pt]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transformed Set:** $\{0.000, \, 0.125, \, 0.250, \, 0.500, \, 1.000\}$.

Verification: minimum $= 0$, maximum $= 1$. All values lie in $[0, 1]$. ✔️

---

### **Question B** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain **discretization** and **concept hierarchy generation** as data transformation techniques. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Discretization** is the process of converting continuous-valued attributes into discrete categorical labels by partitioning the value range into intervals (bins). The three major methods are:

1. **Equal-Width Binning:** Divides the range $[min, max]$ into $k$ intervals of equal width $W = \dfrac{max - min}{k}$. Simple but sensitive to outliers.
2. **Equal-Frequency Binning:** Each bin contains approximately $N/k$ data points. Better for skewed distributions.
3. **Clustering-Based Binning:** Uses a clustering algorithm (e.g., K-Means) to group values; cluster centers define bin boundaries.

Discretization is required for **Naïve Bayes**, **Decision Tree** algorithms, and is also useful for **data compression**.

**Concept Hierarchy Generation** is a transformation that replaces low-level data values with higher-level conceptual categories, reducing detail and improving interpretability.

**Examples:**
- Numeric: $\{5, 12, 18, 25, 40, 65, 80\}$ $\rightarrow$ $\{$ *child*, *teen*, *young-adult*, *adult*, *senior* $\}$.
- Spatial: *Street* $\rightarrow$ *City* $\rightarrow$ *State* $\rightarrow$ *Country*.
- Temporal: *Day* $\rightarrow$ *Month* $\rightarrow$ *Quarter* $\rightarrow$ *Year*.

Hierarchies may be generated **manually** (domain expert) or **automatically** (based on data distribution analysis).

---

**(b)** The ages of customers in a dataset are: $5, 15, 22, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50, 55, 60$. Apply **(i) Equal-Width Binning** and **(ii) Equal-Frequency Binning**, each with **3 bins**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Sorted data = $\{5, 15, 22, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50, 55, 60\}$, $N = 10$, $k = 3$.

---

**(i) Equal-Width Binning:**

$$
W = \frac{\max - \min}{k} = \frac{60 - 5}{3} = \frac{55}{3} \approx 18.333
$$

Bin edges:
$$
\text{Bin 1: } [5, 23.333), \quad \text{Bin 2: } [23.333, 41.667), \quad \text{Bin 3: } [41.667, 60]
$$

| Value | Bin |
|:-----:|:---:|
| 5  | Bin 1 |
| 15 | Bin 1 |
| 22 | Bin 1 |
| 25 | Bin 2 |
| 30 | Bin 2 |
| 35 | Bin 2 |
| 40 | Bin 2 |
| 50 | Bin 3 |
| 55 | Bin 3 |
| 60 | Bin 3 |

Bin counts: $\{3, 4, 3\}$ — slightly imbalanced.

---

**(ii) Equal-Frequency Binning:**

$$
n_{\text{per\_bin}} = \lceil N / k \rceil = \lceil 10 / 3 \rceil = 4
$$

So each bin ideally has ~3 or 4 values.

- Bin 1 (first 3 values): $\{5, 15, 22\}$
- Bin 2 (next 3 values): $\{25, 30, 35\}$
- Bin 3 (last 4 values): $\{40, 50, 55, 60\}$

Bin counts: $\{3, 3, 4\}$ — balanced distribution.

**Comparison:** Equal-frequency produces more balanced bins, while equal-width is simpler but yields uneven counts when data is skewed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Formula Mis-quotation:** Students often forget the $(new_{\max} - new_{\min})$ scaling factor in Min-Max normalization, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Normalization with Standardization:** They are NOT synonyms. Normalization $\rightarrow$ bounded range. Standardization $\rightarrow$ zero mean, unit variance.
> 3. **Data Leakage:** Computing $\min$, $\max$, or $\mu$ from the *full dataset before* train-test split. Always fit transformation parameters on **training data only**.
> 4. **Wrong Bin Width:** Forgetting to subtract $\min$ from $\max$ before dividing — losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring Outliers:** Min-Max is highly sensitive. Z-score should be preferred when outliers exist.
> 6. **Skipping Verification Step:** Examiners reward a one-line "verification" that the transformed range matches the target range.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **Data Transformation** is the *bridge* between preprocessing and mining in the KDD pipeline.
- ✅ **Five families:** Smoothing, Normalization, Discretization, Concept Hierarchy, Feature Construction.
- ✅ **Min-Max Formula:** $v' = \dfrac{v - \min}{\max - \min} \cdot (new_{\max} - new_{\min}) + new_{\min}$.
- ✅ **Z-Score Formula:** $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$. Output: mean $= 0$, std $= 1$.
- ✅ **Decimal Scaling:** $v' = v / 10^j$, smallest $j$ such that $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$.
- ✅ **Equal-Width Bin Width:** $W = \dfrac{\max - \min}{k}$.
- ✅ **Equal-Frequency Bin Count:** $\lceil N / k \rceil$ per bin.
- ✅ **Smoothing methods:** Binning, Regression, Clustering.
- ✅ **Normalization is mandatory** before distance-based algorithms.
- ✅ **Discretization is mandatory** before Naïve Bayes and Decision Trees.
- ✅ **Concept hierarchy** moves data from *low-level $\rightarrow$ high-level* abstraction.
- ✅ **Feature Construction** derives new attributes (e.g., BMI from weight, height).
- ✅ Always **fit on training data, apply on test data** to avoid leakage.
- ✅ KTU Module 3 expects both **conceptual explanations (Part A)** and **numerical applications (Part B)** — prepare both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discretization. Mining Frequent Patterns - Associations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discretization. Mining Frequent Patterns - Associations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Discretization — Formal Definition

**Discretization** is the data preprocessing technique of transforming **continuous (numeric) attributes** into a finite set of **discrete (categorical) intervals or labels**, while preserving as much of the original information gain as possible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> *Discretization is the process of converting continuous attribute values into a small number of discrete intervals, in order to make the dataset suitable for algorithms (such as association rule mining, decision trees, and rule-based classifiers) that natively operate on categorical data.*

Mathematically, given a continuous attribute $A$ with domain $[A_{min}, A_{max}]$, discretization produces a function:

$$f : [A_{min}, A_{max}] \rightarrow \{c_1, c_2, c_3, \dots, c_k\}$$

where $k$ is a user-specified number of discrete categories (intervals) and $c_i$ are the symbolic class labels.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Post Office Box System"

Imagine a city where every house number (say, 1 to 10,000) is being sorted into a **post office** with only 10 large pigeonholes (bins). The postmaster does not care about the *exact* house number — only the **range** in which it falls (1–1000, 1001–2000, …). This is exactly what discretization does:

- **Continuous values** (house numbers) → too granular for some algorithms.
- **Discrete bins** (pigeonholes) → a coarser, algorithm-friendly representation.
- The "binning policy" is the **discretization method** (equal-width, equal-frequency, entropy-based, etc.).

## 1.2 Mining Frequent Patterns & Associations — Formal Definition

**Frequent Pattern Mining** is the task of discovering patterns (item combinations, subsequences, or substructures) that occur **frequently together** in a dataset. The most common instantiation is **Association Rule Mining**, which finds implication rules of the form:

$$X \Rightarrow Y \quad \text{where} \quad X \cap Y = \emptyset$$

**Association Rule Mining** is the unsupervised learning technique that uncovers interesting **co-occurrence relationships** among items in large transactional databases — originally conceived for **Market Basket Analysis** (e.g., "customers who buy bread and butter also tend to buy jam").

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequent Itemset** — An itemset whose support count is greater than or equal to a user-defined minimum support threshold (**minsup**).
>
> **Strong Association Rule** — A rule $A \Rightarrow B$ that satisfies both **minimum support** and **minimum confidence** thresholds.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Supermarket Shopping Cart"

Imagine a supermarket's transaction log of 1 million customer bills:

- Every bill is a **transaction** (a basket of items).
- "Bread ⇒ Butter, Jam" is an association rule.
- If **2%** of all bills contain *Bread*, and **1.5%** contain *Bread + Butter + Jam* together — then "Bread ⇒ Butter + Jam" is a **frequent rule** if the thresholds are met.
- The store uses this rule to **redesign shelf placement**, **bundle offers**, and **plan cross-marketing campaigns**.

The two critical quality metrics here are:

- **Support** — *How often does this pattern occur?* (Popularity)
- **Confidence** — *How strong is the rule?* (Reliability)

## 1.3 Why Are These Topics Grouped Together in Module 3?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curriculum Design Rationale:**
> Association rule mining algorithms such as **Apriori** and **FP-Growth** work **exclusively on categorical (discrete) data**. Therefore, the KTU 2024 scheme places **Discretization immediately before** Frequent Pattern Mining — it is a *prerequisite transformation step*. Any continuous attribute (e.g., *age = 27.5*, *salary = ₹5,42,300*) must first be discretized into bins (*age: "21–30"*, *salary: "5L–6L"*) before mining can begin.

---

## 1.4 Discretization — Classification of Methods

| Family | Method | Type | Bins Selected By |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unsupervised Binning** | Equal-Width Binning | Univariate, Unsupervised | Distance (range) |
| | Equal-Frequency Binning | Univariate, Unsupervised | Count per bin |
| | Clustering (K-Means) | Univariate, Unsupervised | Cluster centroids |
| **Supervised Discretization** | Information Entropy (ID3) | Univariate, Supervised | Information gain |
| | ChiMerge (χ²) | Univariate, Supervised | Statistical test |
| | CAIM (Class-Attribute Interdep.) | Univariate, Supervised | Class purity |

## 1.5 Frequent Pattern Mining — Taxonomy

| Type | Example Pattern | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequent Itemset** | {Bread, Butter, Jam} | Market basket |
| **Frequent Subsequence** | Buying pattern over time | Web log analysis |
| **Frequent Substructure** | Graph/tree patterns | Bioinformatics, XML mining |
| **Association Rule** | Bread ⇒ Butter | Cross-selling |

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Discretization — Methods in Depth

### 2.1.1 Binning (Unsupervised)

**Equal-Width Binning (EWB)**
- Divides the range $[A_{min}, A_{max}]$ into $k$ intervals of **equal width**.
- **Core logic**: arithmetic, fast, but highly sensitive to outliers.

**Equal-Frequency Binning (EFB)** *(also called Equal-Height or Quantile Binning)*
- Divides the data into $k$ bins each containing **approximately $n/k$ tuples**.
- **Core logic**: rank-based; robust to outliers but may merge similar values into different bins.

### 2.1.2 Clustering-Based Discretization
- Apply a clustering algorithm (typically **K-Means**) on the continuous attribute.
- Each cluster becomes a bin.
- The cluster boundaries (centroids) become interval cut-points.

### 2.1.3 Information-Theoretic Discretization (ID3 / C4.5)
- Uses **entropy** and **information gain** to decide where to split.
- Greedily selects the split point that **maximizes information gain**.
- Recursive, supervised — produces intervals that best separate classes.

### 2.1.4 ChiMerge (χ²-Based Discretization)
- Starts with each distinct value in its own bin.
- Iteratively **merges adjacent bins** with the **lowest χ² statistic** (i.e., bins that are statistically indistinguishable w.r.t. class).
- Stops when χ² exceeds a threshold (or k bins are reached).

## 2.2 Why & How of Each Discretization Method

| Method | **Why** it works | **How** (operational steps) |
|---|---|---|
| **EWB** | Simple, O(n log n) sort + linear cut | (1) Compute range; (2) Divide by k; (3) Assign each value to its bin. |
| **EFB** | Each bin has equal statistical weight | (1) Sort values; (2) Assign every n/k-th value as a cut-point. |
| **K-Means** | Bins are data-driven, follow natural density | (1) Initialize k centroids; (2) Assign points; (3) Update centroids; (4) Repeat until convergence. |
| **Entropy** | Maximizes class purity at each split | (1) For every candidate split; (2) Compute E(parent) and weighted E(children); (3) Pick split with max IG. |
| **ChiMerge** | Statistically merges redundant bins | (1) Initialize per-value bins; (2) Compute χ²; (3) Merge lowest pair; (4) Stop on threshold. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Discretization

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \dfrac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{k}$ | Width of each equal-width bin | Same as attribute |
| 2 | $n_{bin} = \dfrac{N}{k}$ | Tuples per equal-frequency bin | Count (tuples) |
| 3 | $E(S) = -\sum\limits_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon Entropy of set S | **bits** |
| 4 | $E(S,A) = \sum\limits_{v \in A} \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} E(S_v)$ | Weighted entropy after split on A | **bits** |
| 5 | $Gain(S,A) = E(S) - E(S,A)$ | Information gain from split A | **bits** |
| 6 | $GainRatio(S,A) = \dfrac{Gain(S,A)}{SplitInfo(S,A)}$ | C4.5 normalized gain | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\chi^2 = \sum\limits_{i=1}^{2} \sum\limits_{j=1}^{c} \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | Chi-square statistic for bin-merging | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Mining Frequent Patterns — Core Concepts

### 2.4.1 Formal Terminology

Let $I = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_m\}$ be the set of all **items**.
Let $D = \{T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n\}$ be the **transaction database**, where each $T_j \subseteq I$.

- **Itemset** — a collection of one or more items. E.g., $\{Bread, Butter\}$.
- **k-Itemset** — an itemset containing exactly $k$ items.
- **Support Count** of an itemset $X$, denoted $\sigma(X)$ — the number of transactions in $D$ that contain $X$ as a subset.
- **Support** of $X$, denoted $s(X)$ — the fraction of transactions containing $X$.
- **Frequent Itemset** — an itemset $X$ with $s(X) \ge minsup$.
- **Association Rule** — an implication of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$, where $X \subset I, \; Y \subset I, \; X \cap Y = \emptyset$.

### 2.4.2 Two-Step Decomposition of Association Rule Mining

1. **Frequent Itemset Generation** — find all itemsets whose support $\ge minsup$. *Computationally expensive step.*
2. **Rule Generation** — from each frequent itemset, extract high-confidence rules. *Less expensive.*

> [!NOTE]
> The cost of Step 1 is exponential ($2^m$ possible itemsets), so the **Apriori property** and **FP-Growth** are essential optimizations.

### 2.4.3 The Apriori Property (Anti-Monotonicity)

> **Apriori Property:** *All non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent.*

Contrapositive: *If an itemset is infrequent, then all of its supersets are also infrequent (superset closure of infrequency).*

This property **prunes the search space** dramatically — it is the foundation of the Apriori algorithm.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Frequent Pattern Mining

| # | Formula | Meaning | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s(X) = \dfrac{\sigma(X)}{\vert D \vert}$ | Support of itemset X | $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | $\sigma(X) = \vert \{T_j \in D \;\vert\; X \subseteq T_j\} \vert$ | Support count | $\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ |
| 3 | $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)}$ | Confidence of rule X ⇒ Y | $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | $Lift(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)} = \dfrac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}$ | Strength of correlation | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 5 | $Conviction(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{1 - s(Y)}{1 - c(X \Rightarrow Y)}$ | Directional dependence | $[0.5, \infty)$ |
| 6 | $N_{candidates} = \sum\limits_{k=1}^{m} \binom{m}{k} = 2^m - 1$ | Total candidate itemsets (worst case) | exponential |

### 2.6 Interpretation of Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lift Interpretation Rule (High-Frequency Exam Topic):**
> - $Lift > 1$ → **Positive correlation** (X & Y co-occur more than random).
> - $Lift = 1$ → **Independence** (no correlation).
> - $Lift < 1$ → **Negative correlation** (X & Y co-occur less than random).
>
> A common student trap: **High confidence ≠ strong rule.** Use Lift to confirm true correlation.

### 2.7 Apriori vs. FP-Growth — Engineering Trade-offs

| Property | Apriori | FP-Growth |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Generate-and-test (BFS) | Pattern fragment growth (divide-and-conquer via FP-Tree) |
| **Candidate Generation** | Yes (Lₖ₋₁ × Lₖ₋₁ join) | **No** candidate generation |
| **Database Scans** | k + 1 scans (k = max pattern length) | **2 scans** only |
| **Memory** | Multiple candidate hash trees | Compressed FP-Tree (often fits in RAM) |
| **Best For** | Small datasets, sparse data | Large, dense datasets |
| **Drawback** | Exponential candidates for low minsup | Tree may be large for highly branching data |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Equal-Width Binning

**Problem (KTU-Style):** Given the continuous attribute `Age` with sorted values $\{12, 15, 18, 22, 25, 28, 31, 35, 40, 45\}$, discretize into $k = 3$ bins using **equal-width binning**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the range:**

$$A_{min} = 12, \quad A_{max} = 45$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bin width $W$:**

$$W = \dfrac{A_{max} - A_{min}}{k} = \dfrac{45 - 12}{3} = \dfrac{33}{3} = 11$$

**Step 3 — Construct the bin boundaries:**

$$\text{Bin 1: } [12, 23) \quad \rightarrow \quad \{12, 15, 18, 22\}$$
$$\text{Bin 2: } [23, 34) \quad \rightarrow \quad \{25, 28, 31\}$$
$$\text{Bin 3: } [34, 45] \quad \rightarrow \quad \{35, 40, 45\}$$

**Step 4 — Verify count:**

$$4 + 3 + 3 = 10 \; \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge-case note:** The last bin is **closed on both ends** $[\,]$ to ensure the maximum value is included. All other bins are **left-closed, right-open** $[\,)$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Information-Entropy-Based Split Selection

**Problem:** Consider the dataset $S$ with **15 tuples** — 9 of class `Yes` and 6 of class `No`. A continuous attribute $A$ has two candidate cut-points: $a_1$ and $a_2$.

- Cut $a_1$: produces subsets $S_1$ (5 Yes, 3 No) and $S_2$ (4 Yes, 3 No).
- Cut $a_2$: produces subsets $S_3$ (6 Yes, 1 No) and $S_4$ (3 Yes, 5 No).

Determine which cut yields the **highest information gain**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Compute the parent entropy $E(S)$:**

$$E(S) = -\left(\dfrac{9}{15}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{9}{15}\right) - \left(\dfrac{6}{15}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{6}{15}\right)$$

$$= -(0.6)\log_2(0.6) - (0.4)\log_2(0.4)$$

$$= -(0.6)(-0.7370) - (0.4)(-1.3219)$$

$$= 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute entropy after cut $a_1$:**

For $S_1$: 5 Yes, 3 No $\rightarrow$ total = 8

$$E(S_1) = -\left(\dfrac{5}{8}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{5}{8}\right) - \left(\dfrac{3}{8}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{3}{8}\right) = 0.6250 + 0.3750 \cdot \log_2(2.667)$$

$$= -(0.625)(-0.6781) - (0.375)(-0.4150) = 0.4238 + 0.1556 = 0.9544$$

For $S_2$: 4 Yes, 3 No $\rightarrow$ total = 7

$$E(S_2) = -\left(\dfrac{4}{7}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{4}{7}\right) - \left(\dfrac{3}{7}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{3}{7}\right) = 0.4614 + 0.5238 = 0.9852$$

Weighted entropy:

$$E(S, a_1) = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot 0.9544 + \dfrac{7}{15} \cdot 0.9852$$

$$= 0.5090 + 0.4598 = 0.9688 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Compute entropy after cut $a_2$:**

For $S_3$: 6 Yes, 1 No $\rightarrow$ total = 7

$$E(S_3) = -\left(\dfrac{6}{7}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{6}{7}\right) - \left(\dfrac{1}{7}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{1}{7}\right)$$

$$= -(0.8571)(-0.2224) - (0.1429)(-2.8074) = 0.1906 + 0.4011 = 0.5917$$

For $S_4$: 3 Yes, 5 No $\rightarrow$ total = 8

$$E(S_4) = -\left(\dfrac{3}{8}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{3}{8}\right) - \left(\dfrac{5}{8}\right)\log_2\left(\dfrac{5}{8}\right)$$

$$= -(0.375)(-1.4150) - (0.625)(-0.6781) = 0.5306 + 0.4238 = 0.9544$$

Weighted entropy:

$$E(S, a_2) = \dfrac{7}{15} \cdot 0.5917 + \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot 0.9544 = 0.2761 + 0.5090 = 0.7851 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Compute information gains:**

$$Gain(S, a_1) = E(S) - E(S, a_1) = 0.9710 - 0.9688 = 0.0022 \text{ bits}$$

$$Gain(S, a_2) = E(S) - E(S, a_2) = 0.9710 - 0.7851 = 0.1859 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$\boxed{a_2 \text{ is the better split, with } Gain = 0.1859 \text{ bits}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Apriori Algorithm — End-to-End

**Problem (Classic KTU Exam Problem):** Given the transaction database $D$ with 5 transactions and minimum support $minsup = 60\%$. Find all **frequent itemsets** using Apriori.

| TID | Items Bought |
|---|---|
| T1 | {Bread, Butter, Jam} |
| T2 | {Bread, Butter} |
| T3 | {Butter, Jam, Milk} |
| T4 | {Bread, Jam, Milk} |
| T5 | {Bread, Butter, Jam, Milk} |

### Step-by-Step Walkthrough:

**Step 1 — Compute $|D|$ and absolute minsup count:**

$$|D| = 5, \quad minsup_{count} = 0.60 \times 5 = 3$$

**Step 2 — $C_1$ (Candidate 1-Itemsets) — count all 1-itemsets:**

| Itemset | Support Count | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|
| {Bread} | 4 (T1, T2, T4, T5) | ✓ |
| {Butter} | 4 (T1, T2, T3, T5) | ✓ |
| {Jam} | 4 (T1, T3, T4, T5) | ✓ |
| {Milk} | 3 (T3, T4, T5) | ✓ |

$$L_1 = \{\{Bread\}, \{Butter\}, \{Jam\}, \{Milk\}\}$$

**Step 3 — $C_2$ — Generate candidate 2-itemsets by self-join $L_1 \bowtie L_1$:**

| Itemset | Count | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|
| {Bread, Butter} | 3 (T1, T2, T5) | ✓ |
| {Bread, Jam} | 3 (T1, T4, T5) | ✓ |
| {Bread, Milk} | 2 (T4, T5) | ✗ |
| {Butter, Jam} | 3 (T1, T3, T5) | ✓ |
| {Butter, Milk} | 2 (T3, T5) | ✗ |
| {Jam, Milk} | 3 (T3, T4, T5) | ✓ |

**Step 4 — Prune step (Apriori property):** Each candidate's subsets (all size-2 subsets of a 2-itemset are itself) — no early pruning since all subsets of $C_2$ are in $L_1$. *Pruning kicks in at $C_3$.*

$$L_2 = \{\{B,Bu\}, \{B,J\}, \{Bu,J\}, \{J,M\}\}$$

**Step 5 — $C_3$ — Self-join $L_2 \bowtie L_2$ and prune:**

Initial join produces: $\{B, Bu, J\}, \{B, J, M\}, \{B, Bu, M\}, \{Bu, J, M\}$.

**Prune check using Apriori property** — any 3-itemset whose 2-subset is NOT in $L_2$ is pruned:

- $\{B, J, M\}$ — its subsets are $\{B, J\}$ ✓, $\{B, M\}$ ✗ (NOT in $L_2$) → **PRUNED**
- $\{B, Bu, M\}$ — subset $\{B, M\}$ ✗ → **PRUNED**
- $\{Bu, J, M\}$ — subset $\{Bu, M\}$ ✗ → **PRUNED**

**Step 6 — Only candidate surviving prune: $\{Bread, Butter, Jam\}$:**

| Itemset | Count | Frequent? |
|---|---|---|
| {Bread, Butter, Jam} | 2 (T1, T5) | ✗ |

**Step 7 — $L_3 = \emptyset$.** Stop.

**Final Frequent Itemsets:**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{F} = \{\{B\}, \{Bu\}, \{J\}, \{M\}, \{B,Bu\}, \{B,J\}, \{Bu,J\}, \{J,M\}\}}$$

**Step 8 — Rule generation from 2-itemsets** (each yields 2 non-trivial rules):

| Rule | Support | Confidence |
|---|---|---|
| Bread ⇒ Butter | 3/5 = 0.60 | 3/4 = **0.75** |
| Butter ⇒ Bread | 3/5 = 0.60 | 3/4 = **0.75** |
| Bread ⇒ Jam | 3/5 = 0.60 | 3/4 = **0.75** |
| Jam ⇒ Bread | 3/5 = 0.60 | 3/4 = **0.75** |
| Butter ⇒ Jam | 3/5 = 0.60 | 3/4 = **0.75** |
| Jam ⇒ Butter | 3/5 = 0.60 | 3/4 = **0.75** |
| Jam ⇒ Milk | 3/5 = 0.60 | 3/4 = **0.75** |
| Milk ⇒ Jam | 3/5 = 0.60 | 3/3 = **1.00** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All 8 rules are strong** (assuming $minconf = 0.6$). The strongest rule is **{Milk ⇒ Jam}** with confidence 1.00 — whenever Milk is bought, Jam is ALWAYS bought.

### 3.4 Lift Computation for Selected Rules

**For Rule: Milk ⇒ Jam:**

$$Lift = \dfrac{c(\text{Milk} \Rightarrow \text{Jam})}{s(\text{Jam})} = \dfrac{1.00}{4/5} = \dfrac{1.00}{0.80} = 1.25$$

> Since $Lift > 1$, Milk and Jam have a **positive correlation** — they are more likely to occur together than independently.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Data Analytics Pipeline — Position of Discretization & ARM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Transactional Data] --> B[Data Cleaning]
    B --> C{Continuous Attributes Present?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Discretization Step]
    C -- No --> E[Already Discrete]
    D --> F[Discrete Dataset]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Frequent Itemset Generation]
    G --> H[Apriori Property Check]
    H -- Subsets Frequent --> I[Candidate Pruning]
    H -- Some Subset Infrequent --> J[Discard Candidate]
    I --> K[Strong Association Rules]
    J --> G
    K --> L[Knowledge / Action]
```

## 4.2 Discretization Methods — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Discretization Methods] --> U[Unsupervised]
    D --> S[Supervised]
    U --> U1[Equal-Width Binning]
    U --> U2[Equal-Frequency Binning]
    U --> U3[K-Means Clustering]
    S --> S1[Entropy-Based ID3]
    S --> S2[Chi-Square ChiMerge]
    S --> S3[CAIM]
    U1 --> M1[Width equals Range over k]
    U2 --> M2[Bins contain N over k tuples]
    U3 --> M3[Cluster centroids as cut-points]
    S1 --> M4[Maximize Information Gain]
    S2 --> M5[Merge low Chi-Square bins]
    S3 --> M6[Maximize class-attribute dependency]
```

## 4.3 Apriori Algorithm — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start: Database D, minsup] --> P1[Scan D: Compute C1]
    P1 --> P2[Derive L1: freq 1-itemsets]
    P2 --> P3{k = 2}
    P3 --> P4[Ck: Self-join Lk-1 x Lk-1]
    P4 --> P5[Prune: Remove itemsets with infrequent subset]
    P5 --> P6[Scan D: Count Ck]
    P6 --> P7{Lk non-empty?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Derive Lk from Ck]
    P8 --> P9[k = k + 1]
    P9 --> P3
    P7 -- No --> P10[Stop: Union of all Lk = Frequent Itemsets]
    P10 --> P11[Generate Rules from each Lk]
    P11 --> P12[Output: Strong Association Rules]
```

## 4.4 Association Rule Quality — Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Rule X implies Y] --> S1[Compute Support]
    S1 --> Q1{Support greater than minsup?}
    Q1 -- No --> X1[Reject: Rule is Rare]
    Q1 -- Yes --> S2[Compute Confidence]
    S2 --> Q2{Confidence greater than minconf?}
    Q2 -- No --> X2[Reject: Rule is Unreliable]
    Q2 -- Yes --> S3[Compute Lift]
    S3 --> Q3{Lift greater than 1?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ACC[Accept: Positive Correlation]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Lift equals 1?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> IND[Independence: No Real Rule]
    Q4 -- No --> NEG[Reject: Negative Correlation]
```

| Attribute Type | Suitable for Apriori? | Discretization Required? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal (Categorical) | ✓ Yes | No | Color = {Red, Green, Blue} |
| Ordinal (Ordered Categories) | ✓ Yes | No | Size = {S, M, L, XL} |
| Continuous Numeric | ✗ No | **Yes** | Age = 27.5 |
| Binary (Boolean) | ✓ Yes | No | HasCard = {0, 1} |
| Ratio-Scaled | ✗ No | **Yes** | Income = ₹5,42,300 |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define discretization. List any three methods of discretization.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Discretization** is the data preprocessing step of converting continuous (numeric) attribute values into a small number of discrete intervals or categories, preserving the inherent information of the data. It is essential because algorithms like **Apriori** and decision trees require categorical input.
>
> **Three Methods of Discretization:**
> 1. **Binning Methods** — Equal-Width Binning (divides range into $k$ equal intervals) and Equal-Frequency Binning (each bin gets $N/k$ tuples).
> 2. **Clustering-Based** — Apply K-Means clustering; each cluster centroid becomes a cut-point.
> 3. **Entropy-Based (Information-Theoretic)** — Uses Shannon's entropy and information gain to greedily select the best split points, as in ID3/C4.5 decision-tree induction.
>
> *(Any other valid method: Histogram Analysis, ChiMerge, CAIM, Natural Break.)*

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is an association rule? Define support and confidence with formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Association Rule:** An implication of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$, where $X$ and $Y$ are disjoint non-empty itemsets ($X \cap Y = \emptyset$). It is generated from a transactional database.
>
> **Support** — the fraction of transactions in the database that contain the itemset $X \cup Y$. It indicates the **popularity** of the rule.
>
> $$s(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{|D|}$$
>
> where $\sigma(X \cup Y)$ is the support count and $|D|$ is the total number of transactions.
>
> **Confidence** — the conditional probability that a transaction containing $X$ also contains $Y$. It measures the **reliability** of the rule.
>
> $$c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)} = \dfrac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)}$$
>
> A rule is **strong** if $s \ge minsup$ AND $c \ge minconf$.

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a suitable example, explain the Apriori algorithm for mining frequent itemsets. Discuss the Apriori property and its role in pruning the search space. (14 marks)**

**Distribution: (a) 7 marks — Algorithm walkthrough with example | (b) 7 marks — Apriori property & pruning logic**

#### Part (a) — Apriori Algorithm with Example [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the input and output of Apriori:**

> **Input:** Transactional database $D$; user-specified minimum support threshold $minsup$.
> **Output:** All frequent itemsets (those with support $\ge minsup$).

**Step 2 — State the Apriori algorithm formally:**

> *The Apriori algorithm is an iterative, level-wise (breadth-first) search that uses the Apriori property to prune the candidate generation. It proceeds as follows:*
>
> 1. $L_1 = \{$ frequent 1-itemsets $\}$.
> 2. **For** $k = 2; \; L_{k-1} \neq \emptyset; \; k++$ **do**
> 3. $\quad C_k =$ candidates generated by self-join $L_{k-1} \bowtie L_{k-1}$.
> 4. $\quad C_k = $ prune($C_k$, $L_{k-1}$) *(remove itemsets whose $(k-1)$-subsets are not in $L_{k-1}$)*.
> 5. $\quad$ For each transaction $t \in D$, increment the count of every candidate in $C_k$ contained in $t$.
> 6. $\quad L_k = \{ c \in C_k \;\vert\; c.count / \vert D \vert \ge minsup \}$.
> 7. **End**
> 8. **Return** $\mathcal{F} = \bigcup_k L_k$.

**Step 3 — Illustrate with a small transactional example:**

> *Consider the database below with 5 transactions and $minsup = 60\%$.*
>
> | TID | Items |
> |---|---|
> | T1 | I1, I2, I5 |
> | T2 | I2, I4 |
> | T3 | I2, I3 |
> | T4 | I1, I2, I4 |
> | T5 | I1, I3 |
> | T6 | I2, I3 |
> | T7 | I1, I3 |
> | T8 | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
> | T9 | I1, I2, I3 |
>
> **$C_1$** — count each item; all four items have count $\ge 5$ (since $6 \times 0.6 = 3.6 \approx 4$). So $L_1 = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}, \{I4\}, \{I5\}\}$.
>
> **$C_2$** — self-join $L_1$: 10 candidates; prune: each subset is in $L_1$, so none pruned. Count and find $L_2$.
>
> **$C_3$** — self-join $L_2$: form 3-itemsets; prune using Apriori property; count and find $L_3$.
>
> *(For full marks, the student should tabulate the candidate-generation, prune, and counting for at least $L_1$, $L_2$, and $L_3$.)*

#### Part (b) — Apriori Property & Pruning [7 Marks]

> **Apriori Property (Anti-Monotonicity of Support):**
> *If an itemset $X$ is frequent, then every non-empty subset of $X$ must also be frequent.*
>
> **Contrapositive (used for pruning):**
> *If an itemset $Y$ is infrequent, then any superset of $Y$ is also infrequent.*
>
> **Role in pruning:**
> 1. The Apriori property **drastically reduces the candidate search space** from $2^m$ subsets to a much smaller manageable subset.
> 2. In the **join step**, two $(k-1)$-itemsets are joined only if their first $(k-2)$ items are identical — a *lexicographic ordering* is used.
> 3. In the **prune step**, any candidate $k$-itemset containing a $(k-1)$-subset not in $L_{k-1}$ is removed.
> 4. The support function is **monotonically non-increasing** as itemset size grows, so once an itemset fails the threshold, all its extensions also fail — **no need to check them**.
>
> **Example:** If $\{A, B, C\}$ is infrequent, then $\{A, B, C, D\}$, $\{A, B, C, E\}$, etc., are **automatically discarded** without scanning the database.

### ⭐ Question B1 — Option B (Alternative Choice) (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the FP-Growth algorithm for mining frequent patterns. Compare it with the Apriori algorithm. (14 marks)**

**Distribution: (a) 7 marks — FP-Tree construction and mining | (b) 7 marks — Comparison with Apriori**

#### Part (a) — FP-Growth Algorithm [7 Marks]

> **FP-Growth (Frequent Pattern Growth)** is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that **avoids candidate generation** entirely. It uses a compact data structure called the **FP-Tree (Frequent Pattern Tree)**.
>
> **Two-Step Process:**
>
> **Step 1 — Build the FP-Tree:**
> 1. **Scan 1:** Find frequent 1-itemsets and sort them in descending order of support (forming the **f-list**).
> 2. **Scan 2:** For each transaction, sort its items according to the f-list, then insert into the FP-Tree. A **header table** links all occurrences of each item.
>
> **Step 2 — Mine the FP-Tree recursively:**
> 1. Start from the **least frequent item** in the f-list.
> 2. Construct its **conditional pattern base** (set of prefix paths).
> 3. Build a **conditional FP-Tree** from the conditional pattern base.
> 4. Mine the conditional tree recursively to grow patterns.
>
> **Example sketch:**
> Suppose $minsup = 3$ and the f-list (after scan 1) is: $f = [f, c, a, b, m, p]$ with frequencies $[4, 4, 3, 3, 3, 3]$.
>
> After scan 2 and tree construction, the FP-Tree has a root and branches. Mining starts from the bottom item $p$, building its conditional pattern base: $\{f, c, a, m : 1\}$, $\{f, b : 1\}$, $\{c, b : 1\}$. Since no item in the conditional base meets $minsup$, no patterns starting with $p$ are produced.
>
> *Recursion continues for $m, b, a, c, f$.* Frequent patterns like $\{f, c, a, m\}$, $\{f, c, a, b\}$, etc., are generated.

#### Part (b) — Comparison: FP-Growth vs. Apriori [7 Marks]

| Parameter | Apriori | FP-Growth |
|---|---|---|
| **Candidate Generation** | Generates candidate itemsets explicitly | **No** candidate generation |
| **Database Scans** | $k+1$ scans (for $k$-itemset mining) | **Only 2 scans** |
| **Memory Usage** | Multiple candidate hash trees | Compact FP-Tree (compressed) |
| **Search Strategy** | Breadth-First (level-wise) | Depth-First, divide-and-conquer |
| **Time Complexity** | Exponential in worst case | Generally linear w.r.t. frequent patterns |
| **Pruning Mechanism** | Apriori property on candidate set | Apriori property on tree paths |
| **Sensitivity to minsup** | Slow for low minsup | Efficient even for low minsup |
| **Data Type** | Works on transactional data | Works on transactional data (compact form) |
| **Best Use Case** | Small/medium datasets, high minsup | **Large datasets**, low minsup, dense data |
| **Drawback** | Candidate explosion at low minsup | FP-Tree construction overhead; complex implementation |

> **Conclusion:** FP-Growth is **more efficient and scalable** than Apriori in most practical scenarios because it eliminates costly candidate generation and reduces database I/O. However, Apriori remains pedagogically important and is simpler to implement.
