The diagram shows the ranker as a dashed (optional) module to highlight that the inverted index is the **shared foundation** of Boolean and ranked retrieval. ## 4.2 Data Structure — Dictionary + Postings File ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DICT["Dictionary File in Memory"] direction TB e1["brutus df=3 pointer=0"] e2["caesar df=3 pointer=24"] e3["killed df=3 pointer=48"] end subgraph POST["Postings File on Disk"] direction TB p1["2, 3, 4"] p2["1, 3, 4"] p3["1, 2, 4"] end e1 -- ptr=0 --> p1 e2 -- ptr=24 --> p2 e3 -- ptr=48 --> p3 ``` **Explanation.** The dictionary fits in memory (it has $\vert V \vert$ entries, typically 1–100 MB even for the web). The postings file lives on disk and is accessed only when a query actually references the term — this is called a **lazy / on-demand** posting fetch, and it is what makes web-scale search feasible. ## 4.3 The Merge (Intersect) Algorithm — Step-by-Step Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB start["Start: two sorted postings lists P_a and P_b"] --> init["Initialize p_a to head of P_a, p_b to head of P_b"] init --> check{"Are p_a and p_b non-null?"} check -- "No" --> done["Return accumulated result list"] check -- "Yes" --> cmp{"p_a == p_b ?"} cmp -- "Yes" --> emit["Emit p_a to result. Advance both pointers."] cmp -- "No" --> lt{"p_a less than p_b ?"} lt -- "Yes" --> advA["Advance p_a to next element"] lt -- "No" --> advB["Advance p_b to next element"] emit --> check advA --> check advB --> check ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The total work is bounded by the **sum of the two list lengths** — this linear-time guarantee is the single most important property of the merge algorithm. ## 4.4 Query Optimization — Heuristic Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB q["User Query"] --> parse["Parse into tree"] parse --> leaves["Leaves: term nodes
each annotated with df_t"] leaves --> sort["Sort children by df_t ascending"] sort --> eval["Evaluate bottom-up
intersect/union/diff shortest first"] eval --> out["Result doc-ID set"] ``` ## 4.5 Positional Index — Phrase Query Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR phrase["Phrase Query t1 t2"] --> intDocs["Intersect document postings
P t1 and P t2"] intDocs --> perDoc["For each common doc d:"] perDoc --> posWalk["Two-pointer walk on position lists pos t1 d and pos t2 d"] posWalk --> adjCheck{"pos t2 minus pos t1 equals 1?"} adjCheck -- "Yes" --> emitDoc["Emit d as phrase hit"] adjCheck -- "No" --> adv["Advance pointer with smaller position"] adv --> posWalk ``` ## 4.6 Comparison — Boolean Retrieval vs. Ranked Retrieval | Aspect | Boolean Retrieval | Ranked Retrieval (e.g., BM25) | |---|---|---| | Query form | `AND` / `OR` / `NOT` expressions | Free-text natural language | | Output | Set of docs, no order | Ordered list by relevance score | | Underlying index | Inverted index (same) | Inverted index (same) | | Strength | Precise, reproducible, expert-friendly | User-friendly, handles vague queries | | Weakness | Hard for casual users; no partial credit | Less transparent; may miss exact matches | | Used in | Legal, enterprise, PubMed, log analytics | Web search, e-commerce, recommendations | # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mark Distribution Reference (PCCDT503 — Data Analytics):** > - **Part A** questions: **3 marks** each, no choice, typically one or two per module. > - **Part B** questions: **14 marks** each, **internal choice**, sub-parts usually **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. > - Cognitive levels tested: **Apply**, **Analyze**, **Evaluate** (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy). --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)] — 3 Marks **Define Boolean Retrieval. Differentiate between `AND`, `OR`, and `NOT` operations in the context of postings lists.** **Model Answer:** Boolean Retrieval is an information retrieval model in which a query is a logical (Boolean) expression over index terms, and the system returns exactly those documents that make the expression evaluate to TRUE. - **`AND` (t₁ AND t₂):** set intersection of postings lists — returns documents containing *both* terms. Cost: $O(\vert P(t_1)\vert + \vert P(t_2)\vert)$. - **`OR` (t₁ OR t₂):** set union — returns documents containing *either* term. - **`NOT` (t₁ AND NOT t₂):** set difference — returns documents containing $t_1$ but *not* $t_2$. The result size is $\le \vert P(t_1)\vert$. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)] — 3 Marks **What is an inverted index? Why is it preferred over the term-document incidence matrix for large collections?** **Model Answer:** An **inverted index** is a data structure that maps each term $t$ in the vocabulary to its **postings list** $P(t)$ — the sorted list of document IDs in which $t$ occurs. It consists of a **dictionary** (term → postings pointer) and a **postings file** (concatenated postings lists). It is preferred over the incidence matrix because: | Property | Incidence Matrix | Inverted Index | |---|---|---| | Storage | $\vert V \vert \times N$ bits (dense; mostly zeros) | $\sum_t \text{df}_t$ entries (sparse, only the 1s) | | Typical size (web) | Petabytes | Tens of GB | | Query time | One matrix multiplication | One merge of two sorted lists | | Practicality | Intractable beyond tiny corpora | Standard for web-scale search | For a collection of $N = 10^8$ documents and $\vert V \vert = 10^6$ terms, the incidence matrix needs $\approx 1.25 \times 10^{14}$ bits, whereas the inverted index needs only $\sum_t \text{df}_t \ll 1.25 \times 10^{14}$ bits. --- ## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout.** > **(1)** Many students confuse *document frequency* (df) with *collection frequency* (cf). Remember: df counts **documents**, cf counts **occurrences**. > **(2)** When the question says *"optimize the query"*, you **must** state the optimization heuristic (shortest-list-first) AND show the cost comparison numerically. Writing only the final answer loses 2–3 marks. > **(3)** For phrase queries, the two-step procedure (**first intersect doc IDs, then check positions**) must be made explicit. > **(4)** Always sort the postings lists before merging — examiners look for this invariant. --- ### **Question A** (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024 (Model)] Consider the following four short documents in a Boolean retrieval system: ``` D1: "data analytics is the science of insight" D2: "machine learning powers modern analytics" D3: "data science combines statistics and analytics" D4: "deep learning is a branch of machine learning" ``` **(a)** Construct the **inverted index** (dictionary + postings lists) for the terms `analytics`, `data`, `learning`, `machine`, and `science`. State each term's document frequency. (7 marks) **(b)** Using the merge (intersect) algorithm, evaluate the query > **`data AND learning AND NOT science`** Show every step of the algorithm. State which documents the query returns. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution to Question A** **(a) Inverted Index Construction** After lowercasing and tokenization, the term occurrences are: | Term | Documents | Document Frequency dfₜ | |---|---|---| | analytics | D1, D2, D3 | 3 | | data | D1, D3 | 2 | | learning | D2, D4 | 2 | | machine | D2, D4 | 2 | | science | D1, D3 | 2 | The postings lists, written as sorted integer doc-IDs: $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{analytics}) &= \langle 1, 2, 3 \rangle \\ P(\text{data}) &= \langle 1, 3 \rangle \\ P(\text{learning}) &= \langle 2, 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{machine}) &= \langle 2, 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{science}) &= \langle 1, 3 \rangle \end{aligned} $$ **Dictionary table (memory-resident):** | Term | dfₜ | Pointer into postings file | |---|---|---| | analytics | 3 | 0 | | data | 2 | 12 | | learning | 2 | 20 | | machine | 2 | 28 | | science | 2 | 36 | *(The byte offsets are illustrative; each entry occupies 4 bytes.)* **Mark allocation (a):** 3 marks for tokenization, 2 marks for correct df, 2 marks for sorted postings lists. --- **(b) Query Evaluation: `data AND learning AND NOT science`** The query is interpreted as: $$ (P(\text{data}) \cap P(\text{learning})) \setminus P(\text{science}) $$ **Step 1 — Intersect `data` and `learning`** using the merge algorithm: $$ P(\text{data}) = \langle 1, 3 \rangle, \quad P(\text{learning}) = \langle 2, 4 \rangle $$ $$ \begin{aligned} p_d = 1,\; p_l = 2: & \quad 1 < 2 \Rightarrow \text{advance } p_d \text{ to } 3 \\ p_d = 3,\; p_l = 2: & \quad 3 > 2 \Rightarrow \text{advance } p_l \text{ to } 4 \\ p_d = 3,\; p_l = 4: & \quad 3 < 4 \Rightarrow \text{advance } p_d \text{ to end} \\ \text{Result:} & \quad \langle \, \rangle \;(\text{empty — no document contains both}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply `NOT science`:** since the intermediate set is empty, the final result is also empty. > **The query returns the empty set of documents.** > [!NOTE] > **Optimization note (for the additional credit):** A smarter evaluator would short-circuit: since $P(\text{data}) \cap P(\text{learning}) = \emptyset$, the system should return immediately *without* scanning $P(\text{science})$. KTU often awards a bonus mark for mentioning this. **Mark allocation (b):** 3 marks for parsing and rewriting as set operations, 3 marks for the merge trace, 1 mark for correct final result. --- ### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)] **(a)** Explain the construction of a **term-document incidence matrix** and an **inverted index** for the corpus below. Compute the document frequency of each term and the total number of postings entries. ``` D1: "big data enables big insights" D2: "data science needs big data" D3: "insights come from big data and analytics" D4: "analytics and data go hand in hand" ``` *(7 marks)* **(b)** A user issues the Boolean query > **`(big AND data) AND NOT (analytics OR insights)`** Evaluate the query using the **shortest-list-first optimization heuristic**. State the final set of matching documents and the total number of postings comparisons. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution to Question B** **(a) Term-Document Matrix and Inverted Index** **Tokenization & unique terms (lowercased):** $$ V = \{\text{big, data, enables, insights, science, needs, come, from, and, analytics, go, hand, in, the, of}\} $$ For brevity, we restrict the incidence matrix to the 5 most-query-relevant terms: | Term \ Doc | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | dfₜ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | big | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 3 | | data | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 4 | | insights | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 2 | | analytics | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | | science | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **Full inverted index postings:** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{big}) &= \langle 1, 2, 3 \rangle \\ P(\text{data}) &= \langle 1, 2, 3, 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{enables}) &= \langle 1 \rangle \\ P(\text{insights}) &= \langle 1, 3 \rangle \\ P(\text{science}) &= \langle 2 \rangle \\ P(\text{needs}) &= \langle 2 \rangle \\ P(\text{come}) &= \langle 3 \rangle \\ P(\text{from}) &= \langle 3 \rangle \\ P(\text{and}) &= \langle 3, 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{analytics}) &= \langle 3, 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{go}) &= \langle 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{hand}) &= \langle 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{in}) &= \langle 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{the}) &= \langle 4 \rangle \\ P(\text{of}) &= \langle 4 \rangle \end{aligned} $$ **Total number of postings entries:** $$ \sum_{t \in V} \text{df}_t \;=\; 3 + 4 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 \;=\; \mathbf{23} $$ **Mark allocation (a):** 2 marks for the matrix, 3 marks for postings lists, 2 marks for the total-postings calculation. --- **(b) Optimized Query Evaluation** Query: `(big AND data) AND NOT (analytics OR insights)` **Step 1 — Identify postings-list lengths and apply the shortest-list-first heuristic.** List lengths: $$ \begin{aligned} \vert P(\text{big}) \vert &= 3 \\ \vert P(\text{data}) \vert &= 4 \\ \vert P(\text{analytics}) \vert &= 2 \\ \vert P(\text{insights}) \vert &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the `OR` sub-expression first (union of two short lists):** $$ P(\text{analytics}) \cup P(\text{insights}) = \langle 3, 4 \rangle \cup \langle 1, 3 \rangle = \langle 1, 3, 4 \rangle $$ Cost: $2 + 2 = 4$ comparisons. **Step 3 — Compute the `AND NOT` with `big`:** $$ P(\text{big}) \setminus \langle 1, 3, 4 \rangle = \langle 1, 2, 3 \rangle \setminus \langle 1, 3, 4 \rangle = \langle 2 \rangle $$ Cost: $3 + 3 = 6$ comparisons. **Step 4 — Intersect the result with `data`:** $$ \langle 2 \rangle \cap P(\text{data}) = \langle 2 \rangle \cap \langle 1, 2, 3, 4 \rangle = \langle 2 \rangle $$ Cost: $1 + 4 = 5$ comparisons. **Total cost: $4 + 6 + 5 = \mathbf{15}$ postings comparisons.** **Final result: only Document D2 matches the query.** > [!NOTE] > **Naïve (unoptimized) order would have been:** `big AND data` first ($3 + 4 = 7$ comparisons yielding $\langle 1, 2, 3 \rangle$), then intersect with the full `(analytics OR insights)` union ($3 + 3 = 6$), giving a total of $7 + 6 = 13$ comparisons — *slightly* cheaper in this tiny example, but the gap widens dramatically for real corpora because the optimized plan keeps intermediate sets small. Examiners award the mark for *stating* the optimization principle, not for the absolute number. **Mark allocation (b):** 2 marks for the heuristic statement, 3 marks for the trace, 2 marks for the final result. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section and revise from it the night before the exam.** - ✅ **Boolean Retrieval = exact-match, set-based IR model.** Output is a *set* of documents, not a ranking. - ✅ **Three core operators:** `AND` = intersection, `OR` = union, `NOT` = set difference. Operator precedence: `NOT` > `AND` > `OR`; use parentheses to override. - ✅ **Incidence matrix** $M$: $\vert V \vert \times N$ binary matrix. Conceptually clean, **practically infeasible** at scale. - ✅ **Inverted index** is the practical replacement: dictionary (in memory) + postings file (on disk). - ✅ **Postings list $P(t)$** is always kept **sorted by docID** — this enables linear-time set operations. - ✅ **Document frequency $\text{df}_t$** = number of docs containing $t$. **Collection frequency $\text{cf}_t$** = total occurrences. These are **not** the same. - ✅ **Merge (intersect) algorithm**: two pointers; cost is $O(\vert P(t_a)\vert + \vert P(t_b)\vert)$. This is the single most-tested algorithm in this module. - ✅ **Query optimization heuristic**: evaluate the **shortest postings lists first** to keep intermediate results small. - ✅ **Negation cost**: `t1 AND NOT t2` costs $O(\vert P(t_1)\vert + \vert P(t_2)\vert)$ — same as intersection. - ✅ **Phrase queries** require a **positional index**; algorithm is **(1) intersect document IDs, (2) check position adjacency within each matching doc**. - ✅ **Biword phrase query cost** is dominated by the term with the most positional hits, not the most docs. - ✅ **Inverted index total size** (in entries) = $\sum_t \text{df}_t$ — the sum of all document frequencies. - ✅ **Vocabulary growth** follows Heaps' Law: $\vert V \vert = K \cdot N^{\beta}$ with $\beta \approx 0.5$. - ✅ **Term frequency** follows Zipf's Law: a few terms dominate, a long tail is rare — this is why *stop-word removal* and *stemming* are preprocessing essentials. - ✅ **Modern search engines** use the *same inverted index* as Boolean retrieval — they only add a **scoring layer** (TF-IDF, BM25, or neural) on top. So mastering Boolean IR = mastering the foundation of *every* modern search engine.

topics.title: Example IR Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Example IR Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Information Retrieval (IR)** is the science and engineering activity of searching, locating, and retrieving unstructured or semi-structured information from a large collection of documents to satisfy an information need expressed as a **user query**. An **Example IR Problem** is a worked computational exercise that demonstrates how a Vector Space Model (VSM) ranks documents in response to a query by combining **Term Frequency (TF)**, **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)**, **TF-IDF weighting**, and the **Cosine Similarity** metric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** The classic IR problem is evaluated under the **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** model, where word order is ignored and each document/query is treated as a vector of term weights in a $t$-dimensional term space ($t$ = number of unique terms in the corpus).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **massive library with 4 million books** and tell the librarian: *"I want books about gold, silver, and trucks."* The librarian cannot read every book. Instead, she:

1. **Counts** how many times your keywords appear in each book (Term Frequency).
2. **Ignores** common, useless words like *"of"*, *"the"*, *"a"* (stop-word removal).
3. **Down-weights** words that appear in *too many* books (because they are not discriminative — Inverse Document Frequency).
4. **Measures** how closely the "fingerprint" (vector) of each book matches the "fingerprint" of your query using the **cosine of the angle** between them.
5. **Returns** the top books in ranked order.

> The **smaller the angle** between a document vector and the query vector, the **more relevant** the document. Cosine similarity converts this geometric intuition into a single number between **0** (irrelevant) and **1** (identical).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

- **Corpus size** $N$ = total number of documents (here **4**).
- **Document Frequency** $df(t)$ = number of documents containing term $t$.
- **Logarithm base** = **10** (KTU board convention; sometimes natural log is accepted, but $\log_{10}$ is preferred for KTU valuation).
- **Stop Words** = a, of, in, and, the, is, are, was, were (removed before processing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stop-Word Removal Rule (KTU):** A document $D_1 = \text{"Shipment of gold damaged in a fire"}$ contains 7 raw tokens, but only **4 content terms** remain after stop-word removal: *{shipment, gold, damaged, fire}*.

---

### 1.4 Working Problem Statement (Canonical KTU 2024 Example)

> Given the following document collection and query, build the TF-IDF weighted term-document matrix and rank the documents by **Cosine Similarity** in descending order of relevance.
>
> **Documents:**
> - $D_1$: "shipment of gold damaged in a fire"
> - $D_2$: "delivery of silver arrived in a silver truck"
> - $D_3$: "shipment of gold arrived in a truck"
> - $D_4$: "purchase of gold and silver arrived"
>
> **Query $Q$:** "gold silver truck"
>
> Use $\log_{10}$ for IDF computation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The IR ranking pipeline executes in **five sequential stages**. Understanding *why* each stage exists is critical for KTU 14-mark derivations.

**Stage 1 — Tokenization & Stop-Word Removal**
- Break each document into raw tokens (words).
- Strip punctuation, lowercase, and delete stop words.
- *Why?* Stop words inflate the term dictionary and add noise to similarity scores.

**Stage 2 — Vocabulary Construction**
- Build a global vocabulary $V = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_m\}$ from the union of all content terms in the corpus.
- *Why?* A common coordinate system is mandatory for vector representation.

**Stage 3 — Term Frequency (TF) Matrix Construction**
- For each $(d_j, t_i)$ pair, count $tf(d_j, t_i)$.
- Apply optional normalization (raw count, binary, or logarithmic).

**Stage 4 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) Computation**
- Compute $idf(t_i)$ once per term to penalize corpus-wide common words.
- *Why?* A word appearing in every document has zero discriminative power.

**Stage 5 — TF-IDF Weighting & Cosine Similarity**
- Multiply $tf \times idf$ element-wise to obtain weighted vectors.
- Compute cosine similarity between the query vector and each document vector.
- Sort in descending order to obtain the ranking.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following five formulas constitute the **complete KTU board-evaluable toolkit** for this topic. Memorize the symbol, the formula, and the *range* of values produced.

| **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Range / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $tf(d, t)$ | Number of times term $t$ appears in document $d$ | Raw term frequency | $\mathbb{N} \cup \{0\}$, dimensionless |
| $df(t)$ | Number of documents containing term $t$ | Document frequency | $1 \le df(t) \le N$ |
| $idf(t)$ | $\log \left( \dfrac{N}{df(t)} \right)$ | Inverse document frequency (log base **10** in KTU) | $idf(t) \ge 0$ |
| $w(d, t)$ | $tf(d, t) \times idf(t)$ | TF-IDF weight of term $t$ in document $d$ | $w \ge 0$, dimensionless |
| $\text{sim}(D, Q)$ | $\dfrac{\vec{D} \cdot \vec{Q}}{\vert\vec{D}\vert_2 \cdot \vert\vec{Q}\vert_2}$ | Cosine similarity between document vector $\vec{D}$ and query vector $\vec{Q}$ | $0 \le \text{sim} \le 1$ |

**Key Intermediate Identities:**

$$
\vec{D} \cdot \vec{Q} = \sum_{t \in V} w(d, t) \cdot w(q, t)
$$

$$
\vert\vec{D}\vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{t \in V} w(d, t)^2}
$$

> **Note on absolute value bars:** The notation $\vert\vec{D}\vert_2$ denotes the **L2 (Euclidean) norm**, not the absolute value scalar. KTU examiners accept either $\vert\vec{x}\vert$ or $\Vert\vec{x}\Vert_2$.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This same VSM framework powers:
- **Web search engines** (Google, Bing) — ranking of billions of web pages.
- **Legal e-discovery** — finding relevant case-law precedents in seconds.
- **E-commerce recommendations** — matching product descriptions to user search terms.
- **Biomedical literature mining** — retrieving PubMed articles for genomics queries.
- **Plagiarism detection** — measuring overlap between document vectors.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern systems augment this baseline with **BM25 weighting**, **Word2Vec embeddings**, and **Transformer-based dense retrieval (DPR)**, but the **TF-IDF + Cosine** model remains the gold-standard pedagogical foundation tested in KTU 2024.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Every numerical sub-step below is shown explicitly. Skipping intermediate calculations (e.g., the squared norm) costs **2–3 marks** per question.

---

### Step 1 — Tokenization, Lowercasing & Stop-Word Removal

Apply lowercase, then remove the stop words $\{a, of, in, and, the, is, are\}$.

| **Document** | **Raw Tokens** | **Content Terms** |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | shipment, of, gold, damaged, in, a, fire | shipment, gold, damaged, fire |
| $D_2$ | delivery, of, silver, arrived, in, a, silver, truck | delivery, silver, arrived, silver, truck |
| $D_3$ | shipment, of, gold, arrived, in, a, truck | shipment, gold, arrived, truck |
| $D_4$ | purchase, of, gold, and, silver, arrived | purchase, gold, silver, arrived |
| $Q$ | gold, silver, truck | gold, silver, truck |

**Vocabulary** $V$ (9 unique content terms):
$$
V = \{\text{shipment, gold, damaged, fire, delivery, silver, arrived, truck, purchase}\}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Build the Raw Term-Frequency (TF) Matrix

Read off the count of each term in each document. Note that *silver* appears **twice** in $D_2$, and is **absent** in $D_1$ and $D_3$.

$$
\text{TF} =
\begin{bmatrix}
t & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & D_4 \\
\text{shipment} & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
\text{gold} & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
\text{damaged} & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{fire} & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{delivery} & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{silver} & 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 \\
\text{arrived} & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\text{truck} & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
\text{purchase} & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute Document Frequencies $df(t)$ and $idf(t)$

Sum the non-zero entries in each row of the TF matrix to get $df(t)$. Then apply:

$$
idf(t) = \log_{10} \left( \frac{N}{df(t)} \right) \quad ; \quad N = 4
$$

| **Term $t$** | $df(t)$ | $\frac{N}{df(t)}$ | $idf(t) = \log_{10}(N/df)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| shipment | 2 | $4/2 = 2.000$ | $0.301$ |
| gold | 3 | $4/3 = 1.333$ | $0.125$ |
| damaged | 1 | $4/1 = 4.000$ | $0.602$ |
| fire | 1 | $4/1 = 4.000$ | $0.602$ |
| delivery | 1 | $4/1 = 4.000$ | $0.602$ |
| silver | 2 | $4/2 = 2.000$ | $0.301$ |
| arrived | 3 | $4/3 = 1.333$ | $0.125$ |
| truck | 2 | $4/2 = 2.000$ | $0.301$ |
| purchase | 1 | $4/1 = 4.000$ | $0.602$ |

**Engineering insight:** Rare terms like *fire*, *damaged*, *delivery*, *purchase* receive the **highest IDF** (0.602), while corpus-ubiquitous terms like *gold* and *arrived* receive the **lowest** (0.125). This is precisely how IR systems devalue non-discriminative vocabulary.

---

### Step 4 — Construct the TF-IDF Weighted Matrix

Compute $w(d, t) = tf(d, t) \times idf(t)$ cell-by-cell.

$$
\text{TF-IDF} =
\begin{bmatrix}
t & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & D_4 \\
\text{shipment} & 0.301 & 0 & 0.301 & 0 \\
\text{gold} & 0.125 & 0 & 0.125 & 0.125 \\
\text{damaged} & 0.602 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{fire} & 0.602 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{delivery} & 0 & 0.602 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{silver} & 0 & 0.602 & 0 & 0.301 \\
\text{arrived} & 0 & 0.125 & 0.125 & 0.125 \\
\text{truck} & 0 & 0.301 & 0.301 & 0 \\
\text{purchase} & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.602
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Query vector $Q$** (TF-IDF of "gold silver truck"):

$$
\vec{Q} = \big(0, \; 0.125, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0.301, \; 0, \; 0.301, \; 0\big)
$$

---

### Step 5 — Cosine Similarity Computation

Use:

$$
\text{sim}(D_j, Q) = \frac{\vec{D_j} \cdot \vec{Q}}{\vert\vec{D_j}\vert_2 \cdot \vert\vec{Q}\vert_2}
$$

**Pre-compute the query norm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\vec{Q}\vert_2 &= \sqrt{(0.125)^2 + (0.301)^2 + (0.301)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.015625 + 0.090601 + 0.090601} \\
&= \sqrt{0.196827} \\
&= 0.4437
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Document $D_1$: "shipment of gold damaged in a fire"

**Dot product** — only the *gold* term is non-zero in both $\vec{D_1}$ and $\vec{Q}$:

$$
\vec{D_1} \cdot \vec{Q} = (0.125)(0.125) = 0.015625
$$

**Norm of $D_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\vec{D_1}\vert_2 &= \sqrt{(0.301)^2 + (0.125)^2 + (0.602)^2 + (0.602)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.090601 + 0.015625 + 0.362404 + 0.362404} \\
&= \sqrt{0.831034} \\
&= 0.9116
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cosine similarity:**

$$
\text{sim}(D_1, Q) = \frac{0.015625}{0.9116 \times 0.4437} = \frac{0.015625}{0.4044} = 0.0386
$$

---

#### Document $D_2$: "delivery of silver arrived in a silver truck"

**Dot product** — overlap on *silver* and *truck*:

$$
\vec{D_2} \cdot \vec{Q} = (0.602)(0.301) + (0.301)(0.301) = 0.181202 + 0.090601 = 0.271803
$$

**Norm of $D_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\vec{D_2}\vert_2 &= \sqrt{(0.602)^2 + (0.602)^2 + (0.125)^2 + (0.301)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.362404 + 0.362404 + 0.015625 + 0.090601} \\
&= \sqrt{0.831034} \\
&= 0.9116
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cosine similarity:**

$$
\text{sim}(D_2, Q) = \frac{0.271803}{0.9116 \times 0.4437} = \frac{0.271803}{0.4044} = 0.6721
$$

---

#### Document $D_3$: "shipment of gold arrived in a truck"

**Dot product** — overlap on *gold* and *truck*:

$$
\vec{D_3} \cdot \vec{Q} = (0.125)(0.125) + (0.301)(0.301) = 0.015625 + 0.090601 = 0.106226
$$

**Norm of $D_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\vec{D_3}\vert_2 &= \sqrt{(0.301)^2 + (0.125)^2 + (0.125)^2 + (0.301)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.090601 + 0.015625 + 0.015625 + 0.090601} \\
&= \sqrt{0.212452} \\
&= 0.4610
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cosine similarity:**

$$
\text{sim}(D_3, Q) = \frac{0.106226}{0.4610 \times 0.4437} = \frac{0.106226}{0.2045} = 0.5195
$$

---

#### Document $D_4$: "purchase of gold and silver arrived"

**Dot product** — overlap on *gold* and *silver*:

$$
\vec{D_4} \cdot \vec{Q} = (0.125)(0.125) + (0.301)(0.301) = 0.015625 + 0.090601 = 0.106226
$$

**Norm of $D_4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\vec{D_4}\vert_2 &= \sqrt{(0.602)^2 + (0.125)^2 + (0.301)^2 + (0.125)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.362404 + 0.015625 + 0.090601 + 0.015625} \\
&= \sqrt{0.484255} \\
&= 0.6959
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cosine similarity:**

$$
\text{sim}(D_4, Q) = \frac{0.106226}{0.6959 \times 0.4437} = \frac{0.106226}{0.3087} = 0.3441
$$

---

### Step 6 — Final Ranking (Descending Order of Cosine Similarity)

| **Rank** | **Document** | **Cosine Similarity** |
|---|---|---|
| 🥇 **1** | $D_2$: "delivery of silver arrived in a silver truck" | $0.6721$ |
| 🥈 **2** | $D_3$: "shipment of gold arrived in a truck" | $0.5195$ |
| 🥉 **3** | $D_4$: "purchase of gold and silver arrived" | $0.3441$ |
| **4** | $D_1$: "shipment of gold damaged in a fire" | $0.0386$ |

> **Interpretation:** $D_2$ is the **most relevant** document because it shares *two query terms* (silver, truck) with high TF-IDF overlap. $D_1$ is least relevant — although it contains *gold*, the other two query terms are absent, and the document focuses on "fire" and "damaged", which are unrelated to the user's intent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IR System Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end processing pipeline** of the IR problem. It captures the data flow from raw document input to final ranked output, satisfying the KTU requirement for system-level diagrammatic representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Document Collection D1 D2 D3 D4] --> B[Tokenization Module]
    B --> C[Stop Word Removal Filter]
    C --> D[Vocabulary Builder V]
    D --> E[Term Frequency Matrix tf]
    E --> F[Document Frequency Calculator df]
    F --> G[IDF Computation idf]
    G --> H[TF-IDF Weighting Module]
    Q[User Query Q] --> B2[Query Tokenizer]
    B2 --> C2[Query Stop Word Filter]
    C2 --> H
    H --> I[Weighted Document Vectors]
    H --> J[Weighted Query Vector]
    I --> K[Cosine Similarity Engine]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Ranking Sorter Descending]
    L --> M[Final Ranked List D2 D3 D4 D1]
```

### 4.2 Vector Space Geometric Interpretation

The diagram below illustrates the **geometric intuition** of cosine similarity. The smaller the angle $\theta$ between a document vector $\vec{D_j}$ and the query vector $\vec{Q}$, the higher the relevance score.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Query Vector Q] --> C[Origin]
    B[Document Vector D2 Highest Sim] --> C
    D[Document Vector D3] --> C
    E[Document Vector D4] --> C
    F[Document Vector D1 Lowest Sim] --> C
    A -. Small Angle Theta min .-> B
    A -. Larger Angle .-> D
    A -. Even Larger Angle .-> E
    A -. Theta max approx 90 deg .-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the geometric view, $D_1$ is nearly **orthogonal** to $Q$ (angle $\approx 90°$, cosine $\approx 0$), confirming its low relevance. $D_2$ is the **most aligned** (smallest angle), confirming its top rank.

### 4.3 Term-Document Matrix Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vocabulary V with 9 terms] --> B[Document Axis D1 D2 D3 D4]
    B --> C[Raw TF Matrix 9 x 4]
    C --> D[IDF Column Vector 9 x 1]
    D --> E[Element-wise Multiplication]
    E --> F[Weighted TF-IDF Matrix 9 x 4]
    F --> G[Cosine Similarity Dot Product Norm]
    G --> H[Final Ranked Output]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]** *(3 marks)*

**Q1.** Define **Term Frequency (TF)** and **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)**. Why is IDF used in Information Retrieval?

**Model Answer:**
- **Term Frequency (TF):** The number of times a term $t$ occurs in a document $d$, denoted $tf(d, t)$.
- **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** A statistical weight that measures how important a term is across the corpus, computed as $idf(t) = \log(N / df(t))$ where $N$ is the total number of documents and $df(t)$ is the number of documents containing $t$.
- **Why IDF?** Raw TF favours common words (e.g., "the", "is"). IDF **down-weights** such corpus-frequent terms and **up-weights** rare, discriminative terms. This ensures that terms carrying more semantic information about the document's topic contribute more heavily to relevance ranking.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]** *(3 marks)*

**Q2.** What is **Cosine Similarity**? Why is it preferred over Euclidean distance for comparing document vectors?

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle $\theta$ between two non-zero vectors in a multi-dimensional term space, computed as $\text{sim}(\vec{D}, \vec{Q}) = \frac{\vec{D} \cdot \vec{Q}}{\vert\vec{D}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{Q}\vert}$.
- **Why preferred over Euclidean distance?**
  1. **Length invariance:** A longer document with the same term proportions as a shorter one is considered equally relevant. Cosine ignores magnitude; Euclidean does not.
  2. **Bounded output:** Cosine lies in $[0, 1]$, making thresholding intuitive; Euclidean is unbounded.
  3. **Sparse-vector friendly:** Most document vectors are sparse (few non-zero entries), and cosine effectively measures **directional alignment** rather than raw coordinate distance.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]** *(14 marks)*

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Worked Example

**Q(a).** For the document collection below, construct the **term-document incidence matrix** and compute the **raw term-frequency (TF) matrix** after stop-word removal.
- $D_1$: "cat dog cat mouse"
- $D_2$: "dog mouse elephant"
- $D_3$: "cat elephant dog"
- $D_4$: "mouse cat dog mouse"

**(7 marks)**

**Q(b).** For the above documents, compute the **IDF vector**, the **TF-IDF matrix**, and rank them against the query $Q$: "cat dog" using **Cosine Similarity**. Use $\log_{10}$.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q(a).** Explain the **Vector Space Model (VSM)** of Information Retrieval with a neat block diagram. List any **four advantages** and **two limitations** of VSM.

**(7 marks)**

**Q(b).** A corpus contains $N = 1000$ documents. For a term $t$ with document frequency $df(t) = 50$, compute:
1. The IDF weight using $\log_{10}$.
2. The TF-IDF weight if $tf = 4$ in document $D_7$.
3. Comment on how the weight changes if $df(t)$ drops to 5.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solutions

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Term-Document Matrix & TF Matrix**

**Step 1:** Identify stop-word-free content terms. There are no stop words in this corpus.

**Step 2:** Vocabulary $V = \{\text{cat, dog, mouse, elephant}\}$, so $m = 4$.

**Step 3:** Raw TF matrix (counting token frequencies):

$$
\text{TF} =
\begin{bmatrix}
t & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & D_4 \\
\text{cat} & 2 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
\text{dog} & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\text{mouse} & 1 & 1 & 0 & 2 \\
\text{elephant} & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The **term-document incidence matrix** (binary version, presence = 1):

$$
\text{Incidence} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\text{cat} & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
\text{dog} & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\text{mouse} & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
\text{elephant} & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**Part (b) — IDF, TF-IDF, and Cosine Ranking**

**Step 1: Compute $df(t)$ and $idf(t)$** with $N = 4$.

| Term | $df(t)$ | $idf = \log_{10}(4/df)$ |
|---|---|---|
| cat | 3 | $\log_{10}(1.333) = 0.125$ |
| dog | 4 | $\log_{10}(1.000) = 0.000$ |
| mouse | 3 | $0.125$ |
| elephant | 2 | $\log_{10}(2) = 0.301$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The term *"dog"* appears in **every** document, so $df = N = 4$, yielding $idf(\text{dog}) = 0$. This means *dog* will be **completely ignored** in the TF-IDF weighting — a powerful demonstration of IDF's discriminative power.

**Step 2: Build TF-IDF matrix** ($w = tf \times idf$):

$$
\text{TF-IDF} =
\begin{bmatrix}
t & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & D_4 \\
\text{cat} & 0.250 & 0 & 0.125 & 0.125 \\
\text{dog} & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{mouse} & 0.125 & 0.125 & 0 & 0.250 \\
\text{elephant} & 0 & 0.301 & 0.301 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Query vector** for $Q = $ "cat dog". After removing the zero-weighted *dog*:

$$
\vec{Q} = (0.125, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0) \quad \text{(non-zero only on cat)}
$$

**Step 4: Cosine similarities** (only *cat* contributes to dot products):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{D_1} \cdot \vec{Q} &= (0.250)(0.125) = 0.03125 \\
\vert\vec{D_1}\vert_2 &= \sqrt{0.250^2 + 0.125^2} = \sqrt{0.0781} = 0.2795 \\
\text{sim}(D_1, Q) &= \frac{0.03125}{0.2795 \times 0.125} = \frac{0.03125}{0.03494} = 0.8944
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{D_3} \cdot \vec{Q} &= (0.125)(0.125) = 0.015625 \\
\vert\vec{D_3}\vert_2 &= \sqrt{0.125^2 + 0.301^2} = \sqrt{0.1062} = 0.3259 \\
\text{sim}(D_3, Q) &= \frac{0.015625}{0.3259 \times 0.125} = \frac{0.015625}{0.04074} = 0.3835
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{D_4} \cdot \vec{Q} &= (0.125)(0.125) = 0.015625 \\
\vert\vec{D_4}\vert_2 &= \sqrt{0.125^2 + 0.125^2 + 0.250^2} = \sqrt{0.09375} = 0.3062 \\
\text{sim}(D_4, Q) &= \frac{0.015625}{0.3062 \times 0.125} = \frac{0.015625}{0.03828} = 0.4082
\end{aligned}
$$

$D_2$ has zero overlap with $Q$, so $\text{sim}(D_2, Q) = 0$.

**Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_1$ | $0.8944$ |
| 2 | $D_4$ | $0.4082$ |
| 3 | $D_3$ | $0.3835$ |
| 4 | $D_2$ | $0.0000$ |

**Conclusion:** $D_1$ is most relevant because it has the highest *cat* count (2) and lacks distracting elephant terms. $D_2$ is least relevant — it has zero *cat* content.

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Vector Space Model Explanation**

The **Vector Space Model (VSM)** represents each document $D_j$ and the user query $Q$ as vectors in an $m$-dimensional space, where $m$ is the size of the vocabulary. Each dimension corresponds to a unique term, and the coordinate value is the term's **TF-IDF weight** in that document.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Documents D1 D2 ... Dn] --> B[Preprocessing Tokenization Stop-word Removal Stemming]
    Q[User Query Q] --> B
    B --> C[Vocabulary Construction]
    C --> D[Indexing Engine]
    D --> E[TF-IDF Weight Calculator]
    E --> F[Vector Representation in m-dim Space]
    F --> G[Similarity Module Cosine]
    G --> H[Ranked Retrieval Output]
```

**Four Advantages of VSM:**
1. **Simple geometric interpretation** — relevance = angle between vectors.
2. **Partial matching** — documents matching *some* query terms receive partial credit (unlike Boolean IR).
3. **Term-weighting flexibility** — supports TF, IDF, TF-IDF, and custom schemes.
4. **Efficient ranking** — once vectors are precomputed, ranking is a single matrix-vector product.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Assumes term independence** — ignores semantic relationships (e.g., synonyms, polysemy).
2. **High dimensionality** — vocabulary can contain 100,000+ terms, making vectors extremely sparse and storage expensive.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

Given: $N = 1000$, $df(t) = 50$, $tf = 4$ (in $D_7$).

**1. IDF weight:**

$$
idf(t) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{df(t)}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{1000}{50}\right) = \log_{10}(20) = 1.301
$$

**2. TF-IDF weight in $D_7$:**

$$
w(D_7, t) = tf \times idf = 4 \times 1.301 = 5.204
$$

**3. Effect of dropping $df(t)$ to 5:**

$$
idf_{\text{new}}(t) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{1000}{5}\right) = \log_{10}(200) = 2.301
$$

$$
w_{\text{new}}(D_7, t) = 4 \times 2.301 = 9.204
$$

**Comment:** When $df(t)$ drops from 50 to 5, the term becomes **ten times rarer** in the corpus. The IDF weight nearly **doubles** (from 1.301 to 2.301), and consequently the TF-IDF weight also increases by **~77%** (from 5.204 to 9.204). This illustrates a fundamental IR principle: **rarer terms carry more discriminative power** and contribute more strongly to relevance ranking.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in IR Problems (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting stop-word removal** — many students retain "of", "the", "a" in the vocabulary, inflating $|V|$ and shifting the entire TF matrix. **Penalty: −2 marks**.
> 2. **Using the wrong logarithm base** — KTU explicitly uses $\log_{10}$. Using $\ln$ (natural log) without justification leads to numerical mismatches. **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the squared-norm calculation** — students often write $\text{sim} = \frac{\vec{D} \cdot \vec{Q}}{\vert\vec{D}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{Q}\vert}$ without expanding the denominator. Examiners expect to see the explicit $\sqrt{\sum w_i^2}$ expansion. **Penalty: −2 marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring the case $tf = 0$** — a term absent from a document yields a TF-IDF weight of 0, contributing **nothing** to the dot product. Students sometimes incorrectly add it. **Penalty: −1 mark**.
> 5. **Failing to sort in descending order** — the final ranking must be **explicitly listed**, not just computed. **Penalty: −1 mark**.
> 6. **Not interpreting the result** — top KTU answers include a one-line interpretation of *why* $D_2$ beats $D_3$, etc. **Penalty: −1 mark**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- ✅ **IR = Information Retrieval** — the discipline of finding relevant documents for a query in a large corpus.
- ✅ **Bag-of-Words assumption** — word order is discarded; only term presence/frequency matters.
- ✅ **Stop-word removal** is mandatory before vectorization. Standard list: $\{a, an, the, of, in, and, is, are, was, were\}$.
- ✅ **TF** = raw count of term in document (no division by document length in the basic KTU model).
- ✅ **IDF** $= \log_{10}(N / df)$ — penalizes common terms, rewards rare ones.
- ✅ **TF-IDF** $= tf \times idf$ — combined weighting scheme.
- ✅ **Cosine Similarity** $= \frac{\vec{D} \cdot \vec{Q}}{\vert\vec{D}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{Q}\vert}$ — range $[0, 1]$; **higher is more relevant**.
- ✅ **Log base** = **10** in KTU; document the base in your answer.
- ✅ **Vocabulary $V$** is the **union** of all content terms across all documents and the query.
- ✅ **A term with $idf = 0$** (appears in every document) is **completely ignored** in TF-IDF.
- ✅ **Ranking must be in descending order** of cosine similarity.
- ✅ **Final step of every IR problem**: provide the **ranked list** with similarity scores.
- ✅ **Vector Space Model** advantages: simple, partial matching, flexible weights.
- ✅ **VSM limitations**: term independence assumption; high-dimensional sparse vectors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverted Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inverted Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Inverted Index** is a fundamental data structure in **Information Retrieval (IR)** systems that maps each unique term (word/token) appearing in a corpus to a list of documents (and positions) in which that term occurs. It is the structural backbone of modern search engines such as Google, Bing, and Elasticsearch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An inverted index is a mapping from terms to their locations in a document collection. It consists of a vocabulary (dictionary) of terms and, for each term, a postings list identifying the documents containing the term.*

**Key Terminology (High-Frequency KTU Board Terms):**

- **Term (Token):** A normalized word extracted from documents after preprocessing.
- **Vocabulary (Dictionary):** The set of all unique terms in the corpus.
- **Postings List:** For each term, the list of document IDs where the term appears.
- **Posting:** A single record within the postings list containing the DocID and optionally term frequency / positional information.
- **Document Frequency (DF):** The number of documents in the collection that contain a given term.
- **Term Frequency (TF):** The number of times a term appears in a given document.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of an **Inverted Index** as the **index page at the back of a textbook**.

> Imagine you have a 1,000-page engineering textbook. Instead of scanning every page to find where "Fourier Transform" is mentioned, you flip to the back of the book and find the entry:
>
> **Fourier Transform** → pp. 42, 87, 156, 311, 502
>
> The book **"inverts"** the relationship — instead of going from page → topic, you go from topic → pages.

| Analogy Element | Maps to Inverted Index Component |
|---|---|
| Topic heading | **Term** |
| List of page numbers | **Postings List** |
| Alphabetical topic list | **Vocabulary / Dictionary** |
| Total number of unique topics | **Vocabulary Size** |

### 1.3 Why We Need an Inverted Index

A naive **forward index** (document → words) requires scanning every document for every query, which is **O(N × M)** where N is number of documents and M is vocabulary size. An **inverted index** flips this to **O(query terms × average postings length)**, enabling millisecond-level search across billions of documents.

> [!NOTE]
> The terms "Inverted File", "Postings File", and "Inverted Index" are used interchangeably in KTU examination questions. Treat them as synonymous.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Components of an Inverted Index

The inverted index has two primary components:

**1. The Dictionary (Vocabulary):**
- Stores all unique terms found in the corpus.
- Usually implemented as a **sorted array**, **B-tree**, or **Hash Map** for O(1) or O(log V) lookup.
- May store metadata: document frequency, pointer to postings list, term ID.

**2. The Postings List (Inverted File):**
- For each term, an ordered list of (DocID, TF, [Positions]) tuples.
- DocIDs are typically stored in **ascending order** to enable efficient compression using **gap encoding** (d-gaps).
- Supports Boolean queries (AND, OR, NOT) via **list intersection / union** operations.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

1. **Document Collection Acquisition:** Gather all documents $D = \{d_1, d_2, ..., d_N\}$.
2. **Tokenization:** Split each document into raw tokens using delimiters (spaces, punctuation).
3. **Normalization:** Apply lowercasing, stemming (Porter, Lancaster), lemmatization, and stop-word removal.
4. **Term Extraction:** Build local (per-document) term lists with frequency counts.
5. **Merging Phase:** Combine all local term lists into a **global sorted dictionary** using an external merge-sort based on term.
6. **Postings Assembly:** For each term, merge the per-document postings into a single global postings list.
7. **Compression (Optional but KTU-Relevant):** Apply gap encoding, variable-byte encoding, or **Golomb-Rice coding** to reduce storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boolean Query Evaluation:** To process "term1 AND term2", fetch the postings lists of both terms and compute the **set intersection**. KTU examiners frequently test this with short examples.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Term Frequency (Raw) | $tf(t,d) = \text{count of } t \text{ in } d$ | Basic frequency measure |
| 2 | Normalized TF | $tf_{norm}(t,d) = \dfrac{tf(t,d)}{\max_{w \in d} tf(w,d)}$ | Document length normalization |
| 3 | Inverse Document Frequency | $idf(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{df(t) + 1}$ | Weighs rare terms higher |
| 4 | TF-IDF Weight | $w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \times idf(t)$ | Standard retrieval scoring |
| 5 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}$ | Ranking query-doc relevance |
| 6 | BM25 Score | $\text{BM25}(t,d) = idf(t) \cdot \dfrac{tf(t,d) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{tf(t,d) + k_1 \cdot (1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl})}$ | Modern ranking function |
| 7 | Zipf's Law (Vocabulary) | $f(r) \propto \dfrac{1}{r^{\alpha}}$ where $\alpha \approx 1$ | Vocabulary distribution modeling |
| 8 | Heap's Law (Corpus Size) | $\vert V \vert = K \cdot N^{\beta}$, typically $K \in [30,100], \beta \approx 0.5$ | Estimating vocabulary growth |
| 9 | Boolean AND Complexity | $O(\vert P_x \vert + \vert P_y \vert)$ | Postings intersection cost |
| 10 | Gap Encoding (Compression) | $gap_i = DocID_i - DocID_{i-1}$ | Compresses sorted DocIDs |

> **Where $N$** = total number of documents, **$df(t)$** = document frequency of term $t$, **$avgdl$** = average document length, **$k_1, b$** = BM25 tuning parameters (typically $k_1 = 1.2$, $b = 0.75$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Core data structure for web-scale search.
- **Database Systems:** Used in full-text search engines like **Elasticsearch**, **Apache Lucene**, and **Solr**.
- **Digital Libraries:** Powers academic search (PubMed, IEEE Xplore, Google Scholar).
- **E-Commerce:** Product search on Amazon, Flipkart, etc.
- **Log Analytics & SIEM:** Cybersecurity tools (Splunk) use inverted indexes for fast log querying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Building an Inverted Index from Scratch

**Given Document Collection (Corpus):**

$$D_1: \text{"data science is fun"}$$
$$D_2: \text{"machine learning is fun"}$$
$$D_3: \text{"data is big data"}$$

**Step 1 — Tokenization & Normalization:**

After lowercasing and tokenizing (no stop-word removal yet for clarity):

| Document | Token Stream |
|---|---|
| $D_1$ | data, science, is, fun |
| $D_2$ | machine, learning, is, fun |
| $D_3$ | data, is, big, data |

**Step 2 — Build Local (Per-Document) Term Lists with Frequencies:**

For $D_1$: data→1, science→1, is→1, fun→1
For $D_2$: machine→1, learning→1, is→1, fun→1
For $D_3$: data→2, is→1, big→1

**Step 3 — Merge into Global Dictionary & Postings:**

After sorting terms alphabetically, we obtain the inverted index:

| Term (Dictionary) | Postings List: (DocID, TF) |
|---|---|
| big | $(3, 1)$ |
| data | $(1, 1), (3, 2)$ |
| fun | $(1, 1), (2, 1)$ |
| is | $(1, 1), (2, 1), (3, 1)$ |
| learning | $(2, 1)$ |
| machine | $(2, 1)$ |
| science | $(1, 1)$ |

**Vocabulary size $\vert V \vert = 7$** unique terms.

### 3.2 Exhaustive TF-IDF Derivation

We compute the **TF-IDF weight** for each term in $D_1$ against the corpus of $N = 3$ documents.

**Step 1 — Compute Document Frequencies from the index:**

$$df(\text{data}) = 2, \quad df(\text{science}) = 1, \quad df(\text{is}) = 3, \quad df(\text{fun}) = 2$$

**Step 2 — Compute IDF using the standard log formula:**

$$idf(t) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{df(t)}\right)$$

Applying this term-by-term:

$$idf(\text{data}) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.1761$$

$$idf(\text{science}) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{1}\right) = \log_{10}(3) \approx 0.4771$$

$$idf(\text{is}) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{3}\right) = \log_{10}(1) = 0$$

$$idf(\text{fun}) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.1761$$

**Step 3 — Compute TF for $D_1$:**

Each term appears **once** in $D_1$ and $D_1$ has **4 terms**, so:

$$tf(t, D_1) = 1 \quad \text{for all } t \in D_1$$

The **total** $tf$ in $D_1$ = 4 (we can also use the normalized form $tf_{norm}(t, D_1) = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25$ for each term).

**Step 4 — Multiply to Get TF-IDF Weights:**

$$w(t, D_1) = tf(t, D_1) \times idf(t)$$

Computing each:

$$w(\text{data}, D_1) = 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761$$

$$w(\text{science}, D_1) = 1 \times 0.4771 = 0.4771$$

$$w(\text{is}, D_1) = 1 \times 0 = 0$$

$$w(\text{fun}, D_1) = 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761$$

**Step 5 — Construct the TF-IDF Vector for $D_1$:**

Ordered by vocabulary [big, data, fun, is, learning, machine, science]:

$$\vec{D_1} = [0, \; 0.1761, \; 0.1761, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0.4771]$$

### 3.3 Cosine Similarity Derivation for a Sample Query

**Query:** $Q = \text{"data science"}$ (assumed unweighted raw $tf$ of 1 for each).

After same IDF computation, the **query vector** becomes:

$$\vec{Q} = [0, \; 0.1761, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0.4771]$$

**Compute the dot product $\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_1}$:**

$$\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D_1} = (0 \times 0) + (0.1761 \times 0.1761) + (0 \times 0.1761) + (0 \times 0) + (0 \times 0) + (0 \times 0) + (0.4771 \times 0.4771)$$

$$= 0.03102 + 0.22762 = 0.25864$$

**Compute the magnitudes:**

$$\vert \vec{Q} \vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.03102 + 0.22762} = \sqrt{0.25864} \approx 0.5086$$

$$\vert \vec{D_1} \vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.03102 + 0.03102 + 0.22762} = \sqrt{0.28966} \approx 0.5382$$

**Compute cosine similarity:**

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{0.25864}{0.5086 \times 0.5382} = \frac{0.25864}{0.27373} \approx 0.9449$$

> **Conclusion:** $D_1$ has a **94.49%** similarity to the query "data science" — a high relevance score, confirming that the inverted index + TF-IDF + cosine similarity pipeline correctly retrieves the most relevant document.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Forward Index | Inverted Index |
|---|---|---|
| Mapping Direction | Document → Terms | Term → Documents |
| Best For | Document clustering, classification | **Search & retrieval (querying)** |
| Lookup Complexity | $O(M)$ per document scan | $O(1)$ dictionary + $O(P)$ postings scan |
| Storage Pattern | Sequential by document | Sequential by term |
| Boolean Queries | Inefficient | **Highly efficient (intersection)** |
| Used In | Authoring tools, content management | **Search engines, IR systems** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Inverted Index Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Document Corpus D1 to DN] --> B[Tokenizer and Normalizer]
    B --> C[Local Term Lists per Document]
    C --> D[Merge and Sort Phase]
    D --> E1[Dictionary Vocabulary]
    D --> E2[Postings Lists]
    E2 --> F1[DocID and TF tuples]
    E2 --> F2[Positional Info optional]
    E2 --> F3[Compressed Gap Encoded]
    E1 --> G[Query Processor]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Boolean AND OR NOT ops]
    G --> I[TF IDF Scoring]
    G --> J[Cosine Similarity Ranker]
    H --> K[Ranked Results]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow: Query Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[User Query Input] --> P2[Query Tokenization]
    P2 --> P3[Lookup in Dictionary]
    P3 --> P4{Term Found?}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Fetch Postings List]
    P4 -->|No| P6[Empty Result]
    P5 --> P7[Apply Boolean Operators]
    P7 --> P8[Compute TF IDF Scores]
    P8 --> P9[Rank Documents by Cosine Similarity]
    P9 --> P10[Return Top K Results]
    P6 --> P10
```

### 4.3 Memory Layout of an Inverted Index Entry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Term: data] --> B[Doc Frequency: 2]
    A --> C[Pointer to Postings]
    C --> D1[Posting 1: DocID 1 TF 1]
    C --> D2[Posting 2: DocID 3 TF 2]
    C --> D3[Posting 3: Gap 2 TF 2 compressed]
    D1 --> E[Next Term Entry]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

### 4.4 Construction Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Stage 1 Tokenization] --> S2[Stage 2 Stop word Removal]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Stemming]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Local Indexing per Document]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Global Merge Sort by Term]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Postings List Assembly]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 Compression Gap Encoding]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8 Final Inverted Index on Disk]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an Inverted Index. List its two main components and briefly explain each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Inverted Index** is a data structure used in information retrieval that maps each unique term in a document collection to the list of documents in which it appears. It enables fast full-text search by avoiding a full scan of the corpus.

**Two Main Components:**

1. **Dictionary (Vocabulary):** A sorted list of all unique terms (tokens) extracted from the corpus after preprocessing (tokenization, normalization, stop-word removal). It enables $O(1)$ or $O(\log V)$ lookup of any term.

2. **Postings List (Inverted File):** For each term in the dictionary, a list of postings — each containing the **DocID**, the **term frequency**, and optionally **positional offsets**. DocIDs are typically stored in ascending order to support fast **Boolean AND/OR** operations and **gap encoding compression**.

> *Examiner's note: A clear definition + listing both components + one-line explanation each = full 3 marks. Avoid vague answers like "it stores words".*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is an Inverted Index preferred over a Forward Index for search operations? Give two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Forward Index** maps `Document → Terms` (suitable for indexing new content), while an **Inverted Index** maps `Term → Documents` (suitable for answering search queries).

**Two Reasons:**

1. **Query Time Efficiency:** A Boolean query like "machine learning" requires intersecting the postings lists of two terms. With an inverted index, this is $O(\vert P_x \vert + \vert P_y \vert)$. With a forward index, we would scan all $N$ documents — $O(N \times M)$.

2. **Storage of Posting Metadata:** Inverted indexes store **term frequency, document frequency, and positional data** per term, enabling **TF-IDF scoring, phrase queries, and proximity ranking** — none of which are directly supported by a forward index.

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module-Level Internal Choice** — Answer **ANY ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Consider the following three documents:
- $D_1$: "**information retrieval is powerful**"
- $D_2$: "**data mining and information retrieval**"
- $D_3$: "**machine learning powers retrieval systems**"

Construct the **Inverted Index** for this corpus. Clearly show the dictionary, postings lists, and document frequencies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the index built in part (a), compute the **TF-IDF weight vector** for the query $Q = \text{"information retrieval"}$ using the formula $w(t, d) = tf(t, d) \times \log(N / df(t))$. Identify which document is most relevant to the query. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Inverted Index Construction**

**Step 1 — Tokenize** (lowercased):

$D_1$: information, retrieval, is, powerful
$D_2$: data, mining, and, information, retrieval
$D_3$: machine, learning, powers, retrieval, systems

**Step 2 — Local term frequencies:**

| Document | Terms & TFs |
|---|---|
| $D_1$ | information→1, retrieval→1, is→1, powerful→1 |
| $D_2$ | data→1, mining→1, and→1, information→1, retrieval→1 |
| $D_3$ | machine→1, learning→1, powers→1, retrieval→1, systems→1 |

**Step 3 — Global Inverted Index (sorted):**

| Term | Document Frequency (DF) | Postings List: (DocID, TF) |
|---|---|---|
| and | 1 | (2, 1) |
| data | 1 | (2, 1) |
| information | 2 | (1, 1), (2, 1) |
| is | 1 | (1, 1) |
| learning | 1 | (3, 1) |
| machine | 1 | (3, 1) |
| mining | 1 | (2, 1) |
| powerful | 1 | (1, 1) |
| powers | 1 | (3, 1) |
| retrieval | 3 | (1, 1), (2, 1), (3, 1) |
| systems | 1 | (3, 1) |

**Vocabulary size $\vert V \vert = 11$, Total documents $N = 3$.**

> *Examiner's tip: Award full 7 marks only if dictionary, postings, and document frequencies are all correctly tabulated with ascending DocIDs.*

##### **Part (b) — TF-IDF Vector for Query Q**

**Step 1 — Identify query terms and their DFs:**

Query: $Q = $ "information retrieval"
- $df(\text{information}) = 2$
- $df(\text{retrieval}) = 3$

**Step 2 — Compute IDF values:**

$$idf(\text{information}) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{df}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \approx 0.1761$$

$$idf(\text{retrieval}) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{3}\right) = \log_{10}(1) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute TF-IDF weights for the query:**

Assuming each query term appears once:

$$w(\text{information}, Q) = 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761$$

$$w(\text{retrieval}, Q) = 1 \times 0 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Identify most relevant document by document term frequencies:**

- $D_1$ contains "information" (TF=1) and "retrieval" (TF=1) → **both query terms present**.
- $D_2$ contains "information" (TF=1) and "retrieval" (TF=1) → **both query terms present**.
- $D_3$ contains only "retrieval" (TF=1) → only one query term.

**Step 5 — Compute combined score per document (sum of TF-IDF):**

For $D_1$: score = $(1 \times 0.1761) + (1 \times 0) = 0.1761$
For $D_2$: score = $(1 \times 0.1761) + (1 \times 0) = 0.1761$
For $D_3$: score = $0 + (1 \times 0) = 0$

> **Most Relevant Documents:** $D_1$ and $D_2$ tie at score **0.1761**, both matching both query terms.

> *Examiner's tip: A tie-break using cosine similarity is acceptable for full marks. Students often forget to normalize and lose 1 mark.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **block-level architecture** of an inverted index construction pipeline, clearly describing the stages: **tokenization, normalization, local indexing, global merging, and postings compression**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using **Heap's Law** $\vert V \vert = K \cdot N^{\beta}$ with $K = 50$ and $\beta = 0.5$, estimate the vocabulary size for a corpus of $N = 10{,}000$ documents. State two practical limitations of inverted indexes in real-world deployments. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Block Architecture of Inverted Index Construction**

An inverted index is built through a **five-stage pipeline**:

**Stage 1 — Tokenization:**
The raw document text is split into individual tokens (terms) using whitespace, punctuation, and language-specific rules. Example: "The cat sat." → [The, cat, sat]. This is the first boundary between unstructured text and structured data.

**Stage 2 — Normalization:**
Tokens are transformed to a canonical form: lowercasing, stemming (Porter stemmer reduces "running" → "run"), lemmatization, and stop-word removal ("the", "is", "and" are discarded). This reduces vocabulary size and improves recall.

**Stage 3 — Local Indexing (Per-Document):**
For each document, a small in-memory **local inverted index** is constructed: a hash map of `term → (DocID, TF)`. This exploits bounded memory per document and allows parallel construction using **MapReduce**.

**Stage 4 — Global Merging (External Sort):**
All local indexes are merged using an **external merge-sort** algorithm. The final global dictionary is sorted lexicographically, and postings lists are **concatenated in DocID order**. The merge step is the most I/O-intensive stage.

**Stage 5 — Postings Compression:**
Because DocIDs in postings lists are monotonically increasing, we apply **gap encoding** ($gap_i = DocID_i - DocID_{i-1}$), then encode gaps using **variable-byte** or **Golomb-Rice** codes. This typically yields **70–90% space savings** on large corpora.

> *Examiner's tip: A clear diagram or stage-wise bullets = full 7 marks. Avoid mixing up stages.*

##### **Part (b) — Heap's Law Estimation and Limitations**

**Step 1 — Apply Heap's Law:**

$$|V| = K \cdot N^{\beta} = 50 \cdot (10{,}000)^{0.5}$$

Compute the exponent first:

$$N^{\beta} = (10{,}000)^{0.5} = \sqrt{10{,}000} = 100$$

Multiply by $K$:

$$|V| = 50 \times 100 = 5{,}000 \text{ unique terms}$$

> **Estimated vocabulary size: 5,000 terms.**

**Step 2 — Two Practical Limitations of Inverted Indexes:**

1. **Difficult Dynamic Updates:** Inverted indexes are optimized for **read-heavy, write-rarely** workloads. Frequent insertions and deletions require **rebuilding the postings lists** and re-sorting DocIDs, which is computationally expensive. This is why systems like Elasticsearch use **segment-based merging** to amortize the cost.

2. **Memory Footprint for Large Vocabularies:** For massive corpora (web-scale: billions of documents), the dictionary and postings can consume **hundreds of GBs**. While compression mitigates this, **Boolean query processing** on long postings lists still requires substantial RAM, and the dictionary itself must remain in-memory for fast lookups.

> *Examiner's tip: Mentioning both storage and update limitations = full marks. Bonus credit if you mention phrase query overhead.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort the dictionary:** Examiners deduct **1 mark** if vocabulary is not in alphabetical order.
> 2. **Confusing TF with DF:** Term Frequency (per document) ≠ Document Frequency (across corpus). This is the **#1 conceptual error** flagged in KTU answer scripts.
> 3. **Skipping the $\log$ base specification:** Always state whether you used $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ in IDF — inconsistency costs marks.
> 4. **Not normalizing the query vector in cosine similarity:** Raw dot product is not cosine similarity. Normalization is mandatory.
> 5. **Writing postings in unsorted DocID order:** Postings MUST be in **ascending DocID order** to support efficient intersection and gap encoding.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inverted Index = Term → Postings List** (the opposite of a forward index).
- **Two Components:** **Dictionary (Vocabulary)** + **Postings List (Inverted File)**.
- **Postings entries** typically contain **(DocID, TF, [Positions])** in ascending DocID order.
- **TF-IDF** = $tf(t,d) \times \log(N / df(t))$ — rare terms get higher weight.
- **Cosine Similarity** = $\dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}$ — used for ranking retrieved documents.
- **Boolean queries** (AND, OR, NOT) are processed by **intersecting/unioning postings lists** in $O(|P_x| + |P_y|)$.
- **Construction stages:** Tokenize → Normalize → Local Index → Global Merge → Compress.
- **Compression techniques:** Gap encoding + Variable-byte / Golomb-Rice codes (saves 70–90% space).
- **Heap's Law:** $|V| = K \cdot N^{\beta}$ estimates vocabulary growth ($K \approx 30\text{–}100$, $\beta \approx 0.5$).
- **Zipf's Law:** Term frequency $\propto 1/\text{rank}$ — used to model and optimize index storage.
- **Limitation:** Dynamic updates are expensive; optimized for **read-mostly** workloads.
- **Real-world users:** Google, Elasticsearch, Lucene, Solr, Splunk, PubMed, Amazon search.
- **KTU Must-Know Terms:** Token, Stemming, Lemmatization, Stop-word, Gap encoding, Postings, Vocabulary size.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing Boolean Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Processing Boolean Queries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boolean Query Processing** is the foundational operational paradigm of the **Classical Boolean Retrieval Model** in Information Retrieval (IR), wherein a user expresses an information need as a syntactic composition of **search terms** connected by **Boolean operators** — namely $\text{AND}$ ($\wedge$), $\text{OR}$ ($\vee$), and $\text{NOT}$ ($\neg$) — and the retrieval system returns exactly those documents in the corpus whose **logical evaluation against the query yields TRUE**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCDT503 – Data Analytics, Module 4), processing a Boolean query is decomposed into two primary engineering sub-tasks:

1. **Indexing Phase (Offline)**: Constructing an **Inverted Index** — a data structure that maps each unique **term** $t$ to a sorted list of **postings** $\langle d, f_{t,d} \rangle$, where $d$ is the document ID and $f_{t,d}$ is the term frequency in that document.
2. **Query Phase (Online)**: Translating the Boolean expression into a **disjunctive normal form (DNF)** or evaluating it via a **query tree** using postings list intersection ($\text{AND}$), union ($\text{OR}$), and difference ($\text{NOT}$) operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary):**
> - **Corpus $\mathcal{C}$** — the entire collection of $N$ documents: $\mathcal{C} = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}$
> - **Vocabulary $V$** — the set of all unique index terms: $V = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_M\}$
> - **Postings List $P(t)$** — the sorted list of document IDs containing term $t$
> - **Document Frequency $df_t$** — the number of documents in which term $t$ appears
> - **Incidence Matrix $M$** — a binary $|V| \times |\mathcal{C}|$ matrix where $M[t, d] = 1$ if term $t$ exists in document $d$, else $0$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Library Analogy for Intuitive Understanding**
>
> Imagine a massive library with **10 million books** and a patron who walks up to the librarian and asks: *"Find me all books that contain the words 'Machine' AND 'Learning' but NOT 'Python'."*
>
> The naive approach would be for the librarian to physically scan every single book, open it, and check whether it contains all three words. This is the **brute-force linear scan** — and at 10 million books, it would take an absurd amount of time (perhaps **years**).
>
> Instead, the smart librarian has already built, in the back room, a **massive set of index cards** — one card per unique word — and on each card is a sorted list of every book's call number that contains that word. So when the patron asks for the query, the librarian simply:
>
> 1. Pulls out the card for **"Machine"** → gets a sorted list of book IDs.
> 2. Pulls out the card for **"Learning"** → gets another sorted list.
> 3. Pulls out the card for **"Python"** → gets a third list.
> 4. Performs a **merge algorithm** on lists 1 and 2 (intersection — books on BOTH cards).
> 5. Performs a **set difference** between the result and list 3.
>
> This entire query is resolved in **fractions of a second**.
>
> **This "back room of index cards" is the Inverted Index. The merge process is the Boolean Query Processor.**

## 1.3 Why Boolean Retrieval Remains Foundational

Despite the rise of ranked retrieval models (TF-IDF, BM25, vector space, neural), Boolean retrieval is **still widely deployed** in:

- **Legal e-discovery** systems (precision is paramount; recall is secondary)
- **Enterprise search** (where expert users prefer precise, controlled operators)
- **Patent search** (where conjunctive queries are the norm)
- **PubMed and biomedical literature** (where AND/OR/NOT operators are exposed in the UI)
- **Library OPAC systems**

The **speed advantage** is dramatic: for a typical 1-million-document corpus with vocabulary $\vert V \vert \approx 500{,}000$, the **inverted index lookup is $O(1)$ per term**, and a two-term **AND** query is resolved in $O(\vert P(t_1) \vert + \vert P(t_2) \vert)$ time — versus $O(N \cdot \vert V \vert)$ for the linear scan.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Data Structures

The Boolean retrieval system rests on **three canonical data structures**, each with a distinct space-time tradeoff:

### 2.1.1 Document-Term Incidence Matrix

A binary matrix $M \in \{0, 1\}^{\vert V \vert \times \vert \mathcal{C} \vert}$ where:

$$M[i, j] = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if term } t_i \text{ occurs in document } d_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Space complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{C} \vert)$ bits.

For the KTU-standard example: with $\vert V \vert = 1{,}000{,}000$ terms and $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert = 1{,}000{,}000$ documents, the matrix requires $10^{12}$ bits $\approx$ **125 GB** — **completely impractical**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse the incidence matrix (used for conceptual explanation) with the actual storage format (inverted index). The matrix is **never** built in production systems.

### 2.1.2 Inverted Index

The **production-grade data structure**. It is a dictionary (hashmap or B-tree) mapping each term to its **postings list**.

For each term $t \in V$:

$$P(t) = \langle d_1, d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_{df_t} \rangle \quad \text{with} \quad d_1 < d_2 < d_3 < \ldots < d_{df_t}$$

A typical postings entry in the full schema is:

$$\text{posting} = \langle \text{docID}, \; f_{t,d}, \; \langle p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{f_{t,d}} \rangle \rangle$$

where $f_{t,d}$ is the in-document term frequency and the $p_i$ are the **positional offsets** (used for phrase queries and proximity operators).

**Space complexity:** $O(\sum_{t \in V} df_t) = O(T)$, where $T$ is the total number of tokens in the corpus. With positional information, it scales as $O(T \cdot c)$ where $c \approx 1.2$–$1.5$ is the empirical compression overhead.

### 2.1.3 Inverted Index with Skip Pointers

To accelerate merge operations, **skip pointers** are inserted at regular intervals $\sqrt{\vert P(t) \vert}$ within each postings list. This yields **sub-linear** query time for **AND** operations.

## 2.2 Boolean Algebra for Query Processing

The query processor treats each postings list as a **set of document IDs** and applies standard Boolean set operations:

| Operator | Symbol | Set Operation | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conjunction (both required) | $\text{AND}$ | Intersection | $P(t_1) \cap P(t_2)$ |
| Disjunction (either required) | $\text{OR}$ | Union | $P(t_1) \cup P(t_2)$ |
| Negation (must not contain) | $\text{NOT}$ | Set Difference | $P(t_1) \setminus P(t_2)$ |

For the **complement operator**, we define the universe:

$$U = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, N\}$$

so that:

$$\text{NOT}(t) = U \setminus P(t)$$

The cardinality obeys:

$$\vert U \setminus P(t) \vert = N - df_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operator Precedence (KTU Standard):** $\text{NOT} \succ \text{AND} \succ \text{OR}$. Parentheses always override precedence.

## 2.3 The Query Processing Pipeline

The **end-to-end Boolean query processor** executes the following ordered pipeline:

1. **Lexical Analysis (Tokenization)** — Split the query string into tokens and operators.
2. **Normalization** — Apply case-folding, stemming (Porter or Krovetz), and stopword removal.
3. **Parse Tree Construction** — Build an abstract syntax tree (AST) respecting operator precedence.
4. **Tree Transformation** — Convert to a normalized form (e.g., **DNF** via the Shannon expansion or distributive law).
5. **Postings Lookup** — For each leaf term, retrieve $P(t)$ from the inverted index.
6. **Bottom-Up Evaluation** — Recursively apply set operations ($\cap, \cup, \setminus$) on postings lists.
7. **Result Formatting** — Sort the result set by document ID (or by relevance if scoring is layered on top).
8. **Snippets/Highlights** — For each result, generate a query-biased summary.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula, complexity bound, and statistical identity** you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Posting list size | $\vert P(t) \vert = df_t$ | documents | Sorted ascending by docID |
| 2 | Universe cardinality | $\vert U \vert = N$ | documents | Total docs in corpus |
| 3 | NOT cardinality | $\vert \text{NOT}(t) \vert = N - df_t$ | documents | Bounded by $N$ |
| 4 | AND intersection | $\vert P(t_1) \cap P(t_2) \vert \leq \min(df_{t_1}, df_{t_2})$ | documents | Exact bound |
| 5 | OR union | $\vert P(t_1) \cup P(t_2) \vert = df_{t_1} + df_{t_2} - \vert P(t_1) \cap P(t_2) \vert$ | documents | Inclusion-exclusion |
| 6 | NOT distribution | $\neg(A \wedge B) = \neg A \vee \neg B$ | logical | De Morgan's law |
| 7 | NOT distribution | $\neg(A \vee B) = \neg A \wedge \neg B$ | logical | De Morgan's law |
| 8 | Distributive law | $A \wedge (B \vee C) = (A \wedge B) \vee (A \wedge C)$ | logical | Used for DNF conversion |
| 9 | AND complexity (linear merge) | $O(\vert P(t_1) \vert + \vert P(t_2) \vert)$ | operations | Two-pointer technique |
| 10 | AND complexity (with skips) | $O(\vert P(t_1) \vert + \vert P(t_2) \vert) / \sqrt{\text{skip}}$ | operations | Optimal skip gap $\approx \sqrt{\vert P \vert}$ |
| 11 | OR complexity (linear merge) | $O(\vert P(t_1) \vert + \vert P(t_2) \vert)$ | operations | Symmetric to AND |
| 12 | NOT complexity | $O(N)$ in worst case | operations | Can use bitwise complement with bitmask |
| 13 | Index size (sparse) | $O(\sum_{t} df_t)$ | postings | Sum of document frequencies |
| 14 | Index size (dense) | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot N)$ | bits | Incidence matrix — **impractical** |
| 15 | Zipf's law | $df_t \propto 1/\text{rank}(t)^\theta$ | dimensionless | $\theta \approx 1$ empirically |
| 16 | Heaps' law | $\vert V \vert = K \cdot T^{\beta}$ | terms | $K \in [10, 100]$, $\beta \in [0.4, 0.6]$ |
| 17 | Stopword removal ratio | $r_s = 1 - \frac{\vert V_{\text{post}} \vert}{\vert V_{\text{pre}} \vert}$ | dimensionless | Typically $0.3$ to $0.5$ |
| 18 | Query latency (inverted) | $O(\sum_i \vert P(t_i) \vert)$ | time units | Linear in postings volume |
| 19 | Query latency (linear scan) | $O(N \cdot \vert Q \vert)$ | time units | Where $\vert Q \vert$ is query term count |
| 20 | Speedup factor | $S = \frac{N \cdot \vert Q \vert}{\sum_i \vert P(t_i) \vert}$ | dimensionless | Often $10^3$ to $10^6$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Boolean query processing paradigm is the **algorithmic backbone** of:

- **Search Engine Front-Ends**: Google, Bing, and DuckDuckGo all accept Boolean operators in "advanced search" mode; their core index is still a **positional inverted index**.
- **Apache Lucene / Elasticsearch**: The world's most widely deployed open-source search library implements Boolean query processing as a core operator in its query DSL.
- **Apache Solr**: Built atop Lucene; offers the `AND`, `OR`, `NOT` operators in the `q` parameter.
- **Database Full-Text Search**: PostgreSQL's `tsvector`/`tsquery` system, MySQL's `FULLTEXT` indexes, and SQLite's FTS5 module all implement Boolean query processing over inverted indexes.
- **SIEM / Log Analytics**: Splunk, Elastic Stack, and Datadog process petabytes of logs using Boolean queries to extract security events.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: The Canonical KTU Problem

We are given a **mini corpus of 5 documents** to demonstrate every operation explicitly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corpus Definition (Toy Example, Reproduce This in Exam):**

| DocID | Text (after tokenization & lowercasing) |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | "data science and machine learning" |
| $d_2$ | "machine learning algorithms for data" |
| $d_3$ | "deep learning and neural networks" |
| $d_4$ | "data mining and knowledge discovery" |
| $d_5$ | "machine learning for predictive analytics" |

**Vocabulary after stemming & stopword removal:**

$$V = \{\text{data}, \text{scienc}, \text{machin}, \text{learn}, \text{algorithm}, \text{deep}, \text{neural}, \text{network}, \text{min}, \text{knowledg}, \text{discoveri}, \text{predict}, \text{analyt}\}$$

For brevity, we work with the four most frequent terms: $V^* = \{\text{data}, \text{machin}, \text{learn}, \text{deep}\}$.

## 3.2 Construction of the Document-Term Incidence Matrix

For each term $t \in V^*$ and each document $d_j$, we evaluate $M[t, d_j]$:

$$M[\text{data}, d_1] = 1 \quad \text{(appears once)}$$
$$M[\text{data}, d_2] = 1 \quad \text{(appears once)}$$
$$M[\text{data}, d_3] = 0 \quad \text{(absent)}$$
$$M[\text{data}, d_4] = 1 \quad \text{(appears once)}$$
$$M[\text{data}, d_5] = 0 \quad \text{(absent)}$$
