$$M[\text{machin}, d_1] = 1, \; M[\text{machin}, d_2] = 1, \; M[\text{machin}, d_3] = 0, \; M[\text{machin}, d_4] = 0, \; M[\text{machin}, d_5] = 1$$

$$M[\text{learn}, d_1] = 1, \; M[\text{learn}, d_2] = 1, \; M[\text{learn}, d_3] = 1, \; M[\text{learn}, d_4] = 0, \; M[\text{learn}, d_5] = 1$$

$$M[\text{deep}, d_1] = 0, \; M[\text{deep}, d_2] = 0, \; M[\text{deep}, d_3] = 1, \; M[\text{deep}, d_4] = 0, \; M[\text{deep}, d_5] = 0$$

**The full incidence matrix is:**

$$
M = \begin{matrix}
& d_1 & d_2 & d_3 & d_4 & d_5 \\
\text{data} & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
\text{machin} & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
\text{learn} & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
\text{deep} & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{matrix}
$$

## 3.3 Construction of the Inverted Index (Postings Lists)

Reading down each column of $M$ above:

$$P(\text{data}) = \langle 1, 2, 4 \rangle, \quad df_{\text{data}} = 3$$

$$P(\text{machin}) = \langle 1, 2, 5 \rangle, \quad df_{\text{machin}} = 3$$

$$P(\text{learn}) = \langle 1, 2, 3, 5 \rangle, \quad df_{\text{learn}} = 4$$

$$P(\text{deep}) = \langle 3 \rangle, \quad df_{\text{deep}} = 1$$

**Verification using the formula:**

$$\sum_{t \in V^*} df_t = 3 + 3 + 4 + 1 = 11$$

This is the total number of (term, document) co-occurrences for $V^*$. The full index would contain this many postings.

## 3.4 Query Processing — Worked Problem 1: $\text{machin} \;\text{AND}\; \text{learn}$

**Query AST:**

$$\text{Root} \;(\wedge) \rightarrow \text{Leaf}(\text{machin}), \; \text{Leaf}(\text{learn})$$

**Algorithm:** Two-pointer linear merge on sorted postings lists.

**Input:**
$$P(\text{machin}) = \langle 1, 2, 5 \rangle, \quad P(\text{learn}) = \langle 1, 2, 3, 5 \rangle$$

**Step-by-step trace:**

| Step | $p_1$ (machin ptr) | $p_2$ (learn ptr) | Comparison | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1 | $1 = 1$ | Match! Emit $1$ to result, advance both | $\text{Result} = \{1\}$ |
| 1 | 2 | 2 | $2 = 2$ | Match! Emit $2$, advance both | $\text{Result} = \{1, 2\}$ |
| 2 | 5 | 3 | $5 > 3$ | $p_2$ smaller, advance $p_2$ | — |
| 3 | 5 | 5 | $5 = 5$ | Match! Emit $5$, advance both | $\text{Result} = \{1, 2, 5\}$ |
| 4 | end | end | — | Halt (either pointer exhausted) | — |

**Final Result:** $\{1, 2, 5\}$.

**Time complexity:** $O(\vert P(\text{machin}) \vert + \vert P(\text{learn}) \vert) = O(3 + 4) = O(7)$.

## 3.5 Query Processing — Worked Problem 2: $\text{data} \;\text{OR}\; \text{deep}$

**Input:**
$$P(\text{data}) = \langle 1, 2, 4 \rangle, \quad P(\text{deep}) = \langle 3 \rangle$$

**Step-by-step trace (two-pointer union):**

| Step | $p_1$ | $p_2$ | Comparison | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 3 | $1 < 3$ | Emit $1$, advance $p_1$ | $\{1\}$ |
| 1 | 2 | 3 | $2 < 3$ | Emit $2$, advance $p_1$ | $\{1, 2\}$ |
| 2 | 4 | 3 | $4 > 3$ | Emit $3$, advance $p_2$ | $\{1, 2, 3\}$ |
| 3 | 4 | end | — | Emit $4$, advance $p_1$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ |
| 4 | end | end | — | Halt | $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ |

**Final Result:** $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

**Verification via the formula:**

$$\vert P(\text{data}) \cup P(\text{deep}) \vert = df_{\text{data}} + df_{\text{deep}} - \vert P(\text{data}) \cap P(\text{deep}) \vert$$

$$= 3 + 1 - \vert \emptyset \vert = 3 + 1 - 0 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.6 Query Processing — Worked Problem 3: $\text{learn} \;\text{AND}\; \text{NOT}\;\text{data}$

**Input:**
$$P(\text{learn}) = \langle 1, 2, 3, 5 \rangle, \quad P(\text{data}) = \langle 1, 2, 4 \rangle$$

**Step-by-step trace (set difference via linear scan):**

| Step | $p_1$ (learn ptr) | $p_2$ (data ptr) | Comparison | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1 | $1 = 1$ | Excluded (in data) — advance $p_1$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 1 | 2 | 1 | $2 > 1$ | Advance $p_2$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 2 | 2 | 2 | $2 = 2$ | Excluded — advance $p_1$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 3 | 3 | 2 | $3 > 2$ | Advance $p_2$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 4 | 3 | 4 | $3 < 4$ | Emit $3$, advance $p_1$ | $\{3\}$ |
| 5 | 5 | 4 | $5 > 4$ | Advance $p_2$ | $\{3\}$ |
| 6 | 5 | end | — | $p_2$ exhausted — emit $5$, advance $p_1$ | $\{3, 5\}$ |
| 7 | end | end | — | Halt | $\{3, 5\}$ |

**Final Result:** $\{3, 5\}$.

**Verification:**

$$\text{NOT}(\text{data}) = U \setminus P(\text{data}) = \{1,2,3,4,5\} \setminus \{1,2,4\} = \{3, 5\}$$

$$P(\text{learn}) \cap \text{NOT}(\text{data}) = \{1,2,3,5\} \cap \{3,5\} = \{3, 5\} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.7 Complex Query — Worked Problem 4: $\text{(data OR deep)} \;\text{AND}\; \text{NOT}\;\text{machin}$

**Step 1 — Parse tree construction (respecting precedence):**

$$\text{Root} \;(\wedge) \rightarrow \text{Left: } \text{Sub-root} \;(\vee) \rightarrow [\text{data}, \text{deep}], \quad \text{Right: } \text{NOT(machin)}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the OR sub-query:**

$$P(\text{data}) \cup P(\text{deep}) = \langle 1, 2, 4 \rangle \cup \langle 3 \rangle = \langle 1, 2, 3, 4 \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the NOT sub-query:**

$$\text{NOT}(\text{machin}) = U \setminus \langle 1, 2, 5 \rangle = \{3, 4\}$$

**Step 4 — Final AND:**

$$\langle 1, 2, 3, 4 \rangle \cap \{3, 4\} = \{3, 4\}$$

**Final Result:** $\{3, 4\}$.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Optimization Techniques

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The following table is the most frequently asked 14-mark question in the Boolean retrieval module. Memorize both the technique name AND the complexity improvement.

| # | Optimization | Mechanism | Complexity Improvement | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Two-pointer merge** | Walk both sorted lists in lockstep | Baseline $O(P_1 + P_2)$ | None — always used |
| 2 | **Skip pointers** | Insert jumps at $\sqrt{P}$ intervals | $O((P_1 + P_2)/\sqrt{\text{skip\_gap}})$ | Extra pointer storage |
| 3 | **Process shortest first** | Iterate AND in order of $\vert P \vert$ ascending | Reduces total work | Requires length metadata |
| 4 | **Hash-based intersection** | Hash smaller list, probe larger | $O(P_{\text{large}})$ expected | Hash table overhead |
| 5 | **Sorted array merge** | Cache-friendly contiguous scan | Better constant factors | — |
| 6 | **Compressed postings (VB/Golomb)** | Store docID gaps with variable-length codes | 4×–8× index size reduction | Decompression CPU cost |
| 7 | **TREC-style block sorting** | Group by document, process in chunks | Better I/O locality | More complex code |
| 8 | **Boolean query rewriting** | Apply De Morgan + distributive laws | Pre-flatten tree | May explode query size |
| 9 | **Caching of frequent queries** | LRU cache on (query, results) | $O(1)$ for hot queries | Cache memory cost |
| 10 | **Bitmap complement for NOT** | Use dense bitmask when $df_t$ is high | $O(N/64)$ for NOT | Memory cost $N/8$ bytes |

## 3.9 Derivation: Optimal Skip Pointer Gap

Suppose a postings list has length $L$ and we insert skip pointers every $g$ positions. The expected number of comparisons for a merge with another list is asymptotically:

$$T(L, g) \approx \frac{L}{g} + \frac{L}{2}$$

because (i) we may need to skip through $\approx L/g$ blocks if no match exists early, and (ii) in the worst case we still walk the entire list. Minimizing with respect to $g$:

$$\frac{dT}{dg} = -\frac{L}{g^2} = 0$$

This has no interior minimum — the optimum is at the boundary. In practice, empirical analysis on real distributions shows:

$$g^* = \sqrt{L}$$

which yields a runtime of $O(2\sqrt{L})$ per list, dramatically faster than $O(L)$ for large $L$. **This is a KTU-favorite derivation; expect it in ESE.**

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Boolean Query Processing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userQ["User Boolean Query<br/>e.g. data AND learn<br/>AND NOT machin"]
    lex["Stage 1: Lexical Analysis<br/>Tokenize, lowercase, stem,<br/>remove stopwords"]
    parse["Stage 2: Parse Tree<br/>Construction<br/>Operator precedence parsing"]
    dnf["Stage 3: DNF<br/>Normalization<br/>Optional optimization"]
    lookup["Stage 4: Postings<br/>Lookup in Inverted Index<br/>Hashmap or B-tree access"]
    merge["Stage 5: Set Operations<br/>AND, OR, NOT<br/>on sorted postings"]
    skip["Skip Pointer<br/>Acceleration Module"]
    rank["Stage 6: Optional<br/>Relevance Scoring<br/>TF-IDF, BM25"]
    sort["Stage 7: Result Sorting<br/>By docID or score"]
    out["Final Result Set<br/>R = {d1, d3, d5}"]
    inv["Inverted Index<br/>(Pre-computed Offline)"]
    inv --> lookup
    userQ --> lex
    lex --> parse
    parse --> dnf
    dnf --> lookup
    lookup --> merge
    merge --> skip
    skip --> rank
    rank --> sort
    sort --> out
```

## 4.2 Parse Tree for Complex Boolean Query

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["AND<br/>∧"]
    left["OR<br/>∨"]
    right["NOT<br/>¬"]
    leafA["Leaf: data<br/>P = 1,2,4"]
    leafB["Leaf: deep<br/>P = 3"]
    leafC["Leaf: machin<br/>P = 1,2,5"]
    root --> left
    root --> right
    left --> leafA
    left --> leafB
    right --> leafC
```

## 4.3 Inverted Index Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    docs["Raw Documents<br/>d1, d2, d3, ...<br/>N total"]
    tok["Tokenizer<br/>+ Normalizer<br/>+ Stemmer<br/>+ Stopword filter"]
    termStream["Term Stream<br/>t1, t1, t2, t3, t1, ..."]
    sortStep["Sort by<br/>term, then docID"]
    invert["Group by term<br/>Build postings lists"]
    dict["Dictionary<br/>term → offset"]
    post["Postings File<br/>compressed docID gaps"]
    final["Complete<br/>Inverted Index"]
    docs --> tok
    tok --> termStream
    termStream --> sortStep
    sortStep --> invert
    invert --> dict
    invert --> post
    dict --> final
    post --> final
```

## 4.4 Two-Pointer AND Merge Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["FUNCTION AND_merge p1, p2"])
    init["Initialize i = 0, j = 0<br/>Result = empty list"]
    check{"i less than<br/>length of p1<br/>AND j less than<br/>length of p2?"}
    compare{p1[i] equals<br/>p2[j]?}
    emit1["Append p1[i] to Result<br/>i = i + 1<br/>j = j + 1"]
    compare2{p1[i] less than<br/>p2[j]?}
    adv1["i = i + 1"]
    adv2["j = j + 1"]
    returnOut["Return Result<br/>Sorted intersection set"]
    start --> init
    init --> check
    check -- "TRUE" --> compare
    check -- "FALSE" --> returnOut
    compare -- "YES" --> emit1
    compare -- "NO" --> compare2
    compare2 -- "YES" --> adv1
    compare2 -- "NO" --> adv2
    emit1 --> check
    adv1 --> check
    adv2 --> check
```

## 4.5 Skip-Pointer Accelerated Merge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["FUNCTION AND_merge_with_skips<br/>p1, skip1, p2, skip2"])
    init["i = 0, j = 0<br/>Result = empty"]
    check{"i less than p1.length<br/>AND j less than p2.length?"}
    compare{p1[i] equals<br/>p2[j]?}
    emit["Append to Result<br/>i = i + 1, j = j + 1"]
    less{p1[i] less than<br/>p2[j]?}
    trySkip1{"p1 has skip pointer<br/>at i AND<br/>p1 skip target<br/>less than equal p2j?"}
    useSkip1["i = skip target position"]
    adv1["i = i + 1"]
    adv2["j = j + 1"]
    returnOut["Return Result"]
    start --> init
    init --> check
    check -- "YES" --> compare
    check -- "NO" --> returnOut
    compare -- "EQUAL" --> emit
    compare -- "NOTEQUAL" --> less
    emit --> check
    less -- "YES" --> trySkip1
    less -- "NO" --> adv2
    trySkip1 -- "YES" --> useSkip1
    trySkip1 -- "NO" --> adv1
    useSkip1 --> check
    adv1 --> check
    adv2 --> check
```

## 4.6 Query Processing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    query["Incoming Query<br/>Q = expression"]
    hasNot{"Contains<br/>NOT operator?"}
    hasParen{"Contains<br/>parentheses?"}
    preprocess["Apply De Morgan Laws<br/>Push NOT inward"]
    respectPrec["Parse with operator<br/>precedence NOT > AND > OR"]
    flatDNF["Convert to DNF<br/>using distributive law<br/>A AND B OR C = A AND B OR C"]
    optOrder["Order sub-queries by<br/>ascending postings size"]
    execute["Execute merges<br/>shortest-list first"]
    result["Return ranked result set"]
    query --> hasNot
    hasNot -- "YES" --> hasParen
    hasNot -- "NO" --> hasParen
    hasParen -- "YES" --> preprocess
    hasParen -- "NO" --> respectPrec
    preprocess --> respectPrec
    respectPrec --> flatDNF
    flatDNF --> optOrder
    optOrder --> execute
    execute --> result
```

## 4.7 Space-Time Tradeoff Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph fast["Fast Query, High Space"]
        bitmap["Bitmap Index<br/>N/8 bytes per term<br/>O N per NOT"]
    end
    subgraph balanced["Balanced Default"]
        invidx["Inverted Index<br/>O sum of df_t<br/>O P1 + P2 per AND"]
    end
    subgraph compact["Compact, Slower Query"]
        compInv["Compressed Inverted Index<br/>Golomb or Variable Byte<br/>4x to 8x smaller<br/>Decompression overhead"]
    end
    bitmap --> invidx
    invidx --> compInv
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the Boolean Retrieval Model. Mention its two main limitations.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key — 3 marks):**

The **Boolean Retrieval Model** is an information retrieval paradigm where a query is expressed as a Boolean expression of search terms connected by the operators **AND**, **OR**, and **NOT**. The system returns documents for which the Boolean expression evaluates to TRUE.

**Two main limitations (1.5 marks each):**

1. **No ranking**: The model returns documents in an arbitrary order (typically by docID). There is no notion of partial match or relevance, so a document containing 1 occurrence of a query term is treated identically to one containing 100 occurrences.

2. **Difficult query formulation for end users**: Constructing effective Boolean expressions requires expert knowledge of operator precedence and domain vocabulary. Casual users struggle to formulate precise queries, leading to either overly broad (OR-heavy) or overly narrow (AND-heavy) result sets.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is an inverted index? How does it differ from a forward index?**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key — 3 marks):**

An **inverted index** is a data structure that maps each unique term in the vocabulary to the sorted list of document IDs in which the term appears, called the **postings list**. (1.5 marks)

It differs from a **forward index** as follows (1.5 marks):

| Aspect | Forward Index | Inverted Index |
|---|---|---|
| Mapping direction | Document → Terms it contains | Term → Documents containing it |
| Storage organization | One postings per document | One postings per term |
| Use case | Document construction, content retrieval | Query-time lookup (Boolean, ranked) |
| Scale of growth | Grows with document size | Grows with vocabulary and term frequency |

The inverted index is the **production choice** for query processing because most queries ask "which documents contain term $t$?" — exactly what the inverted index answers in $O(1)$ dictionary lookup.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**

### Question A (Choice 1)

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the construction of the **inverted index** for a small document collection. Also explain how the **AND**, **OR**, and **NOT** operations are executed on postings lists. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **skip pointers** in accelerating Boolean query processing. Derive the **optimal skip pointer gap** and show the asymptotic time complexity improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Define the corpus (1 mark):**

Consider 4 documents:

| DocID | Text |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | "information retrieval is fun" |
| $d_2$ | "search engines use inverted index" |
| $d_3$ | "boolean queries use operators" |
| $d_4$ | "inverted index is the data structure" |

**Step 2 — Build the inverted index (3 marks):**

After tokenization, lowercasing, and stopword removal:

$$P(\text{inform}) = \langle 1, 4 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{retriev}) = \langle 1 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{fun} = \langle 1 \rangle)$$
$$P(\text{search}) = \langle 2 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{engin}) = \langle 2 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{invert}) = \langle 2, 4 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{index}) = \langle 2, 4 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{boolean}) = \langle 3 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{queri}) = \langle 3 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{oper}) = \langle 3 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{data}) = \langle 4 \rangle$$
$$P(\text{structur}) = \langle 4 \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Demonstrate AND, OR, NOT execution (3 marks):**

**AND** (e.g., $Q_1 = \text{invert} \;\text{AND}\; \text{index}$):

$$P(\text{invert}) \cap P(\text{index}) = \langle 2, 4 \rangle \cap \langle 2, 4 \rangle = \langle 2, 4 \rangle$$

**OR** (e.g., $Q_2 = \text{retriev} \;\text{OR}\; \text{boolean}$):

$$P(\text{retriev}) \cup P(\text{boolean}) = \langle 1 \rangle \cup \langle 3 \rangle = \langle 1, 3 \rangle$$

**NOT** (e.g., $Q_3 = \text{inform} \;\text{AND}\; \text{NOT}\;\text{index}$):

$$P(\text{index}) = \langle 2, 4 \rangle \Rightarrow \text{NOT}(\text{index}) = \{1, 3\}$$
$$P(\text{inform}) \cap \{1, 3\} = \langle 1, 4 \rangle \cap \langle 1, 3 \rangle = \langle 1 \rangle$$

**Result:** $d_1$ is the only document satisfying $Q_3$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Skip pointers are auxiliary pointers inserted at fixed intervals within a postings list. Each skip pointer stores the document ID and byte offset of a position several entries ahead, allowing the merge algorithm to "skip" over blocks of irrelevant documents.

**Layout (1.5 marks):**

$$P(\text{term}) = \langle 4 \to 8 \to 16 \to 25 \to 32 \to 38 \to 41 \to 48 \rangle$$

with skip pointer from entry $4$ jumping to $16$, from $16$ to $32$, and from $32$ to $48$.

**Algorithm sketch (2 marks):** During merge with another list, when $P_1[i] < P_2[j]$, check if $P_1[i + \text{skip}] \leq P_2[j]$. If yes, jump to the skip target; else advance by one.

**Derivation of optimal skip gap (2 marks):**

Let $L$ = list length, $g$ = skip interval. Expected number of comparisons:

$$T(L, g) \approx \frac{L}{g} + g$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\frac{dT}{dg} = -\frac{L}{g^2} + 1 = 0 \implies g^2 = L \implies g^* = \sqrt{L}$$

**Result:** With optimal skip gap $\sqrt{L}$, the merge complexity improves from $O(L)$ to $O(2\sqrt{L})$ — a **speedup of $\sqrt{L}$** for each postings list.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the **document-term incidence matrix** representation for a corpus. Show, with an example, how Boolean queries can be answered using **matrix operations**. Discuss why this representation is **impractical for large corpora**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **De Morgan's Laws** and the **distributive law** in the context of Boolean query rewriting. Apply them to transform the query $Q = \text{NOT}(\text{data} \;\text{AND}\; \text{machin}) \;\text{OR}\; \text{learn}$ into a form containing only **AND** and **OR** (no NOT outside the leaves). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Definition of incidence matrix (2 marks):**

A binary matrix $M \in \{0, 1\}^{\vert V \vert \times N}$ where rows are terms and columns are documents, and $M[t, d] = 1$ iff $t$ appears in $d$.

**Example with 3 documents (2 marks):**

$d_1$: "data science", $d_2$: "machine learning", $d_3$: "data mining"

$$M = \begin{pmatrix} \text{data} & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ \text{scienc} & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ \text{machin} & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ \text{learn} & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ \text{min} & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Query as matrix operation (2 marks):**

Query $Q = \text{data} \;\text{AND}\; \text{machin}$ corresponds to:

$$R = M[\text{data}, :] \wedge M[\text{machin}, :] = (1, 0, 1) \wedge (0, 1, 0) = (0, 0, 0)$$

Result: empty set — no document contains both terms.

Query $Q' = \text{data} \;\text{OR}\; \text{scienc}$:

$$R' = M[\text{data}, :] \vee M[\text{scienc}, :] = (1, 0, 1) \vee (1, 0, 0) = (1, 0, 1)$$

Result: $\{d_1, d_3\}$.

**Why impractical (1 mark):**

For $\vert V \vert = 10^6$ terms and $N = 10^6$ documents, the matrix requires $10^{12}$ bits $\approx 125$ GB. The matrix is **extremely sparse** (typical density $< 0.1\%$), so storing it densely wastes 99.9% of memory. The **inverted index** stores only the 1s and is the production solution.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**De Morgan's Laws (1.5 marks each):**

$$\neg(A \wedge B) = \neg A \vee \neg B$$
$$\neg(A \vee B) = \neg A \wedge \neg B$$

**Distributive Law (1 mark):**

$$A \wedge (B \vee C) = (A \wedge B) \vee (A \wedge C)$$

**Application to the given query (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Original query:

$$Q = \neg(\text{data} \wedge \text{machin}) \vee \text{learn}$$

**Step 2:** Apply De Morgan to $\neg(\text{data} \wedge \text{machin})$:

$$Q = (\neg \text{data} \vee \neg \text{machin}) \vee \text{learn}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the associativity of $\vee$:

$$Q = \neg \text{data} \vee \neg \text{machin} \vee \text{learn}$$

**Final form:** All NOT operators are now pushed down to the leaf level (no NOT above AND/OR). This query is in **Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF)** — a disjunction of literals.

**Result interpretation (1 mark):** The system retrieves the union of documents containing *learn*, plus documents *not* containing *data*, plus documents *not* containing *machine*. This OR-of-literals form is computationally efficient because the union operation can be implemented as a single linear pass.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing union and intersection**: The single most common error is swapping AND with OR. Always verify: $P(t_1) \cap P(t_2)$ returns documents containing **both** terms.
>
> 2. **Forgetting operator precedence**: Writing `data AND learn OR machin` without parentheses. The KTU key will deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguity.
>
> 3. **Not sorting postings before merge**: If a student writes a postings list unsorted, the linear merge algorithm **fails**. The KTU key explicitly requires **ascending sorted** order.
>
> 4. **Neglecting De Morgan's Laws**: In query rewriting questions, students often apply the distributive law without first pushing NOTs to the leaves. KTU examiners award **partial credit** but deduct for incomplete transformation.
>
> 5. **Skipping the complexity derivation**: In skip-pointer questions, the 14-mark version requires the **optimal gap derivation** ($g^* = \sqrt{L}$). Stating the formula without derivation loses up to 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up NOT semantics**: $\text{NOT}(\text{data})$ means "documents that do NOT contain data." A common mistake is to interpret it as "do contain data." Read carefully.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE**

- [x] The **Boolean Retrieval Model** is the **classical** IR model; it uses AND, OR, NOT.
- [x] The **incidence matrix** is a $V \times N$ binary matrix — **conceptual, not used in production** due to $O(VN)$ space.
- [x] The **inverted index** is the **production data structure** — dictionary + postings lists.
- [x] Postings lists are **sorted by document ID** in ascending order.
- [x] The **two-pointer merge** is the canonical AND/OR algorithm with $O(P_1 + P_2)$ complexity.
- [x] For AND, **process the shorter list first** to reduce total work.
- [x] **Skip pointers** with gap $\sqrt{L}$ reduce merge complexity to $O(2\sqrt{L})$.
- [x] **Operator precedence**: $\text{NOT} > \text{AND} > \text{OR}$; parentheses override.
- [x] **De Morgan's Laws**: $\neg(A \wedge B) = \neg A \vee \neg B$ and $\neg(A \vee B) = \neg A \wedge \neg B$.
- [x] **Distributive Law**: $A \wedge (B \vee C) = (A \wedge B) \vee (A \wedge C)$.
- [x] **DNF form** = OR of AND clauses; useful for **query rewriting and optimization**.
- [x] **Stopword removal** reduces index size by 30–50% but may harm recall.
- [x] **Stemming** (Porter algorithm) conflates morphological variants.
- [x] **NOT** has worst-case $O(N)$ cost — it can blow up the result set.
- [x] **Bitmap representation** is useful when $df_t$ is very high — provides $O(N/64)$ NOT.
- [x] **Compressed inverted indexes** (Golomb, Variable Byte) achieve 4×–8× space reduction.
- [x] The total index size is $O(\sum_t df_t)$, not $O(VN)$ — this is the **central advantage**.
- [x] **Heaps' Law**: $\vert V \vert = K \cdot T^\beta$ governs vocabulary growth.
- [x] **Zipf's Law**: term frequency $\propto 1/\text{rank}^\theta$ — a few terms dominate.
- [x] **Limitations of Boolean retrieval**: no ranking, no partial matching, difficult query formulation, no relevance feedback.
- [x] **Real-world usage**: legal e-discovery, PubMed, enterprise search, Elasticsearch, Splunk.
- [x] **Lucene/Elasticsearch query DSL** uses `+` for MUST, `|` for SHOULD, `-` for MUST NOT — directly maps to AND, OR, NOT.
- [x] **AND-NOT queries** in production often use **filtered iteration** on the shorter list.

> **Final Examiner's Verdict:** Boolean Query Processing is the **most heavily weighted** topic in Module 4. Master the postings merge algorithm, De Morgan's Laws, skip pointer derivation, and the 5-step query processing pipeline. These four pillars guarantee ≥ 12/14 marks on any ESE variant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tokenization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tokenization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tokenization** is the foundational pre-processing step in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Text Analytics wherein a continuous stream of textual data (a document, sentence, or corpus) is segmented into discrete, smaller linguistic units called **tokens**. These tokens may correspond to words, sub-words, characters, punctuation marks, or numerical entities depending on the granularity chosen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Tokenization is the process of breaking a stream of text into smaller meaningful elements called tokens, which serve as the atomic input units for downstream analytics, statistical modelling, feature extraction, and machine learning pipelines."*

Mathematically, tokenization can be expressed as a mapping function:
$$T : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{T}$$
where $\mathcal{C}$ is the input corpus and $\mathcal{T} = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_n\}$ is the sequence of extracted tokens.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spice Grinder"

Imagine you have a **whole spice block** (raw text) and you need to use it in a recipe (your analytics model). You cannot use the entire block directly — the recipe requires fine powder or tiny pieces. So, you pass the block through a **grinder** (the tokenizer) and get individual spice particles (tokens) of uniform, usable size.

Similarly, machine learning models cannot process raw text strings directly. The tokenizer acts as a "linguistic grinder" that converts unstructured human language into a structured sequence of symbols that statistical models can ingest, count, vectorize, and learn from.

### 1.3 Why Tokenization Matters in Data Analytics

In any analytics pipeline, the quality of the **input token stream** directly governs:
- **Feature engineering** in Bag-of-Words and TF-IDF representations.
- **Vocabulary construction** for vector space models.
- **Embedding lookups** in modern transformer-based architectures (BERT, GPT).
- **Statistical validity** of n-gram and topic models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric Highlighted:** A well-engineered tokenizer can reduce a corpus of **1,000,000 characters** into approximately **180,000–220,000 tokens** using subword methods, achieving a compression ratio of roughly **4.5:1** without information loss.

### 1.4 Granularity Spectrum

Tokenization operates across a spectrum of linguistic granularity:

| Granularity Level | Token Example (Input: "Unhappiness") | Vocabulary Size | OOV Handling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Character-level** | `U`, `n`, `h`, `a`, `p`, `p`, `i`, `n`, `e`, `s`, `s` | Very Small (~100–500) | Excellent |
| **Subword-level** | `Un`, `happiness` | Medium (~30,000–50,000) | Very Good |
| **Word-level** | `Unhappiness` | Very Large (>1,000,000) | Poor |
| **Sentence-level** | `Unhappiness is a state.` | Smallest | N/A |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorical Taxonomy of Tokenization

Tokenization is broadly classified along two orthogonal axes: **unit of segmentation** and **algorithmic strategy**.

#### A. By Unit of Segmentation
1. **Word Tokenization** — Splits on whitespace and punctuation.
2. **Sentence Tokenization** — Splits on sentence terminators (`.`, `!`, `?`).
3. **Subword Tokenization** — Splits rare words into frequent morphemes (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram).
4. **Character Tokenization** — Splits into individual Unicode characters.
5. **Byte-Level Tokenization (BBPE)** — Splits into UTF-8 bytes (used in GPT-2).

#### B. By Algorithmic Strategy
1. **Rule-Based / Regex Tokenization** — Hand-crafted regex patterns.
2. **Statistical Tokenization** — Frequency-driven merges (BPE).
3. **Probabilistic Tokenization** — Likelihood maximization (Unigram LM).
4. **Linguistic Tokenization** — Grammar-driven, uses POS taggers.

### 2.2 The Five-Step Tokenization Pipeline

Every tokenization system, regardless of complexity, executes these five steps:

- **Step 1 — Normalization:** Convert raw text to canonical form (lowercasing, Unicode normalization, accent stripping, whitespace collapsing).
- **Step 2 — Pre-tokenization:** Initial split into coarse chunks (usually whitespace-based) to bootstrap downstream processing.
- **Step 3 — Model Application:** Apply the chosen algorithm (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram) to determine the final token boundaries.
- **Step 4 — Post-processing:** Add special tokens such as `[CLS]`, `[SEP]`, `[PAD]`, `[UNK]`, `[MASK]`.
- **Step 5 — ID Conversion:** Map each token to an integer index from a fixed vocabulary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The tokenization pipeline is **deterministic and reversible only when the full vocabulary and merge rules are preserved**. Loss of the merge table makes the model unusable for inference.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tokenization Mapping | $T : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{T}$ | Defines the transformation function |
| 2 | Vocabulary Cardinality | $V = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[t_i \in \mathcal{V}]$ | Size of the model's known token set |
| 3 | Token Frequency (Relative) | $f(t_i) = \frac{\text{count}(t_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \text{count}(t_j)}$ | Feature weighting in BoW / TF-IDF |
| 4 | Out-of-Vocabulary Rate | $\text{OOV} = \frac{N_{\text{UNK}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Measures vocabulary coverage gap |
| 5 | Compression Ratio (BPE) | $CR = \frac{N_{\text{original}}}{N_{\text{BPE}}}$ | Efficiency metric of subword encoding |
| 6 | Average Tokens per Sentence | $\bar{L} = \frac{1}{S} \sum_{s=1}^{S} L_s$ | Corpus statistic for model design |
| 7 | BPE Merge Selection (Probability) | $p_{a,b} = \frac{f(\text{ab})}{\sum_{x,y} f(\text{xy})}$ | Most frequent pair merging rule |
| 8 | Unigram Likelihood | $\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{V}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(t_i \vert \mathcal{V})$ | Tokenizer training objective |
| 9 | Tokenizer Throughput | $\tau = \frac{N_{\text{sequences}}}{\Delta t}$ in tokens/sec | Production system performance KPI |
| 10 | Fertility Score (Multilingual) | $\Phi = \frac{\text{avg tokens produced by tokenizer A}}{\text{avg tokens produced by tokenizer B}}$ | Cross-lingual tokenizer comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The **OOV rate** and **Vocabulary Cardinality** are the two most frequently tested formulas in KTU university exams. Master the interplay between these two metrics — they form the **vocabulary-coverage tradeoff** central to subword methods.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Tokenization enables inverted index construction. Each token becomes a key, with document IDs as values.
- **Sentiment Analytics:** Bag-of-tokens is the substrate for polarity scoring.
- **Transformers (BERT, GPT):** Subword tokenization handles morphological variants without exploding the vocabulary.
- **Bioinformatics Text Mining (PubMedBERT):** Custom tokenizers handle chemical nomenclature, gene symbols, and Latin abbreviations.
- **Multilingual Analytics:** BBPE tokenizers (XLM-R, mBERT) provide shared byte representation across 100+ languages with a single ~250,000-token vocabulary.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Word Tokenization (Rule-Based)

**Input Corpus:** `"Data analytics empowers engineers to derive insights from unstructured data!"`

**Step 1 — Lowercase Normalization:**
$$\text{Input}_{\text{norm}} = \text{"data analytics empowers engineers to derive insights from unstructured data!"}$$

The reason: reduces vocabulary sparsity. "Data" and "data" now map to the same index.

**Step 2 — Regex Split Pattern:**

Define the delimiter regex:
$$\mathcal{R} = \left[ \s\.,;:!\?"'\(\)\[\]\{\} \right]+$$

**Step 3 — Apply Split:**

The split is performed at every match boundary of $\mathcal{R}$ in the normalized string. The split produces the token list:
$$\mathcal{T} = \{\text{"data"}, \text{"analytics"}, \text{"empowers"}, \text{"engineers"}, \text{"to"}, \text{"derive"}, \text{"insights"}, \text{"from"}, \text{"unstructured"}, \text{"data"}\}$$

**Step 4 — Drop Empty Strings and Pure Punctuation:**

Tokens consisting solely of matched delimiters (i.e., the `!` at the end) are filtered out using:
$$\mathcal{T}_{\text{final}} = \{ t \in \mathcal{T} \mid t \neq \varepsilon \text{ and } t \notin \mathcal{P} \}$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ is the punctuation-only set and $\varepsilon$ is the empty string.

**Step 5 — Vocabulary Construction:**

$$V = \vert \mathcal{T}_{\text{final}} \vert = 10 \text{ unique tokens}$$

Note that `data` appears twice in the original text but only once in $V$, demonstrating the **de-duplication property** of vocabulary construction.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) Derivation

**Input Training Corpus (with frequencies):**

| Word | Frequency |
|---|---|
| `low` | 5 |
| `lower` | 2 |
| `newest` | 6 |
| `widest` | 3 |

**Step 1 — Initialize Vocabulary with Characters:**

The initial character vocabulary is:
$$\mathcal{V}_0 = \{\text{'l', 'o', 'w', 'e', 'r', 'n', 'w', 'i', 'd', 's', 't'}\}$$

Each word is represented as a sequence of individual characters with an end-of-word marker:
$$\text{low} \rightarrow \text{l o w </w>}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Pair Frequencies:**

We enumerate all adjacent symbol pairs and count their occurrences across the corpus.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pair: ('l', 'o')} &: 5 + 2 = 7 \text{ (from 'low' and 'lower')} \\
\text{Pair: ('o', 'w')} &: 5 + 2 = 7 \\
\text{Pair: ('l', 'o', 'w')} &: \text{evaluated as ('l','o') and ('o','w')} \\
\text{Pair: ('o', 'w', '</w>')} &: \text{evaluated separately} \\
\text{Pair: ('w', '</w>')} &: 5 \text{ (from 'low')} \\
\text{Pair: ('w', 'e')} &: 2 \text{ (from 'lower')} \\
\text{Pair: ('e', 'r')} &: 2 \text{ (from 'lower')} \\
\text{Pair: ('r', '</w>')} &: 2 \text{ (from 'lower')} \\
\text{Pair: ('n', 'e')} &: 6 \text{ (from 'newest')} \\
\text{Pair: ('e', 'w')} &: 6 \text{ (from 'newest')} \\
\text{Pair: ('w', 'e')} &: 6 + 3 = 9 \text{ (from 'newest' and 'widest')} \\
\text{Pair: ('e', 's')} &: 6 + 3 = 9 \text{ (from 'newest' and 'widest')} \\
\text{Pair: ('s', 't')} &: 6 + 3 = 9 \\
\text{Pair: ('t', '</w>')} &: 6 + 3 = 9 \\
\text{Pair: ('w', 'i')} &: 3 \text{ (from 'widest')} \\
\text{Pair: ('i', 'd')} &: 3 \\
\text{Pair: ('d', 'e')} &: 3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Select Most Frequent Pair:**

The highest frequency is **9**, shared by four pairs: `('w','e')`, `('e','s')`, `('s','t')`, `('t','</w>')`. Lexicographic tie-breaking selects `('e','s')` as the first merge.

**Merge Rule 1:** $e + s \rightarrow es$

**Step 4 — Update Vocabulary and Repeat:**

Updated $\mathcal{V}_1 = \mathcal{V}_0 \cup \{\text{'es'}\}$

Recount pairs with the new token. The pair `('e','s')` is replaced by `'es'` everywhere:
- `newest` becomes `n e w es t </w>` → pair `('w','es')` count: 6
- `widest` becomes `w i d es t </w>` → pair `('w','es')` count: 3 (total = 9)

The new maximum frequency pair is `('es','t')` with frequency $6 + 3 = 9$.

**Merge Rule 2:** $es + t \rightarrow est$

**Step 5 — Continue Until Target Vocabulary Size Reached:**

If we continue for 5 more merges, we would obtain: `est</w>`, `n+e → ne`, `ne+w → new`, `lo+w → low`, `low+er → lower`, `wid+est → widest`, and so on. The final tokenizer would encode `newest` as `[new, est]` (2 tokens) instead of `[n, e, w, e, s, t]` (6 characters).

**Compression Ratio Achieved:**
$$CR = \frac{6}{2} = 3.0 \text{ for the word 'newest'}$$

This 3× compression is the *engineering value* of BPE — fewer tokens, shorter sequences, faster transformer training.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — WordPiece Algorithm (BERT-style)

**Conceptual Walk-through (Conceptual — no code):**

WordPiece is similar to BPE but selects the merge pair that **maximizes the likelihood increase** of the training data, rather than raw frequency.

**Selection Criterion:**
$$\Delta \mathcal{L}(a, b) = \frac{f_{ab}}{f_a \cdot f_b}$$

where:
- $f_{ab}$ = frequency of the pair $(a, b)$ occurring together
- $f_a, f_b$ = individual frequencies of tokens $a$ and $b$

The pair with the **highest $\Delta \mathcal{L}$** is merged. This **dampens the influence of high-frequency standalone tokens** and rewards pairings that are statistically distinctive.

**Application to "newest":**
If `n` appears 1000 times corpus-wide and `e` appears 800 times, but `ne` appears 50 times, then:
$$\Delta \mathcal{L}(n, e) = \frac{50}{1000 \times 800} = 6.25 \times 10^{-5}$$

Versus a rarer pair like `wid` (10 occurrences) where `wi` appears 5 times and `d` appears 200 times:
$$\Delta \mathcal{L}(wi, d) = \frac{5}{5 \times 200} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

The `wid` pair has a higher likelihood increase, so it would be merged first — this is why BERT handles rare morphology well.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Tokenization Strategy

| Criterion | Word-Level | Character-Level | BPE | WordPiece | Unigram |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vocabulary Size** | Very Large | Tiny | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| **OOV Robustness** | Poor | Excellent | Very Good | Very Good | Very Good |
| **Sequence Length** | Short | Very Long | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| **Linguistic Meaning Preservation** | High | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| **Training Cost** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Inference Speed** | Fast | Slow | Fast | Fast | Fast |
| **Used In** | Classical ML | Char-CNN | GPT-2, RoBERTa | BERT, DistilBERT | XLNet, ALBERT |
| **Best For** | Sentiment, BoW | Morphology-rich languages | General English | General English | Multilingual tasks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tokenization Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Step 1: Normalization]
    B --> B1[Lowercasing]
    B1 --> B2[Unicode NFC Normalization]
    B2 --> B3[Whitespace Collapse]
    B3 --> C[Step 2: Pre-tokenization]
    C --> C1[Whitespace Split]
    C1 --> C2[Punctuation Isolation]
    C2 --> D[Step 3: Model Application]
    D --> D1{Algorithm Choice}
    D1 -->|BPE| E1[Merge Iterations]
    D1 -->|WordPiece| E2[Likelihood Max]
    D1 -->|Unigram| E3[Probabilistic Pruning]
    E1 --> F[Step 4: Post-Processing]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Add CLS and SEP]
    F1 --> F2[Add PAD Tokens]
    F2 --> G[Step 5: ID Conversion]
    G --> G1[Vocabulary Lookup]
    G1 --> H[Integer Token IDs]
    H --> I[Embedding Layer Input]
```

### 4.2 BPE Merge Iteration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pair Frequency Counter] --> P2[Identify Most Frequent Pair]
    P2 --> P3[Create New Merge Token]
    P3 --> P4[Update All Words in Corpus]
    P4 --> P5{Vocabulary Target\nReached?}
    P5 -->|No| P1
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Serialize Merge Table]
    P6 --> P7[Persist Tokenizer Model]
```

### 4.3 Granularity Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Input: Unhappiness] --> Q{Granularity\nSelection}
    Q -->|Character| R1[Output: U n h a p p i n e s s\n11 tokens]
    Q -->|Subword BPE| R2[Output: Un happiness\n2 tokens]
    Q -->|Word| R3[Output: Unhappiness\n1 token]
    Q -->|Sentence| R4[Output: Unhappiness.\n1 sentence]
```

### 4.4 Tokenization Failure Mode Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Tokenizer Input] --> G{Contraction\nDetected?}
    G -->|Yes, e.g. don't| H1[Expand: do not]
    G -->|No| H2[Split as: don't]
    H1 --> I{User Dict\nAvailable?}
    I -->|Yes| I1[Use Custom Split]
    I -->|No| I2[Apply Heuristic]
    F --> J{Hyphenated Word?}
    J -->|state-of-the-art| K1[Split Hyphens]
    J -->|Co-operate| K2[Preserve Compound]
    F --> L{Multilingual\nScript?}
    L -->|Yes| M1[Apply Language ID]
    L -->|No| M2[Default Path]
```

### 4.5 Vocabulary Construction Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Training Corpus] --> S1[Count Token Frequencies]
    S1 --> S2[Threshold Filtering]
    S2 --> S3{Unknown Token\nSeen N Times?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Add to Vocab]
    S3 -->|No| S5[Map to UNK]
    S4 --> V[Final Vocabulary V]
    S5 --> V
    V --> T[Vocab Persisted to disk]
    T --> P[Production Inference]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define tokenization. List and briefly explain the different types of tokenization techniques used in text analytics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Tokenization is the process of segmenting a continuous stream of text into discrete linguistic units called tokens, which serve as the atomic input for analytics models.

**Types (2 marks):**
1. **Word Tokenization:** Splits text on whitespace and punctuation boundaries. Example: `"Data analytics is fun." → {Data, analytics, is, fun}`.
2. **Sentence Tokenization:** Splits text on sentence terminators (`.`, `?`, `!`). Used in document summarization and document-level feature extraction.
3. **Subword Tokenization:** Decomposes rare words into frequent morphemic sub-units (BPE, WordPiece). Example: `"unhappiness" → {Un, happiness}`. Provides OOV robustness.
4. **Character Tokenization:** Splits into individual characters. Useful for morphologically rich languages and noisy text (e.g., social media).
5. **Byte-Level Tokenization (BBPE):** Splits into UTF-8 bytes, enabling representation of any character with a base-256 vocabulary.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) problem? How does subword tokenization address it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**OOV Definition (1 mark):** The Out-of-Vocabulary problem occurs when a tokenizer encounters a word that is **not present in its pre-built vocabulary** $V$. Such words are typically mapped to a generic `[UNK]` (unknown) token, causing **complete loss of semantic information**.

**Mathematical Expression (1 mark):**
$$\text{OOV Rate} = \frac{N_{\text{UNK}}}{N_{\text{total tokens}}}$$

**Subword Solution (1 mark):** Subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram) addresses OOV by breaking any unseen word into a sequence of **smaller, known sub-units**. For example, if `"cryptocurrency"` is not in the vocabulary, BPE tokenizes it as `["crypto", "currency"]` — both of which are likely present. This eliminates the need for `[UNK]` in modern subword-based systems, achieving **OOV rate ≈ 0%**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Candidates must answer ONE full question from the choice. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Total: **14 marks**.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a) Explain the Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) algorithm in detail with a suitable example. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare and contrast Word Tokenization, Character Tokenization, and Subword Tokenization along the dimensions of vocabulary size, OOV handling, and sequence length. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) is a **subword tokenization algorithm** originally developed for data compression and later adapted to NLP by Sennrich et al. (2016). It iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent symbol pair in a corpus to build a vocabulary of variable-length subword units.

**2. Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

- **Initialize:** Represent the training corpus as a sequence of characters with end-of-word markers.
- **Count Pairs:** Enumerate all adjacent symbol pairs and compute their frequencies.
- **Merge:** Select the pair with maximum frequency, add it as a new vocabulary token, and replace all instances of the pair in the corpus.
- **Repeat:** Continue merging until the target vocabulary size $V_{\text{target}}$ is reached or no pairs remain.

**3. Worked Example (2 marks):**

Corpus: `{"low": 5, "lower": 2, "newest": 6, "widest": 3}`

Iteration 1: Most frequent pair = `('e', 's')` with frequency 9. Merge → `es`.
Iteration 2: Most frequent pair = `('es', 't')` with frequency 9. Merge → `est`.
Iteration 3: Pair `('l', 'o')` has frequency 7. Merge → `lo`.
Iteration 4: Pair `('lo', 'w')` has frequency 7. Merge → `low`.
Final encoding of "newest" = `[new, est]` (2 tokens vs. 6 characters, CR = 3.0).

**4. Properties (1 mark):**
- Deterministic given the merge table.
- Greedy: locally optimal merges may not yield globally optimal segmentation.
- Provides graceful OOV handling via character fallback.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Dimension | Word Tokenization | Character Tokenization | Subword Tokenization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vocabulary Size** | Very large (~1M+) | Tiny (~100–500) | Medium (~30K–50K) |
| **OOV Handling** | Poor — relies on `[UNK]` | Excellent — no OOV possible | Excellent — fallback to chars |
| **Sequence Length** | Short (1 token/word) | Very long (5–10× word count) | Medium (1.3–1.5× word count) |
| **Linguistic Semantics** | High | Low | Medium-High |
| **Embedding Quality** | Suffix/prefix variants fragmented | Character n-grams emerge | Morphemes preserved |
| **Memory Footprint** | Large embedding matrix | Small embedding matrix | Balanced |
| **Training Time** | Fast | Slow (long sequences) | Medium |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram) is the **de facto standard** in modern NLP because it optimally balances vocabulary compactness, OOV robustness, and semantic preservation. Character tokenization is preferred for morphologically rich languages (e.g., Finnish, Turkish) and noisy domains, while word-level tokenization remains useful for classical BoW/TF-IDF pipelines.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a) Describe the complete tokenization pipeline in a modern NLP system. Identify and explain any THREE challenges in tokenization with examples. (7 marks)**

**(b) A training corpus contains the following word frequencies: `{"the": 10, "cat": 4, "sat": 3, "on": 5, "mat": 2, "thecat": 1}`. Apply the first 3 iterations of the BPE algorithm and determine the encoded representation of the word "thecat" after these iterations. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Tokenization Pipeline (4 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Normalization:** Lowercase the text, apply Unicode NFC normalization, collapse multiple whitespaces, and strip accents. Example: `"Café"` → `"cafe"`.
- **Step 2 — Pre-tokenization:** Perform an initial whitespace-based split, isolating punctuation as separate tokens. Example: `"Hello, world!"` → `{"Hello", ",", "world", "!"}`.
- **Step 3 — Model Application:** Apply BPE, WordPiece, or Unigram to determine the final token boundaries.
- **Step 4 — Post-Processing:** Add special tokens — `[CLS]` (start of sequence), `[SEP]` (separator), `[PAD]` (padding), `[MASK]` (masking).
- **Step 5 — ID Conversion:** Map each token to its integer index via the vocabulary lookup table.

**2. Three Challenges (3 marks):**

- **Challenge 1 — Contractions:** Words like `"don't"`, `"I'm"`, `"they'll"` can be tokenized inconsistently. Some systems split as a single token, others expand to `"do not"`. The choice affects downstream semantic interpretation.

- **Challenge 2 — Hyphenated Compounds:** `"state-of-the-art"` may be split into 5 tokens, 1 token, or 2 tokens (`"state-of-the"`, `"art"`) depending on the tokenizer. Hyphen behavior must be standardized for consistency.

- **Challenge 3 — Multilingual and Code-Switched Text:** A single sentence like `"Hello नमस्ते 你好"` mixes scripts. Tokenizers trained on monolingual English data may produce excessive `[UNK]` tokens. Solutions include BBPE and SentencePiece with language-balanced corpora.

- **(Bonus Challenge 4) — Domain Shift:** A tokenizer trained on news text will perform poorly on medical records, social media slang, or legal documents. Domain-adaptive tokenization is an active research area.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Initial State:**

Each word is represented as a character sequence with end-of-word marker `</w>`. The vocabulary starts with all unique characters:
$$\mathcal{V}_0 = \{\text{'t', 'h', 'e', 'c', 'a', 's', 'o', 'n', 'm', '</w>'}\}$$

**Pair Frequency Calculation:**

| Pair | Frequency |
|---|---|
| `('t','h')` | 10 (the) + 1 (thecat) = **11** |
| `('h','e')` | 10 + 1 = **11** |
| `('e','</w>')` | 10 (the) = **10** |
| `('c','a')` | 4 (cat) + 1 (thecat) = **5** |
| `('a','t')` | 4 (cat) + 1 (thecat) = **5** |
| `('t','</w>')` | 4 (cat) + 3 (sat) = **7** |
| `('s','a')` | 3 (sat) = **3** |
| `('a','t')` | 4 (cat) + 1 (thecat) = **5** |
| `('o','n')` | 5 (on) = **5** |
| `('n','</w>')` | 5 (on) = **5** |
| `('m','a')` | 2 (mat) = **2** |
| `('a','t')` | already 5 |
| `('t','c')` | 1 (thecat) = **1** |
| `('h','e')` | already 11 |
| `('e','c')` | 1 (thecat) = **1** |
| `('a','t')` | already 5 |

**Iteration 1:** Maximum frequency pair = `('t', 'h')` and `('h', 'e')` both at **11**. Tie-breaking by lexicographic order selects `('h', 'e')`. 

**Merge 1:** `he` → new token added. `thecat` becomes `t he c a t </w>`.

**Recount and Iterate:**

After Merge 1, we continue similarly. **Merge 2** typically produces `th`, and **Merge 3** produces `the`. 

**Final Encoded Representation of "thecat" after 3 iterations:**

Assuming merges in order: `he`, `th`, `the`:
- After Merge 1 (`he`): "thecat" → `[t, he, c, a, t</w>]` = 5 tokens
- After Merge 2 (`th`): "thecat" → `[th, e, c, a, t</w>]` = 5 tokens
- After Merge 3 (`the`): "thecat" → `[the, c, a, t</w>]` = 4 tokens

**Final Answer:** After 3 BPE iterations, the word **"thecat"** is encoded as **`[the, c, a, t</w>>`** — 4 subword tokens. The compression ratio achieved is $CR = 6/4 = 1.5$ for this word.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **OOV formula mistake:** Students frequently write OOV = $N_{\text{UNK}} \times N_{\text{total}}$ instead of the correct **ratio** $N_{\text{UNK}} / N_{\text{total}}$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **BPE merge table omission:** In BPE derivations, students often forget to **recompute pair frequencies** after each merge. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark per skipped iteration** if the chain is not shown.
> 3. **Confusing BPE with WordPiece:** Writing "BPE selects the most likely pair" is **wrong**. BPE selects the **most frequent** pair. WordPiece selects the **highest likelihood increase** pair. Mixing these definitions costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Missing the `</w>` marker:** In BPE examples, omitting the end-of-word marker means losing the distinction between `"low"` and `"lower"`. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 5. **Ignoring normalization:** Any tokenization answer that skips the **normalization step** in the pipeline is considered incomplete. KTU 2024 valuation penalizes this with a **1-mark deduction**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tokenization** is the **first and most critical** step in any text analytics pipeline. Errors here propagate to all downstream stages.
- The **tokenization mapping** $T : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{T}$ converts raw text into a structured sequence of atomic units.
- **Word tokenization** is simple but suffers from OOV. **Subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram)** is the modern standard.
- **BPE** is **frequency-driven and greedy**. **WordPiece** is **likelihood-driven**. **Unigram** is **probabilistic and prunable**.
- The **OOV rate** formula is $\text{OOV} = N_{\text{UNK}} / N_{\text{total}}$ — always a **ratio**, never a product.
- The **tokenization pipeline** has 5 stages: **Normalization → Pre-tokenization → Model Application → Post-Processing → ID Conversion**.
- The **vocabulary-cardinality vs. sequence-length** is the central tradeoff. Smaller vocabularies increase sequence length; larger vocabularies increase embedding matrix size.
- **Special tokens** `[CLS]`, `[SEP]`, `[PAD]`, `[MASK]`, `[UNK]` are added during post-processing and are essential for transformer models.
- **Compression Ratio (CR)** is the key efficiency metric for subword methods — values of 3–5 are typical for English.
- The **end-of-word marker `</w>`** is mandatory in BPE to distinguish prefixes from complete words.
- **Domain-specific tokenization** is critical — a tokenizer trained on Wikipedia will underperform on medical or legal corpora.
- **Byte-level BPE (BBPE)** uses UTF-8 bytes as the base vocabulary, enabling representation of any language with only 256 base tokens.
- **Fertility score** $\Phi$ is used to compare tokenizers across languages; lower fertility = better compression.
- KTU 2024 frequently tests: **OOV formula**, **BPE algorithm steps**, **comparison of tokenization methods**, and **pipeline components**.
- Always show the **full merge table** in BPE derivations — partial work loses marks.
- Memorize: **GPT uses BPE**, **BERT uses WordPiece**, **XLNet/ALBERT use Unigram**, **mBERT/XLM-R use SentencePiece + BBPE**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stemming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stemming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Stemming** is a heuristic text normalization technique in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Information Retrieval (IR) that reduces inflected (or sometimes derived) words to their word stem, base, or root form — generally a written word form. The stem need not be identical to the morphological root of the word; it is usually sufficient that related words map to the same stem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCDT503 – Module 4):**
> Stemming is the process of reducing a word to its word stem that affixes to suffixes and prefixes, or to the roots of words known as "lemmas." Stemming is important in NLP and Natural Language Understanding (NLU) domains for indexing words, retrieving information, and tagging parts of speech, among other applications.

The fundamental purpose of stemming in any data analytics pipeline is to **improve recall** in search systems by mapping morphological variants to a common canonical form, thereby reducing the dimensionality of the feature space when building text-based machine learning models.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Library Filing Analogy**
>
> Imagine you walk into a massive library where books on the same topic are scattered under hundreds of subtly different labels:
> *"Running," "Runner," "Runs," "Ran," "Rerun," "Outrunning."*
>
> If a student searches for "run," the librarian would have to magically know that "ran" and "running" are related. A **stemmer** acts like a strict but efficient librarian who chops every word down to a common "filing root." Instead of filing "studies," "studying," and "studied" under three different drawers, the stemmer slashes them all to the single folder **"studi."** It's rough, sometimes "over-chops" words (like turning "university" into "univers"), but it dramatically speeds up retrieval by treating similar words as one.

## Why Stemming Matters in Data Analytics

- **Reduces Vocabulary Size:** A corpus with 100,000 unique words may collapse to 40,000–60,000 stems.
- **Enhances IR Recall:** Search query *"connection"* matches documents containing *"connections," "connected," "connecting."*
- **Optimizes ML Models:** Bag-of-Words and TF-IDF matrices become denser and more meaningful.
- **Enables Language-Agnostic Processing:** Works even on noisy user-generated text (tweets, reviews).

## Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Token** | An individual word/unit extracted from text. |
| **Stem** | The chopped base form produced by a stemmer (e.g., *running → runn*). |
| **Lemma** | The true dictionary base form produced by a lemmatizer (e.g., *running → run*). |
| **Suffix Stripping** | Removing end affixes (*-ing, -ed, -ly*). |
| **Prefix Stripping** | Removing start affixes (*-un, -re, -pre*). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Measure of a Word (m) — Porter's Core Concept

The Porter Stemmer (1980) does not blindly chop suffixes. It uses a **measure function `m`** defined as the number of alternations between vowel-sequences (V) and consonant-sequences (C) in a word.

- `C` = sequence of consecutive consonants
- `V` = sequence of consecutive vowels
- **`m` = number of `[VC]` pairs**

**Conditions Derived from `m`:**

| Condition | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `(m > 0)` | Stem contains at least one VC pair (has a vowel in the stem) |
| `(m > 1)` | Stem contains at least two VC pairs (longer word) |
| `(*v*)` | Stem contains a vowel |
| `(*d)` | Stem ends with a double consonant (e.g., `tt`, `ss`) |
| `(*o)` | Stem ends with `CVC` where the final `C` is not `w`, `x`, or `y` |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** `(m>0)` means "the stem has *life* in it (a vowel)." `(m>1)` means "the stem is *substantial*."

## 2.2 The Porter Stemmer — Five-Step Algorithm

The algorithm applies suffix-stripping rules in **five cascading steps**, where each step rewrites the word based on the rules' conditions and the current measure `m`.

### Step 1a — Plural & -S Removal

| Rule | Example |
| :--- | :--- |
| `SSES` → `SS` | *caresses* → *caress* |
| `IES` → `I` | *ponies* → *poni* |
| `SS` → `SS` | *caress* → *caress* |
| `S` → `` | *cats* → *cat* |

### Step 1b — Past Tense & Gerunds

| Rule | Condition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `(m>0)` `EED` → `EE` | stem has vowel | *agreed* → *agree* |
| `(*v*)` `ED` → `` | stem has vowel | *plastered* → *plaster* |
| `(*v*)` `ING` → `` | stem has vowel | *motoring* → *motor* |

**Sub-rule (1b1):** If after stripping, stem ends in `AT`, `BL`, or `IZ` → add `E`. If ends in a doubled consonant (not `L`, `S`, `Z`) → drop last letter. If short word (m=1, ends in CVC) → add `E`.

### Step 1c — Y to I

| Rule | Condition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `(*v*)` `Y` → `I` | stem has vowel | *happy* → *happi* |

### Step 2 — Suffix Removal (Longer Suffixes, m>0)

Examples:

- `ATIONAL` → `ATE` : *relational* → *relate*
- `TIONAL` → `TION` : *conditional* → *condition*
- `ENCI` → `ENCE` : *valenci* → *valence*
- `IZ ER` → `IZE` : *digitizer* → *digitize*
- `BLI` → `BLE` : *conformabli* → *conformable*
- `FULLI` → `FUL` : *painfully* → *painful*
- `NESSLI` → `NESS` : *goodnessli* → *goodness* (rare)
- `IZATION` → `IZE` : *dramatization* → *dramatize*

### Step 3 — Derivational Suffix Removal (m>0)

| Rule | Example |
| :--- | :--- |
| `ICATE` → `IC` | *triplicate* → *triplic* |
| `ATIVE` → `` | *formative* → *form* |
| `ALIZE` → `AL` | *formalize* → *formal* |
| `ICAL` → `IC` | *electrical* → *electric* |
| `FUL` → `` | *hopeful* → *hope* |
| `NESS` → `` | *goodness* → *good* |

### Step 4 — Aggressive Derivational Suffix Removal (m>1)

Removes long derivational suffixes such as `AL`, `ANCE`, `ENCE`, `ER`, `IC`, `ABLE`, `IBLE`, `ANT`, `EMENT`, `MENT`, `ENT`, `ION` (only if preceded by `S` or `T`), `OU`, `ISM`, `ATE`, `ITI`, `OUS`, `IVE`, `IZE`.

### Step 5a — Final E Removal

| Rule | Example |
| :--- | :--- |
| `(m>1)` `E` → `` | *probate* → *probat* |
| `(m=1 and not *o)` `E` → `` | *cease* → *ceas* |

### Step 5b — Final Double Consonant Cleanup

| Rule | Example |
| :--- | :--- |
| `(m>1 and *d and *L)` → single letter | *controll* → *control* |

## 2.3 Comparison: Stemming vs Lemmatization

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**

| Aspect | Stemming | Lemmatization |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Approach | Rule-based chopping | Dictionary + morphological analysis |
| Output | May not be a real word | Always a valid lemma |
| Speed | **Very fast** | Slower |
| Accuracy | Lower (over-stemming risk) | Higher |
| Example | *studies* → *studi* | *studies* → *study* |
| Example | *better* → *better* | *better* → *good* |
| POS Tag Used? | No | Yes (required for context) |
| Library Example | Porter, Snowball | WordNetLemmatizer (spaCy, NLTK) |

## 2.4 Other Major Stemming Algorithms

### Lancaster Stemmer (Paice-Husk)
- More aggressive than Porter.
- Uses an "iterative" rule table; rules applied in order until none match.
- Example: *maximum* → *maxim* → *maxim* (can over-stem)
- Risk: **Over-stemming** (different roots collapsed to same stem).

### Snowball Stemmer (Porter2)
- Improvement over Porter; supports multiple languages (English, French, German, Russian, etc.).
- Used by default in modern NLP pipelines.

### RegexpStemmer
- User-defined regular-expression based stemmer.
- Example: Remove all suffixes matching `ing$`, `ly$`, `ed$`.

### Krovetz Stemmer
- Dictionary-based, less aggressive; produces more readable stems.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU Examinations**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Porter Measure | $m = \text{count of } [VC] \text{ pairs}$ | Quantifies word complexity |
| Step 1a Plural | $SSES \to SS, \; IES \to I, \; S \to \emptyset$ | Search index compaction |
| Step 1b Past | $EED \to EE \text{ if } m>0$ | Sentiment normalization |
| Step 1c Y-to-I | $Y \to I \text{ if } \ast v \ast$ | Verb normalization |
| Step 5a E-drop | $E \to \emptyset \text{ if } m>1$ | Final stem cleanup |
| TF-IDF Post-Stem | $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \times idf(t)$ | Document ranking with stems |
| Vocabulary Reduction | $V_{reduced} = \frac{\vert V_{stems}\vert}{\vert V_{original}\vert}$ | Efficiency metric |
| Zipfian Law | $P(rank) \propto 1/rank^{\alpha}$ | Confirms need for stemming |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Query "running shoes" matches pages containing *run, runs, ran*.
2. **Sentiment Analysis Pipelines:** Models trained on *happy, happiest, happily* generalize better when collapsed.
3. **Spam Classifiers:** Reduce feature count to improve Naive Bayes / SVM throughput.
4. **Document Clustering (LDA, K-Means on TF-IDF):** Prevents similar documents from drifting apart due to morphology.
5. **Chatbots & Virtual Assistants:** Faster intent matching at the retrieval stage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Tracing "GENERALIZATIONS" through the Porter Algorithm

We trace the word **"GENERALIZATIONS"** step-by-step to demonstrate the Porter Stemmer.

### Initial Word Analysis

Word: `GENERALIZATIONS`

Let us break it into vowel/consonant blocks.

- `G` → C (single consonant)
- `E` → V (single vowel)
- `N` → C
- `E` → V
- `R` → C
- `A` → V
- `L` → C
- `I` → V
- `Z` → C
- `A` → V
- `T` → C
- `I` → V
- `O` → V
- `N` → C
- `S` → C

Pattern: `[C][V][C][V][C][V][C][V][C][V][C][V][V][C][C]`

Counting `[VC]` pairs:
1. `EN`
2. `ER`
3. `AL`
4. `IZ`
5. `AT`
6. `ION`

So initial measure **m = 6**.

### Step 1a — Plurals

Rule check: Does the word end in `SSES`, `IES`, `SS`, or `S`?

- `GENERALIZATIONS` ends in `S`. Apply rule `S → `.
- Result: **GENERALIZATION**
- Measure: still m = 6 (suffix removal doesn't affect stem measure).

### Step 1b — Past Tense / Gerund Removal

Rule check: Does the word end in `EED`, `ED`, or `ING`?

- Ends in `TION`, none match.
- Result: **GENERALIZATION** (unchanged)

### Step 1c — Y to I

Rule check: Does the word end in `Y` and stem contain a vowel `(*v*)`?

- Ends in `N`, not `Y`.
- Result: **GENERALIZATION** (unchanged)

### Step 2 — Suffix Removal (m > 0)

Check rules in order:

- `ATIONAL → ATE`: Does it end in `ATIONAL`? The word ends in `IZATION`, no match.
- `IZATION → IZE`: Does it end in `IZATION`? Yes! Apply rule.
- Result: **GENERALIZE**
- Recompute measure: `G-C, E-V, N-C, E-V, R-C, A-V, L-C, I-V, Z-C, E-V` → `[VC]` pairs: `EN, ER, AL, IZ, ZE` → **m = 5**.

### Step 3 — Derivational Suffix Removal (m > 0)

Check rules in order:

- `ICATE → IC`: ends in `ALIZE`, no.
- `ATIVE → `: no.
- `ALIZE → AL`: ends in `ALIZE`, **YES!**
- Result: **GENERAL**
- Measure: `G-C, E-V, N-C, E-V, R-C, A-V, L-C` → `[VC]` pairs: `EN, ER, AL` → **m = 3**.

### Step 4 — Aggressive Derivational Suffix Removal (m > 1)

Check rules in order:

- `AL → `: ends in `AL` (with m=3), **YES!**
- Result: **GENER**
- Measure: `G-C, E-V, N-C, E-V, R-C` → `[VC]` pairs: `EN, ER` → **m = 2**.

### Step 5a — Final E Removal

Check rules:

- `(m>1) E → `: ends in `E` with m=2, **YES!**
- Result: **GENR**

### Step 5b — Final Double Consonant Cleanup

Check rule:

- `(m>1 and *d and *L) → single letter`: Ends in `R` (single consonant), not a doubled one.
- Result: **GENR** (unchanged)

### Final Stemmed Output

$$\text{GENERALIZATIONS} \xrightarrow{\text{Porter Stemmer}} \text{GENR}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The output `GENR` is **not a valid English word**. This is the classic limitation of stemming — it prioritizes mechanical rule application over linguistic correctness. Lemmatization would yield `GENERAL`.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Tracing "RUNNING"

Word: `RUNNING`

### Measure Calculation

- `R` → C
- `U` → V
- `N` → C
- `N` → C
- `I` → V
- `N` → C
- `G` → C

Pattern: `[C][V][CC][V][CC]` → `[VC]` pairs: `UN` → **m = 1**.

### Step 1a — Plurals

Ends in `G`, not `SSES/IES/SS/S`. No change.
- Result: **RUNNING**

### Step 1b — Past / Gerund

Ends in `ING`, and stem `RUNN` contains vowel `U` (`(*v*)`). Apply rule `ING → `.
- Result: **RUNN**

**Sub-rule (1b1):** Does stem end in `AT`, `BL`, `IZ`? No. Does stem end in doubled consonant (`NN`)? Yes! Is it `L`, `S`, `Z`? No, it's `N`. So drop the last letter.
- Result: **RUN**

### Steps 1c, 2, 3, 4, 5

- Step 1c: No `Y`. 
- Step 2: Ends in nothing meaningful. No rule.
- Step 3: No match.
- Step 4: `m=1`, all rules require `m>1`. No match.
- Step 5a: `m=1`, need `(*o)` check. Stem `RUN` ends in `CVC` (R-C, U-V, N-C). Final C is `N`, not `W/X/Y`. So `(*o)` is TRUE. Therefore **do NOT** remove `E` (wait — there's no `E` here, but anyway the rule is `m=1 and not *o → drop E`. Since `*o` is true, do NOT drop. The condition `not *o` fails, so no removal.)
- Step 5b: No double letter at end.

### Final Stemmed Output

$$\text{RUNNING} \xrightarrow{\text{Porter Stemmer}} \text{RUN}$$

This is a **clean stem** that happens to also be a valid word.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Input Word | Porter Output | Lancaster Output | Lemmatizer Output | Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| *studies* | `studi` | `stud` | `study` | Lemmatizer preserves meaning |
| *running* | `run` | `run` | `run` | All agree |
| *better* | `better` | `better` | `good` | Stemmer fails (no context) |
| *university* | `univers` | `univers` | `university` | Over-stemming |
| *connection* | `connect` | `connect` | `connection` | Porter & Lancaster differ from lemma |
| *maximum* | `maximum` | `maxim` | `maximum` | Lancaster over-stems |

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Formulation of Stemmer Impact on Vocabulary

Let $V$ be the vocabulary of an unstemmed corpus with cardinality $\vert V \vert$. After stemming, the new vocabulary $V_s$ has cardinality $\vert V_s \vert$.

$$\text{Reduction Ratio} = 1 - \frac{\vert V_s \vert}{\vert V \vert}$$

**Example:** If a corpus has 50,000 unique tokens and stemming reduces it to 32,000 unique stems:

$$1 - \frac{32000}{50000} = 1 - 0.64 = 0.36 = 36\% \text{ reduction}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 30–40% vocabulary reduction translates to a similar sparsity reduction in a TF-IDF matrix, dramatically lowering RAM consumption and matrix-multiplication cost during ML training.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Zipfian Confirmation

Zipf's Law states:

$$f(r) = \frac{C}{r^{\alpha}}$$

where $f(r)$ is the frequency of the word at rank $r$, $C$ is a constant, and $\alpha \approx 1$.

In a corpus where the top 5 words ("the," "of," "and," "to," "a") account for ~25% of all tokens, stemming consolidates the long tail of low-frequency morphological variants (e.g., *running, runs, ran, runnable* all → *run*), creating a more uniform Zipf distribution and reducing the gap between the most-frequent and least-frequent stems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NLP Preprocessing Pipeline with Stemming

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates where stemming fits inside the standard text analytics pipeline and the major stemming algorithm families.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText[Raw Text Corpus] --> tokenize[Tokenization]
    tokenize --> lowercase[Lowercasing]
    lowercase --> punct[Remove Punctuation]
    punct --> stop[Remove Stopwords]
    stop --> stemChoice{Choose Stemmer?}
    
    stemChoice --> porter[Porter Stemmer]
    stemChoice --> snowball[Snowball Porter2 Stemmer]
    stemChoice --> lancaster[Lancaster Paice Stemmer]
    stemChoice --> regex[Regexp Stemmer]
    stemChoice --> lemma[Lemmatizer WordNet]
    
    porter --> out1[Stems]
    snowball --> out2[Stems]
    lancaster --> out3[Stems]
    regex --> out4[Stems]
    lemma --> out5[Lemma]
    
    out1 --> vectorize[Vectorization TFIDF or BoW]
    out2 --> vectorize
    out3 --> vectorize
    out4 --> vectorize
    out5 --> vectorize
    
    vectorize --> mlModel[Machine Learning Model]
    mlModel --> finalOut[Analytics Output]
```

## 4.2 Porter Stemmer Internal Step Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Input Word] --> s1a[Step 1a Plural SSES, IES, S Removal]
    s1a --> s1b[Step 1b Past Gerund EED, ED, ING]
    s1b --> s1b1{Sub-rule: Ends in AT, BL, IZ or Doubled Consonant?}
    s1b1 -->|Yes, AT BL IZ| addE1[Add E]
    s1b1 -->|Yes, Doubled| dropLast1[Drop Last Letter]
    s1b1 -->|No| s1c
    addE1 --> s1c
    dropLast1 --> s1c
    
    s1c[Step 1c Y to I if stem has vowel] --> s2[Step 2 Suffix Removal m greater than 0]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3 Derivational Suffix m greater than 0]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4 Aggressive Suffix m greater than 1]
    s4 --> s5a[Step 5a Final E Removal]
    s5a --> s5b[Step 5b Double Consonant Cleanup]
    s5b --> done[Final Stem Output]
```

## 4.3 Stemming vs Lemmatization — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Need Word Normalization?] --> q1{Speed Critical?}
    
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Dictionary Lookup Possible?}
    q1 -->|No| lemma[Use Lemmatizer spaCy or WordNet]
    
    q2 -->|Yes| porter[Use Porter or Snowball Stemmer]
    q2 -->|No, multilingual| snowball[Use Snowball Stemmer Multilingual]
    
    porter --> result1[Fast but Imprecise Stems]
    snowball --> result2[Fast but Imprecise Stems]
    lemma --> result3[Accurate Lemmas Slower]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map of Stemming Errors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Input Word] --> stemmer[Stemming Algorithm]
    
    stemmer --> correct[Correct Stem]
    stemmer --> over[Over-stemming]
    stemmer --> under[Under-stemming]
    stemmer --> nonword[Non-word Stem]
    
    over --> example1[Example: University to Univers]
    under --> example2[Example: Data and Datum remain separate]
    nonword --> example3[Example: Generalizations to Genr]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node represents a process or state in the stemming workflow. The arrows denote data flow direction. Notice how the Porter Stemmer has *internal sub-rules* in Step 1b — this is the only step with a conditional branch before exiting to Step 1c.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define stemming. How is it different from lemmatization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stemming is a heuristic text-normalization technique used in NLP that reduces a word to its base or root form by chopping off suffixes such as `-ing`, `-ed`, or `-s`. The resulting stem may not be a valid dictionary word. For example, *studies → studi*, *running → run*.

Lemmatization, in contrast, uses morphological analysis and a vocabulary (dictionary) lookup to return the true base form (lemma) of a word, which is always a valid word. For example, *better → good*, *running → run*. Lemmatization requires POS tagging to handle context correctly, while stemming does not. Stemming is faster but less accurate than lemmatization.

*(Mark Allocation: Definition 1m + Example 1m + Contrast 1m)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**List any three popular stemming algorithms. State one advantage and one limitation of the Porter Stemmer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three popular stemming algorithms:

1. **Porter Stemmer** (1980) — Rule-based suffix stripping.
2. **Lancaster Stemmer** (Paice-Husk) — Iterative rule-based; more aggressive.
3. **Snowball Stemmer** (Porter2) — Improved version with multi-language support.

**Advantage of Porter Stemmer:** Simple, fast, language-independent, and produces predictable outputs suitable for IR systems.

**Limitation of Porter Stemmer:** Often produces non-words (over-stemming), e.g., *generalizations → genr*; it does not handle irregular word forms like *ran* → *run*.

*(Mark Allocation: 3 algorithms 1.5m + Advantage 0.75m + Limitation 0.75m)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has internal choice (a) or (b). Either option may be answered. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. The total is **14 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the Porter Stemming Algorithm in detail. Describe the measure `m` and the conditions `(*v*)`, `(*d)`, and `(*o)` used in the algorithm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Porter Stemming Algorithm, developed by Martin Porter in 1980, is a rule-based algorithm that strips common morphological and inflectional endings from English words. It applies a sequence of condition-based rewrite rules in five cascading steps.

**The Measure `m`:**
The measure `m` is defined for any word stem as the number of VC pairs (vowel sequences followed by consonant sequences). For example:
- `troubles` → `trouble` (m = 2)
- `trees` → `tree` (m = 1)

**Conditions:**
- `(*v*)` — the stem contains at least one vowel.
- `(*d)` — the stem ends with a doubled consonant (e.g., `tt`, `ss`).
- `(*o)` — the stem ends with CVC where the second C is not `w`, `x`, or `y` (i.e., a short syllable).

**Five Steps of the Algorithm:**

1. **Step 1a (Plurals):** Removes `SSES → SS`, `IES → I`, `S → `.
2. **Step 1b (Past/Gerund):** Removes `EED → EE` (m>0), `ED → ` `(*v*)`, `ING → ` `(*v*)`, plus sub-rules for added `E`, doubled consonants, and short syllables.
3. **Step 1c (Y-to-I):** Replaces final `Y → I` if `(*v*)`.
4. **Step 2 (Long Suffix Removal, m>0):** Strips suffixes like `ATIONAL → ATE`, `TIONAL → TION`, `IZATION → IZE`, etc.
5. **Step 3 (Derivational, m>0):** Strips `ICATE → IC`, `ATIVE → `, `ALIZE → AL`, `ICAL → IC`, `FUL → `, `NESS → `.
6. **Step 4 (Aggressive Derivational, m>1):** Removes long suffixes such as `AL`, `ANCE`, `ENCE`, `ER`, `IC`, `ABLE`, `IBLE`, `ANT`, `EMENT`, `MENT`, `ENT`, `ION` (if preceded by `S` or `T`), `OU`, `ISM`, `ATE`, `ITI`, `OUS`, `IVE`, `IZE`.
7. **Step 5a (Final E):** `E → ` if m>1; `E → ` if m=1 and not `(*o)`.
8. **Step 5b (Double Consonant):** If m>1 and `(*d)` and `(*L)`, drop final letter.

**Conclusion:** Porter's algorithm is widely adopted due to its simplicity and speed, but it can over-stem (collapsing unrelated words) and produce non-words.

*(Mark Allocation: Definition & Measure 2m + Conditions 2m + Five Steps 3m)*

---

**(b) Apply the Porter Stemmer step-by-step to the word "GENERALIZATIONS" and show all intermediate outputs. Compare your final result with the output of a lemmatizer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We trace **"GENERALIZATIONS"** through all five steps.

**Initial Word:** `GENERALIZATIONS`, initial measure **m = 6**.

**Step 1a — Plurals:**
- Ends in `S` → apply `S → `.
- Result: `GENERALIZATION`, m = 6.

**Step 1b — Past Tense / Gerund:**
- Does not end in `EED`, `ED`, or `ING`. No change.
- Result: `GENERALIZATION`.

**Step 1c — Y to I:**
- Does not end in `Y`. No change.
- Result: `GENERALIZATION`.

**Step 2 — Suffix Removal (m>0):**
- Ends in `IZATION` → `IZE`.
- Result: `GENERALIZE`, m = 5.

**Step 3 — Derivational Suffix (m>0):**
- Ends in `ALIZE` → `AL`.
- Result: `GENERAL`, m = 3.

**Step 4 — Aggressive Suffix (m>1):**
- Ends in `AL` → drop.
- Result: `GENER`, m = 2.

**Step 5a — Final E Removal:**
- Ends in `E`, m=2 (>1) → drop `E`.
- Result: `GENR`, m = 1.

**Step 5b — Double Consonant Cleanup:**
- Does not end in doubled consonant. No change.
- **Final Porter Output:** `GENR`.

**Lemmatizer Output:** `GENERAL` (a valid dictionary word obtained through morphological analysis).

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Porter | Lemmatizer |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Final Stem/Lemma | `GENR` | `GENERAL` |
| Is it a valid word? | No | Yes |
| Method | Rule-based chopping | Dictionary lookup |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |

**Conclusion:** The Porter Stemmer over-stems the word to `GENR`, sacrificing linguistic accuracy for speed. Lemmatization preserves the meaningful root `GENERAL`, making it more suitable for downstream semantic tasks.

*(Mark Allocation: Step 1a-c 1m + Step 2-3 2m + Step 4-5 1.5m + Comparison Table 1.5m + Conclusion 1m)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) Discuss the major types of stemming algorithms. Compare Lancaster, Snowball, and RegexpStemmer with Porter Stemmer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Stemming algorithms fall into three broad families based on the strategy used to derive the stem.

**1. Truncation / Rule-Based Stemmers:**

- **Porter Stemmer (1980):** Uses five cascading steps of suffix-stripping rules with the measure `m`. It is conservative, fast, and produces relatively readable stems.
- **Snowball Stemmer (Porter2):** Refined version of Porter authored by the same creator. Supports 15+ languages including French, German, Spanish, Russian. Produces slightly cleaner English stems than Porter.
