# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Joint Probability to Chain Rule (LM Foundation)

**Goal:** Show that the joint probability of a sentence can be factorized into a product of conditional probabilities, which is the foundational step of every language model.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of joint probability for two events A and B:**

$$P(A, B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$$

This is read as: *"The probability of A and B equals the probability of A times the probability of B given A."*

**Step 2 — Extend to three events using the chain rule of probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A, B, C) &= P(A) \cdot P(B, C \mid A) \\
           &= P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A, B)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reason:* We treat $(B, C)$ as a single event and apply Step 1, then expand $P(B, C \mid A) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A, B)$.

**Step 3 — Generalize to a sequence of $n$ tokens $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n) &= P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1}) \\
                         &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the *Markov assumption* of order $n-1$:** assume each token depends only on the previous $n-1$ tokens:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(w_i \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}, \ldots, w_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

This reduces an intractable number of parameters (exponential in $n$) to a manageable one.

> [!NOTE]
> The Markov assumption is what makes the language model a **finite-state** model. The state at time $i$ is the tuple $(w_{i-n+1}, \ldots, w_{i-1})$, which takes values in a finite set $\vert V \vert^{n-1}$. The next state is a deterministic function of the current state and the next emitted token. This is a DFA.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Likelihood Estimate for a Bigram LM

**Goal:** Derive the standard formula for estimating bigram probabilities from a corpus.

**Step 1 — Define a corpus as a sequence of $N$ tokens:** $C = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_N)$.

**Step 2 — The likelihood of the corpus under a bigram LM is:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(c_i \mid c_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c_0 = \langle \text{sos} \rangle$ (a special start-of-sentence token).

**Step 3 — We want to choose $P(c_i \mid c_{i-1})$ that *maximizes* $P(C)$.** Take the log (monotonic transformation, so maximizing log is equivalent):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log P(C) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(c_i \mid c_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Subject to the constraint** $\sum_{w} P(w \mid u) = 1$ for every history $u$ (probabilities sum to 1), apply Lagrange multipliers. The closed-form result is the **count ratio**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}(c_i \mid c_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(c_{i-1}, c_i)}{C(c_{i-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C(\cdot)$ denotes the count in the training corpus. This is the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)**.

**Step 5 — Concrete numerical example.** Suppose in a corpus of 100,000 bigrams, the pair "(the, cat)" appears 500 times and "(the, ...)" (any word after "the") appears 5,000 times. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) = \dfrac{C(\text{the}, \text{cat})}{C(\text{the})} = \dfrac{500}{5000} = 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

i.e., **10%** of the time a word follows "the", it is "cat" in this corpus.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** A bigram that never appears in training gets $P = 0/0$ or simply $0$. Multiplying any zero across the chain rule gives $P(\text{sentence}) = 0$, making the entire LM log-infinite on unseen test data. This is the **zero-frequency problem** and is fixed by *smoothing* (Laplace, Kneser-Ney, etc.).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The HMM Forward Algorithm (FA-Style Recursion)

A **Hidden Markov Model** is a probabilistic finite automaton. The states $Q = \{q_1, \ldots, q_N\}$ are *hidden*; the observations $O = (o_1, \ldots, o_T)$ are *visible*. We want to compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$, the probability of the observation sequence.

**Step 1 — Define the *forward variable*:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, q_t = j \mid \lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$

i.e., the probability of seeing the first $t$ observations *and* being in state $j$ at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Initialization (no observations yet):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\pi_j = P(q_1 = j)$ is the initial-state distribution and $b_j(o_1) = P(o_1 \mid q_1 = j)$ is the emission probability for state $j$ observing symbol $o_1$.

**Step 3 — Recursion (this is the FA transition rule applied to probabilities):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reading:* "To be in state $j$ at time $t+1$ having just observed $o_{t+1}$, you must have been in *some* state $i$ at time $t$ (sum over $i$), transitioned to $j$ with probability $a_{ij}$, and emitted $o_{t+1}$ with probability $b_j(o_{t+1})$."

**Step 4 — Termination:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reading:* "The total probability of the observation sequence is the sum of the probabilities of being in *any* state at the final time step."

**Step 5 — Numerical micro-example.** Let $N=2$, $\pi_1 = 0.6, \pi_2 = 0.4$, and the first observation $o_1 = a$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1(1) &= \pi_1 \cdot b_1(a) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30 \\
\alpha_1(2) &= \pi_2 \cdot b_2(a) = 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.28
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $P(\text{first obs is } a) = 0.30 + 0.28 = 0.58$.

> [!NOTE]
> This recursion is **identical in form to a generalized finite-automaton transition** — the only differences being (a) the transitions are *probabilities* rather than Boolean, and (b) the sum is over all predecessor states (the "nondeterministic" version). In CS theory, an HMM is a **probabilistic NFA with hidden states and observable emissions**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Perplexity from a Trained LM

**Goal:** Derive the most widely used evaluation metric for language models.

**Step 1 — Perplexity is defined as the exponentiated average negative log-likelihood (base $e$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PP}(W) = \exp\left( -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{i-1}) \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the chain-rule product form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and simplify:** $P(W) = \prod_i P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$, so

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PP}(W) &= \left[\prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})\right]^{-1/N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.** For a 3-word sentence $(w_1, w_2, w_3)$ with $P(w_1) = 0.1$, $P(w_2 \mid w_1) = 0.2$, $P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(W) &= 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.01 \\
\text{PP}(W) &= (0.01)^{-1/3} = (100)^{1/3} \approx 4.64
\end{aligned}
$$

So this sentence is as "surprising" as choosing uniformly among $\approx 4.64$ alternatives at each step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lower perplexity = better model.** Modern neural LMs achieve perplexities in the 10–30 range on the Penn Treebank; n-gram LMs in the 60–150 range; uniform-random models in the $\vert V \vert \approx 10^5$ range.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DFA State Diagram — Recognizing "Strings Ending in 'ing'"

The diagram below is a 3-state DFA over alphabet $\Sigma = \{a, \ldots, z\}$ that accepts exactly those English words ending in the suffix *"ing"*.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0
    s0 --> s0 : a..z except i
    s0 --> s1 : i
    s1 --> s2 : n
    s2 --> sF : g
    sF --> sF : a..z except g
    s1 --> s0 : a..z except n
    s2 --> s0 : a..z except g
    sF --> s2 : n
    sF --> s0 : a..z except n,g
    s0 --> sF : i,g (shortcut)
    sF --> [*]
```

*Reading the diagram:*
- State `s0` is the *start* state and represents "the suffix so far is not building toward 'ing'."
- State `s1` represents "the last character seen was `i`."
- State `s2` represents "the last two characters were `in`."
- State `sF` (the accepting state) represents "the last three characters were `ing` and we have not yet seen a different character."
- Every state loops back to `s0` on any "wrong" input, ensuring *prefix-free* acceptance.

## 4.2 HMM Topology for Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging

A direct application of the finite-state language model idea: a Hidden Markov Model for POS tagging treats the *tag sequence* (Noun, Verb, Adjective, ...) as a hidden finite-state path and the *words* as observations emitted from each state.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start((START)) -->|π_N| N[Noun]
    Start -->|π_V| V[Verb]
    Start -->|π_A| A[Adj]
    N -->|a_NN| N
    N -->|a_NV| V
    N -->|a_NA| A
    V -->|a_VN| N
    V -->|a_VV| V
    V -->|a_VA| A
    A -->|a_AN| N
    A -->|a_AV| V
    A -->|a_AA| A
    A -->|a_AEND| End((END))
    V -->|a_VEND| End
    N -->|a_NEND| End
    N -.->|b_N cat| Obs1((word: cat))
    N -.->|b_N dog| Obs2((word: dog))
    V -.->|b_V runs| Obs3((word: runs))
    A -.->|b_A quick| Obs4((word: quick))
```

*Reading the diagram:*
- **Solid arrows** are *transitions* $a_{ij}$ (probability of moving from tag $i$ to tag $j$).
- **Dashed arrows** are *emissions* $b_j(w)$ (probability of word $w$ being generated from tag $j$).
- This single picture *is* a probabilistic finite automaton.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — How FA Pieces Fit into a Modern Language Model Pipeline

For topics that require a *system view*, the diagram below maps the **complete data-analytics pipeline** that connects finite automata at the bottom to a fully-trained language model at the top.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Raw Text Input]
        A1[Web Corpus, News, Social Media] --> A2[Raw String s]
    end

    subgraph Preprocess[FA-Based Preprocessing]
        A2 --> B1[DFA Tokenizer]
        B1 --> B2[NFA Regex Cleaner]
        B2 --> B3[FSM Sentence Splitter]
    end

    subgraph Vocabulary[Vocabulary Construction]
        B3 --> C1[Subword Segmentation BPE - FA based]
        C1 --> C2[Vocabulary V of size 50000]
    end

    subgraph StatisticalLM[Statistical Language Model]
        C2 --> D1[Count n-grams in corpus]
        D1 --> D2[Apply MLE / Kneser-Ney smoothing]
        D2 --> D3[n-gram Probability Table P of size V to the n]
    end

    subgraph NeuralLM[Neural Language Model]
        D3 --> E1[Word Embeddings]
        E1 --> E2[RNN or Transformer Backbone]
        E2 --> E3[Softmax Output Layer over V]
    end

    subgraph Evaluation[Model Evaluation]
        E3 --> F1[Compute Perplexity on test set]
        F1 --> F2[Compare with baseline]
    end

    subgraph Deployment[Application]
        F2 --> G1[Autocomplete / Next Word Prediction]
        F2 --> G2[Text Generation / Chatbot]
        F2 --> G3[Sentiment Classification]
        F2 --> G4[Machine Translation]
    end
```

*Reading the architecture:*
- The **bottom three subgraphs** (Preprocess, Vocabulary, StatisticalLM) are *purely finite-state*.
- The **NeuralLM block** is the *only* part that abandons finite-state in favor of continuous representations.
- A KTU examiner may ask you to *point at any block* and explain which FA concept underlies it.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]
**Define a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA). List and explain the components of its formal definition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DFA is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
1. **$Q$** is a finite non-empty set of states, e.g., $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$.
2. **$\Sigma$** is a finite input alphabet, e.g., $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$.
3. **$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$** is the transition function, e.g., $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_1$.
4. **$q_0 \in Q$** is the start state.
5. **$F \subseteq Q$** is the set of accepting (final) states, e.g., $F = \{q_2\}$.

The machine reads an input string one symbol at a time, transitions deterministically, and *accepts* the string if it ends in a state $\in F$; otherwise it rejects. Example: a DFA that accepts all binary strings ending in `1` has $Q = \{q_0, q_1\}$, $\delta(q_0, 0) = q_0$, $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_1$, $\delta(q_1, 0) = q_0$, $\delta(q_1, 1) = q_1$, and $F = \{q_1\}$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]
**What is a Language Model? Write the chain rule of probability used in language modeling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Language Model (LM)** is a probability distribution over sequences of tokens drawn from a vocabulary $V$. It assigns a numerical likelihood to every possible sentence, enabling tasks such as next-word prediction, machine translation, and speech recognition.

**Chain rule of probability for a sentence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$:**

$$P(W) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{n-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$$

This product decomposition is the foundation on which all statistical and neural language models are built. Practical models (n-grams) truncate the conditioning context to the previous $n-1$ tokens to make the model tractable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Define a finite automaton. Construct a DFA that accepts all strings over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ containing the substring `"01"`. Draw its state transition diagram and give its formal 5-tuple definition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of a statistical n-gram language model. Show how the chain rule of probability and the Markov assumption lead to the bigram probability formula. Compute the bigram probabilities for a toy corpus and show the zero-frequency problem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
A finite automaton is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where $Q$ is a finite set of states, $\Sigma$ is the input alphabet, $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ is the transition function, $q_0$ is the start state, and $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of accepting states.

**DFA Construction for "contains 01" (3 marks):**
We need a DFA that remembers whether the most recent relevant symbol was a `0` (so that a following `1` would complete the substring) and a state indicating that the substring `"01"` has already been seen.

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start — nothing seen yet)
- $F = \{q_2\}$ (the substring `"01"` has been seen)
- $\delta$:

| State | Input 0 | Input 1 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$ | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| $q_2$ | $q_2$ | $q_2$ |

**State Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q1 : 0
    q0 --> q0 : 1
    q1 --> q1 : 0
    q1 --> q2 : 1
    q2 --> q2 : 0
    q2 --> q2 : 1
    q2 --> [*]
```

**Verification (1 mark):**
- String `"001"`: $q_0 \to q_1 \to q_1 \to q_2$ ✓ accept.
- String `"100"`: $q_0 \to q_0 \to q_1 \to q_1$ ✗ reject.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Concept (2 marks):** A *statistical n-gram LM* estimates the probability of the next word given the previous $n-1$ words from corpus counts. It is a *finite-state* model: the state encodes the last $n-1$ tokens, and the number of states is bounded by $\vert V \vert^{n-1}$.

**Chain rule + Markov assumption (3 marks):**
By the chain rule of probability, $P(w_1, \ldots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$. Direct estimation of $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ is infeasible (too many contexts). The **Markov assumption** approximates:

$$P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1})$$

For a bigram (n=2) model: $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$.

The MLE estimate from a corpus is: $\hat{P}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$.

**Worked Example (1 mark):** Toy corpus: `["<s> I love NLP </s>", "<s> I love coding </s>"]`. Bigram counts:
- $C(\text{I, love}) = 2$, $C(\text{I}) = 2 \Rightarrow \hat{P}(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = 1.0$
- $C(\text{love, NLP}) = 1$, $C(\text{love}) = 2 \Rightarrow \hat{P}(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = 0.5$
- $C(\text{love, coding}) = 1 \Rightarrow \hat{P}(\text{coding} \mid \text{love}) = 0.5$
- $C(\text{NLP, </s>}) = 1 \Rightarrow \hat{P}(\text{</s>} \mid \text{NLP}) = 1.0$

**Zero-frequency problem (1 mark):** A bigram like `(love, *new_word*)` not in the corpus gets $\hat{P} = 0/2 = 0$. The probability of the test sentence *"I love new_word"* becomes $1.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.0 \times 1.0 = 0$, so $\log P = -\infty$ and **perplexity is infinite**. *Fix:* apply smoothing (e.g., Laplace: $\hat{P}_{Lap}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert}$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** What is a Hidden Markov Model (HMM)? Explain how it is mathematically equivalent to a probabilistic finite automaton. Derive the forward algorithm recursion for computing the probability of an observation sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the Perplexity metric for evaluating a language model. Show step-by-step how to compute the perplexity of a short test sentence given a unigram LM. Discuss why lower perplexity indicates a better model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Definition of HMM (2 marks):** A Hidden Markov Model is a 5-tuple $\lambda = (S, O, A, B, \pi)$ where:
- $S = \{s_1, \ldots, s_N\}$ — finite set of *hidden* states.
- $O = \{o_1, \ldots, o_T\}$ — observation sequence.
- $A = [a_{ij}]$ — transition probability matrix, $a_{ij} = P(s_t = j \mid s_{t-1} = i)$.
- $B = [b_j(o_t)]$ — emission probability matrix, $b_j(o_t) = P(o_t \mid s_t = j)$.
- $\pi = [\pi_i]$ — initial state distribution, $\pi_i = P(s_1 = i)$.

**HMM = Probabilistic FA (2 marks):** The state set $S$ of an HMM is *finite*, exactly like the state set $Q$ of an FA. The transition function $\delta$ of an FA is replaced by a *probability distribution* $A$ over next states. The HMM additionally emits an *observation* $o_t$ from each state (an FA only "emits" the empty symbol by moving to a new state). Therefore, an HMM is a *finite-state machine with probabilistic transitions and probabilistic emissions* — a natural stochastic generalization of an NFA.

**Forward Algorithm Derivation (3 marks):**
We want to compute $P(O \mid \lambda) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_T \mid \lambda)$.

Define the forward variable:

$$\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, s_t = j \mid \lambda)$$

**Initialization** ($t=1$): The first observation $o_1$ is generated from initial state $j$ with probability $\pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)$.

$$\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1), \quad j = 1, \ldots, N$$

**Induction** ($t = 1, 2, \ldots, T-1$): To be in state $j$ at time $t+1$ having observed $o_1, \ldots, o_{t+1}$, we sum over all possible previous states $i$:

$$\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})$$

**Termination:** The total probability of the observation sequence is the probability of being in *any* state at time $T$:

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)$$

This is the *exact probabilistic analogue* of an FA's reachability computation.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** Perplexity (PP) is the standard evaluation metric for probabilistic language models. For a test corpus $W = (w_1, \ldots, w_N)$ and an LM that assigns probability $P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ to the $i$-th word:

$$\text{PP}(W) = \left[\prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})\right]^{-1/N} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})\right)$$

Equivalently, using base-2 logs, $\text{PP} = 2^{H(W)/N}$ where $H(W) = -\sum_i \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$ is the *cross-entropy* in bits.

**Step-by-step computation (3 marks):** Let the test sentence be $W = (w_1, w_2, w_3) = (\text{I}, \text{love}, \text{NLP})$ and let a unigram LM assign:

- $P(\text{I}) = 0.10$
- $P(\text{love}) = 0.05$
- $P(\text{NLP}) = 0.02$

**Step 1:** Compute the joint probability of the sentence:

$$P(W) = 0.10 \times 0.05 \times 0.02 = 0.0001 = 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2:** Take the negative log-likelihood (natural log):

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\log P(W) &= -\log(10^{-4}) = 4 \log_{e}(10) \approx 4 \times 2.3026 = 9.2104
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $N = 3$:

$$-\dfrac{1}{N} \log P(W) = \dfrac{9.2104}{3} = 3.0701$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate:

$$\text{PP}(W) = e^{3.0701} \approx 21.57$$

So the model is "as confused" as one that chooses uniformly from ~21.6 alternatives at each step.

**Why lower is better (2 marks):** Perplexity is the *effective branching factor* of the LM — the number of equally likely choices the model faces on average. A perfect model that assigns $P = 1$ to the test sentence has PP = 1. A uniform model over a vocabulary of size $\vert V \vert$ has PP = $\vert V \vert$. As the model becomes *more confident and accurate* in its predictions, PP decreases monotonically. Modern neural LMs achieve PP ≈ 15–30 on the Penn Treebank; n-gram + Kneser-Ney models ≈ 60–80; uniform models ≈ $10^5$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong formula for the 5-tuple:** Students often write $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^Q$ for a DFA. That is the NFA signature. In a DFA, the result is a *single* state from $Q$. Examiners allocate 1 mark strictly for this distinction.
> 2. **Confusing the Forward variable:** The HMM forward variable is often written as $P(o_1, \ldots, o_t \mid \lambda)$ by students. The *correct* definition is $P(o_1, \ldots, o_t, q_t = j \mid \lambda)$ — the joint probability of the partial observations *and* the state being $j$. Omitting the state-conditional is a -1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Perplexity units:** Always specify whether you used natural log or $\log_2$. Both are valid but must be declared explicitly. Mixing them gives a numerical mismatch of a factor of $\log_e 2 \approx 0.693$.
> 4. **Sparsity trap:** If your bigram MLE is $\dfrac{0}{0}$, you must state the smoothing technique used, not leave it as undefined. Examiners deduct 1 mark for "unspecified division by zero."
> 5. **Forgetting $\langle \text{sos} \rangle$ and $\langle \text{eos} \rangle$ tokens:** A language model over sentences must add explicit start- and end-of-sentence tokens. Omitting these gives wrong bigram estimates for sentence-initial and sentence-final words.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save before the exam)**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ A **DFA** is the 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$.
- ☐ An **NFA** allows $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\} \rightarrow 2^Q$ (multiple next states + $\varepsilon$-transitions).
- ☐ A **Language Model** is a *probability distribution* over token sequences.
- ☐ A **Hidden Markov Model** is a *probabilistic finite automaton* with hidden states and observable emissions.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- ☐ Chain rule: $P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})$.
- ☐ Bigram MLE: $\hat{P}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$.
- ☐ Laplace smoothing: $\hat{P}_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert}$.
- ☐ HMM Forward recursion: $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})$.
- ☐ HMM Termination: $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)$.
- ☐ Perplexity: $\text{PP}(W) = \left[\prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1})\right]^{-1/N} = 2^{H(W)/N}$.

### Engineering Connections
- ☐ Regex engines (Python `re`, Java `Pattern`, Perl) are **backtracking NFAs**.
- ☐ Lexical analyzers in compilers are **DFAs** built from regular expressions via Thompson's construction.
- ☐ Tokenizers in modern NLP (spaCy, BPE used in GPT) operate on **finite-state character transducers**.
- ☐ POS taggers, NER systems, and gene finders use **HMMs** (i.e., probabilistic FAs).
- ☐ The **n-gram model is a DFA** with $\vert V \vert^{n-1}$ states — the reason neural LMs were invented is that $\vert V \vert^{n-1}$ explodes for $n \geq 5$.

### Common Numerical Benchmarks
- ☐ Penn Treebank perplexity: ~140 (5-gram), ~60 (KN smoothing), ~20–35 (neural).
- ☐ Uniform random model: PP = $\vert V \vert$ ≈ $10^5$.
- ☐ ASCII alphabet size: 128. Unicode basic plane: 65,536.

### Frequently Asked Examination Themes
- ☐ Construct a DFA / NFA for a given language description and prove correctness.
- ☐ Apply subset construction to convert NFA → DFA.
- ☐ Compute bigram / trigram probabilities from a small corpus.
- ☐ Apply Laplace or Kneser-Ney smoothing to a small probability table.
- ☐ Implement one or two steps of the HMM Forward algorithm on a 2-state toy HMM.
- ☐ Compute perplexity of a test sentence given a probability table.
- ☐ Justify why an n-gram LM is a finite-state model (state = previous $n-1$ tokens).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Query Likelihood Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Query Likelihood Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Query Likelihood Model (QLM)** is a foundational **probabilistic information retrieval (IR)** framework in which each document $d$ in a collection is assumed to generate a probabilistic **Language Model** $M_d$. The retrieval task is reformulated as: given a user query $q$, rank documents in **descending order of the probability $P(q \mid d)$**, i.e., the likelihood that the query $q$ would have been generated by sampling from the language model of document $d$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *The Query Likelihood Model assumes that the user has a reasonable idea of the terms that are likely to appear in a "ideal" document that would satisfy their information need, and formulates the problem as ranking documents by the probability of generating the query from a document language model.*

Mathematically, applying **Bayes' Theorem** to invert the conditional probability:

$$P(d \mid q) = \frac{P(q \mid d) \cdot P(d)}{P(q)}$$

Since $P(q)$ is identical for all documents (a normalizing constant) and $P(d)$ is generally taken as **uniform a priori** (no prior preference among documents), the ranking criterion reduces to maximizing the query generation likelihood:

$$\text{score}(q, d) \equiv P(q \mid d)$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective Library" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a vast library and ask the librarian, *"Find me documents that could plausibly have 'spoken' my question."* The Query Likelihood Model does exactly this:

- **Document = A Unique Speaker's "Voice"** — Each document $d$ is treated as a person with a distinctive vocabulary distribution (their "language model"). A sports article sounds nothing like a legal brief.
- **Query = A Random Sentence Sampled from That Voice** — Your search query is treated as a *short, possibly imperfect sample* drawn from the underlying vocabulary distribution of the *ideal* document.
- **Retrieval = Reverse Engineering** — The system doesn't ask *"Which document is about my topic?"*; it asks *"If I were reading this document, how surprised would I be to encounter my exact query words?"* Documents that "sound like" they could have produced the query score higher.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The QLM reframes retrieval from a *matching* problem to a *generative probability estimation* problem — a subtle but powerful shift that underlies modern search engines, BM25 (its smoothed descendant), and large language model-based retrieval (RAG systems).

### 1.3 Key Components at a Glance

| Component | Symbol | Standard Choice |
|---|---|---|
| Document Language Model | $M_d$ | **Multinomial Unigram** |
| Smoothing Method | $S(\cdot)$ | **Jelinek-Mercer** or **Dirichlet** |
| Term Frequency in $d$ | $tf(t,d)$ | Raw count |
| Document Length | $\vert d \vert$ | Token count |
| Collection Probability | $P(t \mid C)$ | $tf(t,C) / \sum_t tf(t,C)$ |
| Smoothing Weight (J-M) | $\lambda$ | Typically **0.1 to 0.7** |
| Smoothing Prior (Dirichlet) | $\mu$ | Typically **1000 to 3000** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generative Story (Operational Logic Steps)

The Query Likelihood Model operates through the following logical pipeline:

1. **Document Preprocessing:** Each document $d$ in collection $C$ is tokenized, normalized (lowercased, stemmed, stop-word filtered), yielding a term sequence.
2. **Language Model Estimation:** A probability distribution $P(t \mid d)$ is estimated over all vocabulary terms $t \in V$, representing how likely each term is to occur in document $d$.
3. **Query Generation Assumption:** The query $q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)$ is treated as an *independent* random sample drawn from $M_d$ (the **unigram** or **bag-of-words** assumption — order of terms is ignored).
4. **Likelihood Computation:** Compute $P(q \mid d) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid d)$ using the independence assumption.
5. **Smoothing Application:** Replace zero-probability terms with non-zero estimates by interpolating with the collection model $P(t \mid C)$.
6. **Ranking:** Sort all documents by $P(q \mid d)$ in descending order and return the top-$k$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why unigram (bag-of-words)?** Term order independence makes the model tractable and statistically robust for short queries. The **exchangeability** assumption is the cornerstone.
- **Why smoothing is mandatory?** MLE assigns $P(t \mid d) = 0$ for any term $t$ not seen in $d$. A single unseen query term then makes the entire product $\prod P(q_i \mid d) = 0$, killing all likelihood scores — smoothing prevents this.
- **Why use log-likelihood?** Products of small probabilities underflow numerically; taking logarithms converts products to sums without changing the ranking order.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know for KTU University Exams**. Memorize the **form**, the **role of each variable**, and the **typical parameter values**.

| # | Formula / Equation | Components & Role | Typical Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{score}(q,d) = P(q \mid d) = \prod_{t \in q} P(t \mid d)^{tf(t,q)}$ | Core ranking function (likelihood) | — |
| 2 | $\log P(q \mid d) = \sum_{t \in q} tf(t,q) \cdot \log P(t \mid d)$ | Log-form (numerical stability) | — |
| 3 | $P_{MLE}(t \mid d) = \dfrac{tf(t,d)}{\vert d \vert}$ | Maximum Likelihood Estimator | Unsmoothed; zero for unseen terms |
| 4 | $P(t \mid C) = \dfrac{tf(t,C)}{\sum_{t'} tf(t',C)}$ | Collection model (background) | Computed once globally |
| 5 | $P_{JM}(t \mid d) = \lambda \cdot P_{MLE}(t \mid d) + (1-\lambda) \cdot P(t \mid C)$ | **Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing** | $\lambda \in [0.1, 0.7]$ |
| 6 | $P_{Dir}(t \mid d) = \dfrac{tf(t,d) + \mu \cdot P(t \mid C)}{\vert d \vert + \mu}$ | **Dirichlet Prior Smoothing** | $\mu \approx 1000\text{–}3000$ |
| 7 | $P_{Abs}(t \mid d) = \dfrac{\max(tf(t,d) - \delta, 0)}{\vert d \vert} + \dfrac{\delta \cdot \vert d \vert_u}{\vert d \vert} \cdot P(t \mid C)$ | **Absolute Discounting** | $\delta \in [0.0, 1.0]$ |
| 8 | $KL(M_q \Vert M_d) = \sum_{t} P(t \mid q) \cdot \log \dfrac{P(t \mid q)}{P(t \mid d)}$ | KL-divergence equivalence (advanced) | $P(q \mid d) \propto \exp(-KL)$ |

**Notation Cheat-Sheet:**
- $tf(t,d)$ = frequency of term $t$ in document $d$
- $\vert d \vert$ = total number of tokens in $d$
- $\vert d \vert_u$ = number of *unique* terms in $d$
- $tf(t,C)$ = frequency of term $t$ in the entire collection $C$
- $tf(t,q)$ = frequency of term $t$ in the query $q$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Query Likelihood Model is not merely academic — it directly underpins:
- **Modern search engines** (BM25 is a special case of QLM with Dirichlet smoothing)
- **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** in LLM systems (Q is the user prompt, D is the vector store corpus)
- **Plagiarism detection** (likelihood of a candidate document generating the source)
- **Spam filtering** (likelihood of ham/spam class generating observed words)
- **Speech recognition** (acoustic/language model probability factorization)
- **Recommendation systems** (treating user history as a "query" over item "documents")

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the QLM Ranking Function from Bayes' Theorem

We start from the classical probabilistic ranking principle, which states that the optimal document for a query $q$ is the one maximizing the probability of relevance $P(R=1 \mid d, q)$. The Query Likelihood Model takes a *different but equivalent* path by treating $q$ as a sample.

**Step 1: Posterior Probability of Document Given Query**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(d \mid q) = \frac{P(q \mid d) \cdot P(d)}{P(q)}$$

*Reasoning:* $P(q \mid d)$ is the *likelihood* of $q$ under $d$'s model; $P(d)$ is the *prior* probability of observing $d$; $P(q)$ is the *evidence*, acting as a normalizer.

**Step 2: Dropping the Constant Terms**

Since the ranking depends only on $d$ and we compare across all documents for a *fixed* $q$:
- $P(q)$ is constant with respect to $d$ (same query) — **drop it**.
- $P(d)$ is typically assumed uniform: $P(d) = \frac{1}{N}$ for all $N$ documents — **drop it** (it cancels in the ranking).

This yields the operational ranking criterion:

$$\text{score}(q, d) \propto P(q \mid d)$$

**Step 3: Apply the Unigram (Bag-of-Words) Independence Assumption**

The query $q$ consists of terms $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$. Assuming terms are generated independently:

$$P(q \mid d) = P(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n \mid d) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid d)$$

*Reasoning:* The unigram assumption sacrifices term-order information for tractability — a deliberate trade-off that empirical results have validated for short queries.

**Step 4: Group Identical Terms Using $tf(t,q)$**

For terms appearing multiple times in $q$, we can rewrite the product by collecting duplicates:

$$P(q \mid d) = \prod_{t \in q} P(t \mid d)^{tf(t,q)}$$

**Step 5: Apply Monotonic Logarithm for Numerical Stability**

Since $\log(\cdot)$ is strictly monotonic, $\arg\max_d P(q \mid d) = \arg\max_d \log P(q \mid d)$:

$$\log P(q \mid d) = \sum_{t \in q} tf(t,q) \cdot \log P(t \mid d)$$

This is the **final log-likelihood scoring equation** used in practice.

### 3.2 Derivation of the MLE Estimate $P_{MLE}(t \mid d)$

For a multinomial distribution over the vocabulary, the probability of observing the term sequence in $d$ is:

$$P(d \mid M_d) = \prod_{t \in V} P(t \mid d)^{tf(t,d)}$$

The log-likelihood is:

$$\log P(d \mid M_d) = \sum_{t \in V} tf(t,d) \cdot \log P(t \mid d)$$

To find the MLE, we maximize this subject to the constraint $\sum_t P(t \mid d) = 1$ using a **Lagrange multiplier** $\alpha$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t} tf(t,d) \cdot \log P(t \mid d) + \alpha \left( 1 - \sum_{t} P(t \mid d) \right)$$

Setting $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial P(t \mid d) = 0$:

$$\frac{tf(t,d)}{P(t \mid d)} - \alpha = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P(t \mid d) = \frac{tf(t,d)}{\alpha}$$

Applying the constraint $\sum_t P(t \mid d) = 1$:

$$\sum_{t} \frac{tf(t,d)}{\alpha} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \sum_{t} tf(t,d) = \vert d \vert$$

Substituting back:

$$P_{MLE}(t \mid d) = \frac{tf(t,d)}{\vert d \vert}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Dirichlet Prior (Bayesian) Smoothing

The MLE assigns $P_{MLE}(t \mid d) = 0$ for unseen terms — a fatal flaw. We treat the language model parameter $\theta_d$ as a **random variable** with a **Dirichlet prior** $\text{Dir}(\mu \cdot P(t \mid C))$, where $\mu > 0$ is a concentration parameter.

The Dirichlet prior has the form:

$$P(\theta_d) \propto \prod_{t} \theta_{d,t}^{\mu \cdot P(t \mid C) - 1}$$

The posterior under the Dirichlet-multinomial conjugacy is also Dirichlet:

$$P(\theta_d \mid d) = \text{Dir}\big( tf(t_1,d) + \mu P(t_1 \mid C), \ldots, tf(t_V,d) + \mu P(t_V \mid C) \big)$$

The posterior **mean** (which minimizes expected KL-divergence, an optimal choice for retrieval) is:

$$P(t \mid d) = \frac{tf(t,d) + \mu \cdot P(t \mid C)}{\sum_{t'} (tf(t',d) + \mu \cdot P(t' \mid C))} = \frac{tf(t,d) + \mu \cdot P(t \mid C)}{\vert d \vert + \mu}$$

**Interpretation of the smoothing parameter $\mu$:**
- Acts as the **"pseudo-count"** of observations from the collection model.
- When $\vert d \vert \gg \mu$: $P(t \mid d) \approx P_{MLE}(t \mid d)$ (long documents are trusted more).
- When $\vert d \vert \ll \mu$: $P(t \mid d) \approx P(t \mid C)$ (short documents fall back to background).
- Typical optimal value: $\mu \in [1000, 3000]$, which approximates the average document length in many corpora.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Compute Jelinek-Mercer smoothed query likelihood scores.

**Setup:**
- Query: $q =$ "data analytics"
- Two documents:
  - $d_1 =$ "data mining analytics big data" ($\vert d_1 \vert = 5$)
  - $d_2 =$ "weather sunny forecast" ($\vert d_2 \vert = 3$)
- Collection $C$: Assume $P(\text{data} \mid C) = 0.4$, $P(\text{analytics} \mid C) = 0.2$, $P(\text{mining} \mid C) = 0.1$, $P(\text{big} \mid C) = 0.1$, $P(\text{weather} \mid C) = 0.1$, $P(\text{sunny} \mid C) = 0.05$, $P(\text{forecast} \mid C) = 0.05$.
- Smoothing parameter: $\lambda = 0.7$.

**Step 1: Compute $P_{MLE}(t \mid d)$ for each document**

For $d_1$:
- $P_{MLE}(\text{data} \mid d_1) = 2/5 = 0.4$
- $P_{MLE}(\text{analytics} \mid d_1) = 1/5 = 0.2$

For $d_2$:
- $P_{MLE}(\text{data} \mid d_2) = 0/3 = 0.0$
- $P_{MLE}(\text{analytics} \mid d_2) = 0/3 = 0.0$

**Step 2: Apply Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing**

$$P_{JM}(t \mid d) = 0.7 \cdot P_{MLE}(t \mid d) + 0.3 \cdot P(t \mid C)$$

For $d_1$:
- $P_{JM}(\text{data} \mid d_1) = 0.7(0.4) + 0.3(0.4) = 0.28 + 0.12 = 0.40$
- $P_{JM}(\text{analytics} \mid d_1) = 0.7(0.2) + 0.3(0.2) = 0.14 + 0.06 = 0.20$

For $d_2$:
- $P_{JM}(\text{data} \mid d_2) = 0.7(0) + 0.3(0.4) = 0.12$
- $P_{JM}(\text{analytics} \mid d_2) = 0.7(0) + 0.3(0.2) = 0.06$

**Step 3: Compute the Log-Likelihood Score**

$$\log P(q \mid d) = \sum_{t \in q} \log P_{JM}(t \mid d)$$

For $d_1$:
$$\log P(q \mid d_1) = \log(0.40) + \log(0.20) = -0.916 + -1.609 = -2.525$$

For $d_2$:
$$\log P(q \mid d_2) = \log(0.12) + \log(0.06) = -2.120 + -2.813 = -4.933$$

**Step 4: Rank Documents**

Since $-2.525 > -4.933$ (less negative is better), $d_1$ ranks **higher** than $d_2$ — correctly identifying the more relevant document.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End QLM Retrieval Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[User Query q] --> nodeB[Query Tokenization and Normalization]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Query Term Vector tf t q]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Document Language Model Md for each d in C]
    nodeD --> nodeE[MLE Probability Estimation P t d equals tf t d divided by length of d]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Smoothing Module]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Jelinek Mercer with lambda]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Dirichlet Prior with mu]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Absolute Discounting with delta]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG[Log Likelihood Score log P q d]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
    nodeF3 --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Score Aggregation over Query Terms]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Descending Sort of Documents by Score]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Top k Ranked Documents Returned to User]
    nodeK[Collection Model P t C] --> nodeF
    nodeL[Raw Document Collection C] --> nodeD
    nodeL --> nodeK
```

### 4.2 Smoothing Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Document d and Query q] --> nodeS2{Are query terms absent from d}
    nodeS2 -- Yes --> nodeS3[Apply Smoothing to Avoid Zero Probability]
    nodeS2 -- No --> nodeS4[Use MLE Plus Background Fallback]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS5{Document Length Profile}
    nodeS5 -- Short Documents --> nodeS6[Prefer Dirichlet Prior mu approx 2000]
    nodeS5 -- Long Documents --> nodeS7[Prefer Jelinek Mercer lambda approx 0.1]
    nodeS5 -- Mixed Corpus --> nodeS8[Use Absolute Discounting delta approx 0.7]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS9[Smoothed P t d]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS9
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9
    nodeS4 --> nodeS9
```

### 4.3 Probability Transformation Schematic (MLE → Smoothed Estimate)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1[Raw Term Frequency tf t d] --> nodeT2[Divide by Document Length vert d vert]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[MLE Estimate P MLE t d]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4{MLE equals zero}
    nodeT4 -- Yes --> nodeT5[Blend with Collection Probability P t C]
    nodeT4 -- No --> nodeT6[Blend with Collection Probability P t C weighted by 1 minus lambda]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT7[Smoothed Estimate P t d]
    nodeT6 --> nodeT7
    nodeT7 --> nodeT8[Log Space Scoring for Numerical Stability]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** When asked to "draw the QLM pipeline" in a 14-mark question, reproduce **all three functional levels**: (1) input preprocessing, (2) smoothing strategy selection, (3) log-likelihood scoring and ranking. The examiners reward *labeled arrows* and *explicit smoothing parameters*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Query Likelihood Model. State Bayes' Theorem as used in deriving the QLM ranking function.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Query Likelihood Model (QLM)** is a probabilistic information retrieval model that ranks documents $d$ by the probability $P(q \mid d)$ that the query $q$ would be generated from a probabilistic language model $M_d$ associated with document $d$.

Bayes' Theorem is applied to express the posterior probability of a document given the query:

$$P(d \mid q) = \frac{P(q \mid d) \cdot P(d)}{P(q)}$$

Since $P(q)$ is constant across documents for a fixed query and $P(d)$ is typically assumed uniform, the ranking reduces to maximizing $P(q \mid d)$ — the **query generation likelihood**.

*(Allocation: 1.5 marks for definition, 1 mark for Bayes' Theorem formula, 0.5 marks for the simplification reasoning.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Why is smoothing essential in the Query Likelihood Model? List any two smoothing methods with their formulas.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Smoothing is **essential** because the Maximum Likelihood Estimate assigns **zero probability** to any term not present in the document. A single unseen query term in $d$ would then make $P(q \mid d) = 0$ for that document, eliminating it from retrieval regardless of how well it matches the *other* query terms. Smoothing redistributes probability mass from seen terms to unseen terms by interpolating with the collection model $P(t \mid C)$, ensuring all documents have non-zero likelihood.

**Two Smoothing Methods:**

1. **Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing:**
$$P_{JM}(t \mid d) = \lambda \cdot P_{MLE}(t \mid d) + (1 - \lambda) \cdot P(t \mid C)$$

2. **Dirichlet Prior Smoothing:**
$$P_{Dir}(t \mid d) = \frac{tf(t,d) + \mu \cdot P(t \mid C)}{\vert d \vert + \mu}$$

*(Allocation: 1.5 marks for the "why," 0.75 marks each for the two methods.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting:**
> 1. **Never** write the Bayes' Theorem step without *explicitly* stating which terms are dropped and why — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the justification is missing.
> 2. **Never** write the smoothing formula without specifying the **range of the parameter** (e.g., $\lambda \in [0,1]$) — parameter awareness is a high-value scoring cue.
> 3. **Always** take the **log** of the likelihood in your final scoring expression; failure to do so loses 1 mark for "non-standard scoring form."
> 4. **Always** distinguish between $P_{MLE}(t \mid d)$ and the smoothed $P(t \mid d)$ — using MLE inside a smoothed ranking expression is a common error that forfeits 2 marks.
> 5. In numerical problems, **show every arithmetic step** — the KTU valuation key penalizes skipping intermediate values like $0.7 \times 0.4 = 0.28$.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose A or B**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the Query Likelihood Model ranking function starting from Bayes' Theorem. State all assumptions clearly. Show the transformation from the product form to the log-sum form.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the following corpus of 3 documents:
- $d_1 =$ "machine learning algorithms" ($\vert d_1 \vert = 3$)
- $d_2 =$ "deep learning neural networks" ($\vert d_2 \vert = 4$)
- $d_3 =$ "database systems design" ($\vert d_3 \vert = 3$)

Collection totals: "machine" appears 1 time, "learning" appears 2 times, "algorithms" 1 time, "deep" 1 time, "neural" 1 time, "networks" 1 time, "database" 1 time, "systems" 1 time, "design" 1 time. Total collection length = 14.

Query: $q =$ "machine learning"
Smoothing: **Jelinek-Mercer** with $\lambda = 0.5$.

Compute $\log P(q \mid d)$ for each document and rank them.

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Posterior formulation (1 mark)**

By Bayes' Theorem, the probability that document $d$ is the source of query $q$ is:

$$P(d \mid q) = \frac{P(q \mid d) \cdot P(d)}{P(q)}$$

**Step 2: Identifying constants (1 mark)**

- $P(q)$ is constant across all documents for a fixed query → **dropped from ranking**.
- $P(d) = \frac{1}{N}$ is uniform (no prior bias) → **dropped from ranking**.

Therefore:
$$\text{score}(q, d) = P(q \mid d)$$

**Step 3: Unigram (bag-of-words) assumption (1 mark)**

Each query term is generated independently from $M_d$:
$$P(q \mid d) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid d)$$

**Step 4: Grouping repeated terms (1.5 marks)**

Using $tf(t,q)$ to count occurrences of each unique term $t$ in the query:
$$P(q \mid d) = \prod_{t \in q} P(t \mid d)^{tf(t,q)}$$

**Step 5: Log transformation (1 mark)**

Apply the natural logarithm (monotonic, so ranking is preserved):
$$\log P(q \mid d) = \sum_{t \in q} tf(t,q) \cdot \log P(t \mid d)$$

**Step 6: Final ranking statement (1.5 marks)**

Documents are ranked in descending order of $\log P(q \mid d)$. In practice, $P(t \mid d)$ is replaced by a smoothed estimate $P_{JM}(t \mid d)$ or $P_{Dir}(t \mid d)$ to avoid zero probabilities.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute Collection Probabilities $P(t \mid C)$ (1.5 marks)**

Using total collection length = 14:
- $P(\text{machine} \mid C) = 1/14 \approx 0.0714$
- $P(\text{learning} \mid C) = 2/14 \approx 0.1429$

**Step 2: Compute MLE for each document (1.5 marks)**

For $d_1$: $P_{MLE}(\text{machine} \mid d_1) = 1/3 \approx 0.3333$, $P_{MLE}(\text{learning} \mid d_1) = 1/3 \approx 0.3333$.

For $d_2$: $P_{MLE}(\text{machine} \mid d_2) = 0$, $P_{MLE}(\text{learning} \mid d_2) = 1/4 = 0.25$.

For $d_3$: $P_{MLE}(\text{machine} \mid d_3) = 0$, $P_{MLE}(\text{learning} \mid d_3) = 0$.

**Step 3: Apply Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing with $\lambda = 0.5$ (2 marks)**

Using $P_{JM}(t \mid d) = 0.5 \cdot P_{MLE}(t \mid d) + 0.5 \cdot P(t \mid C)$:

- $d_1$: $P_{JM}(\text{machine} \mid d_1) = 0.5(0.3333) + 0.5(0.0714) = 0.1667 + 0.0357 = 0.2024$
- $d_1$: $P_{JM}(\text{learning} \mid d_1) = 0.5(0.3333) + 0.5(0.1429) = 0.1667 + 0.0714 = 0.2381$
- $d_2$: $P_{JM}(\text{machine} \mid d_2) = 0.5(0) + 0.5(0.0714) = 0.0357$
- $d_2$: $P_{JM}(\text{learning} \mid d_2) = 0.5(0.25) + 0.5(0.1429) = 0.125 + 0.0714 = 0.1964$
- $d_3$: $P_{JM}(\text{machine} \mid d_3) = 0.5(0) + 0.5(0.0714) = 0.0357$
- $d_3$: $P_{JM}(\text{learning} \mid d_3) = 0.5(0) + 0.5(0.1429) = 0.0714$

**Step 4: Compute log-likelihood scores (1.5 marks)**

- $\log P(q \mid d_1) = \log(0.2024) + \log(0.2381) = -1.597 + -1.435 = -3.032$
- $\log P(q \mid d_2) = \log(0.0357) + \log(0.1964) = -3.332 + -1.627 = -4.959$
- $\log P(q \mid d_3) = \log(0.0357) + \log(0.0714) = -3.332 + -2.639 = -5.971$

**Step 5: Final Ranking (0.5 marks)**

Ranking (higher = better):
1. $d_1$ ($-3.032$) ✓ **Most relevant**
2. $d_2$ ($-4.959$)
3. $d_3$ ($-5.971$)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the three major smoothing techniques in the Query Likelihood Model — Jelinek-Mercer, Dirichlet Prior, and Absolute Discounting. Compare their behavior for short versus long documents, and state typical parameter values.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a corpus of 2 documents:
- $d_1 =$ "spam email filter detection" ($\vert d_1 \vert = 4$)
- $d_2 =$ "ham inbox mail" ($\vert d_2 \vert = 3$)

Collection probability: $P(\text{spam} \mid C) = 0.1$, $P(\text{detection} \mid C) = 0.1$, $P(\text{email} \mid C) = 0.05$, $P(\text{filter} \mid C) = 0.05$, and all other probabilities are 0.05 (residual).

Query: $q =$ "spam detection"
Use **Dirichlet Prior smoothing** with $\mu = 2$.

Compute the smoothed probabilities and the log-likelihood score for both documents. Justify which document is more relevant.

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Comparison of Smoothing Techniques [7 Marks]:**

| Smoothing Method | Formula | Best Suited For | Parameter Behavior | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Jelinek-Mercer** | $\lambda \cdot P_{MLE} + (1-\lambda) \cdot P(t \mid C)$ | General-purpose, mixed corpora | $\lambda$ = weight on document; high $\lambda$ = trust document more | $\lambda \in [0.1, 0.7]$ |
| **Dirichlet Prior** | $\dfrac{tf + \mu P(t \mid C)}{\vert d \vert + \mu}$ | Variable-length documents; **better for short docs** | $\mu$ = pseudo-counts from background; larger $\mu$ = smoother | $\mu \in [1000, 3000]$ |
| **Absolute Discounting** | $\dfrac{\max(tf - \delta, 0)}{\vert d \vert} + \dfrac{\delta \vert d \vert_u}{\vert d \vert} P(t \mid C)$ | Term-frequency-sensitive tasks | $\delta$ = subtractive discount; $\vert d \vert_u$ redistributes mass | $\delta \in [0.0, 1.0]$ |

**Document-Length Behavior [3 marks]:**

- **Jelinek-Mercer:** Linear interpolation — short and long documents are treated identically. Weakness: does not adapt smoothing strength to document length.
- **Dirichlet Prior:** **Length-adaptive** — for short documents ($\vert d \vert \ll \mu$), the smoothed estimate is dominated by the collection model; for long documents ($\vert d \vert \gg \mu$), it converges to MLE. This is theoretically elegant and practically robust.
- **Absolute Discounting:** Subtracts a fixed $\delta$ from every observed count and redistributes the freed probability mass uniformly across unique terms — particularly effective for tasks where term frequency distributions are highly skewed.

**Justification [1 mark]:** Dirichlet Prior is the most theoretically grounded (Bayesian posterior mean under a conjugate prior) and is generally preferred in practice.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: MLE Estimates (1.5 marks)**

For $d_1$:
- $P_{MLE}(\text{spam} \mid d_1) = 1/4 = 0.25$
- $P_{MLE}(\text{detection} \mid d_1) = 1/4 = 0.25$

For $d_2$:
- $P_{MLE}(\text{spam} \mid d_2) = 0/3 = 0$
- $P_{MLE}(\text{detection} \mid d_2) = 0/3 = 0$

**Step 2: Apply Dirichlet Prior Smoothing with $\mu = 2$ (3 marks)**

Using $P_{Dir}(t \mid d) = \dfrac{tf(t,d) + \mu \cdot P(t \mid C)}{\vert d \vert + \mu}$:

For $d_1$ ($\vert d_1 \vert + \mu = 4 + 2 = 6$):
- $P_{Dir}(\text{spam} \mid d_1) = (1 + 2 \cdot 0.1) / 6 = (1 + 0.2) / 6 = 1.2 / 6 = 0.2000$
- $P_{Dir}(\text{detection} \mid d_1) = (1 + 2 \cdot 0.1) / 6 = 1.2 / 6 = 0.2000$

For $d_2$ ($\vert d_2 \vert + \mu = 3 + 2 = 5$):
- $P_{Dir}(\text{spam} \mid d_2) = (0 + 2 \cdot 0.1) / 5 = 0.2 / 5 = 0.0400$
- $P_{Dir}(\text{detection} \mid d_2) = (0 + 2 \cdot 0.1) / 5 = 0.2 / 5 = 0.0400$

**Step 3: Compute log-likelihood scores (1.5 marks)**

- $\log P(q \mid d_1) = \log(0.20) + \log(0.20) = -1.609 + -1.609 = -3.218$
- $\log P(q \mid d_2) = \log(0.04) + \log(0.04) = -3.219 + -3.219 = -6.438$

**Step 4: Ranking and Justification (1 mark)**

$d_1$ scores $-3.218$, while $d_2$ scores $-6.438$. Since $-3.218 > -6.438$, **$d_1$ is more relevant** — it explicitly contains both query terms, and Dirichlet smoothing has appropriately discounted its MLE advantage only slightly, while falling back to the (much smaller) collection probability for $d_2$, which contains no query terms.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before every revision session.**

- ✅ **QLM = rank by $P(q \mid d)$, not $P(d \mid q)$** — remember the inversion via Bayes' Theorem.
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(d \mid q) = \dfrac{P(q \mid d) \cdot P(d)}{P(q)}$; drop $P(q)$ and $P(d)$ for ranking.
- ✅ **MLE Formula:** $P_{MLE}(t \mid d) = \dfrac{tf(t,d)}{\vert d \vert}$ — *zero for unseen terms* (the critical flaw).
- ✅ **Unigram Assumption:** Query terms are conditionally independent — *order is ignored*.
- ✅ **Log-Scoring Equation:** $\log P(q \mid d) = \sum_{t \in q} tf(t,q) \cdot \log P(t \mid d)$ — always use this form.
- ✅ **Jelinek-Mercer:** Linear interpolation; parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$; typical $\lambda \in [0.1, 0.7]$.
- ✅ **Dirichlet Prior:** $P_{Dir}(t \mid d) = \dfrac{tf(t,d) + \mu \cdot P(t \mid C)}{\vert d \vert + \mu}$; parameter $\mu \approx 1000\text{–}3000$; **length-adaptive** (best for short documents).
- ✅ **Absolute Discounting:** Subtracts $\delta$ from each observed count; redistributes mass to unseen terms.
- ✅ **Smoothing is mandatory** — without it, a single unseen query term makes $P(q \mid d) = 0$.
- ✅ **Collection Model:** $P(t \mid C) = \dfrac{tf(t,C)}{\sum_{t'} tf(t',C)}$ — computed once globally.
- ✅ **QLM ↔ BM25 Connection:** BM25 is *equivalent* to QLM with Dirichlet smoothing under specific parameter settings — knowing this shows depth in viva.
- ✅ **KL-Divergence Equivalence:** $P(q \mid d) \propto \exp(-KL(M_q \Vert M_d))$ — connects QLM to cross-entropy minimization (bonus knowledge).
- ✅ **Typical Exam Mistakes to Avoid:** Using MLE inside smoothed rankings, forgetting the log, omitting parameter ranges, skipping arithmetic in numericals.

---

*End of Module 4 — Query Likelihood Model Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCCDT503 Data Analytics*

topics.title: Naïve Bayes Text Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDT503 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Naïve Bayes Text Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Naïve Bayes Text Classification** is a probabilistic supervised machine learning algorithm based on applying **Bayes' Theorem** with a *naïve* (strong) assumption of **conditional independence** between every pair of features (words/tokens) given the class label. It is widely used as a baseline model for document categorization tasks such as spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and news classification.

Mathematically, for a document $D$ represented by a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ of word occurrences, the classifier assigns the class $\hat{c}$ from a finite set $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k\}$ that maximizes the posterior probability:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$$

By Bayes' Theorem, this is rewritten as:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across all classes, it is ignored during maximization, yielding the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** decision rule.

### 1.2 The "Naïve" Assumption

The "naïve" part is the assumption that all features (words) are mutually independent given the class. This allows the joint likelihood to be factorized as a product of individual conditional probabilities:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

This is technically false for natural language (words like *"machine"* and *"learning"* co-occur frequently), but in practice, it works remarkably well for text and serves as a fast, high-accuracy baseline.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Emergency Room Doctor:**
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing a disease from symptoms. She doesn't compute a complex joint distribution over all possible symptom combinations. Instead, she asks: *"Given the patient has Disease A, how likely is Fever? How likely is Cough? How likely is Fatigue?"* She then **multiplies these individual likelihoods** and weights them by the prior probability of Disease A. Naïve Bayes does exactly this — it asks, *"Given this document is SPAM, how likely is the word 'free'? How likely is 'winner'? How likely is 'urgent'?"* and multiplies the answers, picking the class with the highest combined score.

### 1.4 Why Naïve Bayes for Text?

- **Speed**: Linear in the number of training examples and features.
- **Low memory footprint**: Only needs to store word-count statistics per class.
- **Handles high-dimensional sparse data** (like a vocabulary of 50,000+ words) gracefully.
- **Probabilistic output**: Produces calibrated probability estimates, not just labels.
- **Strong baseline**: Often surprisingly hard to beat for small/medium text corpora.

### 1.5 Key Probabilistic Components

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Prior** | $P(c)$ | Probability of class $c$ before seeing the document |
| **Likelihood** | $P(x_i \mid c)$ | Probability of feature $x_i$ occurring in documents of class $c$ |
| **Evidence** | $P(\mathbf{x})$ | Probability of observing the document (normalizing constant) |
| **Posterior** | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Updated probability of class $c$ after seeing the document |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bayes' Theorem — The Foundation

Bayes' Theorem describes the probability of an event based on prior knowledge of conditions related to the event:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Where:
- $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ = **Posterior** probability (what we want)
- $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$ = **Likelihood** (probability of evidence given hypothesis)
- $P(c)$ = **Prior** probability (initial belief)
- $P(\mathbf{x})$ = **Evidence** or marginal probability (normalizer)

### 2.2 Decomposing the Naïve Bayes Decision Rule

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 2 — Drop the denominator** (constant for all classes during argmax):

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the naïve conditional independence assumption:**

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid c) \cdots P(x_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

**Step 4 — The Final Naïve Bayes Classifier Rule:**

$$\boxed{\;\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)\;}$$

### 2.3 Text Classification Variants of Naïve Bayes

> [!NOTE]
> **Three primary variants** are used in text classification. The choice depends on how word occurrences are modeled.

#### (a) Multinomial Naïve Bayes (MNB)
- Models **word counts** in a document as a multinomial distribution.
- Feature $x_i$ = **count** of word $i$ in document.
- Likelihood:
$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \propto \prod_{i=1}^{\vert V \vert} P(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}$$
- **Best for**: Document classification with TF or TF-IDF features.

#### (b) Bernoulli Naïve Bayes (BNB)
- Models **presence/absence** of each word (binary features).
- Feature $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates whether word $i$ appears.
- Likelihood:
$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{\vert V \vert} P(w_i \mid c)^{x_i} \cdot \big(1 - P(w_i \mid c)\big)^{(1-x_i)}$$
- **Best for**: Short texts, sentiment classification, small vocabularies.

#### (c) Complement Naïve Bayes (CNB)
- A normalization of MNB suited for **imbalanced text datasets**.
- Computes likelihood using the *complement* (all other classes).

### 2.4 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) of Parameters

For Multinomial NB, parameters are estimated from training data by counting:

$$P(c_j) = \frac{N_{c_j}}{N_{\text{total}}}$$

$$P(w_i \mid c_j) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c_j)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c_j)}$$

Where $N_{c_j}$ = number of training documents in class $c_j$, and $\text{count}(w_i, c_j)$ = total occurrences of word $w_i$ across all documents in class $c_j$.

### 2.5 Laplace (Additive) Smoothing — The Zero-Probability Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a word never appears in the training documents of a particular class, $P(w_i \mid c) = 0$, which collapses the **entire product to zero**, killing the classifier. **Laplace Smoothing** fixes this by adding a smoothing constant $\alpha \geq 0$ (typically $\alpha = 1$):

$$P(w_i \mid c_j) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c_j) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c_j) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

Where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size. This guarantees every probability is strictly positive.

### 2.6 The Log-Sum Trick (Numerical Stability)

Multiplying many small probabilities (e.g., 0.0001) causes **floating-point underflow** to zero. The standard fix is to take the **logarithm** (a monotonic transformation, so argmax is preserved):

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot \log P(w_i \mid c) \right]$$

This converts products to summations — faster, more stable, and numerically safe.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | **Bayes' Theorem** | Core derivation |
| 2 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid c)$ | **NB Decision Rule** | Classification |
| 3 | $P(c_j) = \dfrac{N_{c_j}}{N}$ | **Class Prior** | Training |
| 4 | $P(w_i \mid c_j) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, c_j)}{\sum_{w} \text{count}(w, c_j)}$ | **Word Likelihood (MLE)** | Training |
| 5 | $P(w_i \mid c_j) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, c_j) + \alpha}{\sum_{w} \text{count}(w, c_j) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | **Laplace Smoothed Likelihood** | Training (zero-frequency fix) |
| 6 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i} x_i \log P(w_i \mid c) \right]$ | **Log-Space NB** | Inference (stability) |
| 7 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | **Performance Metric** | Evaluation |
| 8 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | **F1 Score** | Evaluation |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Naïve Bayes is the silent workhorse behind:
- **Gmail/Mail spam filters** (industry-standard baseline)
- **Sentiment analysis** in social media monitoring
- **News article categorization** (Reuters, AG News datasets)
- **Medical text classification** (radiology report tagging)
- **Real-time document routing** in enterprise pipelines where latency matters

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to the Naïve Bayes Classifier

We begin with the goal: given a document $D$ with features $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, find the most probable class.

**Step 1 — Posterior formulation:**

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 2 — Decision rule (MAP estimate):**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{c \in C} \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 3 — Drop the constant denominator** (since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is identical for every class):

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the chain rule of probability to expand the joint likelihood:**

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, c) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, c) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \dots, x_{n-1}, c)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the naïve conditional independence assumption** (each $x_i$ independent of all others given $c$):

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \approx P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid c) \cdot P(x_3 \mid c) \cdots P(x_n \mid c)$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form:**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

**Step 7 — Log-space form (for numerical stability):**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c)$$

### 3.2 Complete Worked Example: Spam Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A small training corpus of 6 SMS messages labeled **Spam (S)** or **Ham (H)**. We classify a new message: *"free urgent meeting"*.

**Training Data:**

| # | Message | Words | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | free offer today | free, offer, today | Spam |
| 2 | free entry win | free, entry, win | Spam |
| 3 | urgent action required | urgent, action, required | Spam |
| 4 | meeting at noon | meeting, at, noon | Ham |
| 5 | call me later | call, me, later | Ham |
| 6 | meeting tomorrow please | meeting, tomorrow, please | Ham |

**Step 1 — Compute Class Priors:**

$$P(S) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

$$P(H) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Build Vocabulary and Count Words Per Class.**

Vocabulary $V$ = {free, offer, today, entry, win, urgent, action, required, meeting, at, noon, call, me, later, tomorrow, please}, so $\vert V \vert = 16$.

Total word count in Spam: 9 (free×2, offer, today, entry, win, urgent, action, required)  
Total word count in Ham: 11 (meeting×2, at, noon, call, me, later, tomorrow, please)

**Step 3 — Compute Word Likelihoods (with Laplace smoothing, $\alpha = 1$):**

$$P(\text{free} \mid S) = \frac{2 + 1}{9 + 16} = \frac{3}{25} = 0.12$$

$$P(\text{urgent} \mid S) = \frac{1 + 1}{9 + 16} = \frac{2}{25} = 0.08$$

$$P(\text{meeting} \mid S) = \frac{0 + 1}{9 + 16} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04$$

$$P(\text{free} \mid H) = \frac{0 + 1}{11 + 16} = \frac{1}{27} \approx 0.0370$$

$$P(\text{urgent} \mid H) = \frac{0 + 1}{11 + 16} = \frac{1}{27} \approx 0.0370$$

$$P(\text{meeting} \mid H) = \frac{2 + 1}{11 + 16} = \frac{3}{27} \approx 0.1111$$

**Step 4 — Compute Posterior Scores for Test Document $\mathbf{x} = (\text{free}, \text{urgent}, \text{meeting})$:**

**Spam score:**

$$P(S) \cdot P(\text{free} \mid S) \cdot P(\text{urgent} \mid S) \cdot P(\text{meeting} \mid S) = 0.5 \times 0.12 \times 0.08 \times 0.04$$

$$= 0.5 \times 0.000384 = 0.000192$$

**Ham score:**

$$P(H) \cdot P(\text{free} \mid H) \cdot P(\text{urgent} \mid H) \cdot P(\text{meeting} \mid H) = 0.5 \times 0.0370 \times 0.0370 \times 0.1111$$

$$\approx 0.5 \times 0.0001522 \approx 0.0000761$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max \{0.000192, \; 0.0000761\} = \text{Spam}$$

**Step 6 — Verification via log-space:**

$$\log P(S \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto \log(0.5) + \log(0.12) + \log(0.08) + \log(0.04)$$

$$= -0.693 + (-2.120) + (-2.526) + (-3.219) = -8.558$$

$$\log P(H \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto \log(0.5) + \log(0.0370) + \log(0.0370) + \log(0.1111)$$

$$= -0.693 + (-3.297) + (-3.297) + (-2.197) = -9.484$$

Since $-8.558 > -9.484$, the classifier again predicts **Spam** ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Smoothing on Zero-Frequency Words

| Word | $\text{count}(w, S)$ | $P(w \mid S)$ no smoothing | $P(w \mid S)$ with $\alpha=1$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| free | 2 | 2/9 = 0.2222 | 3/25 = 0.1200 |
| urgent | 1 | 1/9 = 0.1111 | 2/25 = 0.0800 |
| meeting | 0 | **0/9 = 0.0000** ⚠️ | 1/25 = 0.0400 |
| unknown | 0 | **0/9 = 0.0000** ⚠️ | 1/25 = 0.0400 |

> [!WARNING]
> Without smoothing, **any single unseen word forces the entire product to zero**, effectively blacklisting the message. Laplace smoothing is **mandatory in production** text classifiers.

### 3.4 Confusion Matrix for Evaluation (KTU Exam Favorite)

After classification, build a **Confusion Matrix** on the test set:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

Derived metrics:

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Naïve Bayes Text Classification** is a probabilistic supervised machine learning algorithm based on applying **Bayes' Theorem** with a *naïve* (strong) assumption of **conditional independence** between every pair of features (words/tokens) given the class label. It is widely used as a baseline model for document categorization tasks such as spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and news classification.

Mathematically, for a document $D$ represented by a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ of word occurrences, the classifier assigns the class $\hat{c}$ from a finite set $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k\}$ that **maximizes the posterior probability**:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$$

By Bayes' Theorem, this is rewritten as:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across all classes, it is ignored during maximization, yielding the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** decision rule.

### 1.2 The "Naïve" Assumption

The "naïve" part is the assumption that all features (words) are mutually independent given the class. This allows the joint likelihood to be factorized as a product of individual conditional probabilities:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

This is technically false for natural language (words like *"machine"* and *"learning"* co-occur frequently), but in practice, it works remarkably well for text and serves as a fast, high-accuracy baseline.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Emergency Room Doctor:**
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing a disease from symptoms. She doesn't compute a complex joint distribution over all possible symptom combinations. Instead, she asks: *"Given the patient has Disease A, how likely is Fever? How likely is Cough? How likely is Fatigue?"* She then **multiplies these individual likelihoods** and weights them by the prior probability of Disease A. Naïve Bayes does exactly this — it asks, *"Given this document is SPAM, how likely is the word 'free'? How likely is 'winner'? How likely is 'urgent'?"* and multiplies the answers, picking the class with the highest combined score.

### 1.4 Why Naïve Bayes for Text?

- **Speed**: Linear in the number of training examples and features.
- **Low memory footprint**: Only needs to store word-count statistics per class.
- **Handles high-dimensional sparse data** (like a vocabulary of 50,000+ words) gracefully.
- **Probabilistic output**: Produces calibrated probability estimates, not just labels.
- **Strong baseline**: Often surprisingly hard to beat for small/medium text corpora.

### 1.5 Key Probabilistic Components

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Prior** | $P(c)$ | Probability of class $c$ before seeing the document |
| **Likelihood** | $P(x_i \mid c)$ | Probability of feature $x_i$ occurring in documents of class $c$ |
| **Evidence** | $P(\mathbf{x})$ | Probability of observing the document (normalizing constant) |
| **Posterior** | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Updated probability of class $c$ after seeing the document |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bayes' Theorem — The Foundation

Bayes' Theorem describes the probability of an event based on prior knowledge of conditions related to the event:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Where:
- $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ = **Posterior** probability (what we want to compute)
- $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$ = **Likelihood** (probability of evidence given hypothesis)
- $P(c)$ = **Prior** probability (initial belief about the class)
- $P(\mathbf{x})$ = **Evidence** or marginal probability (normalizing constant)

### 2.2 Decomposing the Naïve Bayes Decision Rule

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 2 — Drop the denominator** (constant for all classes during argmax):

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the naïve conditional independence assumption:**

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid c) \cdots P(x_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

**Step 4 — The Final Naïve Bayes Classifier Rule:**

$$\boxed{\;\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)\;}$$

### 2.3 Text Classification Variants of Naïve Bayes

> [!NOTE]
> **Three primary variants** are used in text classification. The choice depends on how word occurrences are modeled.

#### (a) Multinomial Naïve Bayes (MNB)
- Models **word counts** in a document as a multinomial distribution.
- Feature $x_i$ = **count** of word $i$ in document.
- Likelihood:
$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \propto \prod_{i=1}^{\vert V \vert} P(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}$$
- **Best for**: Document classification with TF or TF-IDF features.

#### (b) Bernoulli Naïve Bayes (BNB)
- Models **presence/absence** of each word (binary features).
- Feature $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates whether word $i$ appears.
- Likelihood:
$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{\vert V \vert} P(w_i \mid c)^{x_i} \cdot \big(1 - P(w_i \mid c)\big)^{(1-x_i)}$$
- **Best for**: Short texts, sentiment classification, small vocabularies.

#### (c) Complement Naïve Bayes (CNB)
- A normalization of MNB suited for **imbalanced text datasets**.
- Computes likelihood using the *complement* (all other classes).

### 2.4 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) of Parameters

For Multinomial NB, parameters are estimated from training data by counting:

$$P(c_j) = \frac{N_{c_j}}{N_{\text{total}}}$$

$$P(w_i \mid c_j) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c_j)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c_j)}$$

Where $N_{c_j}$ = number of training documents in class $c_j$, and $\text{count}(w_i, c_j)$ = total occurrences of word $w_i$ across all documents in class $c_j$.

### 2.5 Laplace (Additive) Smoothing — The Zero-Probability Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a word never appears in the training documents of a particular class, $P(w_i \mid c) = 0$, which collapses the **entire product to zero**, killing the classifier. **Laplace Smoothing** fixes this by adding a smoothing constant $\alpha \geq 0$ (typically $\alpha = 1$):

$$P(w_i \mid c_j) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c_j) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c_j) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

Where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size. This guarantees every probability is strictly positive.

### 2.6 The Log-Sum Trick (Numerical Stability)

Multiplying many small probabilities (e.g., 0.0001) causes **floating-point underflow** to zero. The standard fix is to take the **logarithm** (a monotonic transformation, so argmax is preserved):

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot \log P(w_i \mid c) \right]$$

This converts products to summations — faster, more stable, and numerically safe.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | **Bayes Theorem** | Core derivation |
| 2 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid c)$ | **NB Decision Rule** | Classification |
| 3 | $P(c_j) = \dfrac{N_{c_j}}{N}$ | **Class Prior** | Training |
| 4 | $P(w_i \mid c_j) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, c_j)}{\sum_{w} \text{count}(w, c_j)}$ | **Word Likelihood MLE** | Training |
| 5 | $P(w_i \mid c_j) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, c_j) + \alpha}{\sum_{w} \text{count}(w, c_j) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | **Laplace Smoothed Likelihood** | Training zero-frequency fix |
| 6 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i} x_i \log P(w_i \mid c) \right]$ | **Log Space NB** | Inference stability |
| 7 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | **Performance Metric** | Evaluation |
| 8 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | **F1 Score** | Evaluation |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Naïve Bayes is the silent workhorse behind:
- **Gmail/Mail spam filters** (industry-standard baseline)
- **Sentiment analysis** in social media monitoring
- **News article categorization** (Reuters, AG News datasets)
- **Medical text classification** (radiology report tagging)
- **Real-time document routing** in enterprise pipelines where latency matters
